item.title: You can now walk the entire coast of England – the King Charles III Coast Path is finally open and it’s bigger than you think
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: The brand new walking trail stretches the entirety of England’s coast and has been named in honour of King Charles III, who opened it back in March
item.content:encoded:
After 18 years of planning, the King Charles III England Coast Path has finally opened, and it's officially the largest managed coastal route in the world.

Traversing 2700 miles, the path weaves around England's most beautiful coastal spots, and is also the UK's longest coastal path, connecting our loveliest seaside towns and cities with our most stunning beaches, coves and dramatic cliffs, even unlocking some parts of England's coast for the very first time.

If you're planning to walk the path, or at least some of it, King Charles III England Coast Path 2026: The complete hiking and walking companion is available on Amazon, so you can explore the coastline off-grid and take in some of Europe's most beautiful views.

The King Charles III England Coast Path has been in the pipeline for 18 years and has been meticulously planned by Natural England. It is now finally open for business.

The project was first initiated under Gordon Brown's government back in 2004, but the route required new legislation to be passed (the Marine and Coastal Access Act, so it has taken nearly two decades until it was ready.

Much of the route existed already, but around 1700 miles of existing pathway have been improved with new signage, bridges and boardwalks. Approximately 1000 miles of the route were created especially for the King Charles III England Coast Path, which included several areas that had previously been off-limits to the public.

The route was named in 2023 in honour of King Charles III, who was crowned in May of that year. In March 2026, the king inaugurated the path at the Seven Sisters Country Park in East Sussex. While there, he also opened The Seven Sisters National Nature Reserve, which is the 13th National Nature Reserve to be opened as part of the King's Series of National Nature Reserves, which aims to create and expand 25 reserves by 2027.

View this post on Instagram

As of May 2026, around 80% of the path is now open to the public, and the remaining sections will be set up and running by the end of the year.

When it is completed, the 2700-mile-long trail will be the longest managed coastal path in the world. Hikers will also now be able to walk a continuous route around the whole of the UK, should they wish.

The King Charles III England Coast Path links with the Wales Coast Path, which stretches 870 miles around the Welsh Coast. There is currently no official walking route around the coast of Scotland, but much of it is already accessible due to the Scottish 'right to roam' law.

You can find all the information you need about the trail and planning your routes via the National Trails website, where they even have suggestions for holidays around the path.

If you're interested in walking the Welsh coast path, Walking the Wales Coast Path is available on Amazon and will guide you on your journey from Chester to Chepstow.

item.title: Where to catch Scotland’s most spectacular Highland Games this summer, complete with kilts, cabers and clans
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Discover the origins of Scottish Highland games, and find the best events to attend this summer
item.content:encoded:
What could be more Scottish than Highland Games? An all-round celebration of Scottish culture, complete with tartan, clans, bagpipes and mighty displays of strength.

Highland games have been one of Scotland's most important traditions for hundreds of years, though their modern-day popularity is owed to the Victorians, when they were formally reintroduced as part of the revival of Tartan and Highland Culture by Sir Walter Scott and then given a royal seal of approval by Queen Victoria.

However, the roots of Highland games date back to the 11th century, when King Malcolm III called a foot race to the summit of Craig Choinnich to find the fastest runner in the land to become his personal courier. The games then evolved to include events that tested not only muscles and speed of the strongest clan members, but also creative dance and music skills to keep kings, queens, and clan chiefs entertained. David Webster's The World History of Highland Games is available on Amazon and will guide you through the games from ancient times to modern day.

Highland games as we know them today have been celebrated around Scotland since the 1800s, and many of the events listed below originally began in Victorian times. Taking place in the summer season between May and September, every event has its own unique character and traditions. Here are some of the best events happening in Scotland this summer for you to get a taste of this most Scottish of celebrations.

The 10 best Highland Games in Scotland this summer

1. Atholl Highland Gathering, Blair Castle, Perthshire

24 May 2026

Set in the grounds of the beautiful Blair Castle and overseen by the Marquis of Tullibardine, heir to the 12th Duke of Atholl, the Atholl Gathering kicks off on Sunday 24 May with a parade by the Atholl Highlanders, the only private army in Europe – established 184 years ago – accompanied by pipes and drums. The games will showcase competitors from around the globe battling it out centre stage in traditional events such as tossing the caber and throwing the hammer.

2. Strathmore Highland Games, Angus

14 June 2026

View this post on Instagram

Enjoy a day of traditional events in the glorious grounds of the beautiful Angus Castle at the Strathmore Highland Games. Spectactors can watch track and heavy events, including shot put, and hammer throwing, alongside piping competitions, Highland dancing and the customary Salute to the Chieftain at the end of the day.

3. Aberdeen Highland Games, Aberdeen

4 July 2026

View this post on Instagram

Held at Hazelhead Park, this is a popular event that the whole family will be able to enjoy. With traditional Highland games events alongside stage entertainment from professional performers, as well as local community groups, plus the chance to try a range of activities, from clay pigeon shooting to an inflatable assault course, this is one not to be missed.

4. Luss Highland Gathering, Loch Lomond

4 July 2026

It would be hard to find a more beautiful location for the Luss Highland Gathering, which has been held annually since 1875 (aside from a break during the World Wars). Since then, the games have remained relatively unchanged. Although sock-knitting competitions and shepherd’s crook-making have been dropped, traditional events such as caber tossing, hammer throwing and hill races have stayed, and of course, the event would not be complete without the rousing sound of the pipes and the swish of Highland dancers.

5. Locharron Highland Games, Wester Ross

18 July 2026

View this post on Instagram

The traditional Locharron Highland Games will be held on the beautiful Attadale Estate and will include a mixture of heavy events, track and field events and highland dancing and piping displays.

6. St Andrews Highland Games, St Andrews

26 July 2026

From humble beginnings in 1984, when they were first held on a Thursday afternoon in a field on the outskirts of St Andrews, the St Andrews Highland Games are now firmly established as some of the best in Scotland. This year the games will be held at Station Park in front of the town’s iconic Old Course, overlooking the sea. Enjoy traditional events alongside modern additions, including a cycling competition.

7. Crieff Highland Gathering, Perthshire

16 August 2026

View this post on Instagram

With the first event held in 1870, the Crieff games are known as ‘Scotland’s Premier Highland Games’, and include some much more modern events every year, including acrobatics, motorbike displays and a mini festival celebrating Celtic music.

8. Cowal Highland Gathering, Argyll

27 – 29 August 2026

View this post on Instagram

The largest Highland games in the world, the Cowal Highland Gathering attracts 23,000 visitors every year. First held in 1894, these games include every traditional event, along with some more modern additions, including mountain biking, a fun run, and traditional Celtic musicians. The highlight of the event is the salute to the Chieftain. At the end of the two-day event the massed pipers and drummers play the stirring Highland Laddie in unison as the sun sets over the Firth of Clyde.

9. Lonach Highland Gathering and Games, Aberdeenshire

22 August 2026

The Lonach Gathering returns this summer on its traditional date of the fourth Saturday in August. Famous for the march of the Lonach Highlanders, a unique body of non-military men, the Lonach Highland Games were established in 1823 and are some of the most famous in the world. The games will include heavyweight events alongside hill racing and Highland dancing, too.

10. The Braemar Gathering and Highland Games, Aberdeenshire

5 September 2026

Always held on the first Saturday in September, the Braemar Gathering and Highland Games is one of the most prestigious in the world, having frequently been attended by royalty. Participants can enter themselves to take part in traditional events, such as stone throwing and caber tossing, alongside music and Highland dancing, piping and drumming displays.

Did you know there are actually more Highland Games in the US and Canada every year than there are in Scotland? You can find all the dates and listings on the Scottish Banner website. George Fraser Black's book Scotland's Mark on America is available on Amazon and is a brilliant historical look at how the Scots who came to America changed US culture forever.

item.title: The 25 standout summer gardens across Britain worth planning a trip around this year
item.dc:creator: Janine Wookey, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From grand landscapes to cottage gardens where rose heads hang heavy in full bloom here’s our pick of our 25 favourite British summer gardens...
item.content:encoded:
From the cottage gardens of the populace to the landscaped sweeps of the aristocracy, the best British summer gardens have developed in many styles.

Due to the sheer number of stately home gardens in Britain, there is a diversity of garden styles in Britain that is unmatched anywhere in the world. For me, these are best enjoyed in the summer months, where the heady smell of roses entices you in, and you can wile away hours in the sunshine.

Although here I have attempted to pick just some of my favourites, the list of beautiful gardens in Britain is endless. If you're inspired to visit more, I'd recommend the Royal Horticultural Society's RHS Gardens of Great Britain and Ireland: Your Guide to 100 of the Most Beautiful Gardens, which is available on Amazon and will guide you to horticultural wonders wherever you are in the country.

For now, here are just some of my favourite British summer gardens that should be on every garden-lover's bucket list:

1. Highgrove Gardens, Gloucestershire

The new hot favourite style for British summer gardens is the Wildflower Meadow Garden, which grew from the concept of caring for the environment and its wildlife in particular, from birds to butterflies and worms to wasps.

The movement started at the top, with His Majesty The King, who, bucking the trend 30 years ago and incurring no small scorn, announced he would ensure his garden at Highgrove in Gloucestershire was completely organic.

Highgrove is a garden of many parts, but it is the wildflower meadow with some 32 species of endangered wild plants, from orchid to ox-eye daisies, that has won the King the most admiration for preserving the nation’s native flora, and seed from the meadow is now a popular buy at the Highgrove shop.

The King's own book, Highgrove: A Garden Celebrated, is available on Amazon and illustrates the beauty and thought behind this magical place.

2. Kailzie, Scottish Borders

View this post on Instagram

The large gardens (25 acres) at Kailzie near Peebles 215m (700ft) up on the Scottish Borders include a wild garden – at its best in spring – and a wonderful range of wild birds, as the River Tweed and its burn that rush through the garden provide excellent habitats for kingfishers, herons, oyster catchers, wagtails and dippers. This is one of the few places in Britain to see red squirrels and osprey.

3. Mottisfont Abbey, Hampshire

Mottisfont Abbey in Hampshire is one of the best British summer gardens for a serious fragrance thrill. Mottisfont was an Augustinian priory in 1201, then after the Dissolution of the Monasteries in 1536, Henry VIII gave the estate to his Lord Chamberlain, who turned it into a grand Tudor manor.

It was not until after the National Trust took it over in 1957, that the roses arrived in the walled garden, when the great rosarian and plantsman Graham Stuart Thomas filled the then derelict walled garden with his own collection of historic gallicas, damasks, centifolias, albas and moss roses. Today, these arching old roses tumble through the herbaceous borders as a major June highlight.

4. Borde Hill, West Sussex

The Rose Garden at Borde Hill is packed with 85 varieties of David Austin’s perfumed English Roses, thanks to the inspiration of owners Andrew John and Eleni Stephenson Clarke, who scoured old records for pictures of the garden from its Victorian and Edwardian heyday. The beds of roses, in hues of pink, white and magenta, are edged with lavender and nepeta to add to the fragrance.

5. RHS Rosemoor, North Devon

RHS Rosemoor in North Devon is at the western end of the comfort zone for roses with all that moisture in the very clean air, but, having got the drainage sorted, the rose garden there is a sight to behold, with its planting of 2,000 roses in 200 cultivars in every form and fragrance.

A temperate climate, great plant hunters and possibly global warming allow for many species to be grown that previous generations may not have dreamed of.

6. Tresco Abbey Gardens

Visiting the sub-tropical Scilly Isles is a journey to another land. Thirty miles south of Land’s End, the normally rocky landscape of Tresco Abbey Gardens is swathed in giant agaves, aeoniums and succulents. Seen from offshore in a boat, the tall palms fill the skyline giving it a magical ‘other world’ feel. It has the mildest of climates and the boast is “there is no winter, just an extended autumn” allowing exotic plants originating from Brazil to Burma to thrive.

7. Inverewe, Ross-shire

Location is all for British summer gardens. Despite its northerly situation, beyond the Isle of Skye and the same latitude as St Petersburg, Inverewe, an enormous (54-acre) loch-side Highlands garden in Ross-shire, boasts a wealth of tropical treasures. Being on the wet, west coast of Scotland with its Gulf Stream influence helps.

The sloping walled garden mixes traditional roses with vegetables and herbaceous plants and has the bonus of spectacular views across the sea loch. The garden was created in the late 19th century by local laird, Osgood Mackenzie, who had a passion for exotics from America, New Zealand and China. After his daughter Mairi’s death in 1953, the National Trust for Scotland took over the garden and has continued the theme.

8. Abbotsbury, Dorset

Abbotsbury in Dorset shows how a sub-tropical garden can be conjured up with mild sea influences and a modicum of shelter from northern winds. Established in 1765 as a kitchen garden for a now-demolished castle, the garden has been greatly replanted over the past two decades.

9. Stourhead, Wiltshire

Grand estates there have always been, and grand gardens to go with them, many taking their influence from Europe, but in the 18th century, the landscape movement swept all formality away – changing the look of the British countryside.

Of all English landscape gardens, Stourhead in Wiltshire, created between 1741 and 1780, is among the most poetic – inspiring to walk around in all seasons, even winter. Owner and creator Henry Hoare took inspiration from the classical scene and his travels in Italy, and the garden unfolds as a sequence of views framed like pictures along a winding route. Classical temples seen in the distance across the lake disappear from view as you walk, and are later seen from another angle. You can enter the fabulously decorated grotto and look out on a level with the lake.

10. Rousham, Oxfordshire

Magical Rousham in Oxfordshire has barely changed since William Kent designed it in the 1730s and, compared with other landscape gardens, it’s small enough that visiting makes for an intimate experience. Follow the atmospheric route through trees, past wonderful garden buildings, then visit the Walled Garden for a feast of colour.

11. Stowe, Buckinghamshire

For history and superb garden buildings, you can’t do better than magnificent Stowe in Buckinghamshire. Stowe was ‘the’ influential landscape garden of the 18th century, worked on by Bridgeman, Kent and ‘Capability’ Brown. Idyllic landscape scenes conceal the owner Viscount Cobham’s jaundiced commentary on the politics of the time.

12. Levens Hall, Cumbria

The ancient practice of pruning and clipping trees into ornamental shapes saw a revival in Europe in the 16th century, and topiary became a runaway garden fashion and a very obvious display of wealth and grandeur. The gardens at Levens Hall in Cumbria are famous for their outsized yew topiaries, the oldest in the world, it’s said, and often described as looking like something from Alice in Wonderland. They date back to the 1690s when the garden was first planted, and have outgrown their shapes over the years to become magnificent giants.

13. Packwood House, Warwickshire

Wonderful Packwood House in Warwickshire dates back to Tudor times, and tradition has it that the topiary yews there were planted to represent the Sermon on the Mount. Over 100 trees, some more than 15m (50ft plus), lead to a mound crowned with a single tree, the Master. In addition, there are fine herbaceous borders and Tudor-period courtyards.

For an opportunity to see box and yew topiary of a more manageable size, you can visit topiary specialists Langley Boxwood Nursery in Hampshire, where there is a wide range of ready-clipped shapes and sizes, from balls and cones to spirals and obelisks.

The Arts and Crafts period in the 1900s was important for architecture, art and culture and much of that spilled over into the gardens. A style of gardening was developed, led by Edwin Lutyens and Gertrude Jekyll.

14. Great Dixter, East Sussex

The garden that epitomises the period is undoubtedly Great Dixter in East Sussex, which for many sets the standard for all. The 15th-century house was restored by Lutyens and the garden designed by him, but given the distinctive stamp of its brilliant owner, Christopher Lloyd.

This is a garden for all seasons and all tastes, from a sunken garden to wildflower meadows, exotic areas to deep herbaceous borders – it sums up the period and yet is beautifully timeless.

15. High Glanau, Wye Valley

View this post on Instagram

High Glanau is situated on the slopes of the Wye Valley in Gwent and is seen as the finest of the Arts and Crafts period in Wales. Built in 1922 for H Avray Tipping, the then architectural editor of Country Life magazine, who established the garden. His plans are the basis of the thoughtful garden update by owners Helena and Hilary Gerrish that features a subtle colour palette of white, lavender, blue and green threading through deep, straight 30m-long (100ft) borders.

16. East Lambrook Manor, Somerset

View this post on Instagram

As life improved over the centuries for the general population, the utilitarian cottage garden at East Lambrook Manor evolved with the luxury of growing some pretty flowers among the essential vegetables. By the 19th century, it had come to embody the quintessential country look. The art of cottage-garden planting lies in combining plants naturally, and no one did this more skilfully than plantswoman and writer Margery Fish in the archetypal cottage garden she made at East Lambrook Manor in the 1950s.

Everywhere around the little 15th-century manor house is a sense of relaxed abundance, with mixed borders, old roses, euphorbias and important collections of snowdrops, hellebores and hardy geraniums. There is also a nursery specialising in rare and unusual plants.

18. Monks House, East Sussex

In 1919, Bloomsbury writer Virginia Woolf and her husband Leonard bought Monks House in East Sussex as a refuge from London, and it’s easy to see why. The secluded and tranquil garden remains as it was in their day, with a neat lawn, old trees, informal mixed planting, and a vegetable garden. At the end of the garden is the little building Virginia used as her writing room. New influences in gardening are emerging, and some wonderful contemporary gardens have been created in recent years, made by innovative garden owners and designers.

19. Scampston Hall, North Yorkshire

View this post on Instagram

The old Walled Garden at Scampston Hall was given a new and dramatic layout by designer Piet Oudolf and opened to the public in 2004. A series of ‘rooms’ within the space features sculptural serpentine yew hedges, big drift-waves of ornamental grasses, topiary and a bee and butterfly-friendly perennial meadow.

20. Sissinghurst Castle Gardens, Kent

Designed by writer Vita Sackville-West and her husband Harold Nicholson, the pair fell in love with Sissinghurst Castle when they first saw it in the 1930s, and decided to make it their own. Harold planned the garden rooms, while Vita planted in abundance in the gardens, and today Sissinghurst reflects the romance and intimacy of her poems and writings. Summer highlights at Sissinghurst include the Rose Garden, the White Garden, and the No Dig garden, which is bursting with vegetables, herbs and fruit.

Sissinghurst: Vita Sackville-West and the Creation of a Garden is available is on Amazon and is a beautiful, illustrated history of this iconic garden.

21. Hinton Ampner, Hampshire

Hinton Ampner is an elegant country house near Winchester, which was rebuilt after a fire in the 1960s, and is home to tranquil formal gardens with beautifully manicured lawns that lead down past avenues of sculptured topiary, rose-filled borders and across to breathtaking views of the South Downs National Park.

22. Painswick Rococo Garden, Gloucestershire

Designed as a playground in the 1740s by wealthy owner Benjamin Hyett to entertain his guests, Painswick Rococo Garden is a perfect example of a Georgian pleasure garden. It is utterly flamboyant and eccentric, and today is Britain's sole surviving complete rococo garden.

Explore the serpentine paths to the Red House, Eagle House and Exedra and discover follies and hidden water features around every corner.

23. Greenway, Devon

Agatha Christie described her beloved holiday home, Greenway in Brixham, Devon, as the 'loveliest place in the world, and it's not hard to see why. Now owned by the National Trust, the large, romantic gardens stretch down through woodland towards the Dart estuary, and the walled gardens are home to a vinery and the Peach House. Don't miss the Boathouse, which also played a starring role in Christie's novel, Dead Man's Folly.

24. Kiftsgate Court, Gloucestershire

The beautiful, family-run Kiftsgate Court in the Cotswolds is home to rare plants and shrubs and the world-famous Kiftsgate Court rose, which is said to be England's largest and cascades over three trees. The gardens are the creation of three generations of women gardeners, created over 100 years ago by Heather Muir, and still looked after by her descendants.

25. The Garden House, Dartmoor

Set in a valley on the western edge of Dartmoor National Park, the Garden House is set over 10 acres of stunning gardens, divided into different themed areas. Stroll through the colourful Summer Garden, the sweet Cottage Garden and the romantic Walled Garden.

The Garden House is also the subject of a sweeping love story novel by Marcia Willett, The Garden House, which is available on Amazon, and will make you fall in love with the garden and Dartmoor, all over again.

item.title: Mad about History - Five of the most bizarre and disgusting cures and treatments for the black death that really were used by contempoary doctors in medieval times
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: When the Black Death struck Britain in 1348, medieval doctors didn’t know what caused it, let alone how to cure it, but they sure did get creative with their solutions. Here are some of the weirdest plague treatments that were actually used
item.content:encoded:
Although today we know that the plague was caused by a bacterium called Yersinia pestis, when it ravaged medieval Britain in the 14th-century, killing a third of the population, doctors had no idea where it came from, let alone how to cure it, and some of the treatments for the Black Death were unbelievably bizarre.

First hitting England in the summer of 1348, the Black Death was a defining moment in the Middle Ages in Britain, killing a third of the population, and shaking up the social order, paving the way for the breakdown of the feudal system and shattering the trust of the people in the Catholic church.

If, like me, you're fascinated by this medieval global pandemic, Thomas Asbridge's The Black Death: A Global History is available on Amazon and was just released this spring. He traces the course and impact of the plague across the medieval world and explores the different experiences of people all over the globe.

A brief history of the plague in Britain

The Black Death, which originated in Asia, reached Dorset in June 1348 on a ship from France. By 1350, it had spread throughout England, Wales and Scotland.

The infected bacteria were primarily transmitted to humans by fleas that lived on small rodents, particularly rats, which were common on trade ships, meaning their spread was rapid and easy.

It was not then known as the Black Death - this is a 17th-century term - but was known in the Middle Ages as the 'Great Mortality' or 'Pestilence' and killed between 1.5 and 3 million people, around a third of the population.

The symptoms were painful, swollen lymph nodes (buboes) in the groin or armpits, fever, and a rapid death, usually within a matter of days.

After this first pandemic, plague then became endemic and returned frequently in the 15th, 16th, and 17th centuries, particularly in overcrowded, unsanitary areas.

Plague doctors

You've probably seen pictures of the scary-looking 'Plague doctors' with their sinister, bird-like masks and long wax coats, who claimed to be able to treat patients. These particular doctors practised during the 17th-century outbreak, and the masks were worn to prevent them from inhaling the 'poisonous air' that infected the victims. These 'doctors' were not usually trained and specialised in treating the poor in highly infected areas. They were also crucial for counting the dead - a sinister sight indeed.

Why did the plague end in the 17th century?

The last major pandemic of the plague was the Great Plague of London, 1665-1666. By this time, there was a better understanding of germs and bacteria, so measures were put in place, like quarantining, to prevent the spread of the disease, as well as better sanitary routines and improved living conditions, compared to those who suffered in the Middle Ages.

The Great Fire of London is often cited as causing the end of the 17th-century plague, having ravaged London in 1666. Scientists now claim the pandemic ended more likely due to the cold winter in 1666.

What were the weirdest cures and treatments for the Black Death?

Many cures had roots in herbal medicine, which is generally what physicians and apothecaries would prescribe. But other so-called 'cures' were quackery or fuelled by religious fear-mongering.

Many of the cures were based on the Galen (a Greek physician) that the human body was made up of four fluids, 'humours', called black bile, yellow bile, blood and phlegm. An imbalance of the humours would lead to illness. Medieval medicine followed Galen's theory, and cures would often try to correct an imbalance in the humours of a patient.

Here are five of the weirdest cures and treatments for the Black Death that are hard to believe but totally true:

1. A plucked chicken's bottom

This treatment was named the 'Vicary method' after Thomas Vicary, the 'doctor' who popularised it. It involved plucking feathers from a chicken's bottom and tying the chicken to your armpit, so that it was touching the buboes.

The thinking behind it came from the medieval idea that chickens breathed through their behinds, so therefore the chicken would draw the infection out of the patient.

2. Human excrement

This is probably the foulest of all the Black Death cures. Human excrement was mixed up with other substances, like ground lily root and tree sap, into a paste and smeared onto the buboes, which would be cut open. It would be tightly bound with cloth. The science behind this is unclear, and most definitely only made infections worse.

3. Powdered unicorn horn

According to medieval myth, the legendary unicorn could be subdued and captured by a virginal maiden. One expensive cure for the Black Death was powdered unicorn horn, known as alicorn, which would have only been available to the very wealthy.

The powder, which is believed to have actually been made with either narwhal or rhinoceros tusk, was mixed with water and given to the patient to drink.

4. Flagellation

Given that religious belief governed the lives of almost everyone in medieval Europe, it is no surprise that many believed the Black Death was sent as a punishment from God for the sins of the human race.

'Flagellants' would try to cure the plague by walking the streets stripped to the waist, whipping themselves as public penance, purging their bodies of the sins that had caused the Black Death. The whips often had several tails and were knotted with nails, the more painful the better.

5. Onions

Yes, that humble garden vegetable was believed to cure the Black Death. Chopped raw onions would be rubbed on buboes and were believed to draw out the toxins. It was also believed that onion fumes could combat 'miasma', or 'poisonous fumes' in medieval talk, which was what they believed led to the plague.

More routine cures and treatments included blood-letting and using leeches to draw out the disease, which hark back to the Galenic theory of the four humours being imbalanced. If you're interested in the thought processes behind medieval medicine, I'd recommend Toni Mount's Medieval Medicine: Its Mysteries and Science, which is available on Amazon and is a brilliant insight into science and the human body in the Middle Ages.

item.title: When Britain Burned: From plague to war, revolts, and anarchy, this is our guide to the Middle Ages in Britain from the Norman Conquest to the dawn of the Tudors
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From the Norman Conquest to the dawn of the Tudors, the Middle Ages in Britain spanned 400 years of battles, plagues and major reform. Here is our timeline of medieval England, and just about everything you need to know about the period...
item.content:encoded:
The Middle Ages in Britain have a somewhat clouded reputation when it comes to studying them, with people associating these few hundred years primarily with disease, deaths, wars and general misery, but this isn't actually a very fair depiction.

The Middle Ages or 'Medieval' period in Britain (generally considered to be from 1066 and the Norman Conquest to 1485 and the beginning of the Tudor dynasty) were actually a very important few centuries, with major political, cultural, linguistic, architectural, religious and social developments that changed British history forever.

My favourite book on the subject is the brilliant Ian Mortimer's The Time Traveller's Guide to Medieval England, which is available on Amazon and will guide you through the sights, sounds and smells of England in the Middle Ages.

So what actually happened in the Middle Ages, and where did it end and where did it begin?

Here is our brief timeline of the Middle Ages in Britain:

The Norman Conquest - 1066

With William the Conqueror's victory at the Battle of Hastings in 1066 came great change.

As well as a huge influence on the clergy, Norman rule saw new monasteries and churches constructed across England in the new Romanesque or Norman style, and between 500 and 1000, motte-and-bailey castles across England and Wales to secure William's rule. Many of these, including Windsor Castle and the Tower of London, are still standing today.

The Domesday Book - 1086

William the Conqueror conducted a survey of England known as the Domesday Book in 1086 that served to document all land ownership, resources and taxable values to consolidate Norman control and define who held land before and after the 1066 conquest. It defined feudal obligations so every level of society was under an obligation of service to the class above. It also introduced punitive forest laws to protect the royal hunting preserves.

If you're interested, Penguin actually have a complete translation of the Domesday Book, which is available on Amazon.

The Anarchy - 1135-1153

Baronial revolts plagued the rule of William the Conqueror and his son, William Rufus - William II, bought William's youngest son, Henry I, who brought peace and administrative and legal reform.

Henry I's death in 1135, however, sparked a succession crisis when Henry's nephew Stephen was crowned king, despite a rival claim from Henry's daughter, Matilda.

This 18-year civil conflict, often known as 'The Anarchy' between Empress Matilda and King Stephen resulted in a serious breakdown of law, order, and royal authority.

Teresa Cole's brilliant, The Anarchy: The Darkest Days of Medieval England is available on Amazon and will guide you through this fascinating period in our history.

The Plantagenets - 1154- 1485

Order was restored by Matilda's son, Henry II, the first of the Plantagenet Kings.

Henry is well known for his vast travelling throughout his dominions, from the Scottish borders to the Pyrenees, the fortresses he built, including Dover Castle and for the scandal that saw his friend, or rival, his 'turbulent priest' St Thomas Becket, murdered in Canterbury Cathedral, supposedly on the orders of Henry's own knights in 1170.

Magna Carta

In 1215, in a field at Runnymede, Surrey, squabbles between greedy King John and his barons were halted by the agreement of Magna Carta. Intended to curb kingly powers and redress the barons’ financial and feudal grievances, this Charter of Liberties would yet come to symbolise so much more: shining down the ages as a keystone of English democracy, law and freedoms, and an inspiration for democratic institutions around the world.

Magna Carta was a quintessential product of the 331-year rule of the House of Plantagenet that had begun when King Henry II inherited the crown from his Norman cousin King Stephen. From 1154, 14 kings engaged in power struggles – with barons, the church and among themselves – that defined not simply who ruled but how they ruled. Amid the mayhem, the seeds of parliament were sown.

Henry II (r 1154–1189) set the pace as a giant of kingship, restoring order after years of civil war under Stephen and making his mark as the Father of English Common Law. But his tragic spat that ended in the murder of Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, in 1170 signalled disputes between royal and church authority that would fester for generations.

During the reigns of lesser sovereigns baronial unrest began to reshape government: King John and Magna Carta; King Henry III and the notion of “parleyings” with a council not simply comprising the king’s cronies.

King Edward I - The Warrior King

King Edward I (r 1272–1307), “the Lawgiver”, summoned the Model Parliament in 1295 that broadened representation, and he ruthlessly tamed Wales with his Iron Ring of castles, including Beaumaris, Caernarfon, Conwy and Harlech.

Following a succession dispute, Edward I also invaded Scotland, aiming to merge it into his dominion, earning him the nickname "Hammer of the Scots" (Malleus Scotorum).

In 1290, Edward also ordered all Jews to leave England, confiscating their property to fund his military campaigns.

The Hundred Years War - 1337 - 1453

The Hundred Years' War began on May 24 1337, when the French King Philip VI confiscated the duchy of Aquitaine from the English King Edward III. There followed 116 years of tension, battles and territorial disputes and the English king's claim to the French throne.

The most famous battle of the Hundred Years' War is, of course, Henry V's victory at Agincourt in 1415, when the king's vastly outnumbered army miraculously defeated a superior French force.

The Black Death - 1348 - 1353

The Black Death was a bubonic plague pandemic which reached England in June 1348. One third of the English population was killed, estimated to be between 1.5 and 3 million people.

The unquestioned belief in the supremacy of the Catholic church was destroyed - how could God let this happen? - and the feudal system, which had been brought in 300 years earlier by William the Conqueror, was damaged, as there was an extreme shortage of labour. The ties of serfdom were broken, and peasants demanded higher wages and better conditions.

The Peasants' Revolt - 1381

In the wake of the Black Death, the government passed the Statute of Labourers in 1351 to freeze wages (peasants worked for pre-plague wages) and restrict movement. This inevitably sparked intense anger and open rebellion.

In 1381, to pay for the then-failing Hundred Years' War and enforce new labour laws, King Richard II and his government enforced several unfair poll taxes. The 1381 tax was the breaking point for the riled-up peasants who, led by a man named Wat Tyler, marched on London. They burned buildings, killed nobles and managed to win an audience with the king for an unsuccessful bid for better rights. Wat Tyler was mortally wounded at Smithfield by the mayor of London, William Walworth, in 1381.

Although their rebellion was unsuccessful, the country was shaken by the Peasants' Revolt. Despite not keeping his promises to Tyler for better rights, in November 1381, King Richard pardoned all those still accused of taking part. There wasn't another poll tax for 600 years, until Margaret Thatcher in the 1980s, which proved to be similarly unpopular.

Dan Jones' brilliant Summer of Blood: The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 is available on Amazon and dives deeper into the personalities and politics of this underexplored period of history.

The Wars of the Roses

From 1455, the Plantagenet dynasty turned dramatically upon itself as two rival branches – the Houses of Lancaster and York – fought the Wars of the Roses, so-called after their respective emblems of red and white roses. “The world is right queasy,” wrote one observer even before the sporadic violence plumbed scandalous new depths when the Princes in the Tower, 12-year-old King Edward V and his younger brother Richard, mysteriously “vanished” in 1483: murdered, according to tradition, by their uncle who assumed the crown as King Richard III.

Dynastic change came violently when Henry Tudor snatched Richard III’s crown at the Battle of Bosworth in 1485. A new ruling House had arrived that would out-dazzle any in British history - the Tudors.

With the Tudors came great change in Britain and across Europe, including the invention of the printing press, and, of course, the Protestant Reformation that changed the face of Christianity forever.

For a thorough guide to the Middle Ages in Britain, Edmund King's Medieval England: From Hastings to Bosworth is available on Amazon and presents a broad panorama of the political and cultural development of English society from the Norman Conquest to the end of the Wars of the Roses and the dawn of the Tudors.

item.title: What’s on in London this week 27 April 4 May: unmissable events you’ll want to book now
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: Our guide to the hottest events in the capital this week everyone is rushing to book now, from restaurant openings to the coolest new experiences and cultural events
item.content:encoded:
As Samuel Johnson once said, 'a man who is tired of London is tired of life', and whether you live there or not, the delights of our capital city are never-ending - new surprises await every week, as the city buzzes on in a whirlwind of culture, culinary delights and colourful experiences.

When it comes to the best things to do in London in 2026, there is an almost infinite choice of brilliant events, openings and experiences, as well as annual seasonal happenings that are as part and parcel of Londoners' lives as red telephone boxes and umbrellas, from the Chelsea Flower Show to the Christmas Lights at Kew.

Though you can use this feature for weekly updates on the most exciting new openings and events, Yolanda Zappaterra and Sarah Guy's The London Year: What to Do and When is available on Amazon is a lovely month-by-month guide to the city, featuring unmissable annual festivals, anniversaries filled with pomp and circumstance and seasonal specials such as the rhododendron bloom in Richmond Park.

Here is our guide to five brilliant things to do in London this week that you absolutely don't want to miss.

What's on in London this week, 27 April -4 May?

Celebrate David Attenborough's 100th birthday

National treasure David Attenborough turns 100 this week (8 May), and we all want to celebrate our favourite nature-loving British icon. The Royal Albert Hall is hosting a special concert, David Attenborough's 100 Years On Planet, on the 8th, with the BBC Concert Orchestra honouring his career in wildlife storytelling.

Some tickets are still available here!

If you can't get tickets for that, you could join the Dress like David Attenborough gathering on 7 May in Trafalgar Square at 6:15 pm, where fans will unite dressed either as the legend himself or an animal and sing a rendition of Happy Birthday.

Or, you could head to Holborn's Garden Cinema, which will be showing a special screening of the documentary, David Attenborough: A Life on Our Planet from 1-7 May.

Get tickets here.

1536 at The Ambassador's Theatre

Following a sold-out run at the Almeida, 1536 will transfer to London’s Ambassador’s Theatre from 7 May 2026.

The play made its debut in London’s West End last year at the Almeida Theatre, and is a refreshing and still scarily relevant take on the well-known story of the second of Henry VIII’s six wives, weaving the royal scandal that changed the history of the nation with the rural lives of ‘normal’ 16th-century women in rural England.

Tickets are available to buy through the ATG website.

Banksy Limitless: Reframed in London

View this post on Instagram

This immersive exhibition on the Old Brompton Road allows visitors to experience hundreds of the elusive Banksy's artworks as they have never before. The immersive galleries include an infinity room full of mirrors, a collection of Banksy murals including seven never-before-seen artworks, and a room dedicated to Banksy's installations and sculptures. These are Banksy's artworks designed for the city, in the heart of London's cultural district.

Get your tickets via Fever.

Beatrix Potter-themed afternoon tea at JW Marriott Grosvenor House Hotel

View this post on Instagram

The elegant Mayfair hotel will launch its charming new afternoon tea inspired by the works of Beatrix Potter from Friday, 1 May, to mark the beloved children's author's 160th birthday later this year. The special menu includes treats such as Tom Kitten's Tangle (a white chocolate ball filled with elderflower mousse and wild strawberry compote) and Squirrel Nutkin's Harvest, which is a spiced parsnip cake served with marscarpone cream and lemon curd.

Book your table via JD Marriott.

The Murdér Express: Part Deux, The Jewel of the Empire

View this post on Instagram

This brilliant immersive murder-mystery experience has just launched its new summer menu and combines the very best of fine dining and comedy theatre. You'll travel back in time to the roaring twenties, indulge in delicious 1920s-inspired cocktails, enjoy a delicious 3-course menu and immerse yourself in the murder mystery unfolding around you.

Book your ticket via Fever.

A Midsummer Night's Dream at Shakespeare's Globe

Shakespeare's Globe will open for its summer season with none other than his A Midsummer Night's Dream which will play to audiences under starry skies at the historic theatre from 23 April to 29 August 2026.

A Midsummer Night's Dream is the first of a summer of events, including Much Ado about Nothing and Love Labour's Lost that will open later in the year. Tickets start at £6.00 for standing - a rite of passage for any visitor to this iconic theatre, just as the Elizabethans would have done!

Get your ticket via SeatPlan.

Inter Alia starring Rosamund Pike at Wyndham's Theatre

View this post on Instagram

Following a sold-out run at the National Theatre, this gripping legal drama written by Suzie Miller and starring Oscar-nominated actor Rosamund Pike, will transfer to the West End this weekend to much excitement from the theatre crowd.

The show follows Crown Court Judge Jessica Parks, played by Pike, who is trying to reform the flawed justice system while balancing the pressures of motherhood and personal life and has five-star reviews all round. This is not one to be missed.

Get your tickets via ATG Tickets.

V&A East at Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park

View this post on Instagram

On 18 April, the much-anticipated V&A's new eastern venue, just a short walk from its sister, V&A East Storehouse, will opened its doors to the public.

The free museum will host temporary exhibitions centred on the theme of multiculturalism. The first exhibition the public will be able to enjoy is called The Music is Black: A British Story, and will include Joan Armatrading’s childhood guitar and outfits worn by artist Little Simz.

Find out more here.

Cleopatra: The Experience

I went to experience this brilliant new immersive experience when it opened at Immersive LDN last week, and it is mesmerising, you can read my review of it here. Through immersive technology, Virtual Reality storytelling a free-roaming metaverse and even a 5D sensory experience you'll learn about this legendary Egyptian queen and her incredible legacy.

Book tickets for Cleopatra: The Experience via Fever.

Ramses and the Pharaoh's Gold: The Exhibition

This brilliant new exhibition at Battersea Power Station is a rare chance to step inside the glittering world of Ancient Egypt's most decadent king, Ramses II. The exhibition features nearly 200 incredible ancient Egyptian artefacts, all over 3,000 years old.

Book your ticket via Fever here.

Paddington The Musical at The Savoy Theatre

View this post on Instagram

This brilliant new musical sees everyone's favourite bear take to the stage in a colourful swirl of music, singing, dancing and incredible puppetry - it's a delight for the entire family.

Based on the iconic characters from Michael Bond's books, and on the story of the award-winning 2014 film, the original music and lyrics have been written by McFly's Tom Fletcher.

Book your tickets for Paddington The Musical Via ATG Tickets.

Queen Elizabeth II: Her life in style at The King's Gallery, Buckingham Palace

This much-anticipated exhibition marks the centenary of the birth of Queen Elizabeth II and is the largest exhibition of her fashion ever to be staged.

The exhibition opened to the public on Friday, 10 April and will showcase 200 incredible items, many on display for the first time, from all 10 decades of Queen Elizabeth's life, including her christening robe, bridesmaid dress, wedding dress, coronation dress and the ensemble worn for the wedding of Princess Margaret.

Queen Elizabeth II: Her life in style will run until 18 October 2026; tickets are available via the Royal Collection here.

For those wanting to learn more about Queen Elizabeth II, or perhaps for those who can't make it to the exhibition but would like to be part of it, The Royal Collection have also released an accompanying book by Senior Curator Caroline de Guitaut, who has been in charge of putting together this incredible new exhibition. Queen Elizabeth II: Fashion & Style is available on Amazon and will explore the late Queen's unique style and legacy.

item.title: The real Rutshire - these are all the Rivals filming locations in the Cotswolds that bring Jilly Cooper’s brilliant bonkbuster to life
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: As we eagerly await the second series of Disney’s adaptation of Rivals, we look back on all the Cotswolds filming locations used in series one that you can visit yourself
item.content:encoded:
The second series of Disney's adaptation of Jilly Cooper's 1988 bonkbuster novel, Rivals, will hit our screens this May, and with it comes the return of the beautiful Cotswolds filming locations that stood in for the fictional county of Rutshire in series one.

Anyone who has visited the Cotswolds knows that the villages look like they've come straight out of the pages of a fairytale, so it's no surprise this Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty in South-West England has become one of the most iconic British filming locations.

The 1980s romp is a totally brilliant and over-the-top depiction of what it's like to be wealthy in the English countryside - think Champagne parties, croquet, and, as you've probably guessed by the name 'Rutshire', more affairs and misbehaviour than you could shake a riding crop at during the height of the social season. If you haven't read Jilly Cooper's Rutshire Chronicles, now's your time, and they are all available from Amazon.

Watch the trailer for Rivals series two below:

So, where was Rivals filmed? Here is our guide to all the Rivals filming locations in the Cotswolds and surrounding areas, so you can live out your own Rutshire fantasies...if you so desire...

Chavenage House, Tetbury

This beautiful, typically Cotswoldian Elizabethan manor in the picture-perfect village of Tetbury is no stranger to film crews, or Aidan Turner, as it stood in as Trenwith in the BBC's Poldark series. In Rivals, Chavenage serves as the home of the O'Hara family and is the site of much of the action of series one, and will reprise the role in series two.

Though it is often booked up with film crews, Chavenage does offer tours to the public via booking on their website.

Tetbury, Gloucestershire

The impossibly pretty village of Tetbury, where Chavenage is located, was also used for various street scenes in series one and two, with Aidan Turner spotted in costume as Declan O'Hara back in 2023 and all the shop fronts altered to make them look as if they belonged in the 1980s.

Aside from its classically Cotswolds prettiness, Author Dame Jilly Cooper herself lived near Tetbury for over forty years, so this certainly felt like the right call for Rivals producer Eliza Mellor.

Berkeley House, Tetbury

Another Tetbury house, Berkeley House, was used as the home of American TV producer Cameron Cook, played by Nafessa Williams, in Rivals.

The Grade-II listed house stood in as Hamilton Terrace in the series, and is actually a rental property, so fans could even have the chance to stay in one of the Rivals houses themselves - it's available to book via TripAdvisor if you're interested.

Corsham, Wiltshire and Neston Park

The picturesque market town of Corsham in Wiltshire, just south of Gloucestershire, and known for its pretty streets, stood in as Cotchester in Rivals.

The historic and very grand Neston Park nearby reportedly stood in as The Falconry, the home of TV mogul Tony Baddingham in the series, played by David Tennant. The house itself is privately owned and closed to the public, but the estate it hosts the annual WOMAD Music festival every summer, taking place at the end of July this year.

The Battleaxes, Wraxall

View this post on Instagram

The team had to head even further south for this one, as reportedly, the Battlexaes pub in Wraxall, Somerset, a Tudor-style Grade-II listed pub, was used for some filming. The pub itself closed during lockdown, so it is a prime filming location for producers, and could easily be transformed into a typical 1980s pub.

Cranmore Station, Somerset

Another Somerset location is Cranmore station, the main railway station of the preserved steam East Somerset Railway. This lovely-looking station was the location for the train-side encounter of my favourite love affair in the whole series - Freddie and Lizzie - played by Danny Dyer and Katherine Parkinson. The railway is open all-year round for events and is a haven for heritage train enthusiasts.

If you're looking to visit some of these Rivals filming locations, particularly those in the Cotswolds, we've put together a guide to some of the Cotswolds' best hotels so you can make the most of your time there. If you're in Tetbury, I'd particularly recommend Whatley Manor Hotel & Spa, which is available to book via Expedia and really looks as if it's just fallen out of the pages of a Jilly Cooper novel....

item.title: The bloodiest and most confusing wars in British history? This is your easy guide to the Wars of the Roses, the medieval civil war that inspired Game of Thrones
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: They paved the way for the Tudor dynasty and killed nearly half the noblemen in England - but what actually happened during the Wars of the Roses, and what were they all fighting about? This is your guide to medieval history’s most confusing period
item.content:encoded:
The subject of Shakespeare plays and inspiration for Game of Thrones, the Wars of the Roses are some of the most important and violent years in British history, but my goodness, are they confusing.

Known at the time and in later centuries as the Civil Wars or the Cousins' Wars, the Wars of the Roses were a series of violent, bloody campaigns over control of the English throne between 1455 and 1485.

As the precursor to the Tudor era, everyone knows that the Wars of the Roses were, but not really what happened, for me, it was the Wars of the Roses that really got me interested in history, thanks to the brilliant works of historical fiction by the queen of the genre, Philippa Gregory. Her Cousins' Wars collection includes six books on the Wars and is available on Amazon - they are all brilliant reads.

What actually were the Wars of the Roses?

Here is my guide to everything you need to know about the Wars of the Roses - a simplified version of a very confusing 30-years of history.

How did the Wars of the Roses start?

As most wars do, the Wars of the Roses began thanks to an unsatisfactory conclusion to a previous war, this time the Hundred Years' War.

England's humiliating loss to the French in the Hundred Years' War in 1453 resulted in the loss of almost all English lands in France, which resulted in widespread resentment and economic hardship.

England was also seeing the rise of private factions during the time. Powerful noble landowners were able to grow their own large private armies, and many acted with impunity. They created a lawless environment, which the weak King Henry VI (1422 to 1461) could not control.

Henry VI was mentally unstable and unfit to rule. He allowed rival court factions (one side led by Henry's wife, Queen Margaret of Anjou, and the Duke of Somerset, and the other by Richard, Duke of York and his nephew Richard Neville, 16th Earl of Warwick (also known as the Kingmaker)) to fight for control, which meant his reign was plagued by poor governance and instability. Lauren Johnson's Shadow King: The Life and Death of Henry VI is available on Amazon and is a brilliant look at his fascinating reign.

Both of these factions were descendants of King Edward III and had competing claims to the English throne. Richard Duke of York, of the House of York (the white rose), challenged the reigning House of Lancaster (Henry VI and Queen Margaret) and argued their lineage gave them a superior right to rule.

During Henry's bouts of ill-health (historians believe he had schizophrenia), in 1453 and 1454, Richard, Duke of York, was named Protector of the Realm by the King's Council, despite opposition from Queen Margaret. York's first act was to dismiss some of the Queen's Lancastrian advisors, which, unsurprisingly, caused great upset.

Upon Henry's recovery, York was excluded from power. He took up arms in 1455 in the First Battle of St Albans. It was easy to recruit trained men to fight, as most soldiers had just returned from the Hundred Years' War. Each side chose a badge - the Red Rose for Lancaster and the White Rose for York.

This was the first conflict of the Wars of the Roses, which would rage for the next 30 years.

A timeline of the Wars of the Roses

22 May 1455 - The First Battle of St Albans resulted in a Yorkist victory. The Lancastrian Duke of Somerset was killed. The Duke of York was reappointed Protector and dismissed again in 1456. Queen Margaret created anti-Yorkist sentiment at court, and the Duke of York was excluded from the royal council

23 September 1459 - Battle of Blore Heath - Yorkist victory.

12 October 1459 - Battle of Ludford Bridge - Lancastrian victory, Duke of York fled to Ireland.

10 July 1460 - Battle of Northampton - Yorkist victory and King Henry VI is captured. Queen Margaret flees to Wales.

10 October 1460 - Duke of York returns to England and declares himself heir to the throne. Queen raises a new army.

30 December 1460 - Battle of Wakefield - Yorkists defeated, and the Duke of York killed. He is succeeded by his son Edward.

2 February 1461 - Battle of Mortimer Cross - the Yorkist Edward, Earl of March, is victorious.

17 February 1461 - Second Battle of St Albans - Lancaster victorious, Henry VI is rescued.

4 March 1461 - Edward IV of York is proclaimed king.

9 March 1461 - Battle of Towton - Yorkists and the Earl of Warwick are victorious. Henry, Margaret and the Prince of Wales flee to Scotland.

25 June 1465 - Henry VI is captured and imprisoned in the Tower of London.

1 May 1470 - Warwick the Kingmaker quarrels with King Edward and flees to France. He switched sides to join forces with Queen Margaret and restored Henry VI to the throne on 13th October.

14 March 1471 -Yorkist King Edward flees to France and comes back with a small army.

14 April 1471 - Battle of Barnet - Edward's Yorkist army is victorious, and Warwick is killed.

4 May 1471 - Battle of Tewkesbury - Yorkist victory. The Prince of Wales is killed, and the queen is captured.

21-22 May 1471 - Henry VI killed. Henry Tudor, the Lancastrian claimant to the throne, flees to France. Yorkist King Edward VI is now the undisputed king.

9 April 1483 - Edward IV dies and is succeeded by his son Edward V.

June 1483 - The young Edward V and his brother are declared illegitimate by Parliament. Richard, Duke of York, is asked to take the throne and becomes Richard III.

Summer of 1483 - Edward V and his brother Richard are murdered in the Tower of London - the Princes in the Tower.

7 August 1485 - Lancastrian Henry Tudor lands in Wales.

22 August 1485 - Battle of Bosworth - Richard III is killed, and Henry Tudor becomes King Henry VII.

How did the Wars of the Roses end?

Henry VII married Elizabeth of York, thus uniting the two houses, and founded the Tudor dynasty. The Tudor Rose includes both red and white roses to symbolise the uniting of the Houses of York and Lancaster.

How many people died in the Wars of the Roses?

The total combat deaths during the Wars of the Roses are estimated to have been around 100,000, and, because the focus was on dynastic destruction, the number of noble families in England had dropped by nearly half by the wars' conclusion.

These 30 years of chopping and changing and betrayals and bloody battles are highly interesting, but if you're still a bit confused and would like to know more, I'd recommend the brilliant Dan Jones' The Wars of the Roses: The Fall of the Plantagenets and the Rise of the Tudors, which is available on Amazon and is a rip-roaring guide through these tumultuous three decades that changed the face of British history forever, and paved the way for the soap opera that was the Tudor dynasty.

item.title: Magna Carta and bad King John - the medieval charter that tamed a villainous king and its astounding 800-year impact on politics all over the world
item.dc:creator: Sian Ellis, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: We look back over the history of Magna Carta, the famous charter sealed by King John on 15 June 1215, over 800 years ago at Runnymeade and its impact on global politics today
item.content:encoded:
Forced upon King John by rebel barons in 1215, Magna Carta has been hailed as the greatest constitutional document of all time and a keystone of English liberty, law and democracy. Its principles have served as an inspiration for democratic institutions around the world, and it has become a global symbol of human rights and freedom from oppression.

Those are certainly heady claims, especially as the charter was never intended to be some lofty declaration of enduring legal principle. It was simply thrashed out as a practical solution to squabbles between a greedy and powerful Plantagenet king and the disgruntled barons who stood just below him in the pecking order of medieval society.

It was a charter that primarily served the vested interests of the higher ranks of feudal life. You can read the text itself and a commentary by brilliant medieval historian David Carpenter in this copy by Penguin, available on Amazon.

How did Magna Carta come about?

Well before the barons’ dispute with King John, England’s monarchs had agreed to safeguard certain rights and liberties of their people. There’s even a written precursor to Magna Carta in King Henry I’s Coronation Charter of 1100, relating to the privileges of clergy and nobles, designed to make up for abuses of royal power by King Henry’s predecessor, King William II.

Unfortunately, subsequent rulers conveniently forgot that charter, with King John in particular exhibiting extreme amnesia.

Who was King John and why did he anger the barons?

Ascending the throne in 1199, King John embarked on a reign of tyranny and oppression, and pursued a disastrous foreign policy, which led to him being labelled one of England’s most villainous monarchs.

He angered the barons by levying severe taxes to boost his income, including excessive ‘scutage’ – a tax in lieu of military service. He opposed the appointment of Stephen Langton as Archbishop of Canterbury, offended the Pope and was subsequently excommunicated.

The barons' rebellion

Matters came to a head in 1215 when the barons rebelled. Outmanoeuvred and playing for time, King John agreed to negotiations. The barons, with Archbishop Langton as one of the leading mediators, gathered for a showdown on 10 June 1215 in the ancient ‘meeting meadow’ at Runnymede, Surrey, beside the River Thames, not far from Windsor Castle.

What exactly is Magna Carta?

Coerced by demands to stop riding roughshod over lawful customs, the king grudgingly granted Magna Carta – or the Charter of Liberties, as it was then known – on 15 June. Four days later, the barons made peace with him by renewing their oaths of allegiance, and on 24 June, the first hastily prepared copies of the charter (complete with spelling mistakes) were given the king’s seal and rushed out for distribution to sheriffs and bishops around the kingdom.

What does the Magna Carta say?

Most of the charter’s 63 clauses focused on the nitty-gritty of contemporary feudal issues and the interests of particular parties: regulating scutage, dealing with fines, royal forests, the behaviour of royal officials, freedom of movement of merchants, and the standardisation of wine, ale and corn measures.

Then, buried halfway through, we find clauses 39 and 40:

“No free man shall be seized or imprisoned, or stripped of his rights or possessions, or outlawed or exiled, or deprived of his standing in any other way, nor will we proceed with force against him, or send others to do so, except by the lawful judgment of his equals or by the law of the land.

“To no one will we sell, to no one deny or delay right or justice.”

In June 1215, these guarantees of justice and freedom from unwarranted imprisonment were given no particular prominence, but their interpretation as enduring principles would resonate through the centuries and around the globe.

The charter had also established that the law was a power in its own right, and significantly that the sovereign was not above the law. It decreed that 25 barons be elected to ensure the king’s compliance, with power to seize his property if he failed to stick to the charter’s terms.

Why did King John break the Magna Carta?

However, even before the parchment was dry, King John ignored his promises.

A few months after it had been issued, John persuaded the Pope to declare Magna Carta illegal because it interfered with the rights of the king. Pope Innocent III declared the charter null and void because it had been obtained under duress.

The barons would not accept this and a civil war broke out, in which most of the barons fought for Magna Carta against John. Thus began the first of the Barons’ Wars (1215 to 1217), which, although John died in 1216, ended in royalist victory.

King Henry III

The Charter of Liberties was revised and reissued in 1216, 1217 and 1225 in attempts to stabilise King Henry III’s reign – John’s son was just nine years old when he inherited the crown. After 1217, the name Magna Carta – the Great Charter – was used, when clauses covering the law of the royal forest were added into a separate and shorter Charter of the Forest.

The Second Barons' War

The Barons’ Wars erupted again from 1264 to 1267, with victory for Henry, but not before the barons’ charismatic leader, Simon de Montfort (later killed in battle), called a parliament in 1265. This is seen as an early forerunner of our modern Parliament because it included borough representatives.

The 1225 Magna Carta

In 1297, King Edward I confirmed Henry’s 1225 Magna Carta, and the text was entered on the statute roll. Three clauses remain valid to this day: defending the freedom and rights of the English Church; confirming the liberties and customs of the City of London and other towns and ports; and guaranteeing freedom from imprisonment except by the judgment of one’s peers or the law of the land.

Magna Carta may have begun as a charter that addressed the concerns of the higher ranks and ignored the majority of 13th-century society – the ‘unfree peasants or villeins’ – but in the long term, its key concepts took on a life of their own. Whenever sovereigns exceeded their powers or due process of law was threatened, the Great Charter could be reinterpreted and invoked as a rallying cry.

Several sites in Britain played important roles in the Magna Carta story, including, of course, Runnymede and Salisbury Cathedral, where scribes made up to 40 copies of the original document, and the British Library also holds two copies of the Magna Carta you can get up close to. And if you'd like to learn more, David Starkey's Magna Carta: The True Story Behind the Charter is available on Amazon and is an entertaining, extremely informative guide to this transformative piece of British history.

item.title: The June/July 2026 issue of Discover Britain is out now! Get your copy here
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey, Editor
item.description: This issue we explore the beautiful chocolate-box villages of the Cotswolds
item.content:encoded:
This issue, we visit what is arguably England’s most honeypot region of all: The Cotswolds.

Starting on page 13, we bring you 24 pages of some of the loveliest (and quaintest) things to do in this cute corner – from rubber-duck racing (yes, really) to afternoon tea, castle visits, and knitting escapes. With village fêtes and riverside picnics, summer is a delightful time to visit, but don’t worry if it’s too late for this year, as winter brings its own charms, and if you book now, you could have a cosy Cotswolds Christmas.

Elsewhere on this issue, we have some fascinating history pieces to sink your teeth into. First up, in A Tale of Two Tapestries (page 39), Emily-Ann Elliott tells the story of Britain’s Victorian replica of the famous Bayeux Tapestry, and explains how you can see this, plus the Norman original in Britain this year.

In When Franklin Met Hill (page 56), Michelle Fleming reveals how a little-known meeting at Northern Ireland’s Hillsborough Castle helped ignite the bid for American Independence.

And finally, in Queen of Convenience (page 68), our Tudor expert Jo Romero reveals how far from being a sad, rejected side note in British history, Anne of Cleves was actually an adept political diplomat. Indeed, her shrewd thinking enabled her to negotiate a deal with her ex-husband that at least a couple of Henry VIII’s wives would have (not) died for. A riveting read. Enjoy the issue.

Shop the issue now here!

item.title: Every Outlander fan needs to save this date - you could own your own piece of Outlander history as Bonhams announce exclusive costume and prop sale this summer
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Following in the wake of their highly successful The Crown and Downton Abbey costume auctions, the Outlander auction will take place this August in London
item.content:encoded:
When Bonhams announced it was auctioning off the real costumes from Outlander this summer, I rushed to check how much it would set me back to buy a little piece of this Scottish cultural institution.

The auction, announced earlier this week by Bonhams, will accompany an exhibition in Edinburgh in May, followed by an exhibition at Bonhams on New Bond Street in London, and has gone straight to the top of my list of the best things on in London this summer.

The exhibition and auction come as we wave goodbye to the global sensation that is Outlander, currently airing its eighth and final season on Starz. All six series of Outlander are available to watch on Amazon Prime, but I'd also recommend reading Diana Gabaldon's brilliant novels, which are available to buy as a box set on Amazon too.

View this post on Instagram

The 'Outlander effect' has generated an estimated £140 million in screen tourism for Scotland, and Visit Scotland predicts that one in five tourists to Scotland has been inspired to visit by film, TV and books, including, of course, Outlander.

Outlander filming locations in Scotland are hotspots for fans, and many tour companies run specific Outlander tours of Scotland to see all the places it was filmed and the beautiful landscapes that inspired Gabaldon when she was writing the story. I'd recommend Rabbie's for Outlander tours - they have several different options to suit all travellers, ranging from 1-4 days.

The Bonhams Outlander auction costume exhibition will launch a preview in Bonhams Edinburgh from 26-31 May, where visitors will be able to see the actual costumes themselves. The exhibition will then open at Bonhams New Bond Street in London on 20 July and run until 4 August. Tickets to the preview exhibition are free and can be acquired from Bonhams.

The live auction will then take place at Bonhams in London on 5 July 2026.

The auction's Sale Manager, Madeleine Cater, commented: "When it comes to historical fantasy, Outlander is unmatched in its power of escapism, drama, and romance. For more than a decade, the show has captivated audiences, and we are delighted to bring some of the incredible costumes and props to auction. With exhibitions in Edinburgh and London, fans will be able to see the amazing craftsmanship and attention to detail that have gone into creating the world of Outlander up close. Bonhams is proud to bring yet another landmark series to auction following the success of our sales and exhibitions for The Crown and Downton Abbey – and much like the final series itself, Outlander, the auction is not to be missed."

The items up for sale include some of the series's most iconic costumes and props. From Claire's blue evening gown, known as 'The Peacock Dress', her Red Ball Gown from season two and her 'white dress', worn when she goes through the stones.

You could be the proud owner of Jamie's costume worn at the Battle of Culloden or even Claire's three wedding rings.

Estimated prices range from £300 to £5000. Start saving now if you've got your eye on something! You can see the full list here.

Scotland's Outlander effect is mind-boggling when you think about it, and the impact the books and TV series have had on fans all over the world is unmatched. To mark the end of the series, I put together some of my favourite Outlander moments from all eight series, and if that still isn't enough Outlander in concert has been announced for early next year, you can get your hands on tickets via Ticketmaster.

item.title: The man behind the crown: How King Charles III, Britain’s longest-serving Prince of Wales, became its most unconventional king
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From grandson of King George VI to heir presumptive at just three years old. King Charles has spent his life in royal service. Here we take a look back at the life of our king
item.content:encoded:
When King Charles III ascended the throne on the death of his mother, Queen Elizabeth II, it was an emotional day for royal fans all over the world. Not only had we watched his mother reign for 70 years, but we also had watched Charles grow from a little prince to Prince of Wales and heir to the throne, and now as King.

8 September 2022 marked the day of the death of Queen Elizabeth II, when we added another notch to our timeline of Kings and Queens of England and Great Britain, which dates back over a thousand years.

Now in his fourth reigning year, and aged 77 years old, King Charles III continues to do Britain proud with his hard-working ethic, passion and dedication to his role and this country. Acclaimed royal biographer Robert Hardman has written a brilliant book on Charles's life, Charles III: New King. New Court. The Inside Story. which is available on Amazon, which covers all of Charles's life, from health to interests and relationships.

Here, we've put together a timeline of King Charles III's life, from his early life and life at school, to his accession and role as king.

When was King Charles III born?

Charles Philip Arthur George Mountbatten-Windsor was born at 9.14pm on 14 November 1948 by Caesarian section, after a 30-hour labour.

The Buhl Room at Buckingham Palace had been converted into an operating theatre ready for his arrival. Prince Philip was not present, passing the time playing endless games of squash with his private secretary. Once Charles was born Philip proudly declared that his son resembled a “plum pudding”, and presented his wife with a bouquet of red roses and carnations.

When did King Charles III become heir to the throne?

Following the death of her father King George VI on 6 February 1952, the Queen acceded to the throne. Prince Charles became the heir apparent and was given the title of Duke of Cornwall, as well as six titles in the Scottish peerage. The Queen’s coronation ceremony took place on 2 June the following year. The 4-year-old prince received a specially illustrated invitation, emblazoned with a lion, unicorn and marching soldiers.

Where did King Charles III go to school?

In May 1955, Buckingham Palace announced that Prince Charles was to start school aged eight, the first heir to the throne to be educated outside the palace. He briefly attended Hill House in Knightsbridge, London, before moving to Cheam in Hampshire, the prep school his father had attended, swapping his palatial home life for a shared school dormitory. It was not a happy time. Charles was an easy target for bullies and suffered acute homesickness.

The Queen Mother thought that her sensitive grandson should be sent to Eton, the school that many of his parents’ sons attended, which was near Windsor. But Prince Philip believed that Gordonstoun in Scotland, whose regime was founded on cold showers and physical exertion, would toughen up his timorous son. However, Charles was bullied mercilessly, and in miserable letters home described school as “sheer hell.”

After three years at Gordonstoun, Charles was sent on a 6-month exchange to Timbertop, a remote outpost of Geelong Grammar school in Melbourne, Australia. The experience was challenging – he hiked cross-country in the sweltering heat, camped out in the cold, chopped logs and fed pigs – but after a lifetime spent in the public gaze, Charles embraced life in rural Victoria. He returned for his final year at Gordonstoun relaxed and happy, and was made head boy. He later described his time in Australia as “the most enjoyable part of my whole education.”

Charles broke with royal tradition by enrolling at university instead of the armed forces, opting to study archaeology and anthropology, before changing to history for the second part of his degree. On his first day at Cambridge, 8 October 1967, more than a thousand people thronged the gates of Trinity College. He was taken to the Wren Library, where he was shown the signatures in the college register of his grandfather George VI and great-great-grandfather Edward VII, who had both studied at Trinity.

Charles thrived at Cambridge. He joined Trinity’s drama group, the Dryden Society, writing his own sketches and performed skits for Footlights, the university’s famous comedy troupe, appearing as a weatherman and a dustman, among other guises. The Queen came to see him on stage. Awarded a 2:2 Bachelor of Arts in June 1970, Prince Charles became the first heir to the throne ever to obtain a university degree.

When did King Charles III become Prince of Wales?

The investiture of 20-year-old Charles as Prince of Wales took place on a grey and overcast day at Caernarfon Castle, Wales on 1 July 1969.

As Charles knelt on a pedestal before his mother the Queen, she bestowed upon him the symbols that marked him as Prince of Wales: the sword, coronet, ring, the gold rod and the kingly mantle. Charles pledged his allegiance to the Queen with the oath: “I, Charles, Prince of Wales, do become your liege man of life and limb.”

Some 4000 guests were present in the castle to witness the ceremony, while 500 million watched around the world. He was to be the longest-serving Prince of Wales in British history.

Charles served in the Royal Air Force after studying at RAF Cranwell in Lincolnshire and went on to be a member of the Royal Navy like many of his ancestors. 

Charles and Diana

Prince Charles first met Lady Diana Spencer when she was just a teenager and he was seeing her older sister.

After meeting only 13 times, the couple got engaged and were married within 6 months, on 29 July 1981 at St Paul’s Cathedral: the first royal wedding to be held at the church since 1501.
