The bride travelled to the church in a fairytale glass coach, through streets thronged with 600,000 people. Her dress was made of frothily romantic ivory taffeta, covered in 10,000 pearls and complete with a 25-foot train, the longest in royal history.

In the ceremony itself, Diana omitted the word ‘obey’ from her wedding vows, the first royal to do so.

A year later, the royal couple welcomed their son, Prince William, into the world, shortly followed by Prince Harry in 1984.

After 15 years of marriage, Charles and Diana filed for a divorce and separated in 1996. On August 31 1997, Diana, Princess of Wales, tragically passed away after being in a car crash in Paris.  

King Charles III and Camilla Parker-Bowles, now Queen Consort, became engaged and got married in 2005. They had a civil ceremony rather than a religious ceremony at Windsor Castle with a subsequent blessing at St George’s Chapel. 

King Charles III's interest in nature

The King has been passionate about environmental issues since he was a boy, extolling the benefits of bees, natural medicines, hedgerows, gardening and organic farming.

His first major speech on the environment was in 1970, but it is only in recent years that his views have become more widely shared.

Since becoming monarch, he is subject to different rules when it comes to showing a political stance, though this will not stop the King from being environmentally conscious in his own right.

He has switched to biomass boilers and solar panels at his homes, and even had his vintage Aston Martin converted to run on bioethanol made from wine and cheese.

A proud ‘tree-hugger’, he is happiest in his garden, and according to courtiers, every time he plants a tree he gives a branch “a friendly shake to wish it well.”

He is also a keen watercolourist and often paints pictures of his garden at Highgrove.

In 2010, King Charles even wrote his own book on the subject, Harmony: A New Way of Looking at Our World is available on Amazon and is a very interesting insight into the king's personal views on the matter.

During his time as the Prince of Wales, the King was patron to over 400 organisations, including The Prince’s Charities, including the newly renamed King's Trust, which raise over £100 million each year to help young people.

When did King Charles III become king?

Immediately after the passing of his mother, Queen Elizabeth II, on 8 September 2022, Charles became King Charles III.

She was on the throne for a total of 70 years and 214 days, making her the longest-reigning monarch in British history.

In London, a long procession, with the coffin draped in the Royal Standard on a gun carriage and followed by the King and members of his family, made its way to Westminster Hall for a four-day lying in state. Many thousands queued for days to file past and pay their respects. According to the Queen’s wishes, the state funeral took place at Westminster Abbey for the first time since 1760 (they are usually held at Windsor Castle).

Members of the Royal Family followed the cortège, with the coffin pulled on a gun carriage by ratings from the Royal Navy, from Westminster Hall to the Abbey. After the funeral there was a service of committal at St George’s Chapel in Windsor Castle, and the late Queen was laid to rest in the vault in which her husband Prince Philip, her parents, George VI and Queen Elizabeth, and her sister Margaret had been buried.

King Charles III's coronation took place on Saturday, 6 May 2023 at Westminster Abbey, where he was crowned King alongside his wife, Camilla, after 74 years as heir to the throne - the longest serving Prince of Wales in British history.

After commenting on his mother’s life-long service to her country, he said, “I, too, now solemnly pledge myself, throughout the remaining time God grants me, to uphold the constitutional principles at the heart of our nation.”

2026 marks 100 years since the birth of King Charles III's mother, Queen Elizabeth II, and the nation is celebrating with a special exhibition at the King's Gallery at Buckingham Palace. Queen Elizabeth II: Her life in style will run until 18 October 2026; tickets are available via the Royal Collection here.

For those wanting to learn more about Queen Elizabeth II, or perhaps for those who can’t make it to the exhibition but would like to be part of it, The Royal Collection have also released an accompanying book by Senior Curator Caroline de Guitaut, who has been in charge of putting together this incredible new exhibition. Queen Elizabeth II: Fashion & Style is available on Amazon and will explore the late Queen’s unique style and legacy.

item.title: This is the closest you’ll ever get to meeting Jane Austen herself - this historic house with unrivalled family connections to the author is offering fans a once-in-a-lifetime experience
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: This is how to get your ticket for the hottest Austen-themed event this summer
item.content:encoded:
For the first time ever this spring, you can book tickets for a special tour of Chawton House with Jane Austen's great-niece, the last of the author's nieces to grow up there.

For fans all over the world, including myself, Jane Austen would be the one historical figure they would love to meet - she would certainly be on my dream dinner party guest list - so it's no surprise that tickets to these very special tours of Chawton are selling like hotcakes.

Chawton House in the village of Chawton in Hampshire was owned by Jane Austen's brother, Edward. Edward offered the estate cottage to his mother and sisters, Cassandra and Jane, which is now the Jane Austen House Museum. Chawton Manor and Its Owners: A Family History is available on Amazon and is a history of the house and its connections to the Austen family.

Living just down the road, Jane visited the house often, where she would see her brother, nieces and nephews. The cottage where she lived on the estate was where she wrote and published her most beloved works, including Pride and Prejudice and lived out the last eight years of her life.

The Austen family have stayed in Chawton House ever since, and now, the last of Jane Austen's nieces to grow up in the house, Caroline Jane Knight, is offering visitors the chance to tour the house and village with her this summer.

View post on Facebook

A small number of lucky Jane Austen fans will have the chance to step into her world through the eyes of her family. You'll walk through the village lanes she knew, visit the 'Great House' where she dined with her brother Edward - Caroline's fourth great grandfather - and hear the unique Austen family stories, memories and traditions that have been passed down through generations, from a member of the family itself.

Caroline will regale you with her family's private theory about Austen's novel Emma (I'm particularly intrigued about this one), Austen family recipes from Jane's time that have been passed down through the generations, and what it was really like to grow up as Jane Austen's niece in the heart of her literary legacy.

All proceeds support The Austen Knights of Chawton Family Archive, preserving Jane Austen's family heritage for future generations.

Visitors can choose between a Village Walk on hour walking tour of Chawton with Caroline, where you'll hear family stories and walk where Jane did, or the very special VIP Day, which includes a village walk, lunch with Caroline in the Old Kitchen tearoom at Chawton House, and a walk through Chawton House with a guide, before exploring the gardens with Caroline where you can ask her all your burning questions.

Tickets are limited and available on selected days in June and July 2026. Book them via Tickettailor.

Whether or not you manage to get your hands on one of these much sought-after tickets, I'd recommend getting a copy of Caroline's book, Jane & Me: My Austen Heritage, which is available on Amazon and is a brilliant insight into Jane Austen's family heritage, from a real-life member, and how Caroline embraced her heritage and founded the Jane Austen Literacy Foundation.

item.title: The Best Hotels in Scotland for 2026: Cosy Inns, Castle Stays and Country House Retreats
item.dc:creator: Natasha Foges, Editor, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Whether you want to retreat by the fire to a cosy inn, indulge in a spa stay or play golf on a country estate, here’s our pick of the 13 best hotels in Scotland to book in 2026
item.content:encoded:
Choosing the best hotel in Scotland is like choosing your favourite child - utterly and totally impossible.

Like Scotland's diverse landscapes, its best hotels fall within numerous categories: cosy inns, golf retreats, seaside resorts, country estates or stylish townhouses.

We've had the arduous task of trawling the length and breadth of Scotland, from the lowlands to the Highlands, and the outermost islands to the buzzing city centres, to find the best of Scotland's hotels. If you need something to guide you on your hotel searching, our Scotland Magazine editor, Sally Coffey, has written a brilliant Scottish travel guide. Moon Scotland: Highland Road Trips, Outdoor Adventures, Pubs & Castles is available on Amazon and is packed with expert advice, travel tips and itineraries for your Scottish adventure.

So now, where to stay?

Here is our guide to the very best hotels in Scotland for 2026:

1. Pierhouse Hotel, Argyll, Oban

Tucked away on Scotland’s sublime West Coast in the sleepy village of Port Appin, the simple whitewashed Pierhouse Hotel commands a truly majestic view, taking in the islands of Lismore and Mull, across the waters of Loch Linnhe.

Step inside and you’ll find a convivial inn, with flagstoned floors, a wood-burning fire and chic nautical decor. The restaurant is renowned for its seafood, fished from the crystalline local waters and unfussily but expertly cooked.

The 12 guest rooms are decorated in tasteful, soothing tones, and some have spectacular views of the loch.

There are still plenty of opportunities to get out on the water, too, from whale watching trips to sea kayaking. Or take the little Lady of Lismore ferry from the jetty outside the hotel over the loch to Lismore, a wild and beautiful place of ruined Viking castles, freshwater lochs and hidden coves.

Book now via Expedia

2. Kinloch Lodge, Isle of Skye

Backed by vivid green forest, Kinloch Lodge gazes out over a shimmering loch at the southeastern end of the spectacular Isle of Skye. The hotel has an impressive historic pedigree: in the 16th century, it was the hunting lodge of the Macdonalds, one of the largest and oldest of the Scottish clans, and it is still in the family, now run by Isabella MacDonald.

This is a place of timeless charm, where guests are welcomed like old friends to a country-house weekend: floral wallpaper, squishy sofas and board games in the lounges, and refined luxury in the bedrooms make for a relaxed atmosphere – a place to retreat to after a day exploring Skye’s peaks and lochs.

Lady Claire, Isabella’s mother, is a celebrated chef and ambassador for Scottish cuisine, and Kinloch’s restaurant is the pride of the hotel. Head chef Jordan Webb’s exceptional cooking uses vegetables from Kinloch’s kitchen garden, poultry from Perthshire, fine Highland cheeses, and lobster, crab and scallops from the clear waters of Skye.

Book now via Trivago

3. The Cameron House Resort, Loch Lomond

The Cameron House Resort, which enjoys an unrivalled position on the Loch Lomond's shore, is the best vantage point for enjoying all that the area has to offer. At the heart of the resort is a baronial mansion, smartly refurbished in recent years, while in the grounds nestle the Cameron House Lodges: light, airy and extremely comfortable, with big windows enticing you to sample the delights of the wider resort.

Take in Loch Lomond on a champagne cruise, soar above it by seaplane, go paddleboarding or jet skiing, or simply cycle round the lake’s picturesque perimeter.

The lodges are self-catering, but you might be tempted to sample one of the resort’s restaurants, which include The Boat House, where you dine amid the bobbing boats of the hotel’s marina, and the Cameron Grill, where, after a day’s exploring, you can feast on succulent steak and lobster, served up by tartan-clad waiters.

Book now via Expedia

4. SCHLOSS Roxburghe, Kelso

Don’t overlook the rolling Scottish Borders – a scenic swathe of hills and valleys dotted with grand castles and ruined abbeys – in your haste to get to the Highlands. SCHLOSS Roxburghe in Kelso has recently undergone a sparkling renovation to become a bona fide resort, with an impressive spa and top-hole golf course.

Step inside, and you’ll find tartan carpets, grandfather clocks and antique prints, but beyond the original building is a striking contemporary wing. A unified colour palette, with plenty of natural wood and teal accents, links the hotel’s two halves. The bedrooms in the old house have more of a traditional feel, while the ‘estate rooms’ in the newer wing are bright, airy and modern.

An array of bars and restaurants includes the new offering Charlie’s, which serves up moreish Scottish fare with views of the gardens through floor-to-ceiling windows. But it’s the spa that’s the jewel in the crown of the renovation – a glamorous space boasting a vast relaxation room that overlooks the heated pool and the green lawns beyond. The Highlands can wait.

Book now via Expedia

5. The Torridon, Wester Ross

Picture the perfect Highland scene, and it probably looks much like the approach to The Torridon, a historic hotel that stands in splendid isolation some 50 miles west of Inverness. Inside, the wood-panelled hall, with its stags’ heads and crackling fire, spirits visitors back to the house’s Victorian beginnings.

It was built by the first Earl of Lovelace (husband of the mathematician Ada Lovelace) as a hunting lodge in 1887.

Original features abound, brought gently up to date with elegant decor and cosy, cushioned nooks for sitting with a book or gazing at the view.

Outdoors, a host of activities await, from archery to kayaking. You can also explore the impressive kitchen garden, which provides fruit and vegetables for the hotel’s two restaurants: the refined 1887 and the more informal Bo & Muc.

Book now via Expedia

6. Gleneagles, Perthshire

Gleneagles needs no introduction and is well known for being one of the best hotels in Scotland, if not the world. This venerable hotel in Perthshire has hosted everyone from Vivien Leigh to our late Queen over the years.

The hotel was a glamorous destination from the very start. Elegantly attired guests arriving in 1924 were treated to Art Deco interiors, bedrooms dripping in luxurious jazz-age glamour, and an on-site dance band. Although gently updated, the luxurious interiors retain the style of the Roaring Twenties, and the attentive staff are still primed to cater for their guests’ every whim.

Gleneagles describes itself as ‘The Glorious Playground’, and there’s no shortage of outdoor pursuits to enjoy on the 850-acre estate, from riding, shooting, falconry and fishing to a round on one of the championship golf courses. Aching limbs can be soothed in the impressive spa before you choose from an array of bars and restaurants, including Andrew Fairlie, Scotland’s only restaurant with two Michelin stars.

Book now via Trivago

7. Prestonfield House, Edinburgh

Several hotels in Edinburgh deserve to be on this list, but Prestonfield House tops the mark. Standing proudly at the end of a sweeping driveway in a quiet Edinburgh neighbourhood, Prestonfield, part of the PoB hotel collection, is like a miniature country estate in the city. Step inside and you enter a dazzlingly theatrical world.

The 23 rooms are all individually styled, but all share a heady Rococo glamour, with bold wallpapers, swagged curtains, antique furniture and swathes of luxurious brocade, velvet and silk.

Outdoors, twenty acres of parkland are at guests’ disposal, with views of the iconic Arthur’s Seat. The front lawn is set up for croquet, two well-fed Highland cows graze in the grounds, and there’s even a resident peacock.

Many guests opt to dine at The Witchery, under the same ownership as the hotel. Set at the gates of Edinburgh Castle, it’s pure Gothic romance, with 17th-century oak panelling, tapestries and gilded leather screens, lit by flickering candlelight. Like the hotel, it’s decadent, luxurious and unique.

Book now via Expedia

8. The Ambassador Hotel, Inverness

This grand, stylish hotel in Inverness is the perfect destination for travellers searching for boutique luxury combined with Scottish heritage and impressive Highland scenery. Even better? The hotel has just gone through a £6-million expansion.

The Ambassador Hotel occupies two Victorian townhouses dating back to 1874 and overlooks the River Ness, in the heart of the Highlands' most exciting and vibrant city. Visitors get the best of both worlds, being close to the city, but just a short drive from Scotland's most impressive and breathtaking landscapes, including the deep, mystery-shrouded waters of Loch Ness and the historic postcard-ready Eilean Donan castle.

The hotel has 14 individual bedrooms inspired by 19th-century Highland travel and classic Scottish design, with layered textiles, antiques and tasteful tartan influences.

The hotel restaurant Talk of the Town blends Scottish, European and Indian influences and is led by Corporate Executive Chef Sinclair Pinto, who worked for Michelin-starred kitchens in London and the US for 15 years with chefs including Angela Hartnett and Helene Darroze and features local Highland seafood and meats.

Finish your evening in The Other Room, a cosy library-style bar, where you can enjoy a wee dram before ascending the original Victorian staircase to your cosy bedroom.

Book now via Expedia.

9. Glenapp Castle, Ayrshire

What could be better than staying in a Scottish castle? Ayrshire's Glenapp Castle is the ultimate Scottish castle escape.

Glenapp Castle overlooks the Firth of Clyde on the outskirts of Ballantrae and is less than two hours from Glasgow. The castle is an incredible example of Scottish baronial architecture and looks like it belongs in a fairytale, with turrets galore.

The castle sits within a 110-acre private UNESCO-designated biosphere, and the gardens are well worth a stroll. Within the Victorian garden, you'll find the Azalea restaurant inside a 150ft Victorian glasshouse. Its menu centres on the region's bountiful produce and flavours from the garden just metres away.

The five-star hotel has 21 luxuriously decorated bedrooms, including several suites, which all wonderfully fit the 'castle' description, but with all the modern necessities you could ask for too.

There's plenty of country activities to fill your time too, from archery to sea safaris and a Highland coo experience.

Book now via Expedia.

10. The Fife Arms, Braemar

View this post on Instagram

This charming boutique hotel is about as cosy as it gets when it comes to Scottish hotels, but still manages to ooze effortless, modern style.

The 5-star hotel is an imaginatively transformed Victorian coaching inn within the historic village of Braemar in the Cairngorms National Park, famous for the annual Braemar Gathering Highland Games, which are attended by the Royal Family.

The hotel itself holds two Michelin Keys and blends Scottish heritage, art and a perfect dose of Highland charm within its historic walls. The 16,000 pieces of art it is home to include Picassos (yes, really) and Brueghels and even a watercolour sketch by Queen Victoria herself. The decorative style is eclectic and riotous - think antique taxidermy with abstract neon antler chandeliers.

There are 46 uniquely designed rooms, each telling a different story and divided into three categories: Scottish Culture, Nature & Poetry and Croft - you might stay in a room centred about the Jacobite Risings, or perhaps the Modernist poet Nan Shepherd. What is guaranteed is that each will be heavenly and expertly blend luxury with the hotel's unique style.

There are two restaurants within The Fife Arms, Clunie Dining Room and The Flying Stag, one more casual than the other, but both offering Michelin-Guide worthy fare that will stay with you for years to come.

Book now via Expedia.

11. Fonab Castle Hotel, Pitlochry

This picture-perfect Baronial castle doubles as a glamorous five-star hotel and is the perfect place to live our your Traitors-style dreams in the Scottish Highlands, boasting magnificent views over Loch Faskally and the River Tummel.

The perfect based for exploring the Cairngorms National Park Fonab is a luxury five-star hotel with 42 beautiful bedrooms, including an extra luxurious penthouse suite. Each room is bespoke in its design and furnished with incredible antiques and Scottish fabrics, leaning into the castle's Victorian heritage.

The hotel is also home to two restaurants, the 3 AA-Rosette awarded Sandemans for fine dining, or the more relaxed 1892 on the Loch restaurant with stunnng floor to ceiling windows looking out over the River Tummel.

The best part? Fonab has its very own woodland spa, with views across the lakes and mountains - utter bliss.

Book now via Expedia.

12. The Balmoral, Edinburgh

Of course, we couldn't forget Scotland's most iconic Edinburgh hotel, The Balmoral, which greets weary passengers as their alight onto Prince's Street from Waverley Station with a host of Scottish charm and tastefully extravagant luxury.

Originally the North British Station Hotel, The Balmoral has been welcoming train travellers into Edinburgh since 1902 and, now part of Rocco Forte Hotels, famously keeps its famous clock running three minutes fast to ensure travellers catch their trains.

Edinburgh's most fasionable place to stay for the last 100 years, The Balmoral remains a symbol of luxury and has played host Sophia Loren, Elizabeth Taylor, Paul McCartney, Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother, and British Prime Ministers Edward Heath and Harold Wilson. It is also famous for being the site in which JK Rowling finished writing Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows, and the suite where this happened is named after her today.

The interiors pay homage to Scottish landscapes, with woodland greens, sky blues and mountain greys and, as to be expected, bedrooms are extremely luxuriously appointed.

It boasts its own Michelin-starred restaurant, Number One, and the French-inspired Brasserie Prince, as well as three casual dining and drinking spaces - and what better way to wile away an evening while admiring the unrivalled views of Edinburgh Castle.

Book now via Expedia.

13. Mar Hall Golf & Spa Resort, Glasgow

Though it sits a little outside the city, Mar Hall is jsut a 30 minute drive from the centre of Glasgow, and only 10 minutes from the airport so is perfect for travellers on their way to the Highlands. But this hotel is worth making it a destination by itself.

Built in 1828, Mar Hall reopened in 2025 after a £20 million transformation and was ranked by Condé Nast last year as one of the top 20 resorts in Europe.

The interiors are opulently decorated with rich jewel tones and there are seven categories of rooms, right up to Grand Suites, some of which include grand pianos and pool tables.

There are four excellent drinking and dining options on site, as well as an 18 hole golf course, a spa offering treatments such as Scottish seaweed scrubs and plenty of exciting experiences available to guests, including pony trekking, off road driving and even helicopter tours to admire the surrounding landscape from the best angle.

Book now via Expedia.

If I've inspired you to book your trip to Scotland this year I'd recommend perusing my guide to the 8 most beautiful spots in Scotland that should be on everyone's bucket list, to guide you on your itinerary, and hotel booking. But if you'd like to explore the places a little more off the beaten track, I'd recommend Unseen Scotland: The Hidden Places, History and Lore of the Wild Scottish Landscape from Amazon, which will guide you to the places the tourists don't know about.

item.title: Mad About History - Five seriously surprising facts you never knew about the Vikings and that might just reveal a softer side to the fiercest, most terrifying warriors in British history
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Did you know Vikings dyed their hair for fashion? Or that they enjoyed saunas and had a whole day once a week just for bathing? These weird Viking facts will change your mind about the Scandinavian invaders who have garnered such a terrible reputation
item.content:encoded:
I'm not ashamed to admit that the majority of what I know about the Vikings comes from Terry Deary's brilliant Horrible Histories books, and my trip to York's Viking Danelaw village aged 10.

But really, my knowledge of Vikings and their history and influence in Anglo-Saxon Britain is pretty limited, so when I visited the beautiful Holy Island of Lindisfarne in Northumberland this past weekend, the first place the Vikings ever invaded in Britain, I took the opportunity to learn a little more about these Scandinavian invaders who get such a bad reputation in our history.

Of course, you know me, I'm always going to recommend a Horrible Histories book for anyone starting out on any historical subject. Terry Deary's Vicious Vikings is available on Amazon and is a highly entertaining and extremely informative place to start.

Who were the Vikings?

The Vikings were Scandinavian seafaring people from what is now Denmark, Norway and Sweden who travelled over seas in longships, raided, traded and settled across parts of Europe, North Africa and North America between the late 8th and late 11th centuries.

They were known as fierce warriors but were also skilled explorers, shipbuilders and farmers. The first known Viking attack on Britain was at Lindisfarne Priory in Northumberland in 793AD.

Much of eastern and northern England was under Viking control and law (Danelaw) throughout the 9th and 10th centuries, but it was in 1066 when Viking King of England Harald Hardrada was defeated at the Battle of Stamford Bridge in 1066, in the lead up to William the Conqueror's victory at the Battle of Hastings, that the Viking age of Britain ended, though their cultural influence remained.

View this post on Instagram

As this is Mad About History, here are five seriously weird facts about the Vikings that will definitely surprise you:

1. The word 'Viking' means going on a pirate raid in Old Norse

Vikings were not actually called 'Vikings', i.e. this name doesn't originate from where they came from, whereas 'Danes' did.

The word 'Viking' comes from Old Norse and refers to a pirate raider. It was not a synonym for Scandinavian or Norseman as we often use it today.

It is also often linked to the Norse word vík, which means 'creek' or 'inlet' and could be used to describe someone from a coastal area who went on sea expeditions.

2. Vikings did not wear horns on their helmets

Nope, sadly not, though this does seem like a shame as the image of scary Vikings wearing horn-laden helmets is so ingrained in our brains, it's hard to undo. But yes, Vikings did not actually wear horns on their helmets.

The horned helmet was actually invented by a Victorian costume designer named Carl Doepler who designed them for Wagner's operas.

Archaeologists have discovered that Vikings actually wore simple, functional helmets made of iron or leather, like the Gjermundu helmet discovered in a Viking warrior's grave in Norway.

3. Vikings bathed once a week

These fierce, scary warriors actually had excellent hygiene and were believed to have taken baths once a week, which was far more than any other European at the time.

Saturday was known as laurdag or laugardagur ("washing day") in Viking culture, and Vikings were known to use hot springs (when their surroundings allowed - i.e. in Iceland) as well as their own version of steam baths and saunas.

If you think back to my piece on Regency women's beauty regimes, 800 years later, the English were bathing once a week, so that certainly puts it into perspective.

4. Vikings dyed their hair and beards blonde and wore eyeliner

So we know they cared about smelling clean, but we also know that they cared about how they looked, too. Cleanliness was, in fact, a matter of pride and honour - Vikings really cared about what their contemporaries, and their enemies, thought of them.

Archaeologists have even excavated grooming tools like combs, razors, tweezers and ear cleaners in Viking graves.

We know that they made a soap out of animal fat and wood ash, which would both cleanse their hair and beards, and dye them blonde. The reason they did this? Fashion, of course. Fair hair was highly valued as a beauty ideal in Norse culture and was associated with high status.

5. Vikings had a warrior technique called 'going berserk'

Some elite Viking warriors were known as 'berserkers' and were renowned for their unusual style of fighting. They would fight in a trance-like, uncontrollable fury and howl like wolves, which became known as 'going berserk', which is the origin of the phrase we use today.

The word Berserkr translates to "bear-shirt", meaning they were naked or wore bear skins. The 'berserkers' were incredibly strong and displayed immunity to pain, and, unsurprisingly, as they were often put in the front line during battle, they were used to terrify enemies.

If these surprising Viking facts have piqued your interest in these fierce Scandinavian settlers, I'd recommend Thomas Williams' Viking Britain: A History, which is available on Amazon, which shows that the Vikings did not just come to rape, murder and pillage, but to settle, trade and colonise in Britain. He also looks at the Viking influence on British culture today, from the writings JRR Tolkein, to the art of William Morris.

item.title: From the birthplace of King Arthur to the home of Macbeth, this is your ultimate guide to the best castles in the UK that should be on every history-lover’s bucket-list
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: The country’s abundance of mighty fortresses are an enduring reminder of our turbulent past. Read on for the best castles in the UK that you can visit today
item.content:encoded:
The best castles in the UK – some ruined, some palatial, some still lived in today – are an enduring reminder of our turbulent past.

As a history-lover living in Britain, I am reminded every day of how lucky we are that this incredible history still surrounds us, and can be visited whenever we like. In Lewes, where I live, we are lucky enough to have our own medieval castle that towers over everything, with a brilliantly gory history to match.

There are estimated to be over 4,000 castles and castle sites dotted around the UK, which is a truly staggering number. Of course, they are all worth a visit, but we can't list them all here, so I'd recommend AA's Best of Britain's Castles: 100 of the Most Impressive Historic Sites in Britain, which is available on Amazon and is an essential companion who can't resist visiting a good castle.

If I had to narrow down the list for those with a tight itinerary on their visit to Britain, here is my guide to the 17 best castles in the UK, all of which are open for visitors to explore.

1. Windsor Castle, Berkshire

If you want to gain insight into the Royal Family and more than 900 years of royal history then there really is no better place to visit than this splendid riverside castle, which, as well as being one of the best castles in the UK (if not the best?), is also the largest and oldest occupied castle in the world.

It was here, after all, that King Charles II set out to rival the opulence of King Louis XIV’s Versailles, to create the grandest state apartments in England, while King George IV added the mighty Waterloo Chamber in celebration of the defeat of Napoleon Bonaparte in 1815. All of this was endangered when, in 1992 a fire swept through the castle, destroying 115 rooms.

The meticulous restoration was completed five years to the day after the fire. More recently, the committal service for Queen Elizabeth II took place in St George’s Chapel.

Get your ticket for Windsor Castle via the Visit Britain shop.

2. Bodiam Castle, East Sussex

The outside of this 14th-century moated castle looks impeccably preserved and you’d be forgiven for thinking you could expect much of the same inside, but sadly not.

Like so many other historic buildings, the English civil war reduced the interior to ruins, though mercifully the exterior was saved and throughout the 17th and 18th centuries, as ivy grew around the building, its dilapidated beauty was celebrated. Today the castle is in the hands of the National Trust, and it has been restored where possible, earning it a deserved place on the list of best castles in the UK.

Among its most notable features are spiral staircases and a rare original wooden portcullis.

You can see inside Bodiam at the video below:

View this post on Instagram

Book your ticket through the National Trust here.

3. Hever Castle, Kent

Anne Boleyn’s childhood home is the perfect picture-book castle on a miniature scale, its stout creeper-covered walls encircled by a double moat.

Among its collections are two private prayer books signed by Anne herself. For a real behind-the-scenes experience, make the most of your time here by booking onto a private guided tour before the castle opens for the day, and don’t forget to lose yourself (literally) in the Yew Maze and to wander the Tudor Garden and Chess Garden, where golden yew trees are carved into the shape of chess pieces. There’s so much to see here, so why not stay the night?

Book one of the 28 luxurious bedrooms across two Edwardian wings, and you can stroll the beautiful castle grounds free of the daytime crowds.

Book your tour of Hever Castle via Get Your Guide here.

4. Tintagel Castle, Cornwall

Few places can rival the romance and mystery of Tintagel, on Cornwall’s wild north coast. Perched high on a craggy, sea-lashed headland and accessed via a dramatic footbridge, the ruined castle here swirls with the legends of King Arthur.

If you believe the legend, it was here that Arthur was magically conceived, with the help of Merlin, who lived in the cave below. It’s true that in the period that Arthur is said to have reigned (5th and 6th centuries), Tintagel was a prosperous site, but from the 7th century onwards, little is heard about it until Geoffrey of Monmouth’s romantic depiction of the Arthurian legend in the 12th century.

The ruins that you see today date from the 13th century, but that didn’t stop it from becoming a popular pilgrimage site on the King Arthur trail, and it’s not hard to imagine the life of the heroic king beginning here, in this mystical and elemental place.

Book your ticket via English Heritage.

5. Corfe Castle, Dorset

Looming mysteriously over the Purbeck countryside, if the ruins of Corfe Castle could talk, they would have some interesting – if bloody – stories to tell. Throughout its 1,000-year history, the castle has not only seen its fair share of battles (some say it was on this site that Edward the Martyr met his grisly death), but it has also performed many functions: as a Saxon stronghold, a Norman fortress, a prison, a palace and a family home.

It was under this last guise that its Royalist owner, Lady Mary Bankes, bravely fought off not one but two sieges by Parliamentarians during the English Civil War. When she was betrayed in 1646 by an officer from her own ranks who allowed enemy troops to enter, the castle’s downfall was sealed. Parliamentarians used gunpowder to reduce the castle to its ruinous state, but Lady Bankes got the last laugh when the estate was returned to her following the restoration of the monarchy in 1660.

Book your ticket via the National Trust.

6. Cawdor Castle, Nairn

Cawdor Castle will forever be linked to Shakespeare’s Macbeth (in which Macbeth was made Thane of Cawdor), though the events on which the tragedy is based actually took place before the castle was built. Nevertheless, the association has stuck, and it certainly adds to the sense of intrigue when you visit this ancient fortress. Another legend that is woven into the story of Cawdor is that of its origins.

Apparently, the Thane of Cawdor, who wanted to build a stronger castle, followed instructions he received in a dream that told him to load a coffer of gold onto the back of a donkey; wherever the donkey came to rest should be the site of the castle. And so the medieval tower was built around a holly tree that is still there today.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

7. Lincoln Castle, Lincolnshire

Built by William the Conqueror in 1068, this Norman castle has been a symbol of power and punishment through the ages, and is home to one of only four surviving copies of Magna Carta. You can view this precious artefact in the vault before exploring the castle’s many fascinating corners. Look round the Victorian prison, walk the medieval walls and visit the Archaeology Exhibition, which displays finds from an ancient church, unearthed 3m below ground in 2013.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

8. Lancaster Castle, Lancashire

Owned by the King through the Duchy of Lancaster, Lancaster Castle stands on a hilltop where the Romans once built a series of garrison forts. The Castle was a prison until 2011, though by then its treatment of criminals was a lot less cut-throat than at previous points in its history.

In 1612, the Lancashire Witch Trials saw 10 people hanged on the moor above the town after being found guilty of witchcraft following a dubious trial based on rumour and gossip, while between 1584 and 1646, 15 Catholics known as the Lancaster Martyrs suffered a similar fate.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

9. Raby Castle, Durham

Raby is a picture-book castle, all crenellated battlements and forbidding stone walls, whose towers tantalisingly appear and disappear through the trees as you approach. Built in the 14th century, it was home to the powerful Nevill family until 1569, when it was confiscated by the Crown due to Charles Nevill’s involvement in a plot to depose Elizabeth I.

The estate then passed to Sir Henry Vane, a confidant of Charles I, whose family still live in the castle. You can explore Raby’s wonderful interiors, from the Blue Bedroom, reserved for the castle’s most important guests, to the medieval kitchen, lined with copper pots.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

10. Caernarfon Castle, Gwynedd

This huge fortress, which forms part of King Edward I’s ‘Iron Ring of Castles’, and which together with Beaumaris, Harlech and Conwy is classified as a World Heritage Site, was built by the English king to tighten his grip on power and keep Welsh dissidents at bay. Never one to miss an opportunity to exert his authority, Edward even ensured his first son was born here, thus making him the first English Prince of Wales.

Architecturally more impressive than some of his other castles, its structure includes polygonal towers, such as the Eagle Tower, and, unlike some of the other castles that were created primarily to defend, this castle was built to demonstrate Edward’s dominance over the Welsh and is now one of the best castles in the UK.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor here.

11. Leeds Castle, Kent

This majestic castle rising from the still waters of its moat has famously been described as ‘the loveliest castle in England’. Even if there’s a castle that pips it to the post in your mind, there’s no denying the sheer romanticism of its picture-perfect setting make it one of the best castles in the UK.

A favoured retreat of King Henry VIII and his first wife, Catherine of Aragon – they even stopped here on their way to northern France for the ceremonial meeting with Francis I of France that became known as the Field of the Cloth of Gold – it was under Henry’s orders that it was transformed from a Norman stronghold into the Tudor palace that we see today. 

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

12. Dunnottar Castle, Aberdeenshire

There can be few castle settings as wild and timeless as that of Dunnottar, which sits upon rock that was pushed to the surface 440 million years ago.

Throughout its colourful history the site has been a place of worship, suffered Viking attack, was set alight by William Wallace and played an integral role in protecting the Honours of Scotland from Oliver Cromwell’s army.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

13. Dover Castle, Kent

Thanks to its strategic location guarding the southeast coast, Dover had a pivotal role in the Second World War, in which it became a beacon of hope and defiance. It’s hard to think of it without hearing the dulcet tones of Vera Lynn uttering the immortal lines: “There’ll be bluebirds over the White Cliffs of Dover, tomorrow, just you wait and see.” Secret tunnels that were carved out of the rock face to ward off Nazi invasion are now open to the public. But its military prowess goes back much further.

Evidence suggests there was an Iron Age hillfort on the site, and a Roman lighthouse still stands proud, but it was Henry II who built the stone castle you see today, turning a military garrison into a medieval showpiece where he could host noble visitors to England.

Book your ticket via English Heritage.

14. Edinburgh Castle, Edinburgh

Scotland’s most recognisable landmark, Edinburgh Castle, stands proud above the vibrant capital city. As though its location atop an extinct volcano was not reason enough to visit, its history is pretty astounding too: it was here that Mary, Queen of Scots, gave birth to a baby boy in 1566, who was crowned King James VI of Scotland just one year later, and King James I of England in 1603, creating a union between the two countries.

The castle is also home to Scotland’s ‘Crown Jewels’ – the Honours of Scotland – and the Stone of Destiny, which has been used in Scottish coronations for centuries.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

15. Arundel Castle, West Sussex

Arundel is so perfect it can seem almost unreal. Even its name somehow brings to mind an ancient, mythical stronghold – another Camelot. Undoubtedly one of the best castles in the UK, it was founded in 1067 by Roger de Montgomery, the first Earl of Arundel, one of William the Conqueror’s closest advisors. It is one of England’s longest-inhabited country houses, the seat and still the home of the Duke of Norfolk, England’s premier duke.

The castle was badly damaged in the Civil War, and most of what you see today is the result of an extensive restoration that was completed in 1900. The oldest part of the castle is the Norman keep, whose ancient tower is reached by 131 steps – worth the effort for the breathtaking view of the river, town, South Downs and sea.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

16. Dunluce Castle, County Antrim

If you lost yourself as a child in the magical tales of C S Lewis and his imaginary land of Narnia, then a visit here, which some say was the inspiration for Cair Paravel – the seat of power for the kings and queens of Narnia – is a must. C S Lewis was born in Belfast and spent his early years in Northern Ireland, with regular holidays along the Antrim coast, and he would have known Dunluce Castle, which perches on a small hill atop some sea-lashed jagged rocks, from trips to nearby Portrush and Ballycastle.

C S Lewis wasn’t the only person to recognise the potential of this most dramatic of settings – it was also chosen as the location of the House of Greyjoy in the kingdom of Westeros in TV’s Game of Thrones.

Book your ticket via Tripadvisor.

16. Belvoir Castle, Leicestershire

The fairytale Belvoir Castle occupies a promontory overlooking the beautiful Vale of Belvoir in the north of Leicestershire. The castle has been the ancestral home of the Dukes of Rutland since the land was first gifted to Norman baron Robert de Todeni by William the Conqueror.

And the current castle, designed by the prolific Regency neo-Gothic architect James Wyatt with a dramatic skyline of towers, turrets and crenellations, is the fourth to stand on the same spot since 1067. Its magnificent grounds include the tranquil and elegant Duchess Garden, the Japanese woodland, the seven-acre Hermit Garden with its recently rediscovered Regency grottos, and the restored Capability Brown parkland.

Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

The Duchess of Rutland, who lives at Belvoir Castle, has written a brilliant book on her family's home and all the treasured paintings and furniture it houses. Belvoir Castle: A Thousand Years of Family Art and Architecture is available on Amazon and is a fascinating insight into one of the last great houses of Britain, written from the perspective of the woman who runs it.

item.title: This 250-year-old letter shows what the British really thought of the American Revolution, and it’s about to go on display in London for the very first time
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: This July marks 250 years since American Independence, and the National Maritime Museum are marking the event with a rare opportunity to see very first British reports of America’s history-changing revolution
item.content:encoded:
This July will mark 250 years since the adoption of the Declaration of Independence, and London's National Maritime Museum will unveil a special display showcasing one of the earliest reports of British American independence believed to be in existence.

Opening on 15 June, the display will be a highlight of what's on in London this summer, as visitors will have the opportunity to view this remarkable historic document up close and better understand the impact of transatlantic communication during the American Revolution.

The report was sent to Britain only days after the Declaration was first printed in Philadelphia, and it offers a rare British perspective of the revolution in America. Kyle Hopkins' brilliant, The Other Side of Revolution: British Perspective on the American War of Independence, is available on Amazon and delves deeper into this alternative look at the American Revolution - history from the side of the losers.

The focal point for the display at the museum is a letter dated 10 July 1776 and a handwritten copy of the Declaration made between 5 and 10 July 1776. The documents were sent to John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich, First Lord of the Admiralty, by Vice‑Admiral Molyneux Shuldham, Commander‑in‑Chief of the North American Station.

The documents are believed to be some of the first written accounts of American independence to reach Britain, and their rarity comes from the fact that they show how the history-changing news of the Declaration travelled across the Atlantic.

The news shaped global politics and changed the history of the world forever. Being able to see these first reports up close and in person is an incredible opportunity to explore how information was transmitted in the 18th century, and how such important news was interpreted and acted upon.

The letter dated 10 July 1776, shows that the Vice-Admiral Molyneux Shuldham transcribed a copy of the ‘late Declaration of the Continental Congress’, which is now known as the Dunlap Broadside, shortly after it was sent to him while commanding the British naval blockade of New York.

Shuldman describes the Declaration as 'perhaps the first authentick [sic] confirmation of what has been so long suspected. The establishment of Independence in America’. The first Dunlap Broadside was known to have reached Britain in August 1776, so therefore Shuldman'd transcription, which he sent to the Earl of Sandwich in mid-July may be one of the earliest reports of American independence to reach Britain.

Martin Salmon, Curator of Manuscripts at the National Maritime Museum, said: ‘It is just possible this was the very first text of the Declaration of American Independence to reach British shores. Yet it’s the accompanying letter that’s in some ways even more interesting, because it gives insight into the timing and circumstances in which the document was created. Molyneux Shuldham was relieved of command; his replacement did not arrive until 12 July 1776. It is likely that among the very last acts of his command was to ensure the intelligence of American independence being fact, was communicated home.’

These special documents will be on display in the National Maritime Museum's Caird Library from 15 June 2026, and entry to the exhibition is free.

Britain's role in the American Revolution is often associated with our king at the time, King George III, who is widely known for his bouts of 'madness', which were often blamed for huge political losses, such as the American colonies. But was he really to blame, and what did his health have to do with it? Andrew Roberts' The Last King of America: The Misunderstood Reign of George III is available on Amazon and will completely change the way you view the 'mad king' and his tarnished reputation.

item.title: From Roman soldier to dragon-slaying knight, here’s why St George is still shrouded in mystery, and 9 things about our patron saint you probably didn’t know
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: How did a man born in Cappadocia in AD 270 become the patron saint of England? Here are 9 facts about St George you probably didn’t know
item.content:encoded:
Each year on 23 April, England celebrates the dragon-slaying St George, who was actually born in the Turkish region of Cappadocia around 270 AD - so how did he become England's patron saint, and did he actually slay a dragon?

Just like all of Britain's patron saints, St George's life is shrouded in mystery, and not many people know why or how he came to be the patron saint of a country that he never even set foot in.

St George was one of the most popular medieval saints and a martyr for his cause, dying for his Christian faith in the fourth century. His image has been reinvented repeatedly over the last 1700 years, and Samantha Riches book, St George: A Saint for All, which is available on Amazon, explores his cult-like following all over the world.

St George’s Day takes place on 23 April, which is traditionally accepted as being the date of his death in AD 303, after he was beheaded for resigning his military post and protesting against his pagan leader, the Emperor Diocletian (245-313 AD), who led Rome’s persecution of Christians. The Emperor’s wife was so inspired by St George’s bravery and loyalty to his religion that she too became a Christian and was subsequently executed for her faith.

The rest of St George's life remains somewhat of a mystery, and the real question is, how did we get from a soldier in the Roman army to the well-known image of St George as a heroic knight slaying a ferocious dragon?

Here we debunk the myths of St George and uncover the facts we really do know about his life.

Here are 8 facts about St George you probably didn't know:

1. St George wasn't actually English

Born in Cappadocia in 270 AD, when it was part of the Roman Empire he was likely a Greek speaker. His tomb is at the city of Diospolis or Lydda (now Lod in Israel) in Roman Palestine, which attracted pilgrims after his death.

2. He wasn't England's first choice for patron saint

Before the cult of St George was brought back from the Crusades, the top choice for England’s patron saint was Edmund the Martyr, one of the Wuffings, East Anglia’s ruling family. Edmund is also the patron saint of pandemics, torture victims, and wolves.

3. Richard the Lionheart first introduced the St George's flag

St George’s emblem, a red cross on a white background, was adopted by Richard the Lionheart and brought to England in the 12th century, when the king’s soldiers would wear it on their tunics to avoid confusion in battle.

4. St George didn't actually slay a dragon

The most famous legend of St George is of him slaying a dragon, with the dragon commonly used to represent the Devil in the Middle Ages. The slaying of the dragon by St George was first credited to him in the 12th century, long after his death, and it is therefore likely that the many stories connected with St George’s name from this time are fictitious.

6. St George is also the patron saint of Scouting

St George is the Patron Saint of Scouting, and on the Sunday nearest to 23 April, scouts and guides throughout England parade through the streets.

7. England is not the only country that celebrates St George as its patron saint

Aside from England, other countries that celebrate St George’s Day include Canada, Croatia, Portugal, Cyprus, Greece, Georgia, Serbia, Bulgaria, Romania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and the Republic of Macedonia.

8. You can celebrate St George's Day by eating fish and chips or Morrs Dancing

Though celebrations are somewhat muted in modern times, Englishmen can still be seen to mark St George’s Day with quintessentially British traditions, such as Morris Dancing, eating fish and chips or watching a Punch and Judy puppet show.

9. St George shares a death day with William Shakespeare

The date of 23 April was also the date of the death of the English playwright William Shakespeare. UNESCO marked this historic date by declaring it the International Day of the Book, and it is also traditionally when Shakespeare’s birthday is celebrated.

If you'd like to read more about our patron saint and how England has celebrated St George through history, I'd recommend Hanael Bianchi's St. George's Day: A Cultural History of England's National Day, which is available on Amazon and reveals how a religious feast commemoraring a martyr became the national day of England.

item.title: Most people will never step inside this garden: here’s how to get one of just 50 tickets for a sneak peek behind the garden doors at 10 Downing Street this summer
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Less than 50 people will get the chance to join the exclusive tours of the Prime Minister’s garden this summer. Here’s how to get your hands on one of the hottest tickets of London’s summer events calendar
item.content:encoded:
Have you ever wondered how the Prime Minister relaxes in the summer months? In the 10 Downing Street Garden, of course, and this June, a lucky few visitors will be given their own private tour of this historic garden.

As part of the London Open Gardens festival in June, a real highlight of what's on in London this summer, 10 Downing Street will open up its gardens for a few guests to enjoy private tours, and the ballot to apply for tickets is open now.

The official residence of the Prime Minister in London is, of course, the second most famous address in the city (aside from Buckingham Palace) and has an illustrious history, but is usually firmly locked and bolted to the public unless invited by the PM themself. Jack Brown's No. 10: The Geography of Power at Downing Street is available on Amazon and is a brilliant history of the house behind one of the world's most iconic doors.

So how can you get your hands on one of these exclusive tickets?

Two extremely exclusive tours of the gardens will take place on Saturday, 6 June 2026, but there will only be 24 spaces available on each tour. Keen pryers will have to enter the ballot to be allocated a spot, which is open now and will close on Tuesday, 5 May 2026.

You can enter the ballot via the London Gardens Trust, which also has all the terms and conditions for entering.

Though it does not guarantee you a slot on the 10 Downing Street garden tours, you can also purchase a London Open Gardens ticket via Eventbrite, which will grant you access to over 120 usually hidden green spaces that will be open over the festival weekend. This is a very rare opportunity to see some of London's finest, and usually totally private gardens.

The 10 Downing Street garden was first constructed back in 1736, and its highlight is its rose beds, which were commissioned by the late Baroness Thatcher. It is also home to a bronze sculpture by Barbara Hepworth.

The lucky winners of the tour will also be able to quiz the 10 Downing Street gardens on its wildlife and sustainability, and walk the green lawn that has been an unseen part of British history for the past 300 years.

Before you embark on the weekend of perusing London's gardens in June, I recommend perusing Great Gardens of London: 30 Masterpieces from Private Plots to Palaces, which is available on Amazon, and which unearths the hidden secrets of London's most beautiful gardens.

item.title: After nearly 400 years, an unsolved mystery surrounding Shakespeare’s London home that was destroyed in the Great Fire of London has been resolved, and historians everywhere are in a frenzy
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: A new discovery by a Shakespeare expert at King’s College London has revealed the exact location of Shakespeare’s London home, and shed some light as to where he might have spent the last years of his life
item.content:encoded:
A mystery surrounding the exact location of Shakespeare's London house has finally been resolved as a previously unknown floorpan has been discovered by an expert on the Bard.

Although a lot of the timeline of Shakespeare's life remains a relative mystery, historians have known that the Bard bought a house somewhere where Blackfriars, in around 1602, but we can now pinpoint its exact location.

The discovery has, of course, sent historians into an excited frenzy, and it's also got me thinking about what London would have looked like in Shakespeare's day. Stephen Porter's Shakespeare's London: Everyday Life in London 1580-1616 is available on Amazon and is a brilliant book on the subject of life in the Elizabethan capital city.

So what did they actually discover about Shakespeare's house in London?

The discovery of the floor plan was made by Shakespeare expert Professor Lucy Munro from King's College London. The floor plan identifies the exact spot of the property we know the Bard bought in 1613, as well as its layout and size. The discovery also reveals some previously unknown truths about Shakespeare's later life in London.

There is a City of London blue plaque on the building at 5 St Andrew's Hill that states 'ON 10th MARCH 1613 WILLIAM SHAKESPEARE PURCHASED LODGINGS IN THE BLACKFRIARS GATEHOUSE LOCATED NEAR THIS SITE.'

The mystery of exactly where this property was has puzzled Shakespearean historians since the 18th century. It was believed that the property he bought was part of what was known as 'the Great Gate' over the entrance to the Blackfriars precinct, which had been a major 13th-century Dominican friary, but they were never totally sure exactly where it was.

Professor Munro uncovered two documents, one from the London Archives and one from the National Archives, which shed light on this unsolved Shakespearean mystery.

The document from The London Archives was drawn up in 1668 as a plan of part of the Blackfriars precinct after the Great Fire of London and confirms the precise location of Shakespeare's London house.

Professor Munro was able to decipher that the property measured 45 feet from east to west, 15 feet from north to south at the eastern end and 13 feet at the western end.

Historians have long claimed that Shakespeare retired to his hometown of Stratford-upon-Avon not long after he purchased this property in 1613, Munro's discoveries suggest he might have spent more time in London in his later years than previously thought.

The other document tells us that Shakespeare's granddaughter, Elizabeth Hall Nash Barnard, sold the property in 1665, and it was destroyed in the Great Fire of London just a year later.

Munro's findings confirm that the City of London's blue plaque, which states it is 'near the site', is actually bang on the spot.

Find out more about Professor Munro's discovery in the video below:

There are, of course, hundreds of London spots that claim strong connections to the Bard, not least the Globe Theatre, where his plays are still performed today, come rain or shine. Several brilliant tour companies run walking tours of Shakespeare's London. 'The Shakespeare City Walk' revisits Shakespeare's life on the North bank of the Thames and is available to book on TripAdvisor, which I'd highly recommend for all fans of the Bard visiting the capital.

item.title: While much of London competes for attention, Belgravia remains effortlessly composed and consistently desirable; your guide to this polished yet peaceful pocket of the capital
item.dc:creator: The London Magazine Editors
item.description: A ‘modern-day Bridgerton’, Belgravia offers historic homes, peaceful corners and an enviable stretch of village shops along which to promenade
item.content:encoded:
Well-connected, elegant and genteel, Belgravia is one of central London’s most famous villages. Crisp white Georgian squares and charming cobbled mews are threaded together by immaculate streets of boutiques, restaurants and pubs.

Often associated with prestige and exclusivity, this corner of Belgravia can be overlooked by those seeking a more village-like feel. Yet its blend of refined living and easy access to Chelsea’s boutiques and restaurants gives it a unique charm, balancing sophistication with a welcoming neighbourhood atmosphere.

Here's everything you need to know about the area.

“Belgravia Village exhibits that rare balance of timeless elegance and family liveability. The grand architecture and boutiques are set among beautiful garden squares, notably Belgrave Square, Eaton Square, and Chester Square,” says Shereen Akhtar, senior associate director, Strutt & Parker Knightsbridge and Belgravia. “The neighbourhood is a modern-day Bridgerton – and home to a thriving community largely driven by its enviable position close to central London and wealth of excellent schools.”

Home to some of London’s golden addresses, buyers are drawn to the strong residential element, security, and privacy on offer. “Its tree-lined streets and white stucco architecture create a smart, cohesive setting, and on bright days, when sunlight catches the facades, the area is especially striking,” says William Duckworth-Chad, a director within Savills Private Office notes. “Much of Belgravia’s enduring appeal comes from Grosvenor’s long-term investment,” he adds. “Elizabeth Street is an example: carefully curated with boutiques, quality retail, and cafes and restaurants that give the street a distinctive character.”

Akhtar notes that homes change hands “infrequently and often discretely”. “The market offers considerable value in light of some of the political and economic obstacles it has faced in recent years.”

Thomas Sutton, head of lettings in Belgravia at John D Wood & Co. has noticed strong demand in the past month. “A big part of Belgravia’s appeal for tenants is that it offers a more understated alternative to neighbouring locations. Much of it is only a five-minute walk from Sloane Square, but feels noticeably calmer. Streets like Eaton Place and Ebury Street have a village-like atmosphere, which is attractive to tenants seeking convenience and tranquillity. Belgravia can also represent better value than neighbouring ultra-prime areas such as Mayfair, St James’s and parts of Chelsea, while still delivering the same sense of prestige, connectivity and quality of life.

A day out

9AM

Blossoming breakfast

There’s no better way to greet a summer morning in Belgravia than with a gentle stroll around Belgrave Square or Eaton Square Gardens. From the squares, head to Elizabeth Street, Belgravia’s (belgraviavillage.com) charming ‘high street’, where cafes like Tomtom Coffee House, or Poilâne offer artisan bites and unhurried, pavement-style dining.

11AM

Happily Belgravia after

Transforming streets and squares, Belgravia in Bloom (18-24 May) returns, delighting residents and passersby. This year’s theme, Fairytales of Belgravia, draws from the nostalgia and imagination of beloved stories. Starting in Eccleston Yards, wander the story-book trail to Elizabeth Street, with stores like Daylesford Organic and LINLEY participating in the festivities.

1PM

Garden to table

In the enclave of Eccleston Yards, find alfresco dining at its most relaxed. Choose from vibrant plates at Wild by Tart , The Jones Family Kitchen (jonesfamilykitchen.co.uk) or at Morena (morenalondon.com). Nearby on Eccleston Place, Michelin-star Cornus (cornusrestaurant.co.uk) is sister restaurant to Medlar in Chelsea. If you’re looking for a creative lunchtime class, the Studio Pottery London (studio-pottery-london.com) at Eccleston Place offers hands-on classes.

3PM

Design trail

Also blossoming this month, London Craft Week celebrates the best of creative style between 11-17 May. Pimlico Road is renowned for being the home of British design, with showrooms including Jamb, Robert Kime and Soane Britain, and the recently opened JW Anderson. Find inspiring talks as part of the Pimlico Series, such as Celebrating Three Generations of British Craftsmanship with the Odd Chair Company (12 May) and Go Behind the Scenes at Cox London Atelier (12 and 14 May).

5PM

Summer tea

Wind down with a quintessential British afternoon tea. Settle at The Halkin or the perennially floral-festooned Peggy Porschen Cakes, or the Anya Cafe, next door to Anya Hindmarch.

Ask a local: Joanna Wood, Interior Designer

“I never tire of exploring Belgravia, and if I had one perfect day here, I’d be spoilt for choice. I’d start with a pain au chocolat at Poilâne, then go to Issy’s for a blow-dry and manicure — such a treat. I’d pop into my shop on Elizabeth Street to check arrivals from my latest sourcing trip, like our Parisian handmade lampshades. Then it would be a light, delicious lunch at The Thomas Cubitt, ideally outside in the sun...it's a great place for people-watching. The afternoon might mean browsing Penelope Chilvers’ latest collection, sourcing samples from Chelsea Textiles or popping into the Soane showroom on the Pimlico Road. Finally, a quick stop at Daylesford next door for some supper supplies and a bottle or two of its rosé – only if we’re hosting, of course!”

Food and drink

Follow the garden trail in and around Belgravia to discover local gems that will become regular local favourites

The Thomas Cubitt

44 Elizabeth Street

A quintessential Belgravia gastropub, housed in a handsome Regency townhouse with a warm, wood-panelled interior and a lively yet refined atmosphere. Named after the 19th century architect who shaped much of the neighbourhood, it serves elevated British classics and perfectly executed pub favourites like fish and chips and shepherd’s pie – paired with excellent drink choices. cubitthouse.co.uk

Hunan

51 Pimlico Road

Delivering one of London’s most distinctive dining experiences, this is a family-run Taiwanese-Chinese gem where the no-menu, chef-led approach means dishes arrive in waves, tailored to your preferences. Expect bold, spicy flavours in signature dishes like sizzling beef with chilli, steamed fish, and fragrant stir-fries, all served family-style in an intimate setting.

hunanlondon.com

The Buttery

135-137 Ebury Street

Within The Lime Tree Hotel, The Buttery provides a charming, all-day rustic retreat with a focus on seasonal British comfort food in a cosy, garden-view space. Enjoy hearty breakfasts, light lunches and evening plates of grilled meats, fresh fish, and delicious homemade desserts.

limetreehotel.co.uk/the-buttery

Wild by Tart

Eccleston Yards

Eccleston Yards offers a fresh, contemporary take on sharing plates in a bright, airy space that blends industrial chic with natural elements. Run by the team behind Tart London, the menu highlights seasonal, sustainably sourced ingredients – grilled meats, vibrant salads, inventive vegetable dishes, and wood-fired flatbreads – cooked over coals for smoky depth.

wildbytart.com

Morena

10-11 Eccleston Yards

Morena brings a vibrant dash of Latin-American sunshine to the area. Founded by Colombian sisters, Juliana and Valentina Beleno, the all-day establishment goes from relaxed mornings, with dishes such as zesty breakfast burritos to lively evenings of Latin-inspired dinners and craft cocktails. The restaurant blends Bogotá with Belgravia.

morenalondon.com

Schools

The western edge of Belgravia, towards Chelsea and Sloane Square, boasts a selection of prestigious schools, spanning nurseries through prep with strong feeders to top senior institutions. Standouts for the early years include Eaton Square Prep on Eccleston Square. The school offers play-based foundations that flow seamlessly into its reception and prep up to year 6, while Eaton House Belgravia provides a co-educational nursery (ages 2-4) transitioning to a boys-only pre-prep and prep to age 8, renowned for excellent 7+ and 8+ results.

Nearby in Chelsea’s Turk Row, Garden House School delivers a vibrant, family-run experience from 3-11, with separate boys' and girls' academic sections, and some shared activities. Boys-only prep (ages 8-13) Sussex House on Cadogan Square creatively leans towards art and music. These preps enable pupils to go onto esteemed seniors, such as Westminster and Francis Holland Sloane Square.

On the market

Chester Square

This charming Grade II-listed six-bedroom townhouse is generously arranged across six storeys overlooking one of the capital's finest garden squares. Boasting handsome period features, a double-aspect drawing room and a second kitchen on the lower-ground floor, it's well-appointed for entertaining as well as family life. Residents can apply for communal garden access. £6.8m Agent: savills.com

Eaton Place

Moments from the buzz of Elizabeth Street, this grand, light-filled two-bedroom duplex apartment combines beautiful proportions, classic architecture and modern comforts. £2.65m Agent: struttandparker.com

Eaton Mews

Offering around 2,500sq ft of lateral space in one of the area's most coveted pockets, this well-renovated five-bedroom mews house has a wine cellar and roof terrace. £6m Agent: johndwood.co.uk

Ask an agent: James Waight, head of sales John D. Wood

“Belgravia has long been one of London’s most distinguished neighbourhoods, but in recent years it has undergone a remarkable renaissance. While it has always been synonymous with grand white stucco townhouses, garden squares, security and diplomatic prestige, today there's a renewed buzz to the area that has given it a more contemporary appeal. What’s so striking is just how lively Belgravia has become.

Local hotspots like The Thomas Cubitt are now packed on a Monday night, which says a lot about the energy in the area. Elizabeth Street – the beating heart of Belgravia – is brimming with life and a genuine neighbourhood atmosphere. The arrival of The Peninsula London on Grosvenor Place has been a game changer, bringing one of the world’s most prestigious hospitality brands to the enclave, while the opening of Cleveland Clinic London reflects the scale and quality of investment shaping Belgravia.” johndwood.co.uk

item.title: Every fan of the Bard should know this timeline of William Shakespeare’s life off by heart, from his early life as a glove-maker in Stratford-upon-Avon, to becoming the greatest writer in history
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Despite being the famous writer in the world, we know relatively little about William Shakepeare’s life. Here is a timeline of what we do know, and all the key dates every Shakespeare fan should be able to recite to their friends
item.content:encoded:
Although he is considered the greatest writer in English literature, surprisingly little is known about William Shakespeare's life.

Born 400 years ago, Shakespeare's life as a glove-maker in Stratford-upon-Avon and then a player in London was relatively undocumented, which wasn't unusual for common people in Elizabethan England.

Historians have tried for centuries to piece together William Shakespeare's life, and even now, discoveries are being made about the Bard that even experts didn't know before. For the most thorough biography of his life, I recommend Peter Ackroyd's Shakespeare: The Biography, which is available on Amazon and is an intimate and brilliant depiction of the writer's life and time.

In terms of key dates every Shakespeare fan should know, below is our timeline of William Shakespeare's life, from his birth in 1564, to his death in 1616.

A brief timeline of William Shakepeare's life:

1564 - William Shakespeare is born to parents John Shakespeare and Mary Arden in Stratford-upon-Avon, Warwickshire.

Shakespeare's Birthplace is now open as a museum in Stratford. Book tickets via TripAdvisor.

1571 - 1578 - Shakespeare studied at King's New School in Stratford, where he learnt Latin and literature.

1582 - William Shakespeare marries Anne Hathaway, the daughter of a local farmer, who is eight years his senior.

Anne Hathaway's cottage, where she grew up and where the couple supposedly courted, is also open to visitors. Book your ticket via TripAdvisor.

1583 - William and Anne welcome their first child, Susanna, only six months after their wedding.

1585 - William and Anne welcome twins, Hamnet and Judith, who were baptised at Holy Trinity Church in Stratford-upon-Avon, 1585. Hamnet was the subject of a novel by Maggie O'Farrell, released as an Oscar-winning film in 2026, and with a tragic true story behind it

1593 - Shakespeare’s first poem is published in London – Venus and Adonis.

1594 - Records of Shakespeare’s first plays begin to appear. His first printed play was Titus Andronicus. He joins other playwrights to form a new theatre company under the patronage of Lord Chamberlain. They are known as Lord Chamberlain’s Men.

1596 - Shakespeare's son Hamnet, dies of the plague aged 11.

1597 - Shakespeare buys a second home called New Place in Stratford. He is starting to become wealthy from his work and New Place is the second biggest house in Stratford.

1599 - The Globe Theatre opens in London

1601 - Shakespeare's father, John Shakespeare dies. William writes Hamlet.

1602 - Shakespeare buys a place in London’s Silver Street – the location in which the Barbican Centre now stands. It was here where he wrote many of his great plays such as Hamlet and Macbeth.

1603 - King James I comes to the English throne and grants royal patronage to Shakespeare’s acting company, which then came to be known as the King’s Men.

1609 - The King’s Men acquire an indoor theatre – the Blackfriars.

1609 - Shakespeare published 154 sonnets about love and time.

1610 - Shakespeare returns to Stratford to live out the rest of his life peacefully.

1616 - Shakespeare spends the last few years of his life at New Place before dying on 23 April 1616, aged 52.

1623 - William Shakespeare's friends publish his First Folio, a collection of all of his published plays, which preserved them for future generations and led to his worldwide success.

The First Folio was published under the title Mr. William Shakespeares comedies, histories & tragedies. Published according to the True Originall Copies. In 2020, a First Folio fetched a record $9,978,000 at auction at Christie’s in New York.

Of course, I don't recommend you try to buy your own First Folio at that price, but you can get yourself The Complete Works of William Shakespeare, which is available on Amazon, and features all of his plays, poems and sonnets in beautifully bound pages. Shakespeare's Globe is of course still performing the Bard's plays year round, come rain or shine in their incredible reconstruction of the original Elizabethan theare. Get your tickets for this summer's A Midsummer Nights Dream on SeatPlan.

item.title: These bluebell woods are some of the most stunning in all of Britain, and they are all within an hour’s reach of London by train
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: There’s a magic to bluebell season. For a short time between late April and early May Britain’s ancient woodlands and parks are covered in a carpet...
item.content:encoded:
There’s a magic to bluebell season, and some of the best places to spot bluebells in Britain are just a quick train ride from London. For a short time between late April and May, Britain’s ancient woodlands, parks and beautiful spring gardens are covered in a carpet of the dainty nodding heads that caused Emily Bronte to rhapsodise: “The Bluebell is the sweetest flower/That waves in summer air:/Its blossoms have the mightiest power/To soothe my spirit’s care.”

Native to western Europe, bluebells tend to be found in deciduous woodlands, growing best in undisturbed soil with plenty of dappled light. If you'd like to know more about these dainty little flowers and where to find them, Collins Wild Flower Guide is available on Amazon and is the ultimate reference book for wild flower enthusiasts and searchers.

Here is our guide to the best places to spot bluebells in Britain, all within an hour's train journey from central London.

1. Ashridge Estate, Hertfordshire

This beautiful estate is home to an ancient woodland and is just a 45-minute train ride from central London (book your ticket to Berkhamsted via Trainline). National Trust-owned Ashridge is renowned as a premier spot for bluebell-spotting, and it is believed these pretty blue flowers have been blooming here every April for 400 years.

Dockey Wood is the most spectacular place to view bluebells at Ashridge, and there is a sign-posted 'bluebell walk' for visitors to follow through the woodland.

Find out more via the National Trust website.

2. Banstead Woods, Surrey

Just 54 minutes on the train from London (book your ticket via Trainline), not only do these beautiful woods have a beautiful carpet of bluebells at this time of year, but there is also a Narnia-themed trail for families to follow through the woods with incredible wooden carvings of characters from The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe. The walk is about 3 miles long and will take around 90 minutes.

The Banstead Woods bluebells are particularly spectacular in the old orchard, so make sure to stop there for photo ops.

3. Leith Hill, Surrey

The beautiful Leith Hill in Surrey is home to a short but stunning bluebell walk in Frank's Wood that flowers every April, with gorgeous views across to the South Downs.

Leith Hill is 4km from the nearest train station (Holmwood), just an hour train from London, and you can get a bus, taxi or walk over to Leith Hill from here.

You can find more information on the National Trust's website.

4. Hole Park, Kent

Kent's Hole Park claims to be home to the most spectacular display of bluebells in the South-East of England and it isn't hard to see why. The beautiful park is known for its famous 'Bluebell Barometer', and in spring the bluebell walk is deliciously complemented by breathtaking magnolias, wild garlic, wisteria and rhododendron.

The crown jewel is, of course, the carpet of bluebells, but be sure to visit the 'wild garlic valley' too and perhaps forage some of the delicious leaves for your supper.

The nearest train station to Hole Park is Headcorn, where you can get a bus or taxi to Hole Park.

Find out more about Hole Park via the Historic Houses website.

5. Sheffield Park and Garden, East Sussex
