Sheffield Park is easily reachable via the aptly named Bluebell Railway, a vintage steam railway that runs from East Grinstead (56 minutes from London on the train, book your ticket via Trainline) through the East Sussex countryside.

When you get to Sheffield Park from Sheffield Park station, you'll enjoy 120 acres of Capability Brown-designed landscape, which comes alive with bluebells in April and May. The pretty bluebells carpet the glades and woodlands, and there are even guided bluebell tours to book onto.

Find out more via the National Trust website.

6. Kew Gardens, Richmond

These bluebells are still within the realms of Greater London, but when you step off the tube at Richmond Station, it feels like you are miles away from the hustle of the capital. The woodland near Queen Charlotte's cottage within the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew is well-known for its bluebells, with the ancient woodland having been known to flower bluebells for at least the last 300 years, certainly since Queen Charlotte was on the throne.

Book your ticket for Kew on their website.

Did you know Queen Charlotte was a keen horticulturalist? When she lived in the cottage at Kew, she would have walked the gardens and spent time studying botany, and cataloguing the plants that grew at Kew. She also supported the collection of exotic creatures within the gardens at Kew, which included the first ever kangaroos to arrive in England. Catherine Curzon's The Real Queen Charlotte: Inside the Real Bridgerton Court is available on Amazon and is a brilliant insight into the fascinating life of Britain's longest-serving queen consort.

item.title: From dainty villages and magic to England’s smallest city, this is our ultimate guide to the best things to do in Somerset, the sunlit south-western county filled to the brim with sweet delights
item.dc:creator: Tom Henry, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Sandwiched between its well-known neighbours of Dorset, Devon and Wiltshire, Somerset boasts a varied coastline, a National Park and quaint villages, as well as curious traditions, famous festivals and cider-making heritage
item.content:encoded:
Did you know Somerset is the old English name of 'land of the summer people'? However, the best of Somerset can and should be enjoyed all year round, so don't let this put you off visiting the rest of the year.

If the word Somerset didn’t already exist, it would have to be invented, probably by a screenwriter trying to capture a certain image of rural South West England – rolling hills, lush pastureland, laconic locals, traditions unbroken for hundreds of years – and all shot in the lazy, hazy light of a glorious summer’s evening.

The old English name was for a county whose people celebrated the coming of the warmer days with festivals and gatherings, but who also hunkered down to hibernate when the first snows of winter fell across the Mendip Hills. Mike Dean's book, The Little History of Somerset is available on Amazon and is a friendly, fascinating guide to the colourful history of the county.

Somerset is as beautiful and varied in reality as it is in the imagination. Sunlight really does fall in dramatic shards across the Somerset Levels – an area of wetland between the Mendip and Quantock Hills – and locally produced cider is served in pubs and at festivals all across the county.

Neither is its beauty confined to the countryside; Somerset has miles of coastline that range from the boisterous attractions of seaside towns like Weston-super-Mare and Minehead to the silent expanse of Berrow Flats. Because of its tidal reach –the second highest in the world – many parts of Somerset’s coast have a high biodiversity. But what Somerset loses in terms of sandy beaches, it gains in sheer variation of wildlife.

Sandy beaches are one of the reasons why many visitors to the South West often head through Somerset, en route to the golden shores of Devon and Cornwall. Many don’t break the journey, but those who do are in for a real treat: an eye-opening, inspiring and sometimes eccentric experience.

Here is our expert's guide to the best places to visit in Somerset

1. Glastonbury Tor

Where better to start our tour of the ‘land of the summer people’ than the top of Glastonbury Tor? This distinctive hill, topped with the remains of an abbey, rises some 140m (450ft) above the Somerset Levels, and while not the highest point in Somerset, it is definitely the most enigmatic.

It is a place of deep myth and legend; even today, visitors are as likely to see pagans celebrating the turn of the seasons as they are Christian pilgrims trudging to the top. For all those who make the climb, the views are stunning.

Did you know Glastonbury is also known as the Isle of Avalon and the Heart Chakra of the World? You discover all the mystery, magic and oddities in Vicki Steward's Normal For Glastonbury: Life in England's Most Magical Town, which is available on Amazon.

To the north is Wells, the smallest city in England, and also Bath (once part of Somerset, but now a unitary authority – and very much worth a visit). To the west lie the Levels, Bridgwater and the coast. Far south are the towns of Yeovil and Ilminster, and to the east lies Cley Hill and the border with Wiltshire. Above the Tor, rooks caw and hover in the near-permanent wind. This is a highly atmospheric place and full of significance.

2. Wells

Wells and the surrounding Mendip area is one of Somerset’s picturesque high points. Wells has been a city since the 13th century, and there is a distinctly medieval feel about its winding streets and timbered shop fronts.

The jewel in its crown is Wells Cathedral. Built over a 300-year period, and despite time and the ravages of the English Civil War and the Monmouth Rebellion (of which more later), the cathedral is one of the world’s finest examples of pre-Reformation ecclesiastical architecture. About 300 of its original medieval statues remain, providing a glorious theatrical stone backdrop for the feast day processions.

The famous Wells Cathedral clock is considered to be the second oldest clock mechanism in Britain (if not the world) to survive in original condition and to still be in use.

3. Cheddar Gorge

Heading north-west over the Mendips to Wells takes you through the Cheddar Gorge, a deep gash through limestone riddled with caves and topped with steep rocky outcrops.

Two caves – Gough’s and Cox’s – are the most popular, and the village hosts a good number of tearooms, sweet shops, cider retailers, souvenir stores and, of course, cheese makers. Cheddar gives its name to the world’s most popular cheese, the recipe for which was created on nearby farms. Although Cheddar is made worldwide today, nothing tastes quite the same as the musty, cave-matured variety produced in the village of the same name.

Ernest H. Shepard's Cheddar Gorge: A Book of English Cheeses is available on Amazon and provides a brilliant history of the world's most popular cheese and its origins.

4. North Somerset coast

The North Somerset coast is clearly visible from Cheddar, and it’s a short drive to the towns of Clevedon and Weston-super-Mare. The latter is a typical British seaside spot and, after some years of neglect, is now reinventing itself for the 21st-century visitor.

Further down the coast is Burnham-on-Sea, and between the two runs a vast beach, perfect for power kiting and horse-riding if not always for bathing.

Close by, the peninsula of Brean Down juts almost two miles into the sea like a great green-backed whale. A Victorian fort at its far end was recommissioned during World War II to defend Bristol against German bombers using the Bristol Channel as a navigational tool.

5. Bridgwater

Inland, but once an important port before the river silted up, Bridgwater is one of Somerset’s most historic towns and has a curiously political and radical heritage.

Deeply nonconformist, Bridgwater was almost destroyed in the Civil War, and many of its men later went to fight for the Duke of Monmouth during his ill-fated rebellion against King James II in 1685. In fact, the Duke was proclaimed King on Bridgwater’s Cornhill, but after a nighttime attack on the nearby village of Westonzoyland, the Duke’s army was heavily defeated by the Royalists at the Battle of Sedgemoor, the last ever to be fought on English soil. John Tincey's Sedgemoor, 1685: Marlborough's First Victory is available on Amazon and is a brilliant account of this bloody battle.

A terrible revenge was exacted on those who had joined the rebellion, and many a village across the county had a ‘hanging tree’ at its crossroads. Bridgwater still defiantly celebrates its rebel roots by hosting a huge carnival parade every November to honour Guy Fawkes, the ‘gunpowder plotter’ who tried to blow up Parliament in 1605. This is a spectacular commemoration, featuring costumes, lights and fireworks, and is echoed by similar carnivals in other Somerset towns.

6. The Quantock Hills

The Quantock Hills to the west of Bridgwater were England’s first designated Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty and have been occupied since Roman times. The poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge was inspired by the hills and lived in Nether Stowey for two years, writing The Rime of the Ancient Mariner in a cottage now open to the public, Coleridge Cottage.

7. West Somerset Railway

One of the best ways to see this area is by taking a steam train along the West Somerset Railway from Bishops Lydeard, just outside Taunton, through the Quantocks and along the Exmoor coast to Minehead. The railway celebrates its 150th anniversary in 2012 and its 10 stations are great stopping-off points for walking trips across the Quantocks and Brendon Hills, as well as visits to places like Dunster. The latter is a beautiful medieval village with an ancient castle rebuilt in Victorian times in the height of Gothic taste. It is open to the public and is as much a monument to that 19th-century renovation as it is to its medieval origins.

8. The Exmoor National Park

The Exmoor National Park opens out to the west of Minehead, stretching from the western fringes of Somerset into north Devon. Once a royal hunting ground, Exmoor is still the home of large herds of red deer and wild ponies and, it is said, the occasional ‘big cat’. Sightings of the ‘beast of Exmoor’, plus sheep found killed by something large and fierce, are well documented, but as yet, no one has actually captured the creature. It is the stuff of myth on Exmoor, a place BBC broadcaster and filmmaker Simon King rates as one of his favourites in the UK.

“Because of who I am, I have to be satisfied by the natural world around me,” says Simon, who is now an ambassador for tourism in Somerset, “and Somerset has always represented a gentle, quiet yet exciting corner of the UK. From the Quantocks to the Levels to the coast, it is very varied. There are many wild corners to explore, and if you do, you will almost always find magic.

“There is an eclectic sense of community here which is very contemporary, diverse and welcoming. The area has connections with London, not least because of the large numbers of people who come to live here from London, which gives it a contemporary feel, but it also has its deep-rooted customs and traditions.”

Visit a cider farm

A number of the customs Simon mentions are connected with apples and apple trees. Cider making has been a feature of Somerset for thousands of years, and cider apples are still grown in well-kept orchards across the county. ‘Wassailing’, a ceremony that praises the spirit of the trees and asks for a good harvest in the forthcoming year, takes place in January and cider-sampling festivals are common all through the summer months.

Many farms used to produce the drink and hand-painted signs advertising ‘scrumpy’ or ‘rough’ can still be seen on rural roads. There is also a high number of microbreweries making award-winning ciders for export and domestic consumption. But beware – Somerset cider is deceptively strong, and two pints can render the new drinker incapable of coherent speech, much to the amusement of the locals! If you'd like to learn more, Jon Dathen's Somerset Cider Folklore and Customs is available on Amazon and will guide you through the county's history with the tipple.

item.title: Mad About History - Royal expert Professor Kate Williams shares the surprising side of Queen Elizabeth II, from doing the conga at the Ritz to owning her very own sloth
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Did you know Queen Elizabeth II was a proud owner of a sloth? As we celebrate the centenary of her birth in 1926, we spoke to historian Professor Kate Williams about her wonderful legacy, and her more surprising side
item.content:encoded:
It's a strange thought that, despite being our Queen for 70 years and a beloved princess before that, there are still many surprising facts we are discovering about Queen Elizabeth II.

The span of Queen Elizabeth II's life and reign was monumental, and she was the figurehead of our nation through a period of seismic change, socially, politically and culturally. And yes, there is a side to her behind the scenes that still manages to surprise us, even four years after her death at the age of 96.

The new biography by esteemed royal biographer Hugo Vickers, Queen Elizabeth II is available on Amazon, and is a must-read this month as we celebrate the centenary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth.

But did you know Queen Elizabeth II owned a sloth, or that she once did the conga at the Ritz?

We were lucky enough to speak to royal expert, historian, broadcaster and author Professor Kate Williams to mark this special anniversary, and she shared with us some of the most surprising and little-known facts about our late queen that will have even self-proclaimed royal experts saying 'really!?'.

Queen Elizabeth II: The monarch of the internet age

'There's an incredible transition of change during her majesty's reign. She saw a World War, and many other wars. She saw men on the moon. She received men on the moon in Buckingham Palace. And, of course, the internet - she even sent her first email in 1976.

'It's also, let's face it, in 1976, going to be one of the first ever emails? Not many of us were sending emails in 1976. I think the average person who was born at the same time as the Queen in 1926 probably would've sent their first email, perhaps in the nineties or the early two thousands.  So she was well ahead of it.

'She actually sent her first email. From a military base in Melbourne at a research centre in telecommunications, and it wasn't exactly an email in the way we know it today, but it later became the internet.

'There's  actually a picture of her tapping quite an old school computer,  and the email was headed " A message from Her Majesty the Queen" and signed off, Elizabeth R.

'Monarchs need to be able to use the technologies or images of their time in order to succeed. Elizabeth I used portraiture, Victoria used photography. Queen Elizabeth II was the monarch of television. She used her television addresses to create power during difficult times for the country, like the passing of Diana in 1997. But she wasn't just the monarch of TV; by the end of her reign (she even sent her own tweet in 2014), she was the monarch of the internet.'

Queen Elizabeth II was gifted sloths, jaguars, and two pieces of burnt toast

'The queen has been given many fantastic gifts in her reign.  I was just in Windsor Castle looking at some of the really beautiful arts and crafts, heritage, jewels and presents in the Royal Collection,  but she also had some things that she couldn't really display in Windsor Castle.

'She was given a sloth and two jaguars from the Prefect of Brasília during a state visit to Brazil in 1968.  We don't know the name of the sloth, but the Jaguars were called Marquez and Aita. The Queen has received exotic animals in the past, and they always go to London Zoo, where they can be cared for.

'She received quite a lot of horses during her reign, which she often kept for herself, but she was also given a cow by the  Royal Jersey Agricultural Society and a pig by the people of Fiji. They also gave her a crocodile.

' One of my favourite stories is when she got engaged, two young ladies were rooming together in London who heard the news and were so excited that the queen was getting engaged when they heard it on the wireless that they burnt their toast. Toast was very precious as this was during a period of rationing, so they sent their burnt toast to Queen Elizabeth II as an engagement present.'

The Queen did the conga at the Ritz on VE Day

As Britain celebrated victory over Germany at the end of the Second World War,  Princess Elizabeth and Princess Margaret said to their father King George VI that they wanted to go out and join the celebrations. The crowds were swamping the Mall and the park. There were crowds everywhere, and the king was such a protective father.

'I think he was so overwhelmed with joy and happiness, he agreed. They were accompanied by household staff and their cousins, so they weren't alone and off they went into the crowds, and they shouted, God, save the King!

' There was so much happiness, so much relief, all the dark days of suffering were over. There was drinking, there was, well, some of the sex workers were getting a lot of clients in Green Park, and there were two princesses skipping through it.

'And then they went to the Ritz, and they did the Conga! Margaret Rose, the queen's cousin and really her closest friend,  said that these older ladies were looking at them and saying, " What the earth are you all doing?" None of them recognised Elizabeth, they all must have thought they were just drunk young people in fun, when actually it was the future queen doing the conga, at the Ritz.'

 'She actually said many years later, "We cheered the King and Queen on the balcony and then walked miles through the street. I remember lines of unknown people linking arms and walking down Whitehall, all of us just swept along on a tide of happiness and relief.

'I think this was the last moment in her life she could be a child, conga-ing around, and where she could have this freedom, where nobody would recognise her, it's a very moving moment for our future Queen Elizabeth II.'

Alongside veteran Royal biographer Robert Hardman, Kate hosts her own podcast series, Queens, Kings and Dastardly Things, which has launched its own mini-series all about Queen Elizabeth II to mark her centenary. You can listen to the Queen Elizabeth series, which includes two epsidoes on Queen Elizabeth and David Attenborough, as well as the whole podcast series, on apple podcasts.

Kate Williams' new book, Regina: A New History of Women and Power is available to pre-order on Amazon right now before its release in June. From Cleopatra to Grace Kelly, she'll. guide you through the epic history of royal women, shattering the myths that have been built around them.

item.title: Mad About History - Five seriously surprising facts about Buckingham Palace that even royal experts wouldn’t know, from ghostly sightings, to legendary secret tunnels
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: As we celebrate 100 years since the birth of Queen Elizabeth II, we’re taking a closer look at the history of her most famous royal residence, and the many secrets it holds
item.content:encoded:
Although it is the most recognisable building in London, and a rite of passage for any visitor to the capital, it is pretty baffling that there are still so many secrets and facts about Buckingham Palace that most people don't know.

King Charles III's official residence in London and the administrative centre for the Royal Family, Buckingham Palace, has been the official royal residence of the monarch since 1837 and the iconic backdrop to hundreds of public appearances of the royal family.

However, unlike residences like Windsor Castle, which is the most visited royal site in Britain, Buckingham Palace only opens its doors to visitors for the summer months every year, so what lurks behind those grand gates remains a relative mystery to many. Ashley Hicks' Buckingham Palace: The Interiors is available on Amazon and is the first luxury book to document the lavish state rooms of the palace - if you haven't been, I highly recommend giving this a read.

Here are five seriously surprising facts you never knew about Buckingham Palace

View this post on Instagram

1. It is haunted by a chained monk who used to live on the site

Rumour swirls of a chained monk wearing a brown cloak who haunts the rear terrace of Buckingham Palace, supposedly a former resident of the 16th-century monastery that once occupied the site of the palace.

The ghost of the monk is most often reported by staff on Christmas Day and is seen moaning and dragging his chains. Supposedly, he died a horrid death in a punishment cell in the monastery...

2. Buckingham Palace was once home to a witch

In the 15th-century, when the site of Buckingham Palace was marshland, it is believed it was home to a legendary witch known as Margarie Gourdemaine, otherwise known as the 'Witch of Eye next Westminster.'

She was a local 'wise woman' who was known to provide spells, love potions, fertility aids and concoctions to people in the 1420s, including many high-ranking nobles, most notably, Eleanor Cobham, the wife of Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester.

Margarie was sadly later accused of heresy and witchcraft and was burned at the stake in 1441. She had been caught up in a political scandal in which Eleanor Cobham was accused of treasonable necromancy. Margarie was accused of making a wax figure of King Henry VI to cause him harm - this was a politically motivated charge to target the Duke of Gloucester.

Mari Griffith's brilliant novel, The Witch of Eye, which is available on Amazon, tells the fascinating but deeply sad story of these two women and their fated end.

3. There are supposedly tunnels that connect Buckingham Palace to Whitehall and the Houses of Parliament

Although this is an unproven claim, many people have believed that there are a series of tunnels connecting Buckingham Palace to important sights across the city, including Whitehall, Clarence House and the Houses of Parliament.

If these tunnels do exist, which is highly likely, it is probable that they were built during World War II to connect the palace to nearby government buildings.

4. The palace has everything it needs to be its own self-sufficient village

There are over 800 members of staff who work at Buckingham Palace, and many of them live on site. The palace, therefore, has everything it needs to be its very own self-sufficient village and is home to a post office, a cinema, a police station and a clinic. It even has its own Coutts ATM machine.

5. Buckingham Palace is built on the site of a failed silkworm farm

Before the palace was a palace, King James I and VI ordered 100,000 black mulberry trees to feed silk worms so that he could start his own silk industry in 1609.

Unfortunately for King James, however, it became apparent that silk worms prefer white mulberry trees. The worms failed to thrive, and his project was a failure.

There is a surviving mulberry tree from King James's 17th-century project that still lives in Buckingham Palace garden today.

If you're interested in the palace's spooky. bizarre and surprising history, Patricia Wright's Strange History of Buckingham Palace is available on Amazon and reveals even more secrets from behind the palace's iconic, ceremonial gates.

item.title: An *almost* explosive discovery on Culloden Battlefield sends Scottish archaeologists into a frenzy as we mark 280 years since the last Jacobite rising and the last major battle fought on British soil
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Archaeologists from the National Trust for Scotland have made the discovery of a lifetime on Culloden Battlefield
item.content:encoded:
280 years after one of the bloodiest battles in British history, archaeologists from the National Trust for Scotland have made the discovery of a lifetime: an unexploded shell has been unearthed at Culloden Battlefield.

A place of pilgrimage and remembrance for the ancestors of those killed at the battle, the battlefield marks the last Jacobite uprising led by Bonnie Prince Charlie on 16 April 1746, and the last major battle fought on British soil.

The Jacobites were brutally defeated by an army led by the Duke of Cumberland, not-so affectionately known as the Butcher of Culloden. Trevor Royle's Culloden: Scotland's Last Battle and the Forging of the British Empire is available on Amazon and is probably the most authoritative and vividly descriptive book on this major piece of Scottish history you can get your hands on.

Unexploded shell discovered on Culloden Battlefield

Archaeologists have discovered other projectiles at Culloden Battlefield in the past, including cannon shots, musket balls and fragments of mortar shells, but unexploded ordnance has never been found, you can only imagine the excitement when it was uncovered.

It is believed that because parts of the battlefield are particularly boggy, the shell landed on wet ground and the fuse distinguished before the powder was ignited.

The 5.5-inch shell was discovered intact and packed with gunpowder and is the first piece of complete government ordnance found on the battlefield.

The shell weighs 8kg, which is heavier than the three and four-pound cannonballs used by the Jacobites, so is thought to have been fired from one of the government army's six Coehorn mortars - a lightweight gun that shot explosive shells in high arcs over the troops. Archaeologists have previously found other shells from Coehorn mortars at Culloden, but this is the first complete, unexploded example to have ever been recovered from a Jacobite battle site.

Derek Alexander, The National Trust for Scotland’s Head of Archaeology, said: “This is a remarkable - dare I say, explosive - find of the kind archaeologists dream of, but never expect to encounter.

“Along with the other projectiles recovered, this discovery helps us better understand the formation of troops and concentration of fire during this brief, but brutal, battle. The mortars may have been aiming at the Jacobite artillery, so the place where the shell landed may mark the heart of the Culloden battlefield. The ground rises slightly in this area, providing a better vantage point, and it is adjacent to the old road along which artillery could’ve moved more easily, making it a plausible location for the Jacobite artillery positions."

The shell was discovered in a field north-west of the Memorial Cairn by a team of archaeologists, metal-detectorists and volunteers led by Derek and the National Trust for Scotland's Archaeology team. The dig was part of a week-long programme, and during the excavation, more than 100 projectiles, including lead musket balls and pistol shot, were uncovered.

The shell still retained part of its original plug and its bung, and internal contents were removed under controlled conditions by specialists Ramora UK. When it was exposed to the air, the 280-year-old gunpowder had a small exothermic reaction. The shell was safely cleaned out and certified as free from hazard.

The shell is temporarily on display at the Culloden Battlefield Visitor Centre before being moved to a permanent display.

Derek Alexander commented, “This is our fifth consecutive year of digging at Culloden, so it’s an ideal time to assess what we’ve found and consider how this work impacts on-site interpretation and the long-term management of the battlefield. We didn’t have high expectations for this previously unexplored area of the battlefield – acidic soil was thought to have impacted the preservation of artefacts, and the broken ground makes detecting difficult – so we’ve been invigorated by this unexpected discovery. It’s clear there are many more secrets and stories to be uncovered at Culloden."

2026 marks the 280th anniversary of the Battle of Culloden, and there will be a commemoration service taking place on Saturday, 18th April at 10:30 am at Culloden Battlefield. Get your tickets for anniversary events at the Battlefield via the National Trust for Scotland.

Archaeologists make discoveries of treasure on British soil regularly, with some of the most astonishing finds in history being unearthed in recent years, including a Tudor jewel once belonging to Henry VIII, and hoards of medieval coins found in Scotland. The Handbook of British Archaeology, available on Amazon, is an essential read for anyone interested in the history beneath Britain's soil, and for budding archaeologists to make a start themselves.

item.title: The largest ever memorial to Queen Elizabeth II is set to be unveiled in London’s oldest royal park, and will be a wonderfully moving tribute to her, her incredible legacy and her late husband
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: As we celebrate her centenary year, it has been revealed that Architecture firm Forster + Partners has been tasked with designing a brand new memorial to Queen Elizabeth II, which will sit a stone’s throw from Buckingham Palace
item.content:encoded:
It seems fitting that London's most visited Royal Park, overlooked by Buckingham Palace itself, will now be home to the largest ever Queen Elizabeth II memorial, unveiled in the year we celebrate the centenary of her birth.

Architects Foster + Partners, who are also behind London's Millennium Bridge, have been tasked with designing a permanent national memorial to the late Queen Elizabeth II, who was born 100 years ago this year, in 1926.

The memorial is one of several commemorations to mark 100 years since the Queen was born, including the largest ever exhibition of her fashion, which opened at the King's Gallery at Buckingham Palace last week. This month has also seen a brand new major biography by royal historian Hugo Vickers, Queen Elizabeth II, which is available from Amazon and is unsurprisingly flying off the shelves.

Largest ever tribute to Queen Elizabeth II to be built in St James's Park

Architecture firm Foster + Partners has been handed the mammoth task of designing this brand new permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II, which will be the largest ever tribute to her.

The memorial for the late queen, who passed away in 2022, in the year we celebrated 70 years of her reign, will be at the heart of St James's Park, London's oldest and most visited royal park and within a stone's throw of Buckingham Palace itself.

The firm will redesign St James's Park's Blue Bridge, which has views of the palace itself, into a wider, tiara-shaped crossing. The bridge, featuring a cast-glass balustrade, recalls Queen Elizabeth's wedding tiara.

Norman Foster, Founder and Executive Chairman, Foster + Partners, commented, "At the heart of our masterplan is a translucent bridge symbolic of Her Majesty as a unifying force, bringing together nations, countries, the Commonwealth, charities and the armed forces.”

The firm's concept also includes new sculptures, a brand new Prince Philip Gate and a special garden dedicated to the Commonwealth to create spaces for coming together and celebrating the nation's diversity.

Renowned British artist Martin Jennings has also designed a brand new statue of Queen Elizabeth II, which will be at the park's Marlborough Gate, which is the entrance to the park opposite the palace itself.

There will also be a companion statue of the Queen's late husband, Prince Philip, who died in 2021.

Robin Janvrin, Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee Chair, said: “Foster + Partners' ambitious and thoughtful masterplan will allow us and future generations to appreciate Queen Elizabeth's life of service as she balanced continuity and change with strong values, common sense and optimism throughout her long reign.”

The project will also include a Commonwealth-inspired bronze wind sculpture by artist Yinka Shonibare, which will feature floral decorations and plants that represent all 56 member nations of the Commonwealth.

There will also be a special memorial path connecting the UK and Commonwealth Gardens, designed by landscape architect Michel Desvigne, a bust of Queen Elizabeth II installed at the Birdcage Walk entrance, and a children's discovery trail featuring storytelling elements.

This new statue of Queen Elizabeth II joins the ranks of many other queens who have been immortalised in stone throughout the streets of London, including, of course, Queen Victoria and medieval queens like Eleanor of Castile. It wasn't until the 20th century, however, that non-royal women began to get a look in when it came to statues. Author Juliet Rix has written a brilliant book on the subject, London's Statues of Women, which is available on Amazon and offers a new and fascinating vision of the capital and its history.

item.title: The princess who was never meant to be queen: this is the story of Queen Elizabeth II’s childhood, from care-free Lilibet to heir presumptive in waiting for the crown
item.dc:creator: Florence Sheward, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From the age of 10, Princess Elizabeth knew she was the heir presumptive – and her formative years did much to shape how she would embrace the role of a lifetime
item.content:encoded:
When King Edward VIII made the difficult decision to abdicate the British throne on 11 December 1936, one thing that he could not have considered was the effect that this decision would have on his 10-year-old niece, Princess Elizabeth Alexandra Mary Windsor.

Fewer than 12 months before his reign had begun, he abdicated with one thought in his mind: his love for the American divorcee – and unsuitable queen consort – Wallis Simpson. In that one decision, the life of a young princess changed forever as she suddenly became heir presumptive, the future Queen Elizabeth II.

King Edward VIII's story is fascinating and controversial, and worth studying. I'd recommend reading his autobiography, A King's Story: The Memoirs of HRH the Duke of Windsor, which is available on Amazon, and which tells his side of the story and sets the scene for the start of the reign of Queen Elizabeth II.

When was Queen Elizabeth II born?

Two plaques today commemorate the place where the first child of the Duke and Duchess of York was born at 2.40 am on 21 April 1926 in her grandfather’s house at 17 Bruton Street in Mayfair. (The house is sadly long since demolished after being damaged during the Blitz).

Where did Princess Elizabeth live in London?

After briefly staying with her grandparents while her parents completed a six-month tour of Australia and New Zealand, Princess Elizabeth moved with her family to 145 Piccadilly, her first London home. Overlooking Green Park in the heart of the city, the five-storey house was known simply as “One-Four-Five” (also destroyed in the Second World War, it is replaced today by the InterContinental London Park Lane hotel). On their first day there, a crowd of thousands gathered outside, according to the Western Morning News. “For a long time after the Royal party had entered the house the cheers continued,” the reporter noted. “A crimson cloth was placed on the balcony; Baby was held up to the crowd, who went frantic with excitement.”

The young princess had everything she needed here: a nursery suite, a glass cabinet full of toys and a modest garden in which to ride her tricycle. Homeschooled by her governess Marion Crawford (“Crawfie”) at the family home, the princess was expected to live a privileged yet relatively quiet life as part of the extended Royal Family.

When did Princess Elizabeth become heir to the throne?

Her uncle’s abdication, however, resulted in her stammering father – portrayed brilliantly by Colin Firth in the Oscar-winning film The King’s Speech (the DVD of The King's Speech is available to buy on Amazon) – being crowned King George VI. In the process, Princess Elizabeth had become heir presumptive: the first in line to the throne.

Queen Elizabeth II's childhood

The family, including her younger sister Margaret, moved into Buckingham Palace, as her once-attentive parents became absorbed by the many meetings and ceremonies that came with her father’s new role. Elizabeth – or “Lilibet” as she was known – yearned for a more normal existence. She learned to swim at The Bath Club on Dover Street, enrolled as a Girl Guide and continued to live as ordinary a life as possible in such extraordinary circumstances, while still preparing herself for her future role by brushing up on constitutional law with the vice provost of Eton College and studying religion with the Archbishop of Canterbury.

When Princess Elizabeth was just 13 years old, Britain and many other Commonwealth countries declared war on Hitler’s Germany. Despite being of German descent (the Royal Family was originally part of the historic Saxe-Coburg-Gotha family, before rebranding as the more anglicised ‘House of Windsor’ in 1917), King George and his family took a much more active role in the war effort this time. When it was suggested to the Queen that they should evacuate to the US, she famously replied: “The children won’t go without me, I won’t leave the King, and the King will never leave.”

Princess Elizabeth's life during the Second World War

The princesses were instead moved to Windsor Castle. Princess Elizabeth’s first official speech was made from here during the Battle of Britain on 13 October 1940. The 14-year-old was tasked with addressing evacuees across the Commonwealth on the BBC Home Service radio show Children’s Hour, and she was praised for her morale-boosting efforts. She would later offer more practical help by joining the Auxiliary Territorial Service, a women-only branch of the armed forces.

What did Princess Elizabeth and Princess Margaret do on VE Day?

When victory was announced on 8 May 1945, Princess Elizabeth was visible again, appearing on the balcony at Buckingham Palace with her family during VE Day celebrations. The 19-year-old heir was swept along by the euphoria that marked the end of the war and asked her parents if she could go out into the London crowds with her sister, Princess Margaret, to experience the celebrations for themselves. “We cheered the king and queen on the balcony and then walked miles through the streets,” she later recalled. “I remember lines of unknown people linking arms and walking down Whitehall, all of us just swept along on a tide of happiness and relief.”

Dressed in uniform and with their caps pulled down to disguise their faces, together the royal teenagers cheered, sang and danced, doing the famous Lambeth Walk – a Cockney strut first seen in the 1937 musical Me and My Girl – as well as joining a conga line that danced into the side entrance of The Ritz hotel. “It was a unique burst of personal freedom,” recalled the Queen’s cousin, Margaret Rhodes, “a Cinderella moment in reverse.”

There was a wonderful 2015 film made of this special night called A Royal Night Out, which is available on Amazon, is a fictionalised depiction of what the two young princesses got up to that night - it is well worth a watch.

A true Cinderella moment was to come just two years later, when the princess married her prince.

item.title: These are all the magical Harry Potter movie filming locations in the UK you have to visit before the new series comes out, plus a few secret locations the HBO crew have been spotted filming
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: As we await the release of HBO’s brand new Harry Potter series, these are all the Harry Potter movie filming locations every fan has to visit, as well as a few new locations from the TV series
item.content:encoded:
Around the world, the boy wizard with his lightning scar is synonymous with Britain, and tourists arrive in their thousands to revel in the Harry Potter filming locations that inspired JK Rowling to write the original books, and those that were made famous on the silver screen, when Harry launched from the page to the cinema.

As HBO wraps up filming on their brand new Harry Potter TV series, which will hit our screens at the end of 2026, what better time to revisit some of the iconic British filming locations used for the Harry Potter films, and to look ahead to some of the new spots used for the series.

Harry Potter’s wizarding world has inspired children and adults around the globe for nearly 30 years. The books are among the biggest-selling in children’s literature history, and the resulting films catapulted Harry, Hermione, Ron, all their friends, and enemies into the public eye. If you haven't read them, where have you been? Get your copy of the Harry Potter seven-book set on Amazon before the new series comes out.

Where was JK Rowling inspired to write Harry Potter?

Rowling endured difficult marriages and economic uncertainty living in London and Edinburgh in the 1990s. She has said that the idea for Harry Potter came to her on the train from Manchester to London, and that the book first began life when she lived in Clapham, south London.

Edinburgh

However, things really went into overdrive when she arrived in Edinburgh and began writing on a more full-time basis. Rowling famously spent her time in cafes due to a shortage of cash, writing in places like The Elephant House on Edinburgh’s George IV Bridge, which has a plaque outside the building commemorating her time writing there, and the real table on which she wrote.

Rowling often walked the Edinburgh streets, and although she is loath to attribute places she passed to actual locations in the wizarding world, there’s no doubt that the dour architecture and gloomy gothic grandeur of the Scottish capital inspired the feel of Harry Potter.

Greyfriar’s Kirkyard is a spooky location Rowling passed many times, one where you feel ghosts could easily lurk. The grave of Thomas Riddle – yes, Lord Voldemort himself – is located here.

The 1628 George Heriot School on Lauriston Place is a sprawling and unmissable sight on the Edinburgh skyline, and could easily have galvanised the feel of how Hogwarts might look. It is split into four houses, which compete with each other, just like at Hogwarts.

Victoria Street is an atmospheric thoroughfare that curves uphill and is lined with shops, its hustle and bustle is said to have inspired Rowling’s hidden wizarding street in London, Diagon Alley.

When Rowling started writing she was not wealthy but, thanks to the success of the books, became very rich, finishing her final book in the series, Harry Potter and The Deathly Hallows, in the splendour of the Victorian Balmoral Hotel at the top of Princes Street. The room in which she wrote – 552 – has been rechristened the JK Rowling Suite. Visitors can stay here, and wannabe writers can put pen to paper on the actual desk on which Rowling wrote.

Several tour companies run special Harry Potter tours of Edinburgh itself, which I would highly recommend booking if you are in the city, and a fan! They are all available to book via TripAdvisor.

Harry Potter filming locations in Scotland you can visit

Glenfinnan Viaduct

Travel out towards the West Highlands, and you can see the mighty Glenfinnan Viaduct marching across the moors. This stupendous feat of Victorian railway engineering, completed in 1901, carries the West Highland Line between Fort William and Mallaig through some of Scotland’s most dramatic hills and seascapes.

This is where the Hogwarts Express powers over the valley in the films. The heritage train, known as the Jacobite, still plies this route to keep fans happy. The puffing steam service travels from Fort William to Mallaig daily in spring and summer.

You can book a trip on the Jacobite via TripAdvisor here.

There are also regular rail services (scotrail.co.uk) several times a day, which travel over the viaduct in case you miss the steam service. It’s worth walking the valley from Glenfinnan to see the viaduct from the surrounding hills, especially to watch the steam trains crossing – there are spectacular photo opportunities to be had.

Alnwick Castle Harry Potter filming locations

Leaving Scotland and travelling south across the English border, Alnwick Castle is set in the picturesque Northumberland market town of Alnwick. The tiny town is a maze of small streets and independent stores, with the castle at its heart, overlooking the River Aln. The castle has been home to the Percy family for over 700 years, and the 12th Duke and Duchess live here with their children today.

In 2000, the castle was the location for the filming of large parts of Harry Potter and The Philosopher’s Stone, released the following year. In 2001, the production team returned to film Harry Potter and The Chamber of Secrets. The Lion Arch is the entry point to the castle, leading out to the Forbidden Forest and Hagrid’s Hut.

The Outer Bailey of the castle will be instantly recognisable to fans as the place where Madam Hooch teaches Harry and the rest of his classmates how to fly on their broomsticks and where Harry learns to play Quidditch. The Inner Bailey is, of course, the location where Harry and Ron crash the Weasley’s flying car.

You can book a tour of Alnwick Castle with Rabbie's tour guides via TripAdvisor here.

Harry Potter filming locations in Durham

The ancient cloisters of Durham Cathedral – once home to meditating monks – stood in for Hogwarts in Harry Potter and the Philosopher’s Stone. With a magically snowy backdrop, the cloisters became the quadrangle where Harry releases Hedwig the owl from his hands. Harry, Ron and Hermione were also taught how to turn animals into water goblets here.

Harry Potter filming locations in Oxford

Down in the handsome university city of Oxford, several university buildings provided locations for Harry Potter and The Philosopher’s Stone, and Harry Potter and The Goblet of Fire, including Christ Church College.

The college dates from 1546 and is part of Oxford University. In the historic hallways, Hermione shows Harry the Quidditch trophy won by his father, and the staircase is where Professor McGonagall meets Harry and the other first years on their very first day at Hogwarts.

The production team made the most of Oxford’s old buildings and filming also took place in the famous Bodleian Library complex of the university, where Duke Humfrey’s Library stands in for the Hogwarts Library.

New College Oxford also features – the large oak in its cloisters is where Harry has a face-off with Draco Malfoy in Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire.

Book your walking tour of Harry Potter filming locations in Oxford here.

Harry Potter filming locations in London

One stop on the itinerary must be London's King’s Cross Station, where Rowling used to catch her train from London to Edinburgh. This must have inspired the Hogwarts Express, which, as we all know, travels from the very secret Platform 9 and 3/4 at the station.

Don’t bother looking for that unless you can whip out your magic wand, but follow the throngs of visitors and you’ll find a luggage trolley embedded into the wall next to Platform 9, and next to it a Harry Potter-themed gift shop. Here you can get have your photo taken at the place where the young wizards would use their magic to walk through the wall and on to the secret platform – a spell which the many London commuters passing through the station probably wished they knew too.

Leadenhall Market’s historic covered pathways were a good match for Diagon Alley in Harry Potter and the Philosopher’s Stone, when Hagrid and Harry walk through the market, go shopping for wands and enter the Leaky Cauldron Pub through a blue door.

Where is the new Harry Potter series being filmed?

Despite the initial announcement for the series coming over a year ago, any information about the new HBO Harry Potter TV series has been kept totally secret until March 2026, when we were finally treated to a teaser trailer for the first series, which will cover the first book, Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone.

As a life-long Harry Potter fan, I, like many, was sceptical about whether the new series could live up to the magic of the films. However, after watching the trailer, I found myself thinking it might possibly be better. Filmed over ten years, each series will cover a book, which will allow for a lot more content than could possibly have been included in a two-hour film.

What also struck me from the trailer was the beautiful filming locations I could spot, making use of Britain's beautiful landscapes to create a new Harry Potter universe that will delight fans old and new!

Here are some of the HBO Harry Potter series filming locations we know about so far:

The Cornish coast

Film crews have been spotted in and around the Cornish coast, including at Cadgwith Cove, Kynance Cove and Carnglaze Caverns. Although we aren't 100% sure what these scenes are for yet, we can guess that Cornwall's resemblance to Scotland, where Hogwarts is, might play a part in it, as well as the scene in Philsopher's Stone where Harry first meets Hagrid when the Dursley's escape to a hut on a rock in the sea to avoid the post for Harry.

Skipton, Yorkshire

Again, it isn't totally clear what this market town in Yorkshire will be transformed into for HBO's Harry Potter, but in October 2025, film crews were seen around Skipton and cobbled Westmoreland Street was transformed into a film set.

One actor was even spotted casting spells from a rooftop on the street!

Borough High Street & Embankment, London

There is only one scene in Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone that takes place in London, which is, of course, when Hagrid takes Harry to London to go to Diagon Alley to buy everything he needs for his first time at Hogwarts.

Actor Nick Frost, who will play Hagrid in the new series, was spotted in his Hagrid costume filming on Borough High Street and Embankment.

Hoddesdon, Hertfordshire

The leafy, suburban town of Hoddesdon in Hertfordshire was taken over by film crews in September 2025 as it was transformed into the town of Little Whinging, where Harry lives with the Dursleys in the books.

Some fans also believe the town was used for scenes in which Uncle Vernon encounters excited witches and wizards celebrating the death of You Know Who at the beginning of the book.

Windsor Great Park, Windsor

The beautiful Windsor Great Park, which surrounds Windsor Castle, was used for filming forested scenes - likely Forbidden Forest scenes in the Philosopher's Stone - as it resembles the forests of the Scottish Highlands with its tall Scots pines.

Of course, much of the filming has taken place at Warner Bros Studios in Leavesden, Hertfordshire, which is also home to the brilliant Warner Brothers Studio Tour, where you can walk through the incredible sets from all eight Harry Potter films. Virgin Experience Days offer several different Harry Potter Warner Brothers experiences, including the tour plus lunch, or with afternoon tea, or even with a one-night Harry Potter-themed stay. Book yours via Virgin.

item.title: Where is Young Sherlock filmed? These iconic filming locations were transformed into Victorian England for Amazon Prime’s new Sherlock Holmes origin story of Britain’s best-loved detective
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From the world’s oldest prison to Wales’s answer to the Cotswolds, these are the Young Sherlock filming locations you can visit yourself
item.content:encoded:
For a character that is so fundamentally British, it is only right that the filming locations for Young Sherlock, directed by Guy Ritchie, feature some of the most beautiful places in all of the country.

The series, which was released on Amazon Prime in March, has inspired a new wave of audiences to try out set-jetting, thanks to its featuring some of the most iconic filming locations in all of Britain, including the historic spires of Oxford and the quaint Welsh village of Merthyr Mawr.

Young Sherlock stars Hero Fiennes Tiffin as a young and undisciplined Sherlock Holmes at Oxford University and sets the scene for his evolution into the much-loved detective created by Arthur Conan Doyle. The series, written by Matthew Parkhill, is based on the best-selling Andrew Lane's Young Sherlock Holmes books, which are available on Amazon as a set, and are worth reading before or after you watch the series!

From Oxford to Monmouthshire, here is everywhere Young Sherlock was filmed, so you can step into his wonderful Victorian world yourself.

All the Young Sherlock filming locations in Britain you can visit yourself

1. Oxford

The series kicks off in the 1870s with our youthful Sherlock Holmes being taken to begin his studies at Oxford University by his elder brother, Mycroft.

Although Sherlock is enrolled in Candlin College in the series (something he is not thrilled about, as it isn't the oldest or most prestigious in the university), filming actually took place at Magdalen College, which is one of the most beautiful and historic colleges at Oxford University.

Eagle-eyed viewers will also spot Oxford landmarks like the impressive Radcliffe Camera, the Bodleian Library (which was also used for the Hogwarts Library in Harry Potter), and the pretty cobbled Merton Street lined with medieval stone buildings.

Several tour companies offer walking tours of filming locations in Oxford, which I would highly recommend doing if you're a film buff like me! You can book them via TripAdvisor.

2. Bristol

Although, of course, Sherlock Holmes is best associated with London, the producers of Young Sherlock transformed the streets of Bristol with its historic architecture into Victorian London for the series.

Well-known streets, including Broad Street, Small Street, Corn Street and Queen Square, can be spotted in the series, as well as spots like The Underfall Yard, the Georgian House Museum and the magnificent Bristol Cathedral.

3. Merthyr Mawr, Bridgend

This picturesque village in South Wales is often referred to as Wales's answer to the Cotswolds, and, unsurprisingly, its fairytale-esque thatched cottages have made it a magnet for location scouts.

The sprawling fields and river of Merthyr Mawr doubled as the grounds of Sherlock's family home in Young Sherlock, with some scenes taking place within the village's quiet streets and outside the Church of St Teilo and one of the village's thatched cottages standing in as Professor Ambrose Robert's cottage in the series.

4. Llanvihangel Court, Monmouthshire

View this post on Instagram

This impressive Tudor manor house on the edge of the Black Mountains stood in for Appleton Manor, the Holmes' family home in the series.

Llanvihangel Court dates back to at least 1471 and functions as a private home today, but is open for summer tours and events.

5. Llandaff Cathedral, Cardiff

Cardiff's magnificent cathedral was closed for four days for filming in January 2025, which was transformed into the Victorian era for Young Sherlock. The cathedral features in a particularly dramatic scene in episode 3, The Case of the Unarmed Man.

6. Margam Park, Port Talbot

This Grade I listed house and garden was also used as the setting for the Holmes family's grand country estate.

The 850-acre Margam Park estate is home to a mightily impressive Gothic castle and is open for visitors.

The park is also home to Margam Abbey, a ruined Cistercian monastery that stood in for Abdon Abbey in the series.

7. Monmouth, Monmouthshire

Many spots in the historic Welsh market town of Monmouth were transformed into Oxford for Young Sherlock.

The most prominent Young Sherlock filming location in the town was Beaufort Arms Court, a cosy cobbled courtyard that became a bustling Oxford street in the series.

Monmouth's Shire Hall also features in the court scene of episode one of the series.

8. The Rolls of Monmouth, Monmouthshire

The spectacular Hendre, home to the Rolls of Monmouth Golf Club, stands in as the psychiatric hospital that houses Cordelia Holmes in Young Sherlock. The grand mansion is the only full-scale Victorian country house in Monmouthshire was originally the family home of the Rolls family, co-founders of Rolls-Royce.

View this post on Instagram

You can book a round of golf at the Rolls of Monmouth via TripAdvisor.

8. Shepton Mallet Prison, Somerset

The gloomy, historic interiors of the world's oldest prison, 400-year-old Shepton Mallet Prison in Somerset, which is now open as a heritage attraction, doubled as London's notorious Newgate Prison in Young Sherlock, with filming taking place inside the exercise yard as well as in some of the actual prison cells.

Unsurprisingly, being the oldest prison in the world, Shepton Mallet has a fair few tales of hauntings to tell. Richard Estep's Spirits Behind Bars: The Haunting of Shepton Mallet Prison and the Skirrid Inn is available on Amazon, and tells the spookiest stories of the world's oldest prison and one of the country's oldest pubs - read if you dare!

item.title: The best ways to celebrate World Heritage Day in Britain - from jousting tournaments to dog shows, these are the best UNESCO World Heritage Site events this weekend
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Saturday 18 April 2026 marks World Heritage Day in the UK, an annual celebration of Britain’s incredible 35 UNESCO World Heritage Sites and the work being done to preserve them. Here are the best ways to celebrate this year...
item.content:encoded:
With spring in the air and longer, warmer days, weekends call for visits to Britain's many incredible historic sites. So if you were planning a heritage-themed day out this weekend, you're in luck, because Saturday 18 April marks World Heritage Day 2026.

Britain is home to no less than 35 UNESCO World Heritage Sites, and this annual celebration, organised by UNESCO, is designed to highlight the importance of preserving them all, historic, cultural and natural.

Whether you're planning a heritage trip this weekend or this summer, in the UK or beyond, UNESCO have produced a brand-new book, UNESCO World Heritage Sites to visit in 2026, which is available on Amazon, and is your definitive guide to the world's most spectacular landmarks and natural wonders.

But if you're in the UK this weekend and would like to take part in this country-wide celebration of our fantastic heritage, here is our guide to celebrating World Heritage Day 2026, and the best events to take part in this year.

How to celebrate World Heritage Day 2026 in the UK

Of course, the best way to celebrate World Heritage Day is to simply get out of the house and visit a heritage site near you. With 35 UNESCO World Heritage sites across the UK, we are spoiled for choice; some of the most popular and best-known include Stonehenge, the Roman Baths in Bath, and the Tower of London.

Many UNESCO sites will also open their doors for a special World Heritage Day event this Saturday, with talks, guided tours and workshops.

Here are the best places to celebrate World Heritage Day this year:

World Heritage Day 2026 in Bath

It's not hard to see why the City of Bath is on the UNESCO list, thanks to its incredible Roman heritage and sites and well-preserved Georgian architecture.

The Bath Guildhall will host a day of free celebrations this Saturday, 18 April, from 11 am - 3 pm, with activities, crafts, and fascinating stories from some of Bath's best-loved heritage organisations, including The Roman Baths, Victoria Art Gallery, Bath Abbey, No. 1 Royal Crescent, and Cleveland Pools.

You can also enjoy talks throughout the day from local heritage experts and guided city walks led by the Mayor's Guides.

Find out more about the City of Bath's events this weekend here. You can also book tickets to Bath's Roman Baths via TripAdvisor here.

World Heritage Day 2026 in Durham

The historic city of Durham in England's north-eastern corner will celebrate its history and heritage this World Heritage Day with a day of events centred on the theme 'The People's Story'.

Durham Cathedral will be open to visitors as normal, with a special exhibition on at the Cathedral Museum, A Treasure and a Legacy: Conservation at Durham, Cathedral and special 30-minute talks hosted throughout the day.

There will also be free entry to Durham Castle all day and free entry to the Palace Green Library which includes the Museum of Archaeology and World Heritage Site Visitor Centre.

Find out more at This is Durham.

World Heritage Day in Saltaire

This World Heritage Day, the town of Saltaire in Yorkshire will mark 25 years as a UNESCO World Heritage Site with a day of celebrations in Roberts Park. Visitors can enjoy artisan markets, creative workshops, a dog show, and birds of prey displays, dance performers, street entertainers, and traditional fairground attractions.

Saltaire is one of the best-preserved 'model' 19th-century villages in the country, founded by industrialist Sir Titus Salt in 1853, who made his fortune in the wool trade. The historic village is very attractive and brimming with heritage - it is well-worth an explore. If you'd like to discover more about this fascinating town, I'd recommend Gary Firth's Saltaire History Tour, which is available on Amazon and is a unique insight into the town's heritage.

Find out more via Visit Bradford.

World Heritage Day on the Jurassic Coast in Dorset

One of the UK's natural UNESCO World Heritage Sites, the stunning 95-mile Jurassic Coast in Devon and Dorset, will mark 25 years as a UNESCO site this World Heritage Day.

Celebrated worldwide for its unbroken geological record spanning 185 million years with preserved rock formations from the Triassic, Jurassic and Cretaceous periods, the Jurassic Coast tells the story of the earth's history through landscapes and incredible fossils.

Dennis Kelsall's brilliant The Jurassic Coast - Circular Walks along the South West Coast Path is available on Amazon and is a must-read for anyone wishing to explore the coastline on foot.

The Dorset Museum and Art Gallery will celebrate this World Heritage Day with a special programme featuring talks and presentations exploring the science, art and stories of the Jurassic Coast.

Find out more via the Dorset Museum.

World Heritage Day at Castell Harlech, Gwynedd, Wales

This fabulous Grade-I listed medieval castle built onto a rocky knoll close to the Irish Sea by King Edward I is one of the most spectacular and historic castles in Britain, and we couldn't think of anywhere better to celebrate World Heritage Day than Castell Harlech.

To celebrate this year, the castle will host special guided tours with experienced tour guide Siân Roberts, where visitors can learn the stories behind the stones of this incredible and beautiful piece of Welsh history.

You can book your tickets via Castell Harlech via TripAdvisor here.

World Heritage Day at Blenheim Palace, Oxfordshire

Perhaps Britain's best-loved UNESCO World Heritage site, Blenheim Palace, is a magnificent stately home that is the only grand house to have the title of palace, despite having no royal connection. Although there are no specific events this weekend to celebrate World Heritage Day at the birthplace of Winston Churchill, Blenheim is worth visiting any day of the year, and there are special spring events on at the moment.

There are newborn lambs and the first spring blooms in the magnificent grounds, as well as jousting events to look forward to. Inside the palace, visitors can explore the brand-new Blueprints of Power Exhibition and step into the hidden world of the Blenheim Palace servants in the Life Below Stairs exhibition.

Blenheim Palace has a wealth of history and treasures to explore, and is a must-visit for anyone visiting Britain for the first time. Lady Henrietta Spencer-Churchill, the twelfth generation of the family who owned the palace, has written a wonderful book about the history of the palace and how her family's history has been intertwined with it. Blenheim: 300 Years of Life in a Palace is available on Amazon and will guide you through Britain's most important and most visited stately home.

item.title: A 27-year-old woman, 27 million viewers and a thousand-year-old ceremony - this is why Queen Elizabeth II’s Coronation in 1953 was the most famous royal event in history
item.dc:creator: Laura Silverman
item.description: Queen Elizabeth II was crowned in 1953 in a ceremony of pomp and splendour. We tell the story of this momentous day in her life
item.content:encoded:
The coronation of Queen Elizabeth II was the most British of days: a day of glory and spectacle, steeped in tradition and uniting the Commonwealth. And it rained.

The day was 2 June 1953, the new Queen Elizabeth II had acceded to the throne on 6 February 1952, when her father, King George VI, passed away. The coronation, following a period of mourning and meticulous preparation, marked the occasion. It was to be a day of celebration and solemnity, the centrepiece a ceremony that had remained largely the same for 1,000 years.

There have been 39 coronations held at Westminster Abbey since William the Conqueror's in 1066, and the bones of the ceremony remain little changed in 1000 years. If you'd like to know more about the ceremony itself, I'd recommend Ian Lloyd's brilliant The Throne: 1,000 Years of British Coronations, which is available on Amazon and details every little bit of history about this ultimate British display of pomp and circumstance.

Queen Elizabeth II's Coronation

How old was the queen when she was crowned?

The Queen was 27 at the time. But she had been thinking about the day since she was 11. In May 1937, her father, King George VI, had asked her to record his coronation at Westminster Abbey so that she would appreciate it. “I thought it all very, very wonderful,” she wrote. “The arches and the beams at the top were covered in a sort of haze of wonder as Papa was crowned, or at least I thought so.” Then an admission. The service became “rather boring” towards the end because “it was all prayers”.

The young Queen was unlikely to feel bored when her turn came around, but rather a heady mix of excitement, pride and, perhaps, a little trepidation – not that she showed it. Lady Rosemary Spencer-Churchill, one of her Maids of Honour on the day, recalls her appearing “very confident”. Speaking on BBC Radio 4 in 2013, Lady Rosemary said: “She made us feel very confident too. The minute she arrived, everyone just felt everything was going to be all right.”

Preparations for the day begin

Hundreds of people camped on the pavements the night before in the cold and wet, so that they could catch a glimpse of their new Queen the next day. “The Queen’s subjects remained impressively British in their patient, companionable good nature,” wrote The New York Times. As her subjects waited for the Gold State Coach, wending its way from Buckingham Palace to the Abbey, they sang ‘Singing in the Rain’ and ‘It Ain’t Gonna Rain No More’ with hope and joy at a new Elizabethan era. The crowds would grow on Coronation Day to number three million.

The Abbey, the venue of every coronation since 1066, opened at 6 am to about 8,000 guests. Members of the Royal Family, peers and heads of state from across the world glided in after 8.30 am. Then, at 11.15, came the Queen with a procession of 250 people to verses from Psalm 22.

What did Queen Elizabeth II wear for her Coronation?

She wore a gown and a robe of white satin, made by Norman Hartnell, the fashion designer who had also designed her wedding dress. It was embroidered in gold and silver thread with Commonwealth motifs, from the English rose to Canadian maple leaves. The dress is on display as part of the new exhibition Queen Elizabeth II: Her Life in Style this year.

As glorious as the Queen looked, the gown and robes were so heavy that they got caught in the thick gold and blue carpet as she walked. “At one moment, I was going against the pile of the carpet, and I couldn’t move at all,” she said in a BBC documentary in 2018.

What was so special about Queen Elizabeth II's Coronation?

The Coronation was the first to be televised – and the first event many saw on television. About 27 million people tuned in across the UK, with millions more around the world. About 11 million people tuned in on the radio.

What happens at the Coronation of a British monarch?

The three-hour service, conducted by the Archbishop of Canterbury, comprised six parts: the Recognition, the Oath, the Anointing, the Investiture (including the Crowning), the Enthronement and the Homage.

Every object was rich in significance. The Queen was crowned in St Edward’s Chair, made in 1300 for Edward I and used in every coronation since. During the ceremony, the Queen was handed the four symbols of authority: the Orb, the Sceptre, the Rod of Mercy and the Sovereign’s Ring. The Orb, a globe of gold surrounded by a cross with diamonds and emeralds, shone in the light. The Ring, also known as The Wedding Ring of England, glittered with sapphire and rubies.

At the moment of coronation, the Archbishop placed the St Edward’s Crown, one of the most iconic symbols of royalty, on the Queen’s head. Dating back to 1661, it is made of solid gold and encrusted with 440 precious and semi-precious stones. A cry of “God save the Queen” went up, and gun salutes were fired as crowds outside cheered.

What happened after Queen Elizabeth II's Coronation ceremony?

The Queen held a coronation luncheon for 11 in the Abbey annex, where she and the Duke of Edinburgh ate smoked salmon, sausage rolls and chicken sandwiches. A banquet was held for guests, many from abroad, in the Great Hall of Westminster School, next door to the Abbey. The star dish was coronation chicken, a recipe created for the occasion.

For many people outside, the royal procession back from the Abbey to the Palace meant almost as much as the ceremony. At 7.2km, the route was designed to be seen by as many people as possible and took the participants, in their thousands, two hours. The Queen looked resplendent in her Purple Robe of Estate, embroidered with a cipher of the Queen and a border of olive branches and wheat ears, representing peace and plenty.

The Imperial State Crown

Now she wore the Imperial State Crown, a crown she had also worn at the end of the ceremony. It featured her favourite jewel, the Black Prince’s Ruby, worn by Henry V at the Battle of Agincourt. Both this crown, which the Queen later wore on State Openings of Parliament, and the St Edward’s Crown, were heavy, and despite her grace in the carriage, she endured a feat of strength. As she pointed out in the BBC documentary, “You can’t lean down to read your speech [wearing the crown]... Because if you did, your neck would break, and it would fall off.” To protect her posture, she practised wearing it beforehand. Lady Anne Glenconner, another Maid of Honour, recalled: “She used to wear it when she was writing letters. I think Prince Charles says he remembers going in and seeing her wearing it.”

In 2019, Lady Anne Glenconner published Lady in Waiting, which is available on Amazon. As a Maid of Honour at the Queen's Coronation and former Lady in Waiting to Princess Margaret, this is a fascinating look into behind the scenes of royal life.

The Gold State Coach

On the day, the Queen had the added challenge of wearing the crown in a bumpy carriage - the Gold State Coach- a ride she described as “horrible”. She said, “It’s only sprung on leather, not very comfortable.” Just as she found the crown weighty, the horses had similar troubles with the four-ton coach. “We could only go at a walking pace,” said the Queen. “The horses couldn’t possibly go any faster.”

Observers cheered men from the Royal Navy, the Royal Air Force and the Army in the parade, as well as waving to figureheads from across the Commonwealth. But it was Queen Salote of Tonga who captured their hearts, refusing to put the roof up on her carriage, despite the British rain.

The evening celebrations

That evening, for the finale, the new Queen stood on the balcony of Buckingham Palace and turned on a show of lights along the Mall. She wore a silver evening gown, an ermine stole and a diamond diadem, and smiled at the thousands of people looking back at her. Beside her stood her husband, the Duke of Edinburgh, in a dinner jacket. At 10.30 pm, fireworks lit up the sky. “Portraits etched in lines of fire depicted the Queen, the Duke of Edinburgh and their two children, Prince Charles, Duke of Cornwall, and Princess Anne,” recorded The New York Times. “Each display brought a surge of cheering and applause.”

It was a fitting end to a remarkable day. Recalling her coronation, the Queen later said: “It’s the…beginning of one’s life, really, as a sovereign.” What a life it was.

Many people there on the day have managed to keep and preserve the Queen Elizabeth II Coronation Day book, made to commemorate the day in 1953, and there are copies available to buy on Amazon. As we mark 100 years since she was born, there have been several new biographies of Queen Elizabeth II in the last year as well. The world's most knowledgeable royal biographer, Hugo Vickers, has just released Queen Elizabeth II, which is also available on Amazon, and is a must-read this anniversary year.

item.title: It’s baffled historians for 900 years - everything you need to know about King William II’s mysterious death and what it had to do with his sexuality
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Killed while hunting by an arrow that bounced off a tree, King William II’s death is often believed to have been murder. But by who? We take a closer look at this 900-year-old murder mystery
item.content:encoded:
Killed out hunting by an arrow that bounced off a tree, the mysterious death of King William II is, sadly for him, what he is best remembered for. But who killed him, and can his strange death tell us a bit more about his reign?

We've all heard of his father, the first King William, whose title of 'Conqueror' leaves little to the imagination for what he is best known for. But in the timeline of all the kings and queens of England and Great Britain, poor old King William II, also known as William Rufus, is little studied and unfairly overlooked.

There have been plenty of ridiculous royal deaths in British history, but King William II's is up there. Killed by an arrow meant for a stag - what kind of bad luck do you have to have for that to happen? Someone who wants you off the throne, perhaps? Emma Mason's King Rufus: The Life and Murder of William II of England is available on Amazon and is the best-regarded biography of the Norman king, if you'd like a more in-depth study.

So how did King William II really die, and why might it have been murder?

Who was William II?

The third son of four of William the Conqueror and Matilda of Flanders, William II was King of England from 26 September 1807 until he died in 1100. He is commonly referred to as William Rufus, likely because he had red hair.

William's second eldest brother Richard died in 1075, and his father, King William I, bypassed his eldest brother Robert when choosing his heir, and rewarded his second surviving son, William Rufus, for his loyalty. William's rebellious elder brother Robert was left in Normandy.

Katherine Lack's book, Conqueror's Son: Duke Robert Curthose, Thwarted King is available on Amazon and is a closer study of Duke Robert, known to history as a rebel, but who, Lack argues, was the greatest king England never had.

What was William II like as a king?

Historian Frank Barlow wrote that King William II was a "rumbustious, devil-may-care soldier, without natural dignity or social graces, with no cultivated tastes and little show of conventional religious piety or morality – indeed, according to his critics, addicted to every kind of vice, particularly lust and especially sodomy."

William II never married or had children, which has left many historians, including Barlow, to speculate on his sexuality. William's most frequent advisor and close friend, Ranulf Flambard, who was made Bishop of Durham in 1099, was often implicated as William's sexual partner. There is very little evidence to suggest that either man was homosexual, other than contemporary accounts that they spent lots of time together and William liked to surround himself with 'attractive' men.

Were the accusations of sodomy, therefore, a medieval plot from political or religious opponents to get William off the throne?

What was William II's relationship with the church?

King William II's relationship with the Church was difficult. He often kept bishop positions empty, which meant he could profit from their incomes. This included the position of the Archbishop of Canterbury - the most important church position in England. William claimed these funds until the end of his reign.

William II's military prowess

Despite shortcomings in his relationships with the church, King William II was successful as a military leader and quelled a significant rebellion from the Earl of Northumbria, Robert de Mowbray, during his reign, who was then imprisoned and his lands seized.

William was also successful in defeating numerous Scottish armies, led by King Malcolm III, that invaded England on several occasions. In 1091, Malcolm was forced to offer homage to William and acknowledge him as overlord.

In 1093, William successfully defeated Malcolm at the Battle of Alnwick and Malcolm and his son Edward were both killed. This sent Scotland into a succession dispute, and William was able to assert control.

Neil McGuigan's brand new book on King Malcolm III Máel Coluim III, 'Canmore': An Eleventh-Century Scottish King is available to pre-order on Amazon and will take a closer look at the legendary Scottish king and the world in which he lived.

How did King William II die?

So who killed William II, and why would they have wanted to? Was it someone from the church, angry at his actions, someone who disagreed with his sexuality, or a Scotsman sent to murder the man who killed their king?

William II died on 2 August 1100 whilst on a hunting trip in the New Forest, probably near Brockenhurst. He was on the trip with his younger brother Henry, the future King Henry I, and numerous other knights.

The earliest written record we have of his death was in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicles (which you can order for yourself on Amazon), which writes that the king was 'shot by an arrow by one of his own men.' Supposedly, the arrow, meant for a stag, bounced off a tree and pierced King William II's chest and lungs, killing him.

Later writers mention the name of the killer as nobleman Sir Walter Tirel, but we'll of course never know if this is true.

Tirel did, however, flee to France following the incident - was this an admission of guilt? But the question remains, why would he have wanted to kill William?

What, to me, seems even more suspicious is that William's brother Henry raced to be crowned king, just three days after his brother's death, on 5 August 1100 in Westminster Abbey. Was it an assassination attempt so he could be king?

Of course, frustratingly, we'll never know. What we do know is that hunting was a very dangerous sport, and fatal accidents occurred pretty regularly. William's elder brother Richard had also died in a hunting accident.
