Tirel's fleeing to France could have been out of fear for the fact that he had just killed the king of England, even if it was accidentally.

We should remember, too, that England was an extremely pious country at the time. The murder of one's brother was a heinous crime that would send the killer to hell, future king or not. Would Tirel or Henry have risked their faith to get rid of William?

Accident or not, just like the best mysteries in British history, we'll never know the true story, though I suspect historians will debate forever more the murder of King William II.

Perhaps it was karma for a murderous act, but William's successor wasn't blessed with good luck in his reign either. After dying from eating too many fish (yes, really), Henry I's remains are supposedly now buried under a car park in Reading after the cathedral they were in was destroyed during the Dissolution of the Monasteries. I'd recommend Judith Green's biography of the unfortunate king, Henry I: King of England and Duke of Normandy, which is available on Amazon, if you'd like to know more.

item.title: Issue 146 of Scotland is here! Get the March/April 2026 issue now!
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey, Editor
item.description: In this issue, we explore the historic city of Stirling, often known as ‘Edinburgh in miniature’
item.content:encoded:
This issue feels a little like the ‘Stirling’ issue, as not only was the city that some like to call ‘Edinburgh in miniature’ the scene of our national hero William Wallace’s most rousing victory, but it also signifies the steep ascent of one of Scotland’s most powerful families: the Stewarts.

In Stewart Strongholds (p20), the second in the series of Clan Trails by Graeme Johncock, we go on a tour of the places associated with the family as they rose to become Scotland’s longest-running royal dynasty. Meanwhile in The Man Behind the Myth (p45), history writer Kirsten Henton, surmises what we know of the real Braveheart, William Wallace, and assesses why he still holds such a precious place in Scottish hearts.

Elsewhere this issue, travel writer Mark Rowe visits the home region of our beloved Notes from the Isles columnist and reports back on a vastly underrated peninsula in The Black Isle (p58). Then, I travel a little further north to Dornoch for a truly breathtaking experience that I still haven’t fully recovered from and which you can read about in Above and Beyond (p38). There’s a little clue about what I get up to in the photo above.

For me, though, the richest travel experiences in Scotland always include some element of our deep-rooted culture, and so I implore you to turn to page 64 and read Songs of the Sea. In this piece,Joss Cameron discusses her lifelong love affair with sea shanties and how they can help us connect with home wherever we are, as well as create a sense of togetherness in times of turbulence. In Scotland, singing and storytelling are intertwined, so it’s worth seeking her out on your next visit, or even having a little listen at home. Enjoy the issue.

Get your copy of the issue here!

item.title: Who was Macbeth written for? This is the dark and troubled history behind Shakespeare’s cursed play, the real witches that inspired it and a strange connection to the Gunpowder plot
item.dc:creator: Paul Stafford, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: It’s unknown if Shakespeare ever travelled to Scotland, but perhaps his decision to set Macbeth here was influenced by some higher power? Did he really write his Scottish play for King James VI and I?
item.content:encoded:
Macbeth was Shakespeare’s only play set in Scotland. Despite there being no concrete evidence to suggest the Bard ever travelled north of the border, it became known as ‘the Scottish play' - but who was Macbeth written for?

William Shakespeare's play depicts the twisted rise of a noble soldier, Macbeth, to the Scottish throne after he murders King Duncan – a grisly act that triggers a trail of bloody deeds until Macbeth is killed by Macduff, and Duncan’s son Malcolm is declared king.

One theory is that Shakespeare wrote Macbeth for King James I and VI. In order to understand this theory, we need to understand the king himself. Perhaps King James' most famous legacy is his Daemonologie, which is available on Amazon in its original form. His fear and obsession with witchcraft and magic manifested itself into this book, which is a fascinating insight into his mind.

First, lets set the scene.

When was Macbeth set?

Mist hangs over the wintry River Tay, obscuring an ancient oak tree whose aged branches, thick like the arms of a caber-tosser, are propped up by crutches. It is the same kind of weather that envelops the scenes of Macbeth as the ramifications of his dishonourable deeds slowly consume the king.

This oak – known as the Birnam Oak – is the sole survivor of its species from the original wood that once lined the riverbank just outside Birnam. Aged over 500 years old, it is the only tree remaining that would have stood when William Shakespeare was immortalising this very wood with his quill.

“Macbeth shall never vanquished be, until Great Birnan (sic) Wood to High Dunsinane Hill Shall come against him” (Act IV, Scene I)

“You could argue that Shakespeare is trying to make Scotland less of a knowable space,” says Professor John Jowett, Deputy Director of the Shakespeare Institute at the University of Birmingham. “In the opening scenes, Scotland is a place under attack from all directions, but often we don’t know where we are [physically] in the play.”

Even if he were writing blind, Shakespeare stumbled onto a convenient truth. At the time the play is set (the early 11th century), kings of Scotland had no official seat, instead moving between sites across the country, to make their whereabouts unknowable to their assorted foes. The play’s geographical uncertainty also creates a foggy atmosphere, laced with dread and foreboding, further clouded by paranoia and guilt as the play progresses.

Where is Macbeth set in Scotland, and how did Shakespeare know about it?

Dunsinane Hill and Birnam Wood emerge later as key locations around which much of the action becomes focused, thanks to the witches’ prophecy. This is where Shakespeare reveals himself to be far more aware of Scotland than we might presume.

As Professor Jowett notes, the two locations are “symbolically strategic. Looking north [from Dunsinane Hill] you’re looking right at the Highland Boundary Fault and the Tay gorge beyond Birnam, where the old way into the Highlands would have run.”

Effectively, the Scottish crown’s salvation comes from the direction of the Highlands. Even though Malcolm enlists the help of the English to overthrow Macbeth, he underlines that Macbeth’s defeat was a Scottish victory.

As the crow flies, the Birnam Oak is 13 miles from Dunsinane Hill. A muddy track leads up Birnam Hill, away from the Birnam Oak’s fixed, earth-bound root. Either side of the path, birch trunks mottled white and grey stand like emaciated sentries. From the hill’s peak you can see the silver gleam of the River Tay cutting a languorous, meandering course southwest towards Perth, circumventing the Sidlaw Hills, which appear russet, like an old blood stain in early December. All but one, that is.

Dunsinane Hill is an emerald rebel, with thick grass growing over the contoured outlines of its battlements.

Dunsinane’s peak is unnaturally flat, betraying the presence of a former settlement, but also of a botched 19th-century dig that revealed little information. Here and there some mammal or other has dug at earth, revealing bits of masonry. The peak affords excellent panoramas of Dundee, Fife, Perth and indeed back north. Across the Tay stands Birnam Wood and the Highlands, exactly what Macbeth, King of Scots, would have seen from his battlements.

The only known maps of the area that Shakespeare could have consulted at the time were by Timothy Pont and date to the turn of the 17th century. Although ‘Dounsinnan Hill’ is mapped, there’s no indication of a fort or ruin. There is no mention of Birnam Wood either.

Holinshed’s Chronicles were the primary historical source available to Shakespeare for most of his historical plays. But the sections on Scotland are apocryphal in places, translated from John Bellenden’s original Scots version. You can actually read the original 16th-century versions of Holinshed’s Chronicles from Amazon.

Shakespeare then took further creative licence with them. But perhaps he had somebody else feeding him information?

What was the relationship between King James and Shakespeare?

“King James comes to the English throne in 1603,” explains Dr Nicola Royan, an expert in early Scottish literature, as well as kingship and national identity, at the University of Nottingham.

“He understood kingship from the inside and he also understood the power of theatre,” she says.

Having ruled as King James VI of Scotland since 1567, James acceded to the English throne as King James I upon the death of Queen Elizabeth I.

James was quick to adopt Shakespeare’s theatre company, which changed their name from the ‘Lord Chamberlain’s Men’ to the ‘King’s Men’. And one could certainly argue that if it weren’t for King James, there may not have been a Scottish play at all.

“Importantly, the play is first performed in 1606, which is just post-gunpowder plot,” says Royan.

This context allows us to see Macbeth in a whole new light. It’s not only a play warning us about the corruption of power, but an allegory aimed at those who may have sought to remove King James I from the English throne.

“Macbeth is a play anxious about inheritance and expresses confidence in divine fate,” explains Royan, “Shakespeare fillets down the true story. He does away with years of good rule by Macbeth.”

This helps explain why Shakespeare took liberties with Scottish history in the first place. The Gunpowder Plot of 1605, in which Guy Fawkes et al attempted to blow up the Houses of Parliament, was one of many attempts on King James’s life. Shakespeare may have been hinting that James’s progeny would accede to the throne regardless, which was indeed what eventually happened with Charles I in 1625.

Did Shakespeare write Macbeth for King James I?

One other aspect of Macbeth may have been designed to please the king and Scottish audiences. In Holinshed’s Chronicles, Macbeth is said to have met “nymphs or faeries” on the moors. King James, however, was obsessed with hunting witches at the time, proving a cruel and murderous barb to his own reign. In 1597, Daemonologie, concerning itself with all things double, double, toil and trouble.

It’s highly probable that Shakespeare read this text, giving him added impetus to open Macbeth with the three witches, whom he names the ‘weird sisters’. Calling them weird sisters was merely an extension of the Bard’s love for double entendre, for in Scots the word ‘weird’ means fate, fortune, or destiny, again nodding to the idea of divine fate, as well as their role in prophesying Macbeth’s own rise and fall.

Some people don’t like the idea of Macbeth being called the Scottish play, given that it was written by an Englishman. But given the lack of detailed history or cartography available at the time, that we know of, the Bard’s work seems even more impressive.

Whether King James advised the Bard on the play, we’ll likely never know. But when you consider Shakespeare’s love for double entendre and hidden meaning, we can really begin to appreciate that Macbeth offers genuine, indirect historical insight and value to our understanding of Scotland, even if we’re none the wiser about the real Macbeth, King of Scots, by the final curtain.

If you haven't read Macbeth, where have you been? The Penguin version is available on Amazon and is well worth reading properly, even if you haven't read Shakespeare since school. If this inspires you, I'd also recommend the brilliant crime writer Val McDermid's Queen Macbeth, which is also available on Amazon, and which retells the story of the first queen of Scotland and busts the myth of the murderous queen that Shakespeare created.

item.title: Mad about History - Five bizarre things you definitely never knew about life in a medieval castle, and what films and TV get totally wrong about living in the Middle Ages
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: I visited the magnificent Bodiam Castle in Sussex and uncovered some truths and busted some myths about what life was really like inside a medieval castle
item.content:encoded:
Knights, princesses, banquets, maybe a dragon or two - the image we get of life in a medieval castle from films and TV is, understandably, heavily romanticised. But then it seems odd that despite all the glamour and chivalry of medieval life on the silver screen, castles are always pictured as cold, dark and with bare stone walls, when this is only partly true.

We are unbelievably lucky in this country to have so many incredible British castles still standing, and whether in ruin or preserved, to be able to walk around these buildings and experience their scale and grandeur in person is history at its finest.

But of course, what we see now is only a snapshot, or often just the bricks and mortar of what the castle would have looked like in its heyday and visits to castles require a lot of imagination, and a good foundation of knowledge about medieval life to get the full picture. Joseph and Frances Gies' book Life in a Medieval Castle: The Definitive Social History and Reference on Daily Life in the Middle Ages is available on Amazon and will give you a groundwork on medieval life and its sights, sounds and often pretty pungent smells.

For the next time you visit one of Britain's over 4,000 castles, here are 5 things about life in a medieval castle you definitely never knew to regale your friends with.

View this post on Instagram

1. Staircases were weapons

The stone spiral staircase is a regular feature of any great castle, but do you know why they were built to spiral, or why they all go in one direction?

Many historians believe it was for defence purposes, which would make sense, seeing as the central role of castles and their impregnable walls was for defence. If enemy attackers breached the walls and attempted battle on the stairs, fighting would be awkward and difficult because the weapons would be used in their right hands.

Right-handed attackers would therefore collide with the central pillar of the spiral stairs, while the defenders would have the advantage of higher ground.

2. Toilets were also wardrobes

Medieval castles had small toilets built into their walls called garderobes. Appearing around the 12th century, they were 'privy' chambers that contained chutes that discharged waste directly outside to the moat, or a river or pit, which would reduce the toilet-odour inside the castle.

The term 'garderobe' comes from the French for wardrobe, as it is believed that the strong ammonia fumes from the waste would kill moths in the clothes stored nearby.

3. Medieval Castle walls would have been intensely colourful

Despite what we see in films and TV shows where medieval castles appear cold, dark and bare-stoned inside (think the BBC's King and Conqueror, which seems to have had all the colour drained out of it), medieval castle walls would have been extremely colourful and covered in luxurious tapestries and tiles.

The bare stone walls that we see today are just the shell of what would have been there in the castle's heyday. This image of medieval life and cold, bare stone walls has only contributed to our idea of the Middle Ages as the Dark Ages, when in fact they were full of colour, art, luxury and culture.

Castles like Dover Castle are brilliant for demonstrating this, as it features the vibrantly decorated 12th-century Tower, recreated to show how King Henry II's court would have lived.

4. Huge castle fireplaces were totally inefficient

Roasted face, frozen back syndrome was a real thing. The enormous fireplaces you see in medieval castles today were totally inefficient for keeping out the cold in drafty castles without glass windows.

People would sit with their fronts extremely close to a roaring hearth, but their backs would be cold thanks to the lack of insulation or proper heat circulation in the rest of the castle.

5. Medieval castles didn't have sewage systems

Of course, medieval castles did not have modern sewage systems where waste could be neatly swept away without anyone seeing or smelling it. Instead, waste from everyone living in the castle (which could be over a hundred) was dropped into the moat below, or if a castle didn't have a moat, a cesspit. You can only imagine the stench in the summer.

From peasant life to the customs of kings and queens, and plagues, witches, battles and everything in between, the Middle Ages are endlessly fascinating. If you'd like to know more, I recommend Ian Mortimer's The Time Traveller's Guide to Medieval England: A Handbook for Visitors to the Fourteenth Century, which is available on Amazon - an entertaining and informative guide to this much misrepresented period of our history.

item.title: How long were the Romans in Britain, and why did they come in the first place? This is the epic story of the Roman invasion in Britain and why we still feel its effects today
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor, Penelope Rance
item.description: Their lasting effect on Britain is still visible to this day. From ancient forts, roads and walls, to villas, spas and even language and food, this is the incredible legacy of the Romans in Britain
item.content:encoded:
They came, they conquered, and their lasting effect on Britain is still visible today. But how long were the Romans in Britain?

Over nearly 400 years, from the Claudian invasion in AD 43 to their final withdrawal in AD 410, the Romans ruled Britain, and their legacy is still mightily impressive, with many ancient forts, roads and walls, villas, palaces and spas still standing.

Technology, architecture, language, government, town planning – even a sense of national identity. The depth of the Roman influence on Britain was unmatched by that of any of the invading forces that followed them. For an in-depth study, Romano-British archaeologist Guy de la Bédoyère's brilliant Roman Britain: A New History is available on Amazon and will talk you through the Roman invasion of Britain and how society functioned under Roman rule.

Why were the Romans so successful in Britain, and what was their lasting legacy?

The heirs to the Roman ideal – Saxons, Danes, Normans, Plantagenets, Tudors, Georgians and Victorians – all tried to establish Britain as part of a wider empire, drawing on the example set by those first imperial overlords, the Romans.

When and why did the Romans invade Britain?

Their efforts to regain the military glory and sheen of civilisation shaped the history of these islands, which took their first steps towards unity in 43AD. For more than anything else, the Romans left Britannia with the sense of what it is to be British, in the oldest meaning of the word. Thus, the Welsh claim direct descent from the original Britons, while the Scottish remain fiercely proud of the failure of Rome to subdue the Picts in the north.

“We don’t know what the tribes called themselves – we only know what the Romans called them,” says Christine Medlock, director of Fishbourne Roman Palace. “They created a Celtic empire by naming them. The Romans put an identity onto the locals. We only know about them through their writings.”

If you'd like to know more about Fishbourne, Barry Cunliffe's thorough study of the largest remaining Roman villa in the UK, Fishbourne Roman Palace, is available from Amazon.

But the relationship was by no means all give and no take. Welsh tin and Cornish gold provided valuable trade opportunities, and British jet, pearls, wool and hunting dogs were all prized. The islands provided soldiers for the legions, slaves for the cities, an expansion of the Empire to the end of the known world, and a proving ground for future Emperors. Julius Caesar and Claudius both built their power bases on popular campaigns in the islands at the edge of the western sea. In many ways, Rome’s power was built on British soil.

The Roman influence in Britain

Roman influence began in 55BC with Julius Caesar’s first, ineffectual invasion. After successes in Gaul, he pushed on for a final victory, ill-prepared and undermanned. He came, he saw, and he departed, not even leaving a garrison behind him. The Senate was impressed, but the natives were not. The following year, he tried again and succeeded in coercing some tribal leaders into owing tribute to Rome.

For the next century, the relationship was one largely based on trade and diplomacy. Britain did not become a part of the Empire until another man needed a military victory to shore up his power – Claudius sent Aulus Plautius to subdue Britain for him. Victories on the River Medway at Richborough and on the Thames, as far as Camulodunum (Colchester), brought the south of the islands into the Empire, while the far north was never conquered. Over the next 30 years, the rest of Britannia was brought under Roman control.

As the invaders marched through the kingdoms of the Celts, they founded forts, built roads, and established cities and palaces. The routes of the famously straight roads which cut their way across hills and valleys throughout Britain can still be traced today. The mighty north-south thoroughfare of Watling Street, now partly followed by the M1 motorway, was also used by Boudicca, Queen of the Iceni, to descend on Londinium (London) and Verulamium (St Albans) in 61AD.

“Roads were built by the army as a means of easing passage and were maintained by the local government,” says Emma Stuart, outreach officer at the Corinium Museum, Cirencester.

“In Cirencester, the second largest administrative centre in Britain, three major Roman roads converged – Ermine Street, Akeman Street and the Fosse Way.” At Pickering in Yorkshire you can still stand on the cobbles of Wheedale Roman Road.

The Roman military legacy in Britain

Hadrian's Wall

The military legacy of the Romans is one of the most apparent. They brought with them new weapons, armour and tactics the like of which the native tribes had never seen. Hadrian’s Wall, begun in 122AD following the Emperor’s grand tour of Europe and finished just six years later, was raised to defend against the marauding Picts and Scots.

Although the Romans pushed farther north to Falkirk, where remains of the Antonine Wall can still be seen, Hadrian’s Wall became the effective border of the Empire, and one of the greatest pieces of military architecture in the Empire.

Roman towns in Britain

Heavily garrisoned as it was, Britain was a natural training ground for the Empire’s leading men. Its governors went on to become emperors, and it was a power base for a host of rebels. It also became home to many of those who served there – Sarmatians, Thracians, Batavians. As men from the garrisons retired, they settled near the forts, and cities grew up around their farmsteads. These can be identified today by their names – the suffixes ‘chester’, ‘caster’ and ‘ciester’ all signify Roman origins.

“Towns across Britain also ensured Roman culture was adopted by the indigenous British peoples,” says Emma Stuart. “Public buildings such as the amphitheatre and baths gave the locals a place to spectate, exercise, relax and socialise.”

“If you lived in the Roman towns, you followed the Roman way of life, became Romanised and desired to become a Roman citizen,” says Jenny Hall, Department of Early London History and Collections at the Museum of London. ”There was a real mix of nationalities and religious beliefs all blending into a cultural melting pot.”

Roman architecture in Britain

Bignor Roman Villa

In their architecture, the Romans also brought their culture to bear on the native populace. The finest example of domestic living arrangements can be found at Bignor Roman Villa in West Sussex. Set on Stane Street, which once linked Novoimagnus Regnorum (Chichester) to London, it was composed of more than 70 buildings spread across four acres. Here you will find the most complete floor mosaics in Britain.

Fishbourne Roman Palace

Fishbourne Roman Palace near Chichester reveals much about how the elite lived, with its underfloor heating system, baths, landscaped gardens and elegant decoration. “These were the first gardens in Britain,” says Christine Medlock. The Romans introduced a different concept of the use of land. They’ve left behind a legacy in the beautiful mosaics and painted wall tiles.”

Vindolanda

And with living conditions came lifestyle. Wooden tablets discovered in garbage pits at the fort of Vindolanda on Hadrian’s Wall – the oldest written record found in the country – show that the Romans established the lifestyle of the idle rich centuries before the Georgians made it their own.

Housesteads

Among the tablets are letters between the camp commander’s wife, Sulpicia Lepidnia and her friend Claudia Severa, wife of the commander at Housesteads. They reveal a level of boredom and loneliness that has been the lot of military wives down the centuries, along with descriptions of entertaining local dignitaries and travellers.

The Roman Baths, Bath

The Georgians also followed in Roman footsteps to Bath. The springs at Aquae Sulis were exploited for their healing powers 1,500 years before they became the favoured watering place of Regency bucks and belles. Our other waterworks also owe a debt to the Empire. Not until the Victorians was such an effective system of sewers again built in the country.

Roman food in Britain

The Romans also gave Britain a new diet. “Their main legacy is in the foods they introduced and cultivated here,” says Jenny Hall. “They introduced such things as carrots, cucumbers, plums, cherries and better varieties of apples and pears, which have remained staple parts of the British diet.”

Their taste buds demanded the flavours of home, and what they couldn’t find on the native shores, they imported. Rabbits, chickens, sweet chestnuts and Roman snails were also brought in to grace their tables. Mediterranean herbs were planted and vineyards established, which today produce delicious English wine.

The influence of the Roman language in Britain

And while English is not a Latinate language, we have inherited much of those early invaders’ vocabulary – part of what makes English such a versatile, descriptive language is it absorption and effortless combination of occupying tongues. Scandinavian, Germanic and Norman roots blend with Latin in our speech.

The influence of Roman religious beliefs in Britain

Although the early Roman rulers did little to impose their religious beliefs on the native pagans, religion would later form an important part of their legacy. Pantheistic in structure, the Roman belief system merely absorbed the local deities, morphing them into their closest counterpart. All this changed, at least officially, when Constantine, crowned Emperor at York, adopted the Christian religion.

Seeing the one god as a powerful force, he scrawled the Chi-Rho symbol on his soldiers’ shields at the Battle of Milvian Bridge in 312AD, and won a massive victory. In thanks, he declared Christianity the central religion of the Empire, establishing the Nicene Creed of the Catholic Church, making Britain the birthplace of Roman Catholicism.

For 400 years, Britain played an important role as part of the Empire. However, Barbarian attacks on the capital had weakened the Empire, and troops were pulled out of the west to help defend it. Constantine III led the legions into Gaul, leaving the Britons to face Saxon attacks alone. In 410, the remaining Romanised elite appealed to the Emperor Honorius for help. He responded that they should “look to their own defences”.

The era of Roman Britain had ended.

Britain's Roman legacy can be spotted all over the country, and often in places you might not expect. Did you know there was a large section of Roman wall still standing in the City of London, for example? Richard Hingley and Christina Unwin's Roman Britain in Twenty Towns: A Visitor's Guide is your guide to where to find these amazing sites and their history, written by experts in their field.

item.title: These are the 10 best Roman sites in Britain: from hidden temples dedicated to Roman goddesses to palaces, spas and the remains of Roman Britain’s military legacy
item.dc:creator: Sally Hales, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Fancy a Roman holiday? Here, we round up 10 of the best Roman sites around the country where you can explore the incredible legacy of the Roman empire in Britain.
item.content:encoded:
A farmhouse owner laying cables in Wiltshire recently uncovered the largest Roman villa ever found in UK, and one of the most important Roman sites in Britain ever discovered. Sadly, with conservation work just getting started, it may be many years before the public is able to visit the amazing remains.

And although we might not all be lucky enough to find a villa in our gardens, the legacy of the Romans in Britain can still be felt all around us.

The suffixes ‘chester’, ‘caster’ and ‘ciester’ on the names of our town and cities all signify Roman origins, while many of the country’s important Roman sites have been conserved and are open to the public. There are hundreds of Roman sites still visible in Britain, and The Ordnance Survey have produced a wonderful map of Roman Britain, which is available on Amazon so you can plan your own adventure to discover these sites for yourselves.

If we had to choose our favourites, here is our guide to Britain's best Roman spots that should be on your itinerary:

1. The Roman Baths, Bath

One of the best-preserved Roman sites in the world, the ancient thermal spa at Aquae Sulis, now modern-day Bath, includes the Sacred Spring, the Roman Bath House and the Roman Temple.

The Romans constructed a complex of bathhouses above Bath’s three natural hot springs. The temple was constructed in 60-70 AD, and the bathing complex was gradually built up over the next 300 years.

The baths’ museum collection also contains thousands of archaeological finds from pre-Roman and Roman Britain.

You can book a walking tour of the Roman Baths via TripAdvisor here.

2. Hadrian’s Wall

For around three centuries, Hadrian’s Wall in Northumberland was a vibrant, multi-cultural northern frontier of the Roman Empire sprawling almost 80 miles coast-to-coast.

Built by a force of 15,000 men in under six years, it was an astounding achievement with milecastles, barracks, ramparts and forts.

Today, you can explore the 84-mile National Trail that follows this UNESCO World Heritage Site, from Tyneside to the Solway Estuary in Cumbria.

3. Houseteads Roman Fort, Hadrian’s Wall

Housesteads is the most complete Roman fort in Britain, set high on a dramatic escarpment on Hadrian’s Wall World Heritage Site.

Today, Housesteads lies in an empty landscape, but in Roman times there was a busy garrison of 800 men and, outside the fort walls, a settlement of civilians and traders. The visible remains include four imposing gates, and a complete line of curtain wall and interval towers.

Wander the barrack blocks and the hospital. Peer into the oldest toilets you’ll ever see, and admire the stunning panoramic views from this ancient fortress.

4. London Museum, London

The Romans built the city where London now stands, bridging the Thames and creating Londinium. From around AD 50 to 410, this was the largest city in Britannia and a vital international port.

At the London Museum, which will reopen in its new Smithfield location in 2026, discover how Londinium came to life and what daily life was like in the city 2,000 years ago by exploring the collection of more than 47,000 objects, many of which were recovered during building works in the City of London.

While we wait for the museum to reopen, Simon Webb's brilliant Roman London is available on Amazon and is the ultimate guide to anyone wanting to explore London's Roman legacy.

5. Corinium Museum, Cirencester

View this post on Instagram

The Corinium Museum has one of the finest and most extensive Roman collections in the country. Corinium, Cirencester, was the second largest Roman town after London and the major administration centre for south-western Britain.

The town was the tribal capital and administrative centre or civitas for the Dobunni, the pre-Roman local tribe, and it has been estimated that it had a population of between 10,000 and 20,000, which compares to modern-day Cirencester’s population of around 18,000.

The museum’s collections of mosaics, tombstones and sculpture reflect Cirencester’s importance.

6. Fishbourne Roman Palace, Chichester, West Sussex

Fishbourne Roman Palace is the largest Roman home in Britain. Today you can visit the excavated palace in Chichester – built in the 1st century AD – to imagine the luxury that would once have surrounded the proud owner of this lavish residence. Stroll around the recreated Roman gardens – the earliest gardens found anywhere in the country – and enjoy the largest collection of mosaics in situ in the UK.

7. Bignor Roman Villa, Pulborough, West Sussex

Bignor Roman Villa is a large excavated Roman courtyard villa with world-class mosaic floors. At the site, you can walk among the remains and appreciate the pride and craftsmanship that created exquisite mosaic floors featuring such wonders as Venus and the reluctant Cherub Gladiators, Ganymede being carried off by the Eagle and the Four seasons.

To get a sense of the scale of the site that employed so many people, pay a visit to the North corridor. With only one third exposed, it is still the longest on display in the country, which, in full, stretches the length of three tennis courts

8. Roman Legion Museum, Caerleon, Newport, South Wales

View this post on Instagram

The name Caerleon is derived from the Welsh for “fortress of the legion”, and the variety of remains on view is unparalleled within these isles. Wales was the furthest outpost of the empire. In AD 75, the Romans built a fortress at Caerleon that would guard the region for over 200 years.

The National Roman Legion Museum is one of three Roman sites in Caerleon, where you can step back in time and explore life in a far-flung outpost of the empire, along with the Roman Baths museum and the open-air ruins of the amphitheatre and barrack.

9. Chedworth, Yanworth, near Cirencester, Gloucestershire

Chedworth is a Romano-British villa in a lovely valley setting, with intricate mosaics and a local museum. Evidence for the first stone structure at Chedworth dates to the 2nd century AD and, over the next two centuries, the villa was extended and improved, reaching its heyday in the 4th-century AD, between 360 and 380 AD. During this time, Chedworth Roman Villa was a place of wealth, luxury and comfort.

Now in the trust of the National Trust, it was discovered by accident by a gamekeeper in 1864 and is one of the largest villa sites in the UK.

10. Reculver Roman Fort, Herne Bay, East Kent

The remains of the important Roman ‘Saxon Shore’ fort, Reculver Fort, are now in the care of English Heritage.

During the 1st and 2nd centuries AD, a Roman settlement grew up at Reculver, probably around a harbour.  In the early 3rd century, a fort was built – one of the very earliest of the forts of the Saxon Shore, built against Saxon raids. The southern half of the fort survives as ruined walls and earthworks.

Roman Britain is fascinating, and studying it is seemingly endless - should you focus on architecture, language, food, culture or military prowess? For adults and young learners, the best beginner's guides to any histories are always, always Terry Deary's Horrible Histories books. The Rotten Romans is available on Amazon and will tell you everything you need to know about the Romans and their incredible legacy.

item.title: The May/June 2026 issue of BRITAIN is out now!
item.dc:creator: Natasha Foges, Editor
item.description: This issue we explore the myth’s and legends of Northern Ireland’s Causeway Coast
item.content:encoded:
Perusing the pages of this new issue before it goes to press, I was struck by just how much water runs through it. No surprise for an island nation, perhaps, but our coastline, all 19,000 miles of it, varies enormously in character.

Our cover star, Northern Ireland’s Causeway Coast, is wild and romantic: a place steeped in myth and legend (p72). On Cornwall’s south coast, meanwhile, hidden coves nestle beside a succession of glorious gardens, where exotic blooms and champion trees thrive in the salty Atlantic air (p14). Then there’s the quintessential Victorian seaside town of Broadstairs (p61), once a favourite retreat of Charles Dickens.

Elsewhere, we speak to the poet Roger McGough about a new initiative touring coastal towns this summer, which will be spreading the word – quite literally – with a mobile poetry library (p80). And we step aboard Britannia, Queen Elizabeth II’s beloved yacht, which once carried her around Scotland’s Western Isles every summer, and is now a must-see attraction in Edinburgh (p52).

Back on terra firma, we join the red-coated Chelsea Pensioners on parade (p41), step back in time to visit the settings of our favourite children’s books (p25) and explore some of Britain’s most historic villages, each of which has shaped the nation in its own way (p32). Enjoy the issue!

Plus you could be in with chance of winning a luxury stay at Ballygally Castle in Northern Ireland!

Get your copy here!

item.title: Win a stay at Ballygally Castle on the Causeway Coast
item.dc:creator: Natasha Foges, Editor
item.description: This is your chance to win a luxurious stay at the spectacular Ballygally Castle on the Antrim coast in Northern Ireland for you and a guest
item.content:encoded:
Located on the spectacular Antrim coast, Ballygally Castle is rich in history and full of charm. Dating back to 1625, the castle celebrated its 400-year anniversary in 2025 and is the only 17th-century building still used as a residence in Northern Ireland today.

One of the largest hotels on the Causeway Coastal Route, the hotel has 54 bedrooms, full of character, some with original beamed ceilings, while others enjoy panoramic views of the Irish Sea and Glens of Antrim. One lucky reader and their guest will win a two-night stay at this beautiful hotel, enjoying a prime position on the coast, with sweeping Irish Sea views, a beautifully appointed walled garden, and direct access to the golden sands of Ballygally Bay for cold dips or long walks.

On one night of their stay, our winners will enjoy dinner at the Garden Restaurant, set within the original 17th-century walls. The restaurant serves up a range of delicious bistro classics, using the freshest local produce, from Irish seafood chowder to slow-cooked Irish beef with colcannon mash.

There’s plenty to explore near the hotel, including Carrickfergus Castle, a Norman stronghold, and the gardens and woodland of Carnfunnock Country Park. Best of all, the hotel is just down the coast from the Giant’s Causeway, which celebrates its 40th anniversary as a UNESCO World Heritage Site this year.

A number of celebrated golf courses, including Cairndhu and Royal Portrush, are within easy reach of the hotel, while Bushmills Distillery offers tours of Northern Ireland’s oldest working whiskey distillery. With a stay at this landmark hotel, these and other gems of the Causeway Coast are right on your doorstep.

ballygallycastlehotel.com

Terms and conditions: Closing date for entries is 23.59 GMT on 5 October 2026, and the prize must be taken by 4 October 2027. The prize is a two-night stay for two people sharing a room, with breakfast on each morning and dinner on one evening, including a bottle of wine. The prize is subject to availability, is not transferable, and does not include travel, personal expenditure or incidental costs. The competition is open to entrants in the UK, the US and Canada only.

To be in with a chance of winning this fantastic prize, simply fill out the form below:

View this article on our website to enter

item.title: 9 breathtaking spring gardens in full bloom you absolutely must explore this Bank Holiday weekend
item.dc:creator: Flora Hughes-Onslow, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Britain’s stately home gardens are at their loveliest in the springtime, so we’ve selected 9 of our favourites for a perfect flower-filled spring day out this weekend
item.content:encoded:
The clocks have gone back, the lambs are bleating, and the wild garlic is out in full bloom. Spring is, finally, here, and now is the perfect time to experience nature coming back to life.

The sheer number of beautiful stately home gardens in Britain means there are countless blissful gardens and seasonal events to enjoy all year round, but the spring gardens are definitely my firm favourite.

Many of these gardens are, by no surprise, owned and managed by the National Trust, and their lovely book, Inspirational Gardens Through the Seasons, is available on Amazon and will guide you through the horticultural highlights of their properties throughout the year.

With Easter weekend upon us and a stretch of school holidays and Bank Holidays ahead, I've put together a guide to my favourite spring gardens to visit, which would all make for a lovely day out this weekend, and throughout the spring.

1. Bodnant Garden, Conwy

Created by five generations of one family, the 80-acre Bodnant Garden in Conwy has wonderful views of Snowdonia, as well as grand terraced lawns, a renowned collection of rhododendrons and a wonderfully romantic waterfall. In spring, don’t miss the Dell; hidden deep within a wooded valley, with the river Hiraethlyn chattering through.

Spring is also the perfect season to visit the laburnum arch, which will be in full bloom by late May.

2. Blickling Estate, Norfolk

Blickling Estate is perfect to visit in springtime, when you can follow the winding paths through the great wood and the carpet of English bluebells.

At the heart of the garden lies one of England’s great Jacobean houses, the birthplace of Anne Boleyn, absolutely bursting with important history - and one or two ghosts.

Explore fragrant beds of the parterre and hellebores, daffodils, azaleas, rhododendron, wisteria and peonies, and there are about 500 acres of parkland and woods to discover, and a secret garden to try to find.

3. Sissinghurst Castle Garden, Kent

The creation of English novelist and poet Vita Sackville-West and her husband Harold, Sissinghurst Castle Gardens are ideal for a springtime wander. Enjoy the rich, warm colours of the Cottage Garden.

Long beds of tulips, fritillaries and hyacinths are marked out by an avenue of pleached limes, scattered by generous terracotta pots, every inch bursting with colour for about four weeks. Early spring sees pink chinodoxias, scillas, and white anemones come into bloom, looked over proudly by a Magnolia salicifolia and a big, bold mauve rhododendron.

4. Hidcote, Gloucestershire

A diverse garden, Hidcote features plants from all over the world. British garden designer and plantsman Lawrence Johnston owned, designed and was passionate about the gardens. He went to endless trouble and expense to find unusual varieties that would bring colour, scent, shape and texture to the garden.

The garden is divided into a series of ‘outdoor rooms’, each with its own character. Enjoy drifts of narcissus and later aquilegias and Welsh poppies in the Pillar Garden, and the blossom-filled orchard with emerging wild flowers.

5. Stourhead, Wiltshire

A world-famous landscape garden with a lake as its centrepiece, reflecting classical temples, mystical grottoes and rare trees. Stourhead is set amongst immaculate lawns and parklands filled with colour throughout the spring months, with a succession of azaleas, magnolias and rhododendrons bursting into flower.

6. Clumber Park, Nottinghamshire

With 3,800 acres of parkland and gardens, peaceful woodlands and a magnificent lake, Clumber Park is glorious to visit in spring when the woodland is alive with birdsong and bluebells.

The beautiful walled kitchen garden is famous for growing hundreds of varieties of fruit, vegetables, herbs, and has the biggest collection of culinary rhubarbs in the country. Clumber Park also boasts the longest avenue of double lime trees in Europe. Planted around 1840 to line one of the main entrances into the Park, the majestic Limetree Avenue is over two miles long.

7. Sizergh Castle, Cumbria

Tucked away outside of Kendal, Sizergh Castle has beautiful spring gardens and 1600 acres of estate to explore. During spring, see the brightly coloured tulips on the top terrace, walk beneath cherry blossom in the Dutch garden and enjoy the spring colours in the rock garden.

There are lots of frogs, newts and numerous species of birds to spot, as well as bees and hens in the apple orchard.

8. Fountains Abbey and Studley Royal (Water Garden), North Yorkshire

Explore the ruins of the 12th-century Fountains Abbey and admire the water garden with its moon-shaped ponds and classical statuary. Studley Royal Water Garden was the breathtaking vision of John Aislabie and his son William.

In the early eighteenth century, John Aislabie had great plans to impress visitors to his Yorkshire estate and turned the wild and wooded valley of the river Skell into one of England’s most spectacular Georgian water gardens.

9. Wallington, Northumberland

Discover Wallington in Northumberland, a much-loved home to generations of the Trevelyan family, this spring.

The Trevelyans loved being outdoors and close to nature, and the house is surrounded by an informal landscape of lawns, lakes, woodland, parkland and farmland just waiting to be explored. The tranquil East Woods come alive with the sound of birdsong in spring, and the walled garden is absolutely beautiful.

After a day of exploring these beautiful gardens, why not book yourself in for a decadent afternoon tea at one of these lovely stately homes' tea rooms, and if you're inspired to discover more National Trust properties throughout the country, their book Houses of the National Trust is available on Amazon, and will guide your through the incredible histories and heritage of these amazing homes, and how we can continue to look after them today.

item.title: This is your ultimate guide to the history of clans in Scotland and how they became synonymous with Scotland, including their murky beginnings and all the bloody bits in between
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey, Editor
item.description: Scottish clans are celebrated the world over but what are the origins of this unique part of Scottish culture? We go back to the beginning
item.content:encoded:
Few parts of Scottish culture evoke such a sense of patriotism and pride as its clans. Clan tourism is now big business, with thousands of members of the Scottish diaspora – Scots who emigrated and their descendants – visiting their homeland each year to trace their ancestry.

Though the clan system in Scotland was well established by the 11th and 12th centuries, it’s possible that the seeds for clans in Scotland were sewn as far back as the 6th century, when the Celts began to put roots down here.

Of course, clans are also synonymous with their tartans. If you'd like to know more about clans and their matching tartans and the hows and whys behind it, Ian Grimble's Scotland's Clans & Their Tartans is available on Amazon and is a brilliant exploration of the unique clan system and its origins.

What is the history of clans in Scotland, and where did it all start?

Where does the word clan come from?

The name ‘clan’ derives from the Gaelic word ‘clann’, loosely meaning ‘family’. It is thought, therefore, that the early clans were basically extended family groups who shared a piece of land.

This is not to say that they were all blood relatives: many clan members would have been related by blood but there were also septs – branches of the clan who bore no blood link to a chief but had ties nonetheless, or locals who pledged their allegiance and took on the clan name in return for protection.

How did clans get their names?

Clans were bound by a collective loyalty to each other and their land – a kinship – and early clan names were often linked in some way to the area and landscape in which they lived: Clan Colquhoun, for instance, takes its name from the Gaelic place name ‘cuil cumhann’ (meaning ‘narrow corner’) on the western shores of Loch Lomond. 

Where in Scotland were clans found?

Clans were found in traditionally Gaelic lands – the Hebrides and the Highlands – indeed, until recently, the Court of the Lord Lyon, which oversees all issues to do with Scottish heraldry, didn’t recognise any Lowland families as clans, though today the Scottish Government makes no distinction.

It’s also interesting to note that there were no clans on Orkney and Shetland (with the exception of Clan Sinclair, who at one point became Earls of Orkney), which were held by Norway until the 15th century. However, it is thought there were clans with Viking origins, such as Clan MacLeod, who is said to be descended from a Norse-Gael leader, Leod. 

What is Duthcas?

Underpinning much of Gaelic clan culture was the idea of Duthchas, an ancient belief that people are connected to the land on which they are born, creating a sense of belonging.

Under Duthchas, clan chiefs were elected through a system of tanistry. Rather than inherit the title, the role of chief went to the best person for the job.

The chief’s role was to act in the best interests of the clan, and in return, clan members were to work on the land and offer their services in battle should the chief ever call upon them.

Unfortunately, chiefs frequently called their clans to battle – border disputes and fighting between rival clans was common. In almost all cases, allegiance to the clan came before allegiance to the country.

When did clans become a part of Scottish culture?

The instability that led up to the formation of the Kingdom of Alba (modern-day Scotland) and the first king of Scotland in the 9th century cemented the role of the clans in Scottish culture.

Under tanistry, the clan chief had no right to earn money from people living on the land, nor evict them, but the coming of feudalism from the 11th century onwards began to erode this central ethos by encouraging land ownership and the charging of rent.

How were clans controlled in Scotland?

Over subsequent centuries, further measures were brought in to bring clans, who had grown more and more powerful, under control. King James VI insisted that clan chiefs attend court in Edinburgh each year, which meant they suddenly had a need for money, and so imposing rents on their clan members became increasingly appealing.

And James, like other monarchs before and after him, used clan loyalties to his advantage and would befriend clan chiefs and offer them more land in return for bringing their clans to fight for the Crown.

The Jacobite Rising of 1745

The Jacobite Rising of 1745/6 and its fallout was one of the most pivotal moments in the history of clan culture. As Bonnie ‘Prince’ Charlie landed in Scotland in 1745, raising his father’s standard and staking his family’s claim to the Crown, many Scottish clans put aside their fierce rivalries and rose to support him against an increasingly interfering Hanoverian monarchy under King George II.

Of course, not all clans were loyal to the Jacobite cause; some like Clan Campbell backed the British Hanoverian government.

Though there were a few moments of victory for the Jacobites, ultimately their devastating defeat at the hands of the British at the Battle of Culloden in 1746 led to the dismantling of clan culture forever.

Following Culloden, clan chiefs who had shown support for the Jacobites had their land forfeited to the Crown. Over a period of a century or more, clan members were forced from their land and became dispersed, often as far afield as Canada and Australia.

Sometimes these removals, known as the Highland Clearances, were blatant and brutal, with people dragged from their land to make way for more profitable sheep farming, and sometimes tactics were more subtle, with the promise of new lives dangled in front of them.

It is the diaspora that resulted from this that feeds the romanticism for clan culture across the world and the yearning to return to the land where they once belonged. 

Find out more from our clan profiles: Clan Armstrong | Clan Campbell | Clan Douglas | Clan Kennedy | Clan MacDonald | Clan MacDougall | Clan MacGregor | Clan MacLean | Clan Macleod | Clan Stewart

The Highland Clearances were a violent and difficult period of time for thousands of Scottish people, and one that is regularly overlooked in Scottish history. If you'd like to know more, T.M.Devine's The Scottish Clearances: A History of the Dispossessed, 1600-1900 is available on Amazon is the first to chart this tumultuous saga in one book and is a memorial to all the Scots whose lives were changed forever.

item.title: Skip the mediocre tourist experiences for the must-see sights that are well worth your time - these are the 13 best things to do in London if you’re a first-timer
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Here is your guide to the very best of London for first-time visitors, from the best museums to historic pubs, markets and the tourist sites that are actually worth visiting
item.content:encoded:
'When a man is tired of London, he is tired of life; for there is in London all that life can afford.' Said 18th-century writer and diarist Samuel Johnson, and I think there hasn't been a more accurate summary of London's buzz in the last 300 years than Johnson's.

If we are to take Johnson's quote literally, you will never run out of things to do in London, and the possibilities can feel quite overwhelming. But, as a former resident, true London-lover, and regular frequenter of the capital, if you're looking for the best things to do in London as a first-timer, I can certainly help.

Before you visit anywhere, it's always a good idea to read up on your subject before you arrive. I'd recommend Jack Chesher's London: A Guide for Curious Wanderers, which is available on Amazon. This lovely book presents London as the museum itself and tells the story of the city through the streets and buildings, and all the hidden places you can discover on your visit.

When you arrive in London for the first time, you must have an itinerary for your visit. What do you most want to see, do and experience? And I'm here to help sift the mediocre experiences from the must-sees.

These are the 11 best things to do in London for a first-timer to the capital

1. Buckingham Palace

Of course, it's a rite of passage for every Londoner to see the grand facade of the King's official residence in London with its red-coated King's Guard watching the gold-tipped gates. If you time your visit right, you might catch the iconic Changing of the Guard ceremony - official daily times are posted on The Household Division website.

Buckingham Palace offers tours of the State Rooms to the public in the summer months, which are available to book via Visit Britain, so you can see inside its opulent rooms.

But, if you're visiting during the rest of the year, it's still worth taking your time outside the gates and admiring the palace's impressive facade, the flag-lined Mall, neighbouring St James's Park and the enormous gold Victoria Memorial.

The King's Gallery, adjacent to the palace, is also open all year round and hosts regular, brilliant exhibitions. It's newest will celebrate the centenary of the birth of Queen Elizabeth II with the largest exhibition of her fashion ever staged.

2. The Mall and Trafalgar Square

From the palace, walk down the grand Mall, which was originally created in the 1660s by King Charles II as a field for playing pall-mall, a 17th-century game like croquet. Today it is a busy road, albeit the most impressive in London, with the palace at one end and the Admiralty Arch (which will soon open as a hotel) at the other.

As you walk under Admiralty Arch, you'll enter Trafalgar Square, London's most famous public plaza built to honour Admiral Nelson's Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar and crowned by Nelson's Column, four bronze lions and the National Gallery.

3. The National Gallery and the National Portrait Gallery

If you have time to do both galleries, I highly recommend it, as both are free and the art in both is remarkable.

The National Gallery houses over 2,400 paintings from as early as the 13th-century, with key masterpieces including the Arnolfini Portrait by Van Eyck, Velázquez's Rokeby Venus, Turner's Fighting Temeraire and Van Gogh's Sunflowers.

The National Portrait Gallery is the National's little sister, which sits just behind, but is one of my favourite places in London, if not the world. It is home to 11,000 historic and contemporary portraits, including some of British history's best-known faces. The Tudor gallery is a particular favourite, where you'll see portraits of all of Henry VIII's wives, and the gallery is home to the Chandos Portrait, known worldwide as the face of Shakespeare.

4. Westminster Abbey and the Houses of Parliament

Another rite of passage for any first-time Londoner, of course, Westminster is on the list.

This is a triple, even quadruple whammy, for first-timers because you can see the historic Abbey itself, the site of the Coronations of Britain's kings since 1066, the Houses of Parliament and Big Ben, and, across the River Thames, the iconic London Eye.

If you're here, don't just take a picture and leave, but take time to absorb the history of some of the most important square metre-age of London. Westminster Abbey is open for tours (which are available to book through Visit Britain), so you can walk where kings and queens have walked for a thousand years, see the tombs of British royals and famous names through history, including Elizabeth I, and visit Poet's Corner, where British icons like Charles Dickens are buried.

I'd also recommend a tour of the Palace of Westminster, the Houses of Parliament and Big Ben, where you can walk through hundreds of years of British history, and see where the Government meets today.

5. The Tower of London

This is my favourite place in London, and I can promise you it's worth the ticket price. Home to the Crown Jewels, the Tower of London is probably the most important historic site in the city, having stood since the reign of William the Conqueror and functioned as a royal palace and prison through its thousand-year history.

You'll walk past Traitor's Gate, which prisoners like Anne Boleyn passed through on the way to their execution, see the Chapel where traitors (including Anne) are buried and the rooms and cells were British history's most high-profile prisoners have been kept; Thomas More, Anne Boleyn, Lady Jane Grey, Guy Fawkes, William Wallace and even the Kray Twins.

The Chief Historian of Historic Royal Palaces, which looks after the Tower of London, Tracy Borman, has written a brilliant book on the site; The Story of the Tower of London is available on Amazon and offers a comprehensive history of the fortress from its Roman origins until today.

6. Tower Bridge

London's most famous bridge is another must for first-timers in the city. Supported by its castle-like towers, this is one of the most iconic views in the whole of the city.

A simple walk across the Victorian bridge is impressive enough and offers unmatched views up and down the Thames, but visitors can now also take a tour of the inside of the bridge itself, learn its history and see the mechanics of how it lifts to let ships through. Tickets for tours of Tower Bridge are available from Visit Britain.

7. Borough Market

After a morning spent exploring the Tower of London and an adventure on Tower Bridge, lunch is in store, and there is nowhere with more food options, or that is more iconic of London than Borough Market, which is just an easy walk from Tower Bridge along the river.

Borough Market is one of the oldest and largest markets in London and has roots going back to at least the 12th century. There are over 100 food vendors and stalls open, where you can shop for fresh produce like cheese, meat and sweet treats, or enjoy hot food on the go, from freshly made paella to oysters or Cuban sandwiches.

8. St Paul's Cathedral

Recognised for its iconic dome and crowning London's skyline since the 17th-century, St Paul's Cathedral has been synonymous with London since it was completed by Sir Christopher Wren in 1710, after he was tasked with rebuilding the original cathedral when it was destroyed in the Great Fire of London.

This Anglican cathedral has seen hundreds of historic ceremonies, including the wedding of Prince Charles and Diana in 1981, and even miraculously survived the Blitz of the Second World War.

TripAdvisor offers a walking tour of St Paul's and Westminster, which is available to book on their website, and which I would highly recommend.

9. The Globe Theatre

If you're going to go to any theatre in London, make it the most iconic. This incredible reconstruction of the original theatre built by the Lord Chamberlain's Men in 1599, the company that William Shakespeare wrote for and part-owned, is a must-see for Shakespeare fans.

You can take a tour of the replica building, which is built as an open-air theatre with a standing section, just as it would have been in Shakespeare's time (tickets are available through TripAdvisor), or you can book to see a play, which take place all year-round, and in all weathers (tickets are available through London Theatre Direct)!

10. Covent Garden

Once a vegetable patch for monks (hence the name) and now one of the capital's most popular shopping districts, you can wile away hours shopping, eating, drinking, watching street performers, and exploring in Covent Garden.

You can also visit the Royal Opera House and the London Transport Museum, which is well worth a peruse, featuring some historic London buses and a history of the Tube.

11. Kensington Gardens

Over in leafy West London, you'll find the tranquil and refined setting of Kensington Gardens, just to the west of Hyde Park and once the private gardens of Kensington Palace, the birthplace of Queen Victoria, and now one of London's Royal Parks open to the public.

Here you can explore the tree-lined avenues, blossoming flower walks and calming vistas where royals once walked and London's writers and artists have found inspiration. Kensington Gardens is also home to a famous fictional residence, Peter Pan, whose creator JM Barrie used to enjoy the park and used as the inspiration for Neverland and the boy who wouldn't grow up.

12. Museum of London

There are of course many, many museums in London that are worth your time and attention, most of which are free to enter. If I were to pick one for a first-timer with limited time, the London Museum, which will reopen in a new location at Smithfield Market later this year, would be it.

This brilliant museum will take you on a walk through the entire history of London, from prehistoric origins and Roman Britain to the plague, the Great Fire, Victorian London and right up until the present day. I've been multiple times and I've loved it every time, so I'm particularly excited about what the new and improved version will be like.

13. Have a drink at Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese

You can't visit London without having a drink in a proper London pub. There are about 3,500 pubs in London, so the choice is endless, and there are probably hundreds I'd recommend. But if you'd like somewhere central and historic, with a good atmosphere, Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese on Fleet Street is pretty unique.

A frequent haunt of Charles Dickens and Samuel Johnson, the pub is renowned for its labyrinthine style underground interior, which just seems to go on and on. Keep going down to the bottom of the 13th-century vaulted cellars that once belonged to a monastery and sit and enjoy your drink in proper, cosy confines.

There are many pubs in London with fascinating, bloody, and often haunted histories, all of which deserve your time. John Warland's Liquid History: An Illustrated Guide to London’s Greatest Pubs is available on Amazon and will guide you to Shakespeare's local, Jack the Ripper's favourite haunt and the bloodstained doors of the Bucket of Blood. Cheers!

item.title: Mad about History - Five of the weirdest and most ridiculous royal deaths in British history, from the king who ate too many fish to the most gruesome murder imaginable
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From being killed by friendly fire to being murdered on the toilet, some of these historic deaths are so unbelievably ridiculous, it’s hard to believe they are actually true
item.content:encoded:
'Stupid deaths, stupid deaths, they're funny cause there true' - so goes the Horrible Histories song that was the soundtrack to my childhood. It may even have been these ridiculous but totally true nuggets of British history that inspired my love of history today.

I still think about so many of these utterly ridiculous deaths today, many of which actually happened to reigning monarchs of Great Britain. It's fascinating to think that if they hadn't happened, British history might have looked very different.

From eating too many fish to being murdered on the toilet, there are some monarchs of England and Scotland whose deaths are so bizarre that it seems almost unbelievable. I've picked out my top five below, but if this has piqued your interest, I'd recommend Suzie Edge's Mortal Monarchs: 1000 Years of Royal Deaths, which is available on Amazon and is a highly entertaining read.

Five of the weirdest royal deaths in British history

View this post on Instagram

1. Edmund Ironside (1016) was murdered on the toilet

According to medieval chronicler Henry Huntington, who we will encounter a few times on this journey (perhaps that should be a warning?), poor old Anglo-Saxon King Edmund was ambushed by a Viking and stabbed to death while sitting on the toilet. Other historians claim it was probably by battle or disease that killed him, but I much prefer Huntingdon's story.

Edmund Ironside reigned for only seven months before he was murdered, and is best known for his heroic resistance to a massive invasion led by King Canute, which he'd probably rather have as his legacy rather than his unfortunate toilet death.

If you'd like to know more about poor old Edmund, Francis Young's Edmund: In Search of England's Lost King is available on Amazon is the story of the search for his missing body, just like Richard III's in the car park.

2. William II (1087-1100) was accidentally shot by an arrow that bounced off a tree

The son of William the Conqueror, often known as William Rufus, had a totally avoidable death that is still shrouded in mystery - was it murder or an accident?

According to the 13th-century chronicler Mathew Paris, the king was out hunting in the New Forest with Walter Tyrel, a knight, when they saw a stag. Apparently, the king shouted to Tyrel, 'Shoot, damn you!' and Tyrel hurriedly let an arrow go, but, unfortunately, it bounced off a tree and hit the king right in the heart, killing him instantly.

Of course, nobody knows what happened that day other than Tyrel. Recent historians have claimed that it was,, in fact, murder, which does seem very plausible, but the brilliant, and annoying, thing about history is that we will never know for sure.

3. Henry I (1100-1135) died after eating too many fish

It seems that unfortunate and avoidable deaths run in the family, because King William II's brother, King Henry I, the youngest son of William the Conqueror, supposedly died after eating too many fish - and the question on my mind is, how much fish do you really have to eat to die from it?

Henry Huntingdon (remember him?) wrote about King Henry I as well and claimed that Henry died while on a hunting trip in Lyons-la-Forêt in 1135 after he'd eaten too many lampreys (a type of jawless fish), against the advice of his physician. Today's historians believe he probably actually died from an infection from the bacteria that the fish was carrying, which does seem far more likely, though not half as interesting.

4. Edward II (1307-1327) died after having a red-hot poker stuck up his bottom

Poor old Edward II's death is probably the most gruesome of all these kings, if not in British history ever. According to a medieval chronicler, John Capgrave, Edward was 'slain with a hot spit put into his body’ at Berkeley Castle. What's worse is that this was likely on the orders of his wife, Queen Isabella and her lover Roger Mortimer, who had deposed him earlier the same year.

The very specific murder choice is, according to some historians, in reference to his supposed sexual relations with his male favourite, Gaveston.

Paul Doherty's Isabella and the Strange Death of Edward II is available on Amazon and takes a closer look at the mystery of Edward's murder, and the extraordinary life of his wife and supposed murderer, Queen Isabella.

5. James II of Scotland (1437-1460) died after standing too close to his own cannon

A member of the House of Stewart, King James II was a young king of Scotland who led the Scottish army to besiege the English-held Roxburgh Castle on the River Tweed in the Scottish Borders.

James was, apparently, a fan of artillery and was admiring an expensive cannon that had been imported in especially from Flanders when he ordered it to be fired and, unfortunately for him, was standing too close to it.

16th-century historian Robert Lindsay wrote that James's leg broke in two at the thigh from the impact of the cannon, and he was ‘stricken to the ground and died hastily’ - another particularly unfortunate, and totally avoidable, royal death.

The astute reader might have noticed that most of these weird royal deaths happened in the Middle Ages, which is definitely not just a coincidence. It was a difficult and gruesome time to be alive, but it makes for a brilliant historical study. Although written for children, Terry Deary's Horrible Histories books (the team behind the Stupid Deaths song) are the best way to learn at any age, and his Measly Middle Ages is available on Amazon and is the perfect introduction to this period of British history for love that like their history on the lighter side.

item.title: I think I have found the prettiest teapot in Britain and it is inspired by Jane Austen – and it makes every cup of tea feel like a scene from a novel
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: This charming Jane Austen teapot is the perfect addition to my country kitchen, and is a subtle way to hint at my favourite author throughout my home
item.content:encoded:
Fellow history lovers will understand that it can be hard to incorporate this interest into your home without it becoming overwhelming.

Of course, my favourite author Jane Austen has many nods around my home, but, these are mostly in the form of books - both by her and about her. But then I spotted this Jane Austen Book Ceramic Teapot at Liberty is the sweetest way to incorporate her in a subtle, classy way, and one that Jane would undoubtedly have approved of herself.

This sweet little teapot looks like it has come straight out of the Jane Austen House Museum, and would make the perfect gift for any bookworm or history lover, or frankly, as simply a gift to yourself.

Liberty London Jane Austen Book Ceramic Teapot

This quaint, adorable ceramic teapot was hand-painted in North Yorkshire by creative pottery brand Ceramic Inspirations and is the perfect kitchen accessory for Jane Austen lovers. Colourful and jolly, the teapot features all six of Jane Austen's novels in its body, and the lid is made to be the opening page of Pride & Prejudice, with Austen's most famous line in pride of place; 'It is a truth universally acknowledged that a single man in possession of a good fortune must be in want of a wife.' Now that's a conversation starter over a cup of tea if I ever heard one.

RRP£135
Shop now

I've always thought that having a teapot as a talking point was wonderfully eccentric and charming, and something my grandmother has always done - she collects weird and wonderful teapots and her house is full of them.

I could totally imagine myself sitting in the garden of my little cottage, reading Pride & Prejudice and pouring myself a cup of tea with it. The teapot would also look lovely on a kitchen shelf, and I love how all of Austen's novels are included - Pride & Prejudice, Sense & Sensibility, Emma, Northanger Abbey, Mansfield Park and Persuasion.

From the Desk of Jane Austen: 100 Postcards

RRP
Shop now
Shop now

Liberty London Jane Austen Tapestry Needlepoint Kit

RRP
Shop now
Shop now

Jane Austen: The Complete Works: Classics Hardcover Boxed Set (Penguin Clothbound Classics)

The World of Jane Austen: A Jigsaw Puzzle With 60 Characters and Great Houses to Find; 1000 Pieces

RRP
Shop now

Jane Austen 'Obstinate Headstrong Girl' Sweatshirt

Not on the High Street enamel Jane Austen pin

RRP
Shop now
Shop now

What charmed me the most about the teapot was the thought that Jane herself would have sat drinking tea whilst writing these works, which earned her a place amongst the greatest British writers of all time.

What's more, her novels revolve around the lives of Regency women who didn't have much more that they could do than call on each other and drink tea.

Austen's cleverness and wit mean that although on the surface her works seem to be entirely about tea parties and balls, what they are really about is the strict social structures of 18th-century England, and the limited autonomy that women had over their own lives. Today, her unheard-of success as a woman author means that now we can drink from a teapot made in her honour and revel in her brilliance, 250 years later.

The Liberty London Jane Austen teapot would look be particularly special to use whilst watching the BBC's new Jane Austen-themed period drama, The Other Bennet Sister, which continues the story of Mary Bennet after Pride & Prejudice. It is a refreshing take on the lives of Regency women who weren't all as witty and beautiful as Lizzie Bennet, and how Mary finds her own Mr Darcy.

item.title: Where is The Other Bennet Sister filmed? 7 Regency filming locations from the BBC’s new Austen-era drama that is less Mr Darcy and far more Mr Collins
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: We’re exploring the beautiful filming locations from the BBC’s brand-new period drama, The Other Bennet Sister, that continues the story of the Pride & Prejudice sister we all forgot - Mary
item.content:encoded:
The BBC absolutely understood the assignment with their brand new period drama based on Jane Austen's Pride & Prejudice world, which continues the story of the overlooked and overshadowed Mary Bennet in a colourful swish of Regency elegance that is much more refined to my taste than Bridgerton.

The BBC's The Other Bennet Sister offers a new perspective on the story we know so well - Mary's version of events. What I have particularly enjoyed is its focus on what happened to women in 18th-century Britain who weren't blessed with the good looks, wit and charm of the Lizzie Bennets of the world - and what on earth could an unmarried woman do with her life?

The series is based on Janice Hadlow's debut novel of the same name, which was released in 2020 to much acclaim, and is available to buy on Amazon. It will delight fans of Jane Austen and Bridgerton. The BBC's series differs slightly from the original book. Still, the producers have brilliantly brought the story to life with a stellar cast, beautiful costumes and, of course, the all-important film sets, showing off the very best of Regency Britain as well as taking us to some of the country's most beautiful landscapes.

Here is everywhere The Other Bennet Sister is filmed, so you can step into Mary Bennet's Regency world yourself.

Where is The Other Bennet Sister filmed?

1. Bridgend, Wales

The story starts in a familiar setting for all Pride & Prejudice fans, the Bennets' fictional family home of Longbourn. We have, of course, had various different versions of Longbourn in each Pride & Prejudice adaptation (and we patiently await the newest, as Netflix's version of the Austen classic that will come out in September 2026).

For The Other Bennet Sister, a gorgeous private house in the small town of Bridgend in South Wales, tucked between Cardiff and Swansea, was used to recreate Longbourn.

Although, as a private home, we obviously can't all descend on this lovely house, we can admire its charming exterior and garden and stylish interiors in every episode of The Other Bennet Sister.

2. St Fagans National Museum of History, Cardiff

Wales proved profitable for the producers of The Other Bennet Sister as Bridgend was one of several Welsh filming locations used for the series.

St Fagans National Museum of History was used as the interior of the inn in the show, chosen by the producers thanks to its accurate Regency-era settings. The museum, which is open to visitors, is open-air and chronicles the history, lifestyle, culture and architecture of the Welsh people.

3. Llancaiach Fawr Manor, Nelson

Our third Welsh filming location takes us to the village of Nelson, where the Tudor manor house, Llancaiach Fawr Manor was used as the inn in The Other Bennet Sister.

The historic 16th-century manor house was the perfect find for the producers of the show. Unfortunately for us, however, the site has been closed to the public for over a year. Here's hoping the heritage site opens back up soon so we can admire it again and walk in the footsteps of Mary.

4. Dyffryn House and Gardens, Vale of Glamorgan

Of course, no one can forget the breathtaking wisteria-covered scene in episode 3 of The Other Bennet Sister, in which the charming Mr Hayward invites Mary and her aunt and uncle on a mystery poetry reading outing.

Though under the guise of being in London in the show, this beautiful garden was actually filmed in the Pompeian Garden at Dyffryn House and Gardens in the Vale of Glamorgan in Wales. Lucky for us, the real garden is open for visitors, and you can explore the Edwardian gardens, the arboretum and the glasshouse, and perhaps even indulge in a little Wordsworth amongst the wisteria yourself.

5. Penpont House, Bannau Brycheiniog National Park

This family-run estate on the banks of the River Usk is in the heart of the Bannau Brycheiniog National Park, one of the most beautiful National Parks in Britain. Penpont House served as the picturesque backdrop for the fictional village of Meryton, near the Bennet's family home, and another place very familiar to Pride & Prejudice fans.

Penpont House's historic buildings were a perfectly charming filming location for these rural scenes, and visitors can explore what the 17th-century Grade I Manor House has to offer, from camping in the grounds to lovely self-catering accommodation for a family holiday.

6. Bannau Brycheiniog National Park, Wales
