Whilst in the Bannau Brcheiniog National Park, conveniently, its landscapes were also used to recreate the boat scenes set on Lake Windermere in the Lake District in The Other Bennet Sister.

Although the actual lake used for filming is privately owned, there are plenty of similar landscapes within the Bannau Brcheiniog National Park to explore, whilst the dramatic landscapes of the Black Mountains were used to recreate the scenes on Scafell Pike, England's highest mountain.

7. Bristol

Moving east from Wales into England, the producers of The Other Bennet Sister chose the Regency architecture of Bristol to double as Austen-era London in the series, in a similar vein to the way Bridgerton's producers used Bath as a filming location - London is too difficult to film in because of its busyness.

Bristol's elegant Orchard Street doubled as Cheapside, the historic street near St Paul's Cathedral where Mary's aunt and uncle, Mr and Mrs Gardiner, live. The street was chosen thanks to its row of beautifully preserved Georgian architecture, having originally been built as merchants' houses in the late 18th century.

Bristol's Berkeley Square in Clifton was also used for several outdoor scenes for the series, and is the perfect place to walk and imagine yourself as a Regency lady or gentleman, because of its stunning Georgian architecture.

The Other Bennet Sister has offered a totally new view on Regency Britain for me, and I've cheered for Mary all the way - a character I, like Jane Austen, have always laughed at. It's made me think differently about the plight of all women in this time period, not just those blessed with good looks and charm, and has given Austen fans a fresh dive into the wonderful world of the Bennet family and their many, many foibles - I'd recommend it to anyone, period drama fan or not.

If The Other Bennet Sister, all its beautiful Regency settings, and its fascinating social history, have inspired you too, I'd recommend Ian Mortimer's The Time Traveller's Guide to Regency Britain: The immersive and brilliant historical guide to Regency Britain, which is available to buy on Amazon. This is everything you need to know about Mary Bennet's and Jane Austen's world, from disease and criminals to social etiquette and politics in Regency Britain.

item.title: Did King Alfred the Great really burn the cakes? The true story of the Anglo-Saxon warrior king - Viking challenger, Christian scholar and law-maker
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: A fierce warrior, by the time King Alfred the Great succeeded his brother Ethelred I as King of Wessex, he was a battle veteran, but he was also a reformer
item.content:encoded:
It is a pretty impressive accolade to be the only British monarch to be given the accolade 'the Great'. In fact, King Alfred was given this title thanks to his 'warrior-scholar' reputation, after defending his country against the invading Vikings.

Although Alfred the Great was not technically the first king of England, he is often thought of as such because he was the first West Saxon King to adopt the title King of the Anglo-Saxons in 886. It is actually his grandson, Athelstan, who is thought of as the first king of England, though Alfred's reputation means he thoroughly deserves his place on our timeline of the kings of England.

The Anglo-Saxon period can be a little complicated to get your head around, there are many different kingdoms and a lot of names that sound the same. Gerry McGyver's A Potted History Of The Anglo-Saxon Kings: England’s Early Rulers is available on Amazon and is a brilliant, easy-to-follow guide of this fairly complex, but fascinating period in our history.

So, let's start at the beginning.

What did England look like when King Alfred inherited the throne?

In the early Middle Ages, England was divided into seven kingdoms. As the 9th century progressed, marauding hordes of Vikings took control of large areas in the north and east.

Who was King Alfred?

Alfred was the youngest son of King Æthelwulf and his first wife Osburh. Three of Alfred's brothers reigned in turn before him.

By the time King Alfred the Great succeeded the throne of Wessex on 23 April 871, following the death of his elder brother Ethelred I, he had already been involved in skirmishes with the ‘Great Heathen Army‘.

After seven years of defensive manoeuvres, however, Alfred the Great oversaw a key victory at Edington in May 878. This defeat of the Vikings prompted a peace treaty with the Danish King Guthrum. The treaty divided England between Anglo-Saxon territory and Viking-ruled Danelaw, which consisted of Scandinavian York, the north-east Midlands and East Anglia.

There followed a period of relative prosperity for the kingdoms of Mercia (broadly the West Midlands, Lancashire and Cheshire) and Wessex (the south of England stretching from Cornwall in the west to London and Kent in the east).

Aside from his prowess on the battlefield, Alfred’s main claim to greatness lay in his willingness to reform. Alfred helped to establish one of the UK’s first naval fleets, as well as a system of burhs (or fortified towns) at regular intervals across Wessex.

Where did the title Alfred the Great come from?

Aside from his military successes, Alfred had a good reputation as a merciful, gracious man. He was level-headed and encouraged education. He established a court school for both nobles and commoners to be educated in English and Latin, improved the legal system and military structure and improved the quality of life for his people.

Alfred also oversaw the conversion of the Viking leader Guthrum to Christianity.

His title of 'the Great' was probably not actually used during his lifetime, and historians believe he was given the epithet in the 13th century, though it was only popularised from the 16th century.

Key dates in King Alfred the Great's reign

  • 878 – Rhodri the Great, known as ’King of Wales’, is killed in battle by the Anglo-Saxons

  • 886 – Alfred the Great restores English rule in London and renames it ‘Lundenburh’

  • 893 – The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, an annual record of events, is compiled for the first time

Where did King Alfred the Great and the legend of the burnt cake from?

During a Viking attack, Alfred fled to the Somerset Levels and was supposedly given shelter by a peasant who woman who didn't know his identity. The legend goes that she asked him to mind some wheaten cakes she had left baking by the fire.

Preoccupied by the problems of his kingdom, Alfred let the cakes burn and was scolded by the woman on her return. The first written account of the legend appeared 100 years after Alfred's death and has lived on forever in folklore.

Alfred the Great's Anglo Saxon Chronicles are actually available to buy on Amazon, and make a fascinating resource for anyone looking to understand the Anglo-Saxon period. Justin Pollard has also written a brilliant biography of this warrior king, Alfred the Great is available on Amazon and takes a closer look at incredible story of the the scholar, soldier, statesman king.

item.title: Was the sixth wife of Henry VIII the most boring? Here’s why Catherine Parr’s story is far more interesting than you might think, and her tragic tale of love after the tyrant king
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: We examine the legacy of Catherine Parr, Henry VIII’s sixth and final queen and the ‘survivor’, and discover that her ending wasn’t quite as happy as we all thought
item.content:encoded:
The term 'survived' is how we tend to remember Queen Catherine Parr, but did you know that her life after the tyrant king wasn't the happy ending we all hoped it would be.

The sixth and final of the wives of King Henry VIII, Catherine is often overlooked purely because her story isn't as dramatic as the other five - she outlived her husband with her head still attached to her body, which is actually a pretty impressive feat.

But I'm here to change your mind about this 'quiet' queen, and prove she wasn't the most boring and, in fact, deserved a great deal of our sympathy too - her ending wasn't quite how she planned it. My interest in this sixth and final queen has been inspired by Elizabeth Norton's Catherine Parr, which is available on Amazon, and which brilliantly tells the tragic story of the widow who caught the elderly King Henry VIII's attention.

Who was Catherine Parr?

Born in August 1512, Catherine Parr was, ironically, allegedly named after Henry VIII's first wife, Catherine of Aragon.

Her father, Sir Thomas Parr, was a descendant of King Edward III. Sir Thomas was a close companion to King Henry VIII and was rewarded with responsibilities like Sheriff of Northamptonshire and Master of the Wards. Catherine's mother was a close friend and attendant of Catherine of Aragon, hence why she was probably named after her.

Catherine was raised as a Catholic, but like her predecessor Anne Boleyn, turned to Protestantism during her lifetime.

Catherine Parr's first husband

Aged 17, in 1529, Catherine married Sir Edward Burgh. According to some sources, the young man was in poor health in his 20s and died in the spring of 1533.

Catherine Parr's second husband

Still a young woman, in 1534, Catherine married John Neville, 3rd Baron Latimer, her father's second cousin. With this marriage, she became the second woman in her family to marry into the peerage. Of course, her next husband would take her right to the top.

John Neville was nearly twice Catherine's age and had two children from his first marriage. Catherine had a home of her own to look after, a title and a husband with an impressive position and influence in the North.

Latimer was a Catholic and opposed the King's annulment of Catherine of Aragon and his marriage to Anne Boleyn. During the 1536 Lincolnshire Rising, Catholic rebels came to their home and threatened Latimer if he did not join in their efforts to reinstate the links with Rome in England. Her husband was taken prisoner, and Catherine lived alone with her step-children between October 1536 and April 1537.

During the 1537 Pilgrimage of Grace, Catherine and her step-children were held hostage at Snape Castle in North Yorkshire and her husband was told that if he did not return immediately (he was returning from London), they would kill his family. Latimer managed to get his family released, but the consequences were devastating, and Latimer's reputation in the eyes of the king was damaged forever. Was he a conspirator?

Over the next seven years, the family spent time in the south as Latimer attended parliament, and it was here that Catherine met her future fourth husband, and probably the only one she really loved, Sir Thomas Seymour, brother of Queen Jane Seymour.

Latimer died in 1543 and left Catherine several properties and the guardianship of his daughter and an income for life. She was a rich widow at this point.

Catherine enters the household of Lady Mary, the King's daughter

Using her mother's friendship with Catherine of Aragon, Catherine Parr got herself a position in her daughter's household, Lady Mary, the future Queen Mary I. It was here that she caught the attention of the king.

She had by this point established a romantic relationship with Sir Thomas Seymour, but, of course, it was her duty to accept Henry's proposal over Seymour's. He was given a posting abroad in Brussels to remove him from court - no doubt Henry was being extra cautious after his previous wife, Catherine Howard's, affair with her childhood sweetheart.

How old was Catherine Parr when she married Henry VIII?

Catherine Parr was 31 years old when she married King Henry VIII, who was 52 and ailing.

Catherine Parr - wife and nurse of the king

Some historians argue that Henry married Catherine, who was interested and well-versed in medicine, because he needed someone to nurse him in his old age, and with his growing list of illnesses. But maybe he was still hopeful for another heir to secure his throne - he only had one male heir after all.

Henry was probably also looking for a companion in the final years of his life. After the disaster of his young and inexperienced fifth wife, Catherine Howard, who embarrassed him and left the world without her head, Catherine Parr was mature, clever and experienced in marriage. Henry was known to call her 'sweetheart' during their marriage, and he trusted her with his children and with looking after the country.

Of course, it is highly unlikely Catherine actually wanted to marry Henry. She was in love with another man and knew the fates of his five previous wives. But he was old and ill and probably wouldn't last very long, and she knew better than to refuse him. She had to do her duty and accept.

The pair were married in the Queen's Closet in Hampton Court Palace in July 1543. Her motto as queen was 'to be useful in all I do'.

Catherine as stepmother

One of Catherine's triumphs as queen was her relationship with Henry VIII's three children, Lady Mary, Lady Elizabeth (the future Queen Elizabeth I) and the young Prince Edward, who would inherit the throne as King Edward VI.

Catherine became close friends with Mary, who was a similar age, and she encouraged Elizabeth in her studies. Some historians argue that both Elizabeth and Mary modelled their queenship on Katherine Parr. It is also believed that it was Catherine's influence on Henry that led to the decision to restore both Mary and Elizabeth to the line of succession.

Catherine Parr as Regent Queen

In 1544, Henry allied with Charles V and went to war with France, leaving Catherine as his Regent-General with the power to rule in his name, a remarkable sign of the king's trust. She signed five royal proclamations as regent and oversaw domestic issues in her husband's absence.

Catherine Parr's religious views

If you've seen the film Firebrand (based on Elizabeth Fremantle's novel Queen's Gambit which is available on Amazon), you'll know that Catherine and Henry's marriage wasn't always plain sailing, and she survived a conspiracy to remove her from the throne.

Catherine was a passionate supporter of religious reform and even wrote books as her own - the first English queen to publish a book under her own name.

But the last years of Henry VIII's reign were dangerous for reformers, and Catherine's interest in reform got her into trouble. Conservatives at court were threatened by the idea of 'heretical' Protestant influence in England, especially with the King's health deteriorating and the young Prince Edward set to inherit.

Although Henry was fond of Catherine, he grew irritated by her passionate debates about reform, as a religious conservative at heart. Bishop Stephen Gardiner and his men jumped on this and attempted to implicate Catherine in heresy, launching an investigation into the queen and her ladies.

Catherine's rooms were searched for radical religious books, and Gardiner secured a warrant for her arrest.

Catherine's allies happened to see her arrest warrant and warned her of the plot; she immediately took to her bed and said she was terribly ill. Henry arrived at her bedside, where she told him she was just a 'silly poor woman' and the King was her 'only anchor, supreme head and governor here on earth, next to God’.

Perhaps due to his age and tiredness, Henry forgave her, and when the men arrived to arrest Catherine, he sent them away. Catherine had done what no other wife could: survive a plot to overthrow her.

What happened to Catherine Parr after Henry VIII's death?

Henry VIII died in January 1547, aged 55. The new king Edward's uncle, Edward Seymour, Duke of Somerset, was named Lord Protector, and Catherine was free to decide her own future.

In 1547, Catherine reconnected with the man she truly loved, Thomas Seymour, brother to the Lord Protector. They married in secret in May 1547, without King Edward VI's permission, a provocative move that damaged her relationship with Edward and Mary, who felt it was disrespectful to marry so soon after the king's death.

Elizabeth and Catherine, however, remained friends, and the young princess was invited to live with her and Seymour. Unfortunately, Seymour began harassing Elizabeth, and was even caught visiting her bedroom in his night clothes and with his arms around her. Elizabeth was sent away.

Catherine Parr's death

Finally, with her (almost) happy ending, Catherine fell pregnant in December 1547. In August 1548, Catherine gave birth to a daughter, Mary, but then fell ill with childbed fever and died just six days later on 5 September 1548 at Sudeley Castle, Gloucestershire.

In 1549, Thomas Seymour was executed for treason for his role in the plot to place Lady Jane Grey on the throne and his young orphaned daughter, Lady Mary Seymour, disappeared from the history books. I hope she found the happiness her mother never could.

Catherine's legacy as queen is undeniably impressive. As well as being the only one to survive a plot to overthrow her, she published a book in her own name and was trusted to serve as Regent - a role she performed successfully. It is quite tragic that her happy ending after the king's death didn't end as we'd hoped it would, and I can't help but feel the 'survivor' was somewhat hard done by when it came to life after Henry VIII.

I'd love to know what happened to the young Mary Seymour, and no one as of yet has yet written a story for her - perhaps there is an opportunity for a best-selling novel? When it comes to historical fiction on Catherine Parr's story, I always return to Philippa Gregory, queen of the Tudor novel. The Taming of the Queen is available on Amazon is an intimate portrayal of Henry's last queen, who wasn't so lucky as we all thought.

item.title: Mad about History - Five seriously weird things about Regency women’s beauty regimes I’ve learned from watching far too many Jane Austen adaptations
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: With The Other Bennet Sister on our screens at the moment, I have a newfound interest in the nitty gritty of Regency women’s daily toilette
item.content:encoded:
Anyone who knows me knows I am absolutely Jane Austen-obsessed, and, as well as a penchant for men in riding boots, I've also developed a fairly unhealthy interest in the fascinating, intricate details of the lives of Regency women.

With the release of the BBC's new adaptation of Janice Hadlow's Pride and Prejudice sequel, The Other Bennet Sister, which continues the story of Mary, my obsession reached its peak. This brilliant series, based on Hadlow's novel, shows us a side to the private lives of these women that Austen could never really have because of her prudish contemporary audience - the nitty-gritty of their daily toilette.

Happily, 2026 will also see the release of a brand-new Pride & Prejudice adaptation on Netflix, and a new Sense & Sensibility film, both of which come as we celebrate 250 years since Jane Austen was born. I know I shout about Lucy Worlsey an awful lot, but her biography Jane Austen at Home is available on Amazon and was written specifically for this anniversary - it's the only Austen biography you'll ever need.

So what do we really know about Jane Austen and her heroines' beauty regimes?

View this post on Instagram

Here are 5 weird facts about Regency women that you definitely didn't know

1. Women should be pale - being tanned was considered coarse and common

Anyone who has read an Austen novel knows there is an obsession with a woman's complexion and her skin - this was one of the few ways women could catch the eyes of men after all.

Says Caroline Bingley in Pride & Prejudice of Lizzie's complexion, "I never in my life saw anyone so much altered as she is since the winter. She has grown so brown and coarse!

The aim was to be as pale as possible; tanned skin was considered common and 'coarse', and a well-bred woman would never be found outside without a bonnet, parasol and gloves, lest they risk a tan.

2. Regency women didn't wear knickers

With everything they had going on with their numerous dresses and petticoats, this is hardly that surprising. Although it does contradict the image of 18th and 19th-century society as strict and prudish.

The only women who wore drawers or pantaloons during Georgian and Regency England were prostitutes who wore them underneath their slitted skirts.

3. It was scandalous to say the word 'legs' in polite company

Instead, 'limbs' was the proper term. Of course, women's dresses were always long and flowing, so they would never reveal anything and the word 'legs' was far too direct a term for what was underneath them.

Although, to bust a common myth, showing ankles in this period wasn't actually scandalous - though of course showing your legs would have been - sorry, ahem, I mean limbs.

4. Women washed fully once a week

This was something I'd only thought about after watching The Other Bennet Sister, because female characters never seem to wash at all in Austen's novels. We see Mary and her sisters bathing before the Netherfield ball, but on other days, she merely does a simple sniff and wash under the armpits.

5. Women did not shave their legs and armpits

This again was something I didn't even consider until watching the new BBC series, in which we see Mary with hairy armpits, and of course they would have been hairy.

In fact, up until the early 20th century, it was totally normal for women to have body hair, and it wasn't until 1915 that the first female razor was advertised. As hemlines rose, women's legs became barer and barer.

If this whirlwind through Regency era women's beauty regimes has piqued your interest, I'd recommend Rachel Knowles' What Regency Women Did for Us, which is available on Amazon and tells the inspirational stories of 12 Regency women who achieved trailblazing success, including Jane Austen, Madame Tussaud and many others you've probably never heard of.

item.title: Did Cleopatra actually marry her 10 year-old brother? Here’s why Cleopatra: The Experience left me with a newfound obsession for the Egyptian queen and how to get your ticket
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: I went to try out London’s brand new immersive show, Cleopatra: The Experience and left with a wealth of knowledge on this fascinating historical enigma, and a new respect for Virtual Reality storytelling
item.content:encoded:
I have to admit, I've never been a particular fan of immersive experiences, but last night's visit to London's brand-new Cleopatra: The Experience totally changed my mind - it was mesmerising.

Definitely the most exciting event opening in London this week, the experience comes from Madrid Artes Digitales (MAD), who are also the team behind hit experiences including Tutankhamun: The Immersive Exhibition, The Legend of the Titanic, and The Last Days of Pompeii.

A real whirlwind for the senses, it was almost like a piece of theatre in its extravagance, and left me with a newfound obsession with Cleopatra that I can't believe I didn't have before. As I left the experience, I purchased Saara El-Arifi's Cleopatra, which is available on Amazon and is a reimagining of the ancient world's most famous queen, on her own terms - I'm so excited to start reading it.

Of course, Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra means we all know what happened at the end, but did you know that, aged 18, Cleopatra married her 10-year-old brother to secure her dynasty? Yes, really.

Developed by historic curators and Egyptologists, Cleopatra: The Experience brought this historic enigma to life for me in a way that I never thought possible, using incredible immersive technology, VR, a free-roaming metaverse and even a 5D sensory experience. I was transported back to ancient Egypt and came face-to-face with one of the most important and fascinating figures in the history of the world.

Cleopatra: The Experience opens today, 26 March 2026, and will run for a limited 15 weeks until 12 July 2026. You can get your tickets via fever.

What can you expect from Cleopatra: The Experience

Your journey begins, rightly, at the beginning. You'll discover the story of Cleopatra's ancestors, the Ptolemaic Dynasty, including Alexander the Great, through an incredible hologram presentation - it was like a 3D cinema experience I'd never seen before. This will set the grounding of your knowledge on the life and reign of Queen Cleopatra in the context of ancient Egypt.

The next section was by far the most memorable of all - the free-roaming Metaverse room. I was a little sceptical when I approached, and none of us had ever done it before. It was a bizarre, but brilliant experience. We had a pair of enormous Virtual Reality goggles placed on each of our heads, and as soon as they were on, the room around us disappeared - follow the yellow dots to explore the catacombs of Alexandra, step inside Mark Antony’s tent during the Battle of Actium and enter Cleopatra's royal chamber as she gazes across the Bay of Alexandria.

The experience cleverly takes you on a path through the room, so when it ends, and the goggles are removed, you're at the exit, and other people in the experience are portrayed as green figures, so you won't bump into anyone! This was really thrilling, and we all came out with huge smiles on our faces, having giggled a lot - it is a strange feeling walking into the unknown. One section asks you to step out into space, which I was almost reluctant to do; it felt so real.

Another highlight was the 22 original Egyptian artefacts on display, as well as Cleopatra's scent-infused private chamber, designed to smell like her perfume and evoking the grandeur of her world. Here we learned all about her iconic make-up, hairstyles and beauty regimes and the symbolism they held in ancient Egypt.

There was also an enormous 360-degree cinematic projection room, which played a 26-minute-long video telling the story of Cleopatra's life and reign. This was immersive in a different way from the metaverse, and we relaxed into the space, taking in every bit of the incredible projections that we could.

The last section of the experience is a full-motion Virtual Reality experience which takes place seated on a chair, wearing goggles and headphones. You can spin around in your chair to witness the earthquake and catastrophe that caused Alexandria to sink beneath the sea, explore submerged ruins, forgotten temples and sphinxes and enter the Temple of Isis to witness Cleopatra's burial ritual.

We all emerged from the 90-minute-long experience bright-eyed and full of wonder - this is the most innovative way to engage with history, and I am really, really impressed.

Cleopatra: The Experience is not the only immersive experience in London, and some of other top-rated experiences include Frameless, the immersive art experience and, if you're interested in history, Vikings: The Immersive Experience is available to book on fever and will take you back to the golden age of these invaders from the North and uncover the mysterious world of Norse mythology.

item.title: Think you could survive a night at the most haunted spot in Edinburgh? The Real Mary King’s Close open a prize draw to one brave winner - the first person to spend the night in 400 years
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: This is your chance to make history as Edinburgh’s spookiest attraction open prize draw to offer one winner and their guest an exclusive after dark experience at The Real Mary King’s Close
item.content:encoded:
If the idea of spending the night in one of the most haunted places in Scotland, if not all of the UK, is quite thrilling to you, then read on because you could be in with a chance of being the first person to stay overnight at The Real Mary King's Close in 400 years.

This brilliant attraction underneath the Royal Mile is one of the best things to do in Edinburgh and should be on every visitor's bucket list. Offering a unique chance to see what life was like in 17th-century Edinburgh on a real preserved street that is now underground, the unique attraction has been a highlight of my visits to Edinburgh in recent years.

The capital's most famous street, the Royal Mile, is brimming with history, and there would have been many more streets just like Mary King's Close a few hundred years ago, which have now all been built on top of. To learn more about the layered stories of the Mile, Eric Melvin's A Walk Down Edinburgh's Royal Mile is available on Amazon and is a brilliant history of the UNESCO World Heritage Site, which stretches back 1000 years.

So, how do you fancy staying the night in this remarkably historic place?

The Real Mary King's Close have launched a special competition which will offer one lucky winner the keys to the attraction for the night - the first time anyone will have stayed overnight in this supposedly haunted spot since its residents were driven out 400 years ago.

One winner and their guest will get the chance to experience the attraction when the dark has fallen, and all the public has gone home. The prize includes:

  • An overnight stay for two on Mary King’s Close, with exclusive after-hours access to the site

  • A private, after-dark tour led by an experienced costumed character, revealing stories and details rarely shared during public visits,

  • An immersive experience with the Plague Doctor or Dr Arnott, exploring the realities of illness, belief and survival in 17th-century Edinburgh

  • Dinner and breakfast for two

10 runners up from the prize draw will also each win two tickets to the attraction.

Entries to the competition cost £5, and every penny from the prize draw will go towards preserving this incredible piece of history for future generations. You can enter the prize draw here - hurry, it closes on 30th March 2026!

What is The Real Mary King's Close?

Situated directly under the Royal Mile, Mary King's Close was once an open-air street and was named after Mary King, a merchant burgess and businesswoman who lived and worked there in the 17th century.

Visitors to the attraction can walk through the dark maze of real preserved streets and see the actual houses where residents lived and worked, including during outbreaks of the Plague.

You'll be led by costumed guides who will portray real residents of the street and share stories of plague, murder and, of course, ghosts.

The most famous ghost of the street is a young girl named 'Annie' who is said to haunt the close after she died of the plague in the 17th century. According to popular lore, Annie was abandoned by her family in the Close when they fled to escape the plague, and she remains there to this day.

In 1992, a Japanese Medium, Aiko Gibo, was visiting the Close to film a documentary and felt a cold presence - she thought this was the lonely spirit of Annie, who had lost her doll. She bought a new doll for her and started a tradition that continues today - Annie's room is now filled with dolls and toys - creepy or sweet? You decide.

Other ghosts at The Real Mary King's Close include a formless phantom of a child, a menacing dog and disembodied limbs.

So if you'd like to meet these spectres yourself, why not enter the prize draw to win to be the first person to stay overnight in 400 years - a pretty impressive accolade to hold.

If the story of Annie's ghost has unleashed an intrigue for ghostly tales, Edinburgh is brimming with haunted stories that expand much further than just Mary King's Close. Ross Guthrie's Haunted Edinburgh: A Look At Edinburgh's Most Haunted Locations is available on Amazon and will guide you through the city's spookiest spots, which is without a doubt one of the most haunted places in all of the UK, if not the world - venture on if you dare!

item.title: While everyone else chases the usual postcodes, Barnsbury is quietly emerging as the smarter move – your guide to this beautiful and under-the-radar London neighbourhood
item.dc:creator: The London Magazine Editors
item.description: Find peace within inner London in a neighbourhood that continues to draw growing families and city creatives who want the best of both worlds
item.content:encoded:
Tucked between King’s Cross and Islington’s Upper Street, Barnsbury is the definition of a hidden gem. Filled with period properties, and with city transport connections, it is an oasis. 

When thinking about the best places to live in London, Barnsbury is often overlooked. But its the fine balance between city and suburb makes it enduringly appealing, with a host of independent restaurants and shops.

Here’s everything you need to know about this lovely leafy suburb. 

‘Barnsbury is one of those wonderfully varied pockets of London that genuinely offers something for everyone,’ says Ele Robertson, a director at Savills Islington. The area boasts a varied demographic, ‘from first‑time buyers taking their first step on the ladder, to upsizers looking for more space, right through to older couples who love the calm, villagey feel.’

 'It’s only moments from the bustle of Upper Street or the shops and restaurants at Granary Square in King’s Cross,’ says Oliver Sharp, lister at Hamptons Islington. ‘Locals love the area’s array of pubs and cafes – from The Albion, The Drapers Arms and The Tamil Prince, to brunch at Sunday Café, coffee from Southpaw Coffee, or a pint at Hammerton Brewery & Taproom,’ he says.

The area’s schools are equally as varied. ‘Local popular schools include William Tyndale and Thornhill primary schools,’ comments Sebastian Toy, head of Islington sales at Knight Frank. ‘Buyers and renters tend to like the leafy and quiet residential streets, while still being within close proximity to Upper Street and transport links. There are a few garden squares that are very popular, such as Lonsdale Square and Barnsbury Square.’ Sharp adds that some of the most highly regarded secondary schools in the capital are all ‘within easy reach’.

Above all, it’s Barnsbury’s ever-present community spirit that distinguishes the area in particular, according to James Teulon, office head at Cluttons Islington. 'There’s a sense of calm and architectural continuity here that feels increasingly rare in central London,' he says. 'Residents tend to stay for years, viewing Barnsbury as a long-term home rather than a stepping stone. Ultimately it’s Barnsbury’s mix of character, connectivity and community that keeps it so consistently in demand.'

A day in Barnsbury

9AM 

Spring awakening

Start the morning in true north London style, unrushed. Grab a flat white or seasonal beverage at Sunday, the cosy and much-loved local cafe that's become synonymous with exciting brunch menus. Alternatively, begin your day at Samya Studio on Cobble Lane, where visitors can enrol in a yoga or Pilates class, and explore a menu of massages from lymphatic to prenatal.

11AM

Wooded wanders

By mid-morning, venture to Barnsbury Wood Nature Reserve, the capital’s tiniest ecological beauty spot (just 0.35 hectares), hidden within a triangle of residential streets. Wander the woodland paths among ancient trees in the serenely quiet space. Link this stroll with a short loop along nearby Regent’s Canal towpath (above, accessible from the Caledonian Road area) for waterside views, industrial echoes, and the occasional passing narrowboat. 

1PM

Pub lunch

Lunch calls for one of North London’s finest gastropubs. Head to The Drapers Arms on Barnsbury Street with vintage-chic vibes and a seasonal British menu. Try the spiced aubergine ragout or roasted hake with creamed cucumber, or go classic with a hearty roast on a cosy Sunday. Alternatively, The Albion offers wisteria-draped charm, striped parasols, and a sprawling garden – ideal for springtime alfresco seating. 

3PM

Curated retail therapy

Barnsbury edges Upper Street, so head south towards Barnsbury Street for indie gems: browse contemporary furnishings at Aria, retro pieces and curios at Twentytwentyone, or quirky homewares and books at After Noah. If you're feeling more contemplative, trace the quieter streets toward Thornhill Road or Liverpool Road.

5PM

Sunset drinks and golden-hour glow

As light softens, claim a table at The Tamil Prince for flaky rotis, rich curries, and mango lassis in a cosy, creative pub-style setting – or opt for organic pints and farm-to-table dining at The Pig and Butcher. If hosting at home, wine merchant Must and Lees on Liverpool Road has options from around the world that can pair with any menu, as well as a tasting room with events. 

Ask a local: Poppy Sherbrooke, founder of Percy Langley

“I adore Islington; it was the neighbourhood where I first landed as a young adult in London, and one that has found its way back into my life for work. Its concentration of theatres is unrivalled: the Sadler’s Wells and the Almeida offer consistently high-calibre work, while the King’s Head and Park Theatre host a distinctly British wit. The shops are independent in the truest sense. Percy Langley, Aria and Borough Kitchen set a standard few neighbourhoods can match. Confident, intelligent cooking in low-key settings always makes for a lovely evening out. My restaurant recommendations would include Frederick's, Ottolenghi and Trullo. All of this is found alongside some of London’s finest pubs! Best of all, it unfolds just a short walk from the roar of central London.” percylangley.com

Food & Drink

With neighbourhood haunts ranging from authentic global dining to cosy pub roasts, Barnsbury is a foodie’s  paradise

Little Georgia

14 Barnsbury Road

This restaurant stands out as one of London’s best introductions to authentic Georgian cuisine. Housed in a converted space with retro charm and vintage artefacts it’s a cosy pub-restaurant hybrid. The menu delivers hearty, homemade dishes prepared from scratch. Find cuisine classics, such as indulgent khachapuri (cheese bread), stews and khinkali dumplings, with a thoughtful selection of Georgian wines. littlegeorgia.co.uk 

The Albion

10 Thornhill Road

Standing as one of the area’s most enduring Georgian gems, this gastropub radiates warmth all year round. With the ambience of a village inn, its outdoor space is perfect to find shade under mature trees and climbing wisteria with large gatherings or small groups alike. Tuck into sumptuous seasonal British dishes, including generous Sunday roasts with all the trimmings. the-albion.co.uk 

The Tamil Prince

115 Hemingford Road

The pub is one of north London’s most talked-about spots, since evolving from a classic corner pub to a venue serving south-Indian delights. While retaining its pub bones, the kitchen is led by chef Prince Durairaj, who delivers bold, regional Tamil Nadu-inspired dishes with ample flair. Think crispy dosas, fiery chicken with layered spicing and chutneys that pack a punch.  thetamilprince.com 

The Hemingford Arms 

158 Hemingford Road

Built in 1855, The Hemingford Arms or ‘the Hemi’ as some call it, is the oldest pub in Barnsbury. It’s famous for its characterful decor, from items hung from the ceiling to walls filled with vintage curiosities and abundant floral exterior. With Thai food on the menu, join the punters for regular quiz nights.  hemingfordarms.com 

Southpaw Coffee

2 Roman Way

Established in 2018, Southpaw Coffee is known for serving up delicious and robust coffees. The neighbourhood eatery curates special supper clubs designed to help diners mingle and unwind in an intimate, relaxed setting. Southpaw’s next supper clubs will take place on 17 and 18 April. southpawcoffee.co.uk

 Schools

The area boasts a diverse array of schools from junior to senior. The Gower School is a co-ed independent Montessori nursery (North Road), starting from 6 months, and primary school (Cynthia Street). Based on a Scandinavian ethos, London Scandinavian School on Highbury Crescent was founded in 2013. Teaching in English, the kindergarten and primary school uses the English National Curriculum. Children's House School helps pupils thrive in early years and prep and is split across two sites: Elmore Street and Upper Street.

Other options include family owned Dallington School, which teaches from 3 to 11. Non-selective co-ed St Paul’s Waldorf provides holistic learning for pupils 3 to 14, within a Grade II* listed building. Secondary options include: NBH Canonbury from 11 to 18, City of London School for boys, Channing School in Highgate and all-through Highgate School.

On the market

Barnsbury's elegant and well-connected Georgian homes and garden squares are highly desirable

Barnsbury Park

Set in the heart of the Barnsbury Conservation Park, this remarkable Grade II-listed six-bedroom home is a rare find, as one of the few fully detached, double-fronted properties in the area. Blending seamless grandeur and practical family living across three floors, its generous 6,060 sq ft footprint and gardens make it ideal for large-scale entertaining. £8.5m Agent: knightfrank.co.uk

Barnsbury Street

This handsome five-storey Grade II-listed Georgian terrace property comes with four bedrooms, a basement annexe, garden office, roof terrace and bags of renovation potential. £3.5m Agent: chestertons.co.uk

Ripplevale Grove

On one of Barnsbury's most coveted streets, this freshly renovated two-bedroom period-conversion flat enjoys a private garden and good transport links. £1.195m Agent: savills.com

Ask an agent: Iga Honeyman, Associate Director at Chestertons Islington

“Barnsbury offers a varied selection of attractive period properties and new-build homes. Prices start from around £300,000 for a studio apartment to £8m+ for spacious family houses. Ripplevale Grove is one of the more popular roads, but homes on quiet garden squares such as Lonsdale, Gibson and Thornhill are equally attractive. Recently, the market has seen an increase in buy-to-let investors putting rental properties up for sale; many of which are new-builds. The uplift in available homes is giving some buyers the upper hand, depending on how quickly the vendor's looking to sell.

 “This, alongside an abundance of lifestyle offerings and excellent transport links has established Barnsbury as a sought-after Islington enclave. Buyers range from first-time buyers and second-steppers to families upsizing and back to downsizers. Once you’re here, it’s hard to move elsewhere!” chestertons.co.uk Barnsbury is proof that you don’t have to sacrifice tranquillity to stay connected in London. With rich architectural charm, and strong sense of community, it offers a lifestyle that feels both grounded and effortlessly convenient.

It may fly under the radar, but that only adds to its appeal. For those seeking a neighbourhood that blends character, calm, and city access in equal measure, Barnsbury quietly stands out as one of London’s best-kept secrets.

item.title: A magical first look at HBO’s Harry Potter TV series has been released, and I can hardly contain my excitement - here is everything you need to know before we head back to Hogwarts again
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton
item.description: From cast to soundtrack, teaser trailer and filming locations this is everything we know so far about the upcoming HBO Harry Potter TV series, which will hit our screens early next year
item.content:encoded:
It's what we've all been waiting for - HBO have released a long-awaited first look at their brand new Harry Potter series, with the first official teaser trailer launching today.

From casting to all the Harry Potter filming locations, I've scoured the internet for everything I can find out about the brand new series ever since it was announced, and finally, we've been rewarded with an on-set image of the young Dominic McLaughlin as Harry, in his Gryffindor Quidditch uniform.

If you haven't already read JK Rowling's world-altering series on the boy wizard, then, seriously, where have you been? And, no, seeing the films doesn't count. There is a lovely box-set version of all seven Harry Potter books available on Amazon, so get reading quickly before the new series hits our TV screens Christmas 2026.

View this post on Instagram

It was a very exciting day when HBO announced they would be releasing a faithful 10-year adaptation of Harry Potter, with each season covering one book, and bringing the author on board as an executive producer. Although the films are wonderful and nostalgic - I grew up with them - as a superfan, there were so many plot points and key details that didn't make their way into the films. A full series gives the producers the chance to cover everything they possibly can and really do each book justice in a way that a two-hour feature film simply can't.

Watch the official Harry Potter series teaser trailer below:

The cast of the Harry Potter TV series

Daniel Radcliffe, Emma Watson, and Rupert Grint have handed over their batons to three brand new actors playing the fab three, Harry, Ron and Hermione, as well as a brand-new star-studded cast to take on the roles of Hogwarts teachers and beloved other characters.

Here is all of the Harry Potter series cast we know so far:

Harry Potter will be played by Dominic McLaughlin, Hermione Granger will be played by Arabella Stanton, and Alastair Stout will play Ron Weasley. More than 30,000 children auditioned for the three main parts, and these three came out triumphant.

Albus Dumbledore will be played by John Lithgow, Nick Frost will play Rubeus Hagrid, Janet McTeer will play Minerva McGonagall, and Paapa Essiedu will play Severus Snape.

Lox Pratt will play Harry's nemesis, Draco Malfoy, while his father, Lucius, will be played by Johnny Flyyn.

Other important cast members include Katherine Parkinson as Molly Weasley, Paul Whitehouse as Argus Filch and Bel Powley and Daniel Rigby as Mr and Mrs Dursley.

Other major characters have yet to have their casting announced. Rumours swirl that Cillian Murphy (of Peaky Blinders fame) will play Lord Voldemort, but that hasn't been officially confirmed - though I do think he would be brilliant.

Where is the Harry Potter series being filmed?

The new HBO Harry Potter series is primarily being filmed at Warner Bros Studios Leavesden in Hertfordshire, which also served as the home of the films, but the cast and crew have been spotted filming out and about in Britain over the past year.

In London, Borough High Street was used for city scenes - presumably Harry and Hagrid's visit to London in The Philosopher's Stone, whilst Windsor Great Park was transformed into Hogsmeade Station.

In England's South West, the cast was spotted filming near the water at Cornwall's beautiful Cadgwith and Kynance Cove, and in the picturesque Devonshire village of Lustleigh.

North Yorkshire's Skipton has also been cited as a filming location, with an actor spotted casting spells from a rooftop - how very mysterious.

Harry Potter TV series soundtrack

To much excitement, it has been announced that Oscar-winning composer Hans Zimmer, along with Bleeding Fingers, will compose a brand new score for the Harry Potter TV series.

Zimmer commented, "The musical legacy of Harry Potter is a touch point for composers everywhere and we are humbled to join such a remarkable team on a project of this magnitude. The responsibility is something that myself, Kara Talve and Anže Rozman do not take lightly. Magic is all around us, often just beyond reach, but as in the world of Harry Potter, you simply must look for it. With this score, we hope to bring audiences that little bit closer to it whilst honouring what has come before.”

When can I watch the Harry Potter TV series?

The new series is expected to be released Christmas 2026 and will premiere on HBO in the US, with the first season comprising eight episodes.

If it seems like a long time to wait until early next year, there is plenty of Potter-ing to be done in the meantime. The Warner Bros Harry Potter Studio Tours are a brilliant day out where you can walk through all the real-life sets from the films, and tickets are available to book on TripAdvisor.

JK Rowling was, of course, also famously inspired by the landscapes of Britain, particularly Scotland, and there are plenty of Harry Potter filming locations to visit throughout the country, so you can get your magic fix before we delve back into Harry's wonderful world.

item.title: Enid Blyton was racist, sexist and xenophobic - here’s how the legacy of world’s most prolific children’s author unravelled, and why we’re still reading her today
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Despite writing some of the most beloved children’s stories of all time, Enid Blyton has been cancelled in the eyes of 21st-century readers. Here’s why she is so controversial
item.content:encoded:
The Famous Five, Malory Towers, The Magic Faraway Tree, and even Noddy, Enid Blyton was the creator of some of the most beloved British children's stories of all time, her books selling over 600 million copies worldwide.

And although she is certainly one of the most famous British writers of all time, she is not often revered as a favourite. Despite her continued cultural relevance - The Magic Faraway Tree has just been adapted into a major film - Enid Blyton is actually a very controversial figure, and many have of her works have undergone revisions to remove material that is racist, sexist and xenophobic.

Of course, there is a strong argument for separating the art from the artist, and there are many brilliant versions of Blyton's books that are perfect for modern young readers. As a girl, my sisters and I loved Malory Towers, and I was desperate to go to school on a clifftop in Cornwall (of course?). A 21st-century version of all 12 Malory Towers books is available on Amazon as a box set, or if you're planning to watch the new film, The Magic Faraway Tree is also available on Amazon and is a lovely escape into a fantasy world for younger children.

So what do we know about Enid Blyton's life, and why is she so controversial now?

Who was Enid Blyton?

Enid Blyton was born in August 1897 in East Dulwich, London, the daughter of a cutlery salesman. It was from her father, Thomas that she inherited a love of nature and the outdoors.

In her autobiography she wrote that he 'loved flowers and birds and wild animals, and knew more about them than anyone I had ever met'. Enid was devastated when he left to live with another woman. Enid didn't have a good relationship with her mother and, after she left home, she never saw her mother again, giving people the impression that she was dead.

It was at school that Enid discovered her passion for writing, particularly poetry writing. After finishing school she decided to train as a teacher, whilst continuing to write and sending manuscripts into publishers - which were continuously rejected.

By 1920, Enid was living in Surrey as a teacher and was writing in her spare time. She began to have essays, short stories and poems published in magazines, and her writings on teaching were particularly successful. Her first children's books were published in the 1930s.

By the 1940s Enid Blyton became a prolific author and the first of her boarding school stories, The Naughtiest Girl in the School and the St.Clare's stories first hit our shelves - and were immediate successes. It was in 1944 that we first met Julian, Dick, Anne and George in the Famous Five series.

Many of Enid Blyton's novels enjoyed continued success into the 1960s, and by 1962, 26 million copies of Noddy had been sold.

By this time Blyton was displaying signs of dementia, and her agent, George Greenfield, recalled that it was "unthinkable" for the "most famous and successful of children's authors with her enormous energy and computerlike memory."

Enid Blyton died just a few months after her husband in 1967. She died of dementia at Greenways Nursing Home in Hampstead, London.

Why was Enid Blyton controversial?

So 600 books and a storming legacy later, why has Enid Blyton's character been crossed off the list?

Her 'cancellation'- to use a 21st-century term - began in the 1950s when literary critics, teachers and parents picked up on some controversial themes in her writing, particularly in the Noddy and The Famous Five series.

Racism in Noddy

The early Noddy books featured Golliwogs which were widely recognised as promoting negative, racist black stereotypes. In Noddy the characters were depicted as dishonest, thieving villains and the books were banned and removed from many libraries.

There was also concern about the close relationship between Noddy and Big Ears - they often shared a bed - and this was deemed inapropriate for children. Later versions of Noddy replaced Golliwogs with Goblins and Noddy and Big Ears lived apart.

At the time some libraries and schools banned Blyton's works and from the 1930s until the 1950s the BBC refused to broadcast her stories - supposedly because of their perceived lack of literary merit. Despite her aim of encouraging children to read, teachers claimed her writing was so simplistic it stunted children's literary development.

You can watch an updated version of Noddy at the link below:

Racism and sexisim in The Famous Five

Another popular series of Blyton's criticised for its themes and content was The Famous Five - the swashbuckling series that saw four children and their dog go on exciting adventures often involving missing treasure, shipwrecks and thieving criminals.

Firstly, The Famous Five was perceived as having outdated gender roles; the female characters are placed in traditional, passive roles and often aren't as adventurous as their male counterparts.

The series was also criticised for enforcing rigid class stereotypes, which, in a post World War II world, did not land very well with readers. In many of her series', particularly The Famous Five, working-class individuals are often depicted as 'dirty ragamuffins' and 'forlorn waifs' who are uneducated and in need of saving from the civilised middle class children.

As a child, I probably wouldn't have noticed this, but thinking about it now, it seems oh so obvious. It's clear why teachers and parents were reluctant to let their children read the works, worrying about the subliminal messages it portrayed.

Critics suggested that Blyton's world created a 'myth of Britain' that focussed on affluent, leisure-based childhood and emphasising a sense of entitlement and privilege for the characters, who often have servants and live in country houses; they could spend their summers swanning around Cornwall drinking ginger pop and searching for treasure.

It got me thinking, if Blyton had written these works 50 or even a hundred years earlier, before the World Wars helped society to deconstruct its class stereotypes, before women got the vote, and before Britain became more multicultural, would we think it was controversial, or would we accept it as a product of its time?

I think the answer is probably the latter, there seems to be a universal free-pass for writers who didn't have the 20th-century social enlightenment that we do now. Unfortunately for Blyton, she didn't move with the times and her criticism came in heavy.

Today, English Heritage have acknowledge that her works often reflect outdated and prejudiced views which has led to the revision and removal of certain characters and stories, and it was revealed that in 2016, the Royal Mint blocked a proposal to honour the author with a commemorative 50p coin.

So can and should we still read Enid Blyton novels?

The answer is yes, of course. We still read Charles Dickens even though he was a raging misogynist, and even Shakespeare, being a 17th-century man would have held many views we today would consider terrible. The key, of course, is recognising these problems and, where we can, correcting them.

There are revised versions of nearly all of Blyton's most famous works that remove the offensive themes and focus instead on the brilliant stories that inspired a generation of children to be brave and adventurous, no matter their race, gender of class.

If you'd like to know more about this controversial author, Nadia Cohen's The Real Enid Blyton is available on Amazon and is a brilliant deep dive into the complicated and fascinating life of the most prolific children's author in history.

item.title: Outlander in concert has been announced for 2027 - here’s how to get your tickets to the most swoonworthy event of the year, so you can say goodbye to Jamie and Claire one final time
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Just when we thought we had made our final goodbyes to our favourite time-travelling lovers, a very special concert celebrating the beautiful Scottish soundtrack of Outlander has been announced - here’s how to get your ticket
item.content:encoded:
If, like me, you've been mourning the end of Outlander, fear not, for a very special show celebrating all eight seasons will be making its way to a theatre near you in 2027.

Going straight into our guide to the best things to do in London in the next year is Outlander in Concert: Echoes Through the Highlands, which will tour the UK across four nights in February 2027, and will be a musical celebration of this TV phenomenon that transported us through time and made us fall in love with Scotland all over again.

If you haven't read or seen Outlander, where have you been? But don't worry, it's not too late. Diana Gabaldon's novels are available as a box set on Amazon. Once you've read them, you can move on to indulging in the TV series, the eighth and final series of which has just been released on Starz.

The epic time-travelling love story set in Scotland follows heroine Claire Randall, a British combat nurse from 1945 who is mysteriously transported back to 1743 Scotland, where she meets the handsome Jamie Fraser, a young Scottish warrior. The series whisks you through Scotland's most beautiful landscapes and many of the Outlander filming locations can be visited on a trip to Scotland, from ruined castles to mysterious standing stones.

Like all good films and TV series, what sticks with viewers is the wonderfully moving soundtrack, which will transport you to Scotland wherever you are in the world.

When Outlander in Concert: Echoes Through the Highlands was announced therefore it was music to my ears. This special show will feature stunning cinematic visuals from all eight scenes presented on an HD cinema screen, and paired with the iconic Celtic Outlander score composed by Emmy Award-winning Bear McCreary, whose work also includes The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power.

Watch the trailer for Outlander in Concert below:

The concert will be performed by a live ensemble of musicians and vocalists playing traditional Scottish instruments including bagpipes (of course), fiddles and the hurdy-gurdy (a Scottish medieval stringed instrument that is a bit like a mechanical violin), and of course audiences will hear the most beloved pieces of music from the series, most notably Outlander - The Skye Boat Song.

The series' beautiful score has carried its soul for audiences through all eight series, surpassing 49 million plays and this will be a wonderful way for fans to get together to celebrate and mark the end of this cultural global sensation that brought Scotland's beauty and history into the hearts of people all over the world.

Tickets for Outlander in Concert are expected to sell out very quickly and are on sale now here.

If you can't wait until then, we have put together 14 of our favourite moments from all eight series of Outlander in preparation for the end of series eight (sob!) and Phoebe Taplin's Outlander's Guide to Scotland is available on Amazon and is your ultimate guide to the real destinations that inspired Diana Gabaldon when she was writing Jamie and Claire's love story, from wild hills to craggy castles.

item.title: ‘I am what you call a hooligan’ - this is the story of the brilliant women behind the Suffragette movement who fought to win the vote and exactly how they did it, from hunger strikes to martyrdom
item.dc:creator: Caroline Ellis, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: We chart the history of the Suffrage movement, from the Suffragists to the Suffragettes - a group of remarkable women who dedicated their lives to changing the course of British history, and look at some of their stories
item.content:encoded:
“We have to free half of the human race, the women, so that they can help to free the other half", said Emmeline Pankhurst, founder of the Women's Social and Political Union, in her 1913 Freedom or Death speech.

Emmeline Pankhurst was, undoubtedly, one of the greatest women in British history, and the most famous Suffragette of all. An inspirational figurehead of the women's suffrage movement, who worked tirelessly, and with whatever means she could, to get women the vote. Her legacy lives on, 100 years after her death, and she inspired a generation.

To understand Emmeline's brilliance, I'd recommend reading her own work, My Own Story, which is available on Amazon - an autobiography of her fascinating life and political career and a reminder of her unsailable passion and spirit for her cause, a must-read for anyone looking to understand the feminist movement.

But where did it all start, and did the Suffrage movement really succeed? Here, we follow the story of the Suffragettes and their fight and take a closer look at some of the stories of their best-known characters.

Who were the Suffragettes?

The Suffragettes were militant women's rights activists in the early 20th century, primarily in the UK, who used direct action (including arson, breaking windows and hunger strikes) to get the government's attention to further their cause - they wanted to win women the right to vote.

But where did they come from?

Who were the Suffragists?

The Suffragettes were born from the Suffragists, and there was a distinct difference between the two. The Suffragist movement began in the 19th-century, advocating for women's rights through peaceful methods, such as petitions and lobbying.

Mary Wollstonecraft may have written A Vindication of the Rights of Woman as early as 1792 (the original version of the Vindication is available on Amazon and is well worth a read), but the typical Victorian view was still that women were ‘The Angel in the House’, the title of a popular poem. So women fought to have a say in their children and property and to be able to go to university or enter a profession. But the symbol of the Suffragette struggle was the right to vote. While the number of men who could vote increased throughout the 1800s, women were excluded. The goal was universal suffrage – ‘Votes for All’.

The most tireless campaigner in the Suffragist movement was Millicent Garrett Fawcett. She and her sister Elizabeth were born and brought up in the Suffolk fishing town of Aldeburgh, the first town to elect a female mayor. This was Elizabeth, who, as Elizabeth Garrett Anderson, had become Britain’s first female doctor before retiring to her home town.

Her sister Millicent was based in Kensington, a fashionable area of London with attractive villas around the new museums. In 1865, she married the radical Liberal Member of Parliament Henry Fawcett and became part of the influential Kensington Society. They met at 44 Phillimore Gardens, one of several suffrage sites that can still be seen in the area. The society delivered the first petition for women’s suffrage – the vote – to Parliament and was the catalyst for many more societies, which later joined under Mrs Fawcett’s leadership as the National Union of Women’s Suffrage Societies (NUWSS) in 1897.

Tessa Blackstone's Millicent Garrett Fawcett: The Fight for Votes for Women is available on Amazon is a brilliant biography of this remarkable woman, made more interesting by the fact that the pair shared an address in London.

The National Union of Women's Suffrage Societies

The National Union believed in the constitutional route to the vote and campaigned peacefully. Their work was well funded, and they lobbied Parliament through petitions, public meetings, pamphlets and posters. They believed “women’s suffrage is for the common good of all”, and Millicent Fawcett spread the message throughout the UK. Over 50,000 men and women belonged to the National Union in 449 branches throughout the country.

The greatest event of their campaign was the 1908 march through London. Over 10,000 women marched from the Embankment to the Albert Hall, carrying 700 banners. Women’s suffrage supporters had often been jeered by men, but this procession impressed and gained their respect. The Observer newspaper quoted a watching regimental major: “Fine lot o’sportswomen; I wish’em luck!” The report concluded with the words, “the Women made London their own”.

Who was Emmeline Pankhurst?

But many women wanted more than respect; they wanted progress – and that came from Manchester. Emmeline Goulden was born in Manchester’s Moss Side in 1858 and married barrister Richard Pankhurst. He was a strong supporter of women’s rights, and Emmeline Pankhurst became active in the suffrage movement. On Richard’s death in 1898, she and her daughters became a leading force for votes for women.

In those key years they lived in Nelson Street in Manchester. Their home is now the Pankhurst Centre and this Victorian villa in the suburb of Chorlton on Medlock contains a small museum and centre for women. Frustrated by the lack of progress towards the vote, Emmeline Pankhurst founded the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU) in 1903. This was a militant organisation, and its motto was ‘Deeds not Words’. It was the WSPU who were dubbed ‘Suffragettes’, while the peaceful campaigners were known as ‘suffragists’.

The Suffragettes first made an impact at a Liberal Party Rally at the Manchester Free Trade Hall. Built on the site of the infamous Peterloo Massacre, this magnificent building is now a five-star hotel, the Radisson Blu Edwardian.

In 1905, Mrs Pankhurst’s daughter, Christabel and ex-mill girl Annie Kenney heckled the speakers and were arrested. They refused to pay their fines and were sent to prison. This was the start of a very different type of campaign. Politicians could not ignore Suffragettes who smashed windows, set fire to letter boxes or chained themselves to the railings at Parliament. Some of their tactics were ingenious. They attempted to post themselves and disrupted the 1911 census. On the night of the census, Emily Davison hid in a cupboard in the House of Commons, and the census had to record this as her address.

Emmeline Pankhurst was a charismatic leader and a fine speaker. She said to her troops: “The argument of the broken pane of glass is the most valuable argument in modern politics”. Many argued that this new militancy was turning people against votes for women.

Arthur Conan Doyle, for instance, condemned it as “malicious monkey tricks”. But in the early years, the Suffragettes drew attention back to the women’s cause. Millicent Fawcett herself said, “I hope the old-fashioned suffragists will stand by them. [The Suffragettes] have done more during the last 12 months than we have been able to accomplish in the last 12 years.”

Unfortunately, it seemed that the more militant the Suffragettes became, the more reluctant Parliament was to give women the vote. The Morning Post newspaper summed up this feeling in 1912. “Nothing could indicate more plainly their lack of fitness to be entrusted with the exercise of political power.”The women’s prisons were now filling with suffragettes and Mrs Pankhurst and her daughters were among those who went on hunger strike for better conditions.

The government did not want martyrs for the cause, so passed what became known, because of the way it toyed with prisoners, as the Cat and Mouse Act. Hunger strikers were force-fed, then released before they became too ill. When their health had improved, they were rearrested.

The Suffragettes provided a number of houses for convalescence and comradeship. Mouse Castle in Kensington’s Campden Hill is now a listed building, while the Sussex farmhouse of Backseltown in Henfield was for ‘country mice’. At Eagle House near Bath, recuperating Suffragettes were encouraged to plant a tree in the grounds. This became known as Annie’s Arboretum, after frequent visitor Annie Kenney. The most militant campaigners were imprisoned many times.

Who was Emily Davison?

Emily Wilding Davison was one of these ‘guerrilla’ Suffragettes. She had funded herself through first-class honours degrees at Oxford and London universities and, after joining the WSPU in 1906, soon became a full-time campaigner.

It is still not known if she intended to become a martyr for her cause at the 1913 Derby. Many of her possessions are held by The Women’s Library in London, including the purse she carried on that day. Its poignant contents included a return rail ticket. Many believe she intended to pin a flag on one of the horses, and it was just a chance that it was the king’s horse that struck her. Her death shocked many, but she was also sent hate mail as she lay unconscious in the hospital.

Lucy Fisher has written an excellent biography of the great Suffragette martyr, Emily Wilding Davison: The Martyr Suffragette is available on Amazon and tells the real story of this governess turned militant activist and her tragic final ending.

What happened to the Suffragette movement during the First World War?

In less than a year, international events overtook the campaign. When war was declared against Germany in 1914 the government immediately negotiated with Mrs Pankhurst to support the war. Only six days after the outbreak it agreed to release all Suffragette prisoners and fund a patriotic rally with the slogan ‘Man must fight and women must work’.

And women did work, from factories and farming to transport and offices. Women’s work in the First World War was one of the factors that changed the attitude of the government, but perhaps the vital one was the continuing industry of Millicent Fawcett.

The National Union had also stopped campaigning at the outbreak of war, but unlike the Pankhursts, did not actively recruit for the armed forces. Instead, they formed political alliances and carefully negotiated with the government.

In 1918, Mrs Fawcett accepted the compromise of the vote for women over 30 who were householders or married and then universal suffrage in the Equal Franchise Act of 1928. She died in 1929, soon after the building of Millicent Fawcett Hall in Marsham Street in Westminster, an educational and training centre for women. The hall is now part of Westminster School, but you can still see the foundation stone laid by the then Dame Millicent Fawcett.

After 1918, Emmeline Pankhurst changed the  Women’s Social and Political Union into the broader Women’s Party, to promote women’s equality throughout life. She died in 1928 and is honoured by a statue in London’s Victoria Tower Gardens.

In 1999 an unusual ceremony took place in the Houses of Parliament. A commemorative plaque was secretly placed on the door of a broom cupboard. It was, of course, the cupboard in which Emily Davison defied the government at the 1911 census, and the plaque also recalls her death: ‘By such means was democracy won for the people of Britain’.

A century on from women winning the vote, there is still so much work to be done in terms of gender equality. We can and should use the Suffragettes as an example and motivation to change things for the better. David Roberts' Suffragette: The Battle for Equality is available on Amazon and is a wonderfully illustrated history of their campaign, demonstrating their outstanding bravery and strength and putting it all into perspective.

Of course, there were many women before the Suffragettes who believed in change for women and advocated for their rights, from playwrights and authors to actresses and queens. Some of my favourites are best known as 'She Wolves', the queens of Medieval and Early Modern Britain, and some of the greatest queens in British history, who defied the bounds of their gender and built themselves a legacy that has lasted hundreds of years - perhaps it was these women that inspired our brave Suffragettes?

item.title: The best places to stay along the Cornish coast path for stunning views, charming cottages, and unforgettable coastal adventures
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey, Editor
item.description: Our pick of some of the best places to stay along the Cornwall coastal path, where it really is lovely to be beside the seaside
item.content:encoded:
Cornwall's allure is timeless, and it's not hard to see why. This most westerly county of England is fringed with golden sandy beaches, brimming with charming fishing villages, peppered with fascinating history, and swirling with myths and legends.

There is plenty to explore in Cornwall, and some of the best places to visit in England's South-West are within the bounds of this beautiful part of the country, from exotic gardens to historic houses and some of the best surfing beaches in the country.

The best way to see Cornwall? Walking along its diverse and beautiful coastal path of course, à la Poldark. Before you set off, Howard McCarthy's CORNWALL'S COASTAL PATHS UNCOVERED: Inspiring Coastal Walks Through Cliffs, Hidden Coves, and Quiet Cornish Shores is available on Amazon and is a brilliant guide to the very best of this stunning path - helping you know exactly where to start and planning your itinerary every step of the way.

When it comes to your itinerary, we've got you covered in one aspect: where to stay. Here is our guide to the best places to stay along the Cornish coastal path, where you can cosy up and enjoy the sea views after a long day exploring.

The best places to stay on the Cornish coastal path

Gurnard’s Head, near St Ives

Part of a small group of pubs with rooms designed to make you pause and take in your surroundings, The Gurnard’s Head is perfect for guests looking to slow down and experience the real Cornwall.

Located between St Ives and St Just, on a stretch of coastline that looks out over the rather volatile Atlantic, it’s a place for cheek-pinching coastal walks, sitting down and watching the skies for birdlife, and yomping through countryside before returning to your cosy sanctuary for good food, done well.

With Roberts radios, Bramley toiletries and fresh-cut flowers, the rooms are very welcoming, but the absolute highlight is the Vispring beds, which are so good guests are constantly enquiring about them.

Open all year round, recommended day trips in the area include Penzance, Zennor, and the famous Minnack Theatre.

Gurnard's Head is available to book via TripAdvisor here.

St Michaels Resort, Falmouth

Situated above Gyllyngvase Beach, in Falmouth, St Michaels Resort is one of the region’s leading independent hotels and is still beaming from its multimillion pound renovation in 2018.

That renovation, which saw it turned into a destination spa hotel, included the addition of the biggest hydrothermal pool in southwest England, a well-equipped health club, subtropical spa gardens, and outdoor tubs from which to soak up this new, serene atmosphere. Local fare, sourced from Cornish producers, farmers and fishermen can be enjoyed in the Brasserie on the Bay, while Nourish offers more laid-back dining with sparkling sea views.

Despite its name, this is not the best place to stay if visiting St Michael’s Mount is your only reason for coming to Cornwall, as it’s a 40-minute drive away.

However, the resort is well placed for visits to Falmouth’s art galleries, independent shops, restaurants and beaches, as well as Pendennis Castle, the Eden Project, and ferry trips to St Mawes.

St Michael's Resort is available to book via Expedia here.

Rick Stein’s Café, Padstow

The transformation of Padstow from a quiet North Cornwall harbour town into a food-lover’s dream began 50 years ago when Rick Stein opened his famous Seafood Restaurant here. Since then, the chef has opened several other establishments in the town and inspired some of his fellow chefs to follow suit.

By far the most casual of his eateries is this sweet café, which specialises in Asian-inspired flavours, and which just so happens to have three gorgeous rooms available to guests who need to rest up as they eat their way around town.

With decor as fresh as the sea breeze outside, bathrooms stacked with toiletries made by Rick’s co-owner (and ex-wife) Jill, and the promise of a high-quality Cornish breakfast in the morning, you’re destined to have a good night’s sleep here.

Rick Stein's Café is available to book via Michelin Guide Hotels here.

The Nare, Roseland Peninsula

For a country-house-by-the-sea vibe offering impeccable service, this five-star boutique hotel (there are just 40 rooms) is simply exquisite.

A traditional approach to hospitality pervades here, with nothing too much trouble for the hotel’s guests – the hotel can even provide car transfers from your previous accommodation or airport (within reason).

Afternoon cream tea is laid out in the smart drawing room each day for guests to help themselves, and added extras that can be arranged upon request include exclusive tours of nearby historic homes (the owners of which are often personal friends of The Nare).

The recently reopened Quarterdeck Seafood restaurant has a tempting menu that includes dishes such as monkfish wellington and seafood platters piled high with locally sourced Cornish fish. And all of this can be enjoyed while looking out over the hotel’s pool to the beach, the sea and the cliffs at the end of the Roseland Peninsula beyond.

The Nare is available to book via TripAdvisor here.

Alba Beach House, St Ives

We love the rustic, barefoot luxury feel of this self-catering seaside retreat overlooking Porthmeor Beach, just a short walk down the road from Tate St Ives.

Set within an old, pink fisherman’s cottage, the crisp-white and soft-pink interiors take on a pleasingly weathered look thanks to 200-year-old floorboards and window frames that have been salvaged from old ships.

And yet this beach house offers exceptional comfort, from sofas you want to sink right into to an indulgent Victorian slipper bath and a balcony overhanging the beach, which seems ready-made for your morning coffee.

Alba Beach House is available to book via Unique Homestays here.

Of course, once your accommodation is all booked and you've read up on this beautiful place, you're ready to set off on your adventure along the Cornish coastal path. If there's one thing I wouldn't leave without however, it's a good old-fashioned map. Cornwall Coast Path: (Trailblazer British Walking Guide) is available on Amazon and covers the entire 288-mile footpath with 142 large-scale walking maps to guide you on your way. Happy walking - don't forget your pasty!

item.title: I thought I knew St Patrick – then I discovered these 9 shocking facts – and how I’m celebrating St Patrick’s Day 2026 like a true Irish local
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: St Patrick – the patron saint of Ireland – is steeped in mythology and legend. From shamrocks to slavery, shepherds, and snakes, here are 9 facts about St Patrick that we think we know to be true, and our guide to celebrating St Patrick’s Day, wherever you are in the world
item.content:encoded:
Around the world, 17 March can usually be identified by a sea of people wearing green, more shamrocks than you've ever seen before and a whole lot of Guinness. But what are we celebrating?
