Of course, this is St Patrick's Day, the annual celebration of St Patrick, Ireland's Patron Saint, which, just like other Saints Days, is now a day for celebrating the rich culture and heritage of the Irish.

17 March is generally considered to be St Patrick's death day, and his saint's day traditionally commemorates the arrival of Christianity in Ireland. Today, in Ireland St Patrick’s Day is a public holiday, and celebrations involve public parades, festivals, céilidhs and wearing green and shamrocks.

Celebrations too, are echoed around the world in places with large Irish communities, particularly in the United States, Australia, Canada, the United Kingdom, and Argentina. For a deeper dive into St Patrick's strong legacy around the world, Saint Patrick: Life, Legacy and Legend is available on Amazon and is an engaging exploration of his story and why he is celebrated around the world today, not just in Ireland.

Who was St Patrick?

St Patrick was a 5th-century missionary who arrived in Ireland in the second half of the century to spread the word of Christianity. He made his roots in Northern Ireland in County Armagh and County Down.

Here are 10 things about St Patrick we think we know to be true

1. St Patrick was born around 385AD

St Patrick was born circa 385 in England, to a father who was a deacon and a mother who was supposedly related to St Martin of Tours. Despite his holy connections, St Patrick is not thought to have had a particularly zealous religious upbringing.

2. His real name was thought to be Maewyn Succat

3. At 16, he was kidnapped and taken as a slave to Ireland

The story of St Patrick really starts when he was 16 years old. He was captured by Irish bandits and sold into slavery in County Antrim. Legend has it he was sold to a druid chief. He remained in captivity here for six years, tending sheep in the Slemish mountains.

4. While among the sheep, he “found God”

According to folklore he saw this time as God’s way of testing his faith, and he soon became devoted to Christianity.

5. He escaped and returned to his family in England, where he became a priest

Around 408, St Patrick is said to have had a dream in which he escaped slavery and returned to England. Upon waking he decided to make this dream a reality. He escaped his captors and convinced some sailors to let him travel on their ship.

But, according to one interpretation of the tale, he and the rest of the crew had to abandon ship in France. St Patrick then wandered lost for 28 days and 200 miles.

6. He took the Christian-Roman name of Patricius, which was later known as Patrick

After being reunited with his family back in England, Patrick returned to France, where he entered the priesthood under the leadership of St Germain.

7. He returned to Ireland to convert the pagan Irish to Christianity

Around the year 431, the story of St Patrick in Ireland continues. He was sent back to Ireland on the instruction of Pope St Celestine I. St Patrick returned with the aim of introducing Christianity to the country, something he had vowed to do since his days in captivity.

He initially aimed for the coast of Antrim. However, strong currents swept his boat through Strangford Lough and he landed in County Down instead.

8. Over three decades, he is said to have travelled the Emerald Isle

Although initially treated with hostility, he persevered, even using the three leaves of the shamrock to teach about the Holy Trinity. He established monasteries and schools and baptised and ordained priests.

While legend credits St Patrick’s evangelising with converting the Irish to Christianity, historians believe there were already Christian believers in Ireland by the time Patrick arrived.

9. It is unclear where the myth of him banishing snakes from Ireland originated

It is widely accepted that there never were any snakes in Ireland!

Patrick is said to have died in Downpatrick on 17 March 461. He was buried in the graveyard of the town’s modest church. You can still visit his grave today. Downpatrick is also home to the only permanent exhibition in the world about Saint Patrick at the Saint Patrick Centre.

It wasn’t until the 17th century that St Patrick’s Day was made an official feast day. Over the centuries it has grown into one of the biggest celebrations in the international calendar.

How to celebrate St Patrick's Day 2026

Ireland

Of course, the most iconic celebrations for St Patrick's Day will take place in Ireland itself. Dublin and Belfast hold the most spectacular St Patrick's Day celebrations in Ireland, and both cities will transform into an enormous, colourful St Patrick's Day playground.

But where better to celebrate St Patrick's Day than at the place he called home? The Home of Saint Patrick Festival takes place in Armagh every year and is a lively showcase of music, culture, heritage and arts in his home county.

For a full list of Irish St Patrick's Day events, you can go to the Ireland.com website here.

New York

Established in 1762, New York's St Patrick's Day parade is iconic and wonderful. It will take place Tuesday, 17 March 2026, along Fifth Avenue, Manhattan, beginning at 11 am. Expect a joyous display of colour, dancing and music, and a fantastic day out!

For more information on who will be parading and timings, visit their website here.

Chicago

The US city of Chicago's annual river dyeing (yes, it turns green) is probably the most unique St Patrick's Day celebration in the world. Just like New York, there will also be a lively parade and events all over the city. Find out more information about St Patrick's Day in Chicago here.

How to celebrate St. Patrick's Day at home

If you'll be celebrating in the comfort of your own home, as well as dressing up and drinking Guinness, why not have a go at making some authentic Irish recipes? Irish Delights: St. Patrick's Day Cookbook: Authentic Recipes and Irish Traditions is available on Amazon and is your passport to a vibrant celebration of Ireland, and will bring the warmth and flavours of Ireland in your own kitchen.

Many visitors to Ireland will also celebrate St Patrick's Day by walking the St Patrick's Way through the counties of Armagh and Down in Northern Ireland, a route rich in natural beauty and fascinating heritage.

item.title: This is the closest you’ll ever get to sleeping inside Buckingham Palace - an iconic London royal landmark brimming with history is set open this spring as this year’s most exciting luxury hotel
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: The Mall’s Admiralty Arch will soon offer guests the chance to experience views previously only reserved for royalty as it is set to open as a new luxury hotel this spring
item.content:encoded:
If you've ever wanted to know what it's like to sleep inside an actual royal palace, you may just be one step closer to achieving that dream thanks to a new opening in London this spring.

Joining the ranks of London's most historic hotels, where stories of the past are etched into the walls, the Mall's Admiralty Arch, which was built for Queen Victoria, will soon open as a luxurious Waldorf Astoria hotel.

The building, which sits at the end of the Mall on the opposite end from Buckingham Palace, was commissioned by the King Edward VII and has played host to Winston Churchill and Ian Fleming, has spent the last hundred years serving as a ceremonial gateway to London's royal centre - it is probably the closest you'll ever get to staying in the palace itself.

Admiralty Arch has also been the subject of several crime thrillers, including SJT Riley's Under Admiralty Arch, which is available on Amazon, set in 1950s London and telling the story of a London Herald crime-reporter who gets wrapped up in a real-life murder - a must-read before you book your stay.

What is the history of London's Admiralty Arch?

Completed in 1912, Admiralty Arch was originally commissioned by King Edward VII in honour of his mother, Queen Victoria and was designed by architect Aston Webb, who also designed the Victoria Memorial.

The grand neoclassical building, which crowns the Mall, has long been a symbol of royalty in London, framing some of the most iconic moments of the last century, from royal celebrations to Olympic parades. It features three large arches through which you walk to head out to Trafalgar Square one way and the Mall the other; only royalty are permitted to drive through the central arch.

Its name originates from the fact that it adjoins the Old Admiralty Building, and when it was first built, it served as the official residence of the First Sea Lord, including Louis Mountbatten. Winston Churchill also had an office there.

The transformation of the Admiralty Arch into a luxury hotel

With renovations to the building having begun in 2020, in spring 2026, Admiralty Arch will open to the public for the first time in its history, this time in a very different capacity.

Following hot on the heels of another London-heritage-icon-to-hotel transformation as Raffles London at the OWO opened last summer, the £1 billion project will see the landmark transformed into London's newest luxury hotel by Hilton's Waldorf Astoria.

Featuring around 100 rooms and suites, a holistic spa and wellness centre, a ballroom, and a signature restaurant and bar, the hotel will elegantly combine British heritage and modern luxury so the building's fascinating and important history remains central to its future.

Many suites will offer guests views directly down the Mall towards Buckingham Palace, the official residence of King Charles III, and London's most iconic royal building. This once-in-a-lifetime view has historically been reserved exclusively for monarchs during royal ceremonies on the mall, but will now be open to any paying guest.

Guests of the hotel can expect interiors fit for royalty, with polished marble, sculptured light fittings and beautiful bespoke furnishings, offering an elegant, luxurious stay that nods to the landmark's royal and Edwardian history.

This is your chance to walk the same corridors as Winston Churchill, dine where kings once dined and sleep within the most important royal stretch of road in Britain, or even the world.

When will Admiralty Arch open?

The hotel is scheduled for a spring or summer 2026 opening - keep your eyes peeled here for further updates.

The landmark's opening comes in an exciting year for royal history fans, as we celebrate the centenary of the birth of Queen Elizabeth II, which will be marked with the largest ever exhibition of her fashion at Buckingham Palace - why not book into the Admiralty Arch afterwards?

item.title: These are the loveliest daffodil gardens to visit in Britain this spring - from the castle of a queen, to Wordsworth’s favourite spot, carpets of these happy yellow flowers await
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: It’s a sure sign that spring is on its way when the dark green shoots of daffodils start to break through the frost-covered ground. Here is our guide to the best daffodil gardens this spring, and some of the best daffodil-themed events
item.content:encoded:
When St David's Day comes around on 1 March, spring is heralded in Britain thanks to the lovely displays of cream-coloured petals and yellow trumpets of our happiest wild flower: daffodils.

Throughout March, the jolly heads of daffodils continuously appear, letting us know that warmer days are on their way and Britain's best spring gardens begin their preparations for the bright and blooming season ahead.

As many stately homes and gardens reopen their doors to visitors for spring and summer in mid-March, many places will mark the beginning of the season by boasting of their daffodil-carpeted gardens, or even with special daffodil-themed events - a perfect early spring afternoon out.

We've rounded up the best places to see daffodils in Britain in 2026, to bring a little sunshine into your March, even if the sun itself isn't shining.

If your trip to one of these beautiful daffodil gardens has inspired you to grow your own, I'd recommend Daffodils: The ultimate guide for growing, flowering and cutting beautiful narcissi varieties for daffodils at home and in the garden, which is available on Amazon and is your ultimate guide to growing this beautiful spring flower, and all its different varieties in all their colourful glory.

The best daffodil gardens to visit in Britain this spring

1. Hever Castle, Kent

Beautiful all year round, the historic home of Queen Anne Boleyn is considered one of the best daffodil gardens in the UK.

In 2026, Hever Castle will mark the arrival of spring with a very special announcement this March - their very own signature daffodil named after the famous castle. The 'Hever Castle' daffodil has been grown exclusively for the estate by the renowned Taylors Bulbs of Lincolnshire.

Neil Miller, Hever Castle's head gardener said, “The ‘Hever Castle’ daffodil is a truly magnificent specimen with a bright golden trumpet - its colour immediately reminded me of the bright yellows loved by William Waldorf Astor, who transformed the estate with daffodils over 120 years ago, and it feels very special to be continuing that story today with this new introduction that’s so aptly named for a place now considered one of the best daffodil gardens in the UK.”

The announcement comes just in time for Hever's Dazzling Daffodils event, from 16-22 March 2026. Visitors will be able to see 250,000 marvellous daffodil trumpets dancing on Hever's historic lawns, alongside special workshops, tours of Anne Boleyn's orchard (usually closed to visitors) and a specialist plant fair.

2. The Golden Triangle, Gloucestershire

Though not technically a garden, more of an area, Gloucestershire's 'Golden Triangle' is one of the best places to see daffodils in the UK. At one time, Gloucestershire was the commercial centre for growing wild daffodils; the flowers were sent for sale in London’s major markets. A vast area within the county became known as the Golden Triangle for the sheer number of daffodils growing there.

Over time, the wild flowers have declined as modern agricultural practices have taken over. However, the Wildlife Trust has now taken over conservation of much of the area, and visitors can still make their way around the circular, 10-mile Daffodil Way, taking in Kempley and Dymock Wood en route.

3. Leeds Castle, Kent

From 15 March - 30 April 2026, visitors to the beautiful storybook-esque Leeds Castle in Kent will be treated to a display of 25 different daffodil species and 15,000 hybrids carpeting its grounds.

You can download the Darling Daffodil Map from their website, which will guide you through the grounds, where you'll be able to admire the incredible daffodil displays.

4. Felley Priory, Nottinghamshire

In the grounds surrounding Felley Priory, daffodils dominate the orchard in springtime. Some extremely rare species can be found amid the blaze of yellow, apricot and orange flowers. Varieties include Green Pearl, Toto and the minute Baby Moon.

Parts of the house itself date from the 12th century, as sections from the original priory church were repurposed within its construction after the Dissolution of the Monasteries. The gardens, however, are the creation of the Chaworth-Musters family, which first took ownership of the property in 1822, but wouldn’t go on to create the spectacular horticultural haven until the 1970s, when the family went to live at the property full-time.

5. Ullswater, Cumbria

Again, not technically a garden, but Ullswater in the Lake District is one of the best places to see daffodils every spring.

William Wordsworth would often visit Ullswater and stroll around the wonderful Lake District landscape. It was one such stroll that is thought to have inspired the Romantic poet to write one of his most famous works, Daffodils, in 1804.

Today, visitors to the area can still see golden rows of daffodils along the banks of the lake, whether travelling on foot or on the Ullswater Steamer, which has been ferrying people up and down the length of the water for over 150 years.

6. Anglesey Abbey, Cambridgeshire

The beautiful Anglesey Abbey in Cambridgeshire is well-known for its vibrant daffodil displays in March and April, featuring more than 40 varieties, including massive plantings of rare types like 'Dutch Master' and 'Rijnveld’s Early Sensation'.

To get the best displays, wander along the main drive, the Winter Garden and Coronation Avenue. The Abbey also runs special guided daffodil tours of the gardens in the spring months - there are more details on their website.

7. Wimpole, Cambridgeshire

The working estate of Wimpole in Cambridgeshire, with its impressive 17th-century manor house, has one of the most impressive daffodil displays in the country every spring.

Their daffodils, which number over 80 different varieties, flower as early as January and continue into early May, transforming the pleasure grounds into a sea of yellow.

This spring, book onto a special tour of their daffodils with a daffodil expert and learn about all the different varieties. Book your slot via their website here.

For more beautiful gardens, the National Trust's Gardens of the National Trust is the definitive guide to the most beautiful gardens in Britain, and their contexts within Britain's fascinating horticultural history.

item.title: Was Queen Charlotte Britain’s first black queen? This is the powerful legacy of the enigmatic Bridgerton star - Britain’s longest-serving queen consort
item.dc:creator: Felicity Day, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Despite holding a royal record and having numerous places named in her honour, Queen Charlotte has stayed out of the limelight until Bridgerton came along. Here, we explore the legacy of the woman who may have been Britain’s first black queen
item.content:encoded:
Late on the sultry evening of 8 September 1761, in the capital city of a country she had never set foot in before, a 17-year-old Princess Charlotte married a man she had only set eyes on for the first time six hours earlier, Britain’s new King, George III. 

It was the beginning of one of royal history’s longest and most eventful unions. Yet despite being labelled by historians as one of Britain's greatest queens, Queen Charlotte has enjoyed little of the limelight.

This has changed in recent years, thanks to her starring role in Netflix's Queen Charlotte: A Bridgerton Story, which has portrayed her as one of Britain's 'most iconic, powerful, and unforgettable' Queens, and also, thanks to its casting, has re-raised the long-debated historical question, was Queen Charlotte black?

But still, few people know much about the nation’s longest-serving Queen Consort.

If you haven't seen the brilliant Bridgerton spin-off series created by Netflix in her honour, then I would recommend reading the book version first, Queen Charlotte, which is available on Amazon. Written by Bridgerton's clever author, Julia Quinn, it is a highly romantic Regency story that will leave you swooning.

So, who was Queen Charlotte, and how did she become queen?

Born in 1744, Princess Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz came from a tiny German principality. Though she spoke no English, her royal blood and strong Protestant faith, sound education and obliging nature had made her an ideal candidate for the serious young George III, who had ascended to the throne at 22, determined to marry a suitable, like-minded bride, who would help to improve the moral reputation of the royals.

Was Queen Charlotte a woman of colour?

Bridgerton and Queen Charlotte have stirred up some debate about Queen Charlotte's possible bi-racial heritage, which Netflix has championed in the series with their casting, though historians are still in great debate about it.

According to some historians, Charlotte was descended from a Portuguese ancestor, 500 years removed, who was a woman of colour. Whilst there are other claims that Charlotte has an 'African appearance' in contemporary portraiture.

The theory of Queen Charlotte's Portuguese descent gained popularity in the late 20th century thanks to Mario De Valdez y Cocom, a historian of the African diaspora. He argued that Charlotte was directly descended from Margarita de Castro y Sousa, a 15th-century Portuguese noblewoman whose ancestry can be traced back from 13th-century ruler Alfonso III and his lover Madragana, who Valdez believes was North African, and thus, in his opnion, black.

Valdez used contemporary portraits of the queen by Sir Allan Ramsay to support his theory, claiming that 'his representations of her were the most decidedly African of all her portraits."

However, while it is possible that Valdez's ancestry theory was correct, given the 15 generations (500 years) between Charlotte and her ancestor, it seems highly unlikely that she would exhibit these features.

There are also arguments that artists of the era often 'softened' features to align with European standards, which makes any contemporary portrait of her highly unreliable for establishing racial characteristics. Considering how groundbreaking it would have been for Britain to have a black queen in 1761, you would have thought that if it were true, contemporary reporters would have had a field day, and we would certainly still know about it today.

The truth is, frustratingly, we'll never really know, but the idea that her ancestors were of African origin is entirely possible.

If, like me, you enjoy some harmless historical fiction, Tina Andrews Queen Charlotte Sophia: A Royal Affair is available on Amazon, and is a charming fictional take on Charlotte and George's love story and Charlotte's ethnicity, which is a totally different perspective to Julia Quinn's.

Were Queen Charlotte and King George III really in love?

What has never been in doubt is that Charlotte’s union with the King quickly blossomed from an arranged marriage to a love match. The couple discovered a shared passion for music and shared interests in everything from old masters to astronomy. They rejoiced in retreating together from the eyes of the world, mocked by some for their middle-class domesticity. Buckingham House, purchased by George for his new wife in 1762, became their favourite abode. Fourteen of their fifteen children were born there. 

But what might seem like a blissful existence, combining love with every luxury, was not entirely so for Charlotte. An affectionate marriage meant she found herself pregnant more or less continuously for over two decades. No ‘prisoner could wish more ardently for his liberty,’ she once complained.

Similarly, her royal duties provided little scope for intellectual stimulation. The clever Charlotte – privately ‘of the opinion that if women had the same advantages as men in their education, they might do as well’ – often reported boredom and frustration. Her status was also socially isolating. Involvement in politics was firmly discouraged by George, as was the forming of close friendships with aristocratic ladies, which might be construed as political bias.

King George III and his mental illness

Of course, the greatest trial of her life was King George III's mental illness, now thought by many scholars to be the result of bipolar disorder. His first serious episode in 1788 was shattering for Charlotte. She had to watch her sleepless, violent and incessantly chattering husband confined by doctors; her distress compounded by unexpected humiliation.

During his derangement, the King was prone, in front of various courtiers, to make unmistakably amorous remarks about her lady-in-waiting, the Countess of Pembroke, whom he called his true wife. In lewd language he would accuse Charlotte of adultery. Often he treated her with intense hostility, once gloating that he loved their dogs more than her. It shocked observers who had been more used to witnessing the King’s spontaneous displays of affection towards his wife. 

The experience changed Charlotte. Her hair turned white. She became reluctant to spend time alone with George, increasingly living a separate life – especially after his progressively more violent bouts of illness in 1801 and 1804 – and locking her door against him at night. She became depressed and bad-tempered, particularly towards her unmarried daughters, who themselves resented being kept closeted away from society, especially after George’s most serious relapse in 1810, after which he was largely a recluse.

Queen Charlotte as a champion for women

But Queen Charlotte deserves to be remembered for far more than her misfortunes. Not only was she a champion for her husband – controlling the sensitive information released about his health, encouraging loyalty to the absent King and protecting his political interests – but in charitable terms, she was a champion of her female subjects.

She was Patron of both the first maternity hospital and the Magdalen, a refuge for ‘penitent’ prostitutes, with her approval helping to challenge public prejudice. She also supported schools for girls and almshouses for impoverished women. 

Charlotte also promoted female artists. At Frogmore House near Windsor, which she purchased in 1792 as her own retreat from the court, she commissioned Mary Moser, whom she also employed as a drawing master for her daughters, to create a spectacular decorative scheme, in which the flower paintings for which she was famed seem to cascade from the walls.

In fact, it’s probable that Moser and fellow artist Angelica Kauffman owed their positions as founder members of London’s Royal Academy of Arts – the only female academicians in the first 150 years of its history – to Charlotte’s influence. 

Moser’s room at Frogmore was said to be her favourite, perhaps because of the sense of harmony with the gardens beyond, so loved by Charlotte, a keen botanist. At Kew, she became actively involved with the famous gardens founded by George’s grandmother, acquiring and attempting to grow exotic specimens.

A keen collector in general, at Kew Palace she also assembled a menagerie which included some of Britain’s first kangaroos; while Frogmore held her vast library of 4,000 books, and the decorative snuff boxes that attest to her addiction to finely ground tobacco.

Private pursuits aside, Charlotte was a tireless figurehead for high society, even after 1811, when her son George (the future George IV) reigned as Regent in his father’s place. Though in her late 60s and ailing by then, she continued to conduct royal engagements and preside over presentations at court, right up until a few months before her death at Kew in November 1818.

She was ‘not gifted with any splendid or commanding endowments’, ran the obituary in The Times. But while she might not have been the most memorable or pioneering of the queens in our history, like so many of them, Charlotte had both a strong sense of duty and remarkable resilience. 

For a historian's take on this enigmatic, under-celebrated queen, Natalee Garrett's biography Queen Charlotte: Family, Duty Scandal is available on Amazon and is a well-rounded, comprehensive, nuanced, and sensitive analysis of Queen Charlotte’s life and legacy, far beyond Bridgerton.

item.title: These are the loveliest Mother’s Day events happening at a stately home near you this weekend, from indulgent afternoon teas to crafty workshops and admiring the beautiful first spring blooms
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: If you are yet to plan your Mother’s Day outing, here is our guide to the very best Mother’s Day events happening at stately homes up and down the country this weekend
item.content:encoded:
By the time Mother's Day comes around every March, the world always feels a little brighter. Lovely yellow daffodils are out in full bloom, lambs can be spotted playing in green fields, and everyone seems a little bit more smiley as the spring sun shines down on us after winter.

In Britain, there is no better way to celebrate the arrival of spring, or Mother's Day, than a visit to one of our finest stately homes, many of which reopen their doors around mid-March, and to make the day even more perfect, you can enjoy an indulgent afternoon tea at so many beautiful stately homes, castles and palaces.

So when it comes to choosing a treat for mum on Mother's Day, the right answer is right in front of you - a historic day at a beautiful stately home, followed by a luxurious afternoon tea, and a glass or two of Champagne, of course!

If you'd like a little gift for mum to take home after your perfect stately day, The National Trust Afternoon Tea Cookbook is available on Amazon and is the ideal gift for mums, grannies, aunts, or just about anyone who loves afternoon tea and stately homes!

And for mums who love beautiful gardens, The National Trust School of Gardening is available at the National Trust gift shop, and is a perfect Mother's Day gift after a day exploring some of Britain's most beautiful stately home gardens, packed full of helpful gardening advice and tips.

Mother's Day events at stately homes 2026

Many stately homes and castles are hosting special events for Mother's Day 2026, which will fall on Sunday, 15 March. Here is our guide to the best stately homes to visit this Mother's Day.

1. Waddesdon Manor, Buckinghamshire

Easily accessible from London, the incredible Waddesdon Manor in Buckinghamshire makes for a fantastic day out all year round, with its extraordinary collection of art and antiques, fascinating history and beautiful grounds.

Their special Mother's Day afternoon tea has been lovingly created by Waddesdon's Head Pâtissier and features an indulgent selection of savoury treats and sweet fancies. Afterwards, enjoy a relaxing stroll through the daffodil-covered grounds in the spring sunshine.

Book your afternoon tea here.

2. Hampton Court Palace, London

This Mother's Day weekend, the historic Hampton Court Palace will be hosting one of its semi-regular Open Garden Weekends, with free entry to parts of the incredible gardens, with no ticket or pre-booking required, which are just starting to come into bloom.

Take mum to see the early spring displays in Henry VIII's favourite palace and book into afternoon tea, or a lovely Sunday roast at The Mitre hotel, which occupies a prime riverside spot opposite the magnificent Tudor palace.

3. Hever Castle, Kent

Probably my favourite castle in Britain, there is nowhere better than Hever Castle in the springtime, and this weekend they are hosting a special Mother's Day event, so you can treat your mum to a charming historic day out at Anne Boleyn's childhood home.

You'll find special Mother's Day stalls in the grounds as you wonder around, selling plants and gifts, and take the opportunity to see their new exhibition Capturing a Queen, which brings together the largest ever gathering of portraits believed to be Anne Boleyn.

You can also book into a luxury afternoon tea in the oak-panelled Tudor Suite, overlooking the castle and lawns - hurry, there are limited spaces left! Book yours here.

4. Michelham Priory, East Sussex

Mums go free this Mother's Day weekend at the historic Michelham Priory House and Gardens in the heart of beautiful East Sussex (when accompanied by a paying guest).

Wander through their stunning display of 80,000 blooming daffodils, signalling the start of spring, explore the Tudor mansion and soak up the atmosphere at this beautiful historic site, nestled in the South Downs.

To make her day even sweeter, mums can even get a free slice of cake when purchasing a hot drink in the café!

Book your spot here.

5. Burghley House, Lincolnshire

The Orangery restaurant at the magnificent Elizabethan Burghley House in Lincolnshire will be serving up a very special afternoon tea this Mother's Day, with delectable sweet treats, sandwiches, scones and lashings of tea.

Their South Gardens will also open for the spring this weekend, so enjoy a post-meal stroll through the blankets of daffodils in the 18th-century Capability Brown-designed parkland.

Book your ticket here.

6. Stansted Park, West Sussex

Also offering free admission for mums this Mother's Day is the beautiful Stansted Park in West Sussex, which will open its doors for tours of the historic home and its lovely gardens. The Park's stunning Chapel will also be open for visitors to step in and admire it this Mother's Day.

On Saturday 14 March, Stansted will also host spring floristry workshops - so if you haven't already ordered your bouquet for mum, you can make her a handmade one instead!

Book your slot here.

7. Leeds Castle, Kent

Finishing our list of Mothering Sunday events is the special Mother's Day afternoon tea at Leeds Castle in Kent, where you can enjoy a luxurious feast, topped off with a glass of bubbly, overlooking the storybook castle.

As part of your visit, take time to enjoy the breathtaking springtime blooms with a walk through the park, and why not experience the brand new Eleanor of Castile AI Exhibition for the history lovers!

Book your ticket here.

While we're celebrating Mother's Days, why not peruse our guide to the greatest mothers in British history - from queens to authors, politicians and housewives, they all have one thing in common - motherhood.

item.title: This is your ultra-decadent guide to the very best English stately homes and castles to enjoy afternoon tea, so you can have your cake and eat it too
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: There can be nothing more perfect on a spring or summer’s day than a visit to a stately home, castle or palace, topped off with an English afternoon tea. Here is our guide to 9 of the best stately homes for afternoon tea
item.content:encoded:
Cake? tea? sandwich? Admire a priceless work of Renaissance art before a stroll around a manicured garden and a look around the gift shop? I can't think of a more perfect way to spend a day.

There can be nothing more lovely than a visit to one of England’s finest stately homes, castles or palaces, and no more perfect, or British, way to top off the day than with an elegant afternoon tea.

Invented by Britain's own Anna Maria Russell, the 7th Duchess of Bedford, who needed something to cure her 'sinking feeling' of hunger between lunch and a late dinner, the first afternoon tea was enjoyed at the beautiful Woburn Abbey in Bedfordshire, so it seems only right to continue this tradition of enjoying afternoon teas in the beautiful surrounds of England's most impressive stately homes and castles.

If you'd like to bring a little slice of English afternoon tea elegance to your own home, I recommend The National Trust Book of Afternoon Tea, which is available on Amazon, and is packed full of delectable recipes for a delectable afternoon tea.

So, without further ado, here is our guide to 9 of the most perfect stately homes, castles and palaces to enjoy afternoon tea, where you can have your cake...and eat it too.

1. Woburn Abbey, Bedfordshire

Of course, we had to start our list at the very place where afternoon tea was invented, Woburn Abbey in Bedfordshire.

The quintessential English tradition of afternoon tea is said to have started around 1840 by Duchess Anna Maria, the 7th Duchess of Bedford, and lifelong friend of Queen Victoria, who entertained her friends in the Blue Drawing Room at Woburn Abbey.

Although Woburn Abbey is currently undergoing major refurbishment and is closed to visitors, afternoon-tea seekers can head to the estate's Woburn Hotel in the village, where they will be treated to a traditional spread of sandwiches, scones, jam and, of course, tea.

2. Blenheim Palace, Woodstock

The only non-royal palace in the UK, Blenheim Palace, is the perfect place to enjoy a traditional afternoon tea in the elegant surroundings only a palace could provide. Being Winston Churchill's birthplace, his favourite tipple of Champagne is a must.

Guests are served afternoon tea in the elegant and light Orangery Restaurant, overlooking the Duke's private Italian gardens, a truly inspired setting.

3. Burghley House, Lincolnshire

Visitors to the magnificent Elizabethan Burghley House are served afternoon tea in the beautiful Orangery restaurant, overlooking the Capability Brown-designed topiary in the South Gardens.

On sunny days, take your homemade scones, jam and clotted cream to be enjoyed in the peaceful and romantic Rose Garden, à la Elizabeth I and her Lord Burghley (well, almost).

4. Castle Howard, York

This Baroque masterpiece in Yorkshire, well known for standing in as Brideshead in Brideshead Revisited, is set within a landscape of unparalleled beauty, and afternoon tea is the only way to complement a perfect day here.

There are several cafés within the grounds of Castle Howard, where you can enjoy a refreshing cup of tea and a piece of cake, but my favourite is the picnic afternoon tea on offer. Pre-order and collect on the day of your visit to enjoy dainty sandwiches, hand-baked scones, cakes and iced tea anywhere you like in Castle Howard's beautiful grounds - bliss!

5. Chatsworth, Derbyshire

I think Lizzie and Mr Darcy would certainly be fans of afternoon tea, so where better to enjoy the tradition than at the house that stood in for Pemberley in the 2005 Pride & Prejudice?

Afternoon tea at Chatsworth is served in the Cavendish restaurant, in the early 18th-century stables designed by architect James Paine. A decadent treat, you'll indulge in four courses of luxurious savouries, sandwiches, cakes and scones, washed down with a pot of tea and a glass of Champagne.

6. Harewood House, Leeds

Another of England's great treasure houses, and home to some of the finest Chippendale furniture in Britain, afternoon tea at the Robert Adam-designed Harewood House in Yorkshire is served in a relaxed fashion beneath iconic landscape paintings by J. M. W. Turner.

Enjoy a traditional spread with spectacular views over the award-winning formal parterre and “Capability” Brown landscape as Red Kites soar overhead. 

7. Hatfield House, Hertfordshire

Probably Britain's best-preserved Jacobean House, this beautiful stately home has been a royal residence (it was the nursery of the future Queen Elizabeth I), a tank testing station in World War I, and a filming location for one of Netflix's best-loved period dramas, The Crown.

Afternoon tea at Hatfield House is served in the historic Coach House bakery, where you'll enjoy delicious cakes, pastries, sandwiches and scones, and on warmer days, homemade treats can be enjoyed from the outside upper terrace of the restaurant, which looks out towards the front of the House. 

8. Leeds Castle, Kent

There can be no place more perfect to enjoy afternoon tea than at the storybook Leeds Castle in Kent.

Afternoon tea is served at Castle View restaurant, which does exactly what it says on the tin, and offers the most stunning view of this medieval castle with its moat and surrounding parkland.

The sumptuous afternoon tea itself is inspired by the previous owner Lady Baillie's chef de patisserie, Maurice Sauget. Enjoy a selection of teas and coffee, traditional finger sandwiches, freshly baked scones with all the trimmings and a selection of fine handmade cakes.  

9. Highclere Castle, Hampshire

Last but far from least, what could be better than afternoon tea at the real-life Downton Abbey, Highclere Castle?

You'll have the choice between a special picnic afternoon tea for two, which includes a selection of cakes, scones and sandwiches and a half bottle of Joseph Perrier Champagne to enjoy in the grounds after your tour of the house, or a Champagne afternoon tea served in the Coach House. This includes a selection of delicious finger sandwiches, cakes and scones served with a glass of Joseph Perrier ‘Highclere Castle’ Champagne - the perfect way to end a wonderfully English day.

If my guide to the most perfect stately homes and castles to enjoy afternoon tea has inspired you, why not read up a little more on the history of this wonderfully British tradition? Afternoon Tea: A History and Guide to the Great Edwardian Tradition is available on Amazon and will guide you through its history, afternoon tea etiquette, what to wear and also includes some delicious contemporary recipes to try at home - chin chin!

item.title: Are the royal family actually German, and why on earth did they change their name? This is the hidden secret of the most famous family in the world
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: It doesn’t get more British than the Windsors, but did you know that that isn’t really their surname? This is the history of the German roots of the British royal family, dating back 300 years
item.content:encoded:
Ask anyone what they think of when they think of Great Britain, and chances are they'll mention the Royal Family, most likely our late Queen Elizabeth, probably with a few of her beloved corgis following behind her.

But did you know that if you look at a timeline of all the kings and queens of Great Britain, our Royal House of Windsor, which includes Queen Elizabeth II, King Charles III and the future king Prince William, actually has German roots, dating back 300 years?

Of course, the British royal family are undeniably the most British thing about this country, but that's what makes it even more interesting. Yes, the royal family is actually German, and their links to Germany and to the royal families across Europe make for a fascinating historical study.

For more on this endlessly interesting subject, Robert Palmer's Crowned Cousins: Anglo-German Royal Connection is available on Amazon and delves deeper into the two key periods of our recent royal history, during which our monarch looked to the British and German peoples as inseparably connected.

For now, here is a brief timeline of the royal family's historical links to Germany, and an explanation as to why we can say that they are, in fact, German.

The House of Hanover

The British Royal Family's German reputation began in 1714, when Queen Anne, the last Stuart monarch, died without an heir.

Although Anne had several much closer relatives by blood, including the 'Old Pretender', her Catholic half-brother James Francis Edward Stuart (father of Bonnie Prince Charlie), the issue was that he was the wrong religion. The Act of Settlement of 1701 excluded all Catholics from being eligible for the throne, so all eyes turned to Queen Anne's closest living Protestant relative.

This just happened to be her German second cousin of the House of Hanover, who was crowned as King George I.

When George ascended the throne in 1714, he was probably not fluent in English, preferring to speak to his ministers in French, Italian or even Latin. This language barrier reduced his presence in cabinet meetings, along with frequent return trips to Hanover, making him unpopular and leading to the increased power of his ministers, most notably Robert Walpole, who effectively became Britain's first Prime Minister.

For a concise overview of King George I's reign, I'd recommend Tim Blanning's George I: The Lucky King, which is available on Amazon, and tells his story as the son of a landless German Duke to becoming the sovereign of three kingdoms.

The German line continued with George II, George III, and, you've guessed it, George IV. Although, of course, we don't tend to think of these kings as German, but rather British. Particularly George III and George IV, two of British history's best-known kings, one for his role in American independence and his so-called 'madness', and the other for his party-loving lifestyle and long stream of mistresses...

But these kings' German connection didn't stop with their grandfather and then great-grandfather, for generations, British royals continued to marry into German families with royal connections.

George II married Caroline of Brandenburg-Ansbach, George III married Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz and in 1795, George IV married the German princess Caroline of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel, who was also his first cousin.

They had no surviving children, so the throne then passed to George's younger brother, who became William IV. William married another German princess, Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen.

With no surviving children either, the throne then passed to the young Princess Victoria, daughter of the Duke of Kent and his wife, yes, another German princess, Victoria of Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld.

Was Queen Victoria German?

The short answer is yes, Queen Victoria's mother was German, which made her half German.

Of course, Victoria had to carry on the marrying-German tradition, and she married her first cousin, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha.

It is Prince Albert's German influence on British culture that we still feel today, most notably at Christmas. It is thanks to Prince Albert's German-ness that we have Christmas trees inside.

Prince Albert and Queen Victoria had nine children, whom they strategically married off across the royal families of Europe. Their eldest son, Edward VII, became the first British monarch of the House of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha, which cemented their German identity.

Why did the royal family change their name?

During the First World War, however, Edward's son, George V, changed their name because of the connotations with Germany, with whom they were at war. It was then that they became the Windsor family that we know today.

The German blood continued, though, despite the name change. George V's wife, Mary of Teck, had German blood on both sides and when Princess Elizabeth, the future Queen Elizabeth II, married Prince Philip, his German links initially attracted controversy, the country having just emerged from another devastating war with Germany.

Prince Philip's mother was Princess Alice of Battenberg, who was a Hessian princess (from Hessen in Germany) by birth, descended from Louis IV, the Grand Duke of Hesse.

In 1947, when Elizabeth and Philip married, Philip changed his 'Battenberg' surname and adopted his maternal grandparents' surname of Mountbatten (an Anglicised version of Battenberg), to help distance himself from his German roots in the eyes of the public.

So, when you ask, are the royal family actually German? The answer is, undeniably, yes.

Although we know them as the Windsors, our royal family's official surname is Saxe-Coburg-Gotha. Though written before the death of Queen Elizabeth II, John Pearson's The Ultimate Family: The Making Of The Royal House Of Windsor is available on Amazon and is a brilliant guide to the huge dynasty that is our royal family and the soap opera of drama that followed them through the 20th century.

item.title: These 8 Scottish spots are guaranteed to take your breath away - this is the ultimate guide to the best places to visit in Scotland that should be on every traveller’s bucket list
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From the bonniest banks to the bonniest braes, vibrant cities and ruined castles, this is our list of the best places to visit in Scotland that are a rite of passage for any visitor to this beautiful country
item.content:encoded:
Is it Scotland's dramatic landscapes that drive its 4 million visitors every year? Perhaps its the 2000 mightily impressive castles, abundant wildlife, or historic towns and cities with vibrant cultural scenes? Or, perhaps, it is all of this, combined with a storied history of brave battles and unassuming heroes, haunting myths and legends that still swirl over the lochs and mountains, and, above all, the strong sense of Scottish-ness that pulses proudly through the blood of every Scot.

Whatever draws you to Scotland, you'll be guaranteed a warm welcome, the chance to see some of the most beautiful scenery in the world, a wee dram to send you on your way, and the feeling that you absolutely have to return - as soon as possible.

If you're planning your trip to Scotland for 2026 or 2027, our very own editor of Scotland Magazine, Sally Coffey's guide to the country is your ultimate travel companion. Moon Scotland: Highland Road Trips, Outdoor Adventures, Pubs & Castles is available on Amazon and had its newest addition released in 2025. Her book will guide you through planning your itinerary with practical, expert advice, honest tips and in-depth coverage of really, everything you need to know.

Choosing my ultimate list of the best places to visit in Scotland is an impossible task, because every place I've ever been to in Scotland has impressed me. Having lived in Scotland during my student days, there are a few places very close to my heart that I simply couldn't leave off this list, but others I have chosen for their cultural significance, sheer beauty, or fascinating history.

So without further ado, I present my ultimate guide to the 8 best places to visit in Scotland (in the full knowledge that hundreds of others should be included too).

The 8 best places to visit in Scotland that should be on every traveller's bucket list

1. Edinburgh

One of my favourite places in the world, Edinburgh, is pure magic - which is why it's hardly surprising JK Rowling was influenced by the Scottish capital when creating Harry Potter.

Absolutely bursting with history, there are interesting things around every corner, and even below your feet (don't miss The Real Mary King's Close the preserved underground street), in the medieval Old Town, from the mighty Edinburgh Castle, standing tall at over 900 years old, to the impressive Palace of Holyroodhouse, the official residence of the monarch in Scotland.

Over in the elegant New Town amongst the beautiful Georgian buildings, you'll discover the enlightenment buzz that inspired so many writers like Sir Walter Scott and philosophers such as David Hume.

From galleries and restaurants to museums and must-see sights, Edinburgh has countless opportunities. Our guide to the 35 best things to do in Edinburgh covers just about everything.

2. Glencoe

The breathtaking, dramatic scenery of Glencoe makes it a must-visit for any visitor to Scotland, but especially history-lovers, hikers, nature-lovers and photographers - so just about everyone, really.

The valley's moody scenery is pretty hard to beat, and is what made it a perfect filming location for Harry Potter, James Bond and Monty Python.

Highlights of the landscape include the Three Sisters mountains - the most photographed spot in Glencoe - the Buachaille Etive Mòr (pyramid-shaped mountain at the valley's head), the Glencoe Valley itself and the Meeting of Three Waters waterfall.

Of course, it is essential to learn the sad history of the Glencoe Massacre before any visit to the beautiful valley, one of the most shocking betrayals in Scottish history, in which the rivalry between Clan MacDonald and Clan Campbell took a dark turn. John Buchan's Massacre of Glencoe is available on Amazon and is a must-read before you visit.

3. Glasgow

Scotland's 'second' city would probably hate its unofficial title, seeing as it has as much going for it as the capital, and is significantly bigger.

An easy gateway to the Highlands and islands, Glasgow is worth stopping and spending time in, with a buzzing cultural centre and some of the best art galleries in all of Scotland.

Home of the realists, 'The Glasgow Boys', and architect, designer and painter Charles Rennie Mackintosh, every city guide to Glasgow should include the city's iconic Kelvingrove art gallery and tea for two at Mackintosh at the Willow tea rooms.

Don't miss the magnificent Gothic cathedral, historic university and the impressive treasures housed at the Burrell Collection.

4. St Andrews

I mentioned there was one place I couldn't not list on my guide to the best places to visit in Scotland and, well, St Andrews is that one place.

This beautiful seaside town in Fife on Scotland's west coast, just an hour north of Edinburgh, is home to some of the country's most interesting history, including being home to Scotland's oldest university, founded in 1413, and the ruins of what was once the country's greatest Cathedral. It is also the home of golf, with the most famous golf course in the world holding pride of place in its beachside spot.

The town's long and beautiful West Sands beach also featured in Chariots of Fire for *that* beach running scene.

Whether you explore the castle and cathedral, peruse the museums and galleries, visit the aquarium or spend an afternoon cosied up in one of the lovely coffee shops, St Andrews has something for everyone.

5. Isle of Skye

The Isle of Skye is so beautiful and so unique that it is almost otherworldly. As you drive 'over the sea to Skye', there is a Tolkien-esque Middle-earthness about the landscape that is truly captivating. It really feels like you are living in a postcard.

Every visitor to the Isle of Skye must hike the dramatic landscapes of the Old Man of Storr rock formation and the 10,000-year-old Quiraing, dip a toe in the crystal-clear waters of the Fairy Pools in Glenbrittle and get lost in the magic of the mystical Fairy Glen near Uig.

They should also take in the history of the Isle's incredible castles, including Dunvegan, home to the Highland clan Macleod, take in the stunning scenery and views from Neist Point Lighthouse and warm their cockles at the colourful harbour town of Portree.

6. Loch Ness

Of course, world-famous for its legendary monster, we can't have a list of the best places to visit in Scotland without Loch Ness.

It's not hard to see why the UK's most famous lake has drawn scientists, artists and tourists for centuries, thanks to its expansive sprawling landscape of mountains, incredible wildlife and, of course, the engaging tale of a mythical monster.

Whether or not you manage to spot the elusive Nessie, this incredible Loch has plenty more to offer up. Take a boat cruise to admire the incredible scenery of the loch (and of course keep an eye out for the monster), explore the dramatic ruins of Urquhart Castle, once one of Scotland's most important defences, and admire even more views from the charming village of Fort Augustus at Loch Ness's southern tip.

7. Loch Lomond

From Scotland's most famous lake, to its most beautiful, Loch Lomond's bonnie, bonnie banks are of course included in our guide to the very best places to visit in Scotland.

The glorious natural beauty of Loch Lomond has also been the inspiration of artists and writers for centuries, and the loch itself is immortalised in legend and song.

Just 25 miles north of Glasgow city centre, Loch Lomond is easily accessible by rail and road, making it understandably popular all year round for walkers, hikers, wildlife lovers and adventure seekers.

8. Isle of Mull

Hundreds of Scottish islands deserve a place on my list, but if I were to choose just one, the Isle of Mull tops it for me.

The second-largest of the Inner Hebrides, Mull, is easily accessible by ferry from the popular port of Oban and will satisfy travellers with a range of interests, from wildlife and whisky to history and castles.

Explore white-sand beaches, dramatic waterfalls, and take boat trips to the spiritual Isle of Iona and the geological wonder of Fingal's Cave. The island's colourful harbour town, Tobermory, recognisable to many as the fictional town of Balamory in the BBC children's series, is a charming base whilst on the island.

Don's miss, the magnificent Duart Castle, home of Clan Maclean, that sits on its own rocky promontory, guarding Mull's sea cliffs. From here you are almost guaranteed to see Mull's incredible wildlife, including seals, sea eagles, dolphins and even whales.

Of course, I've barely touched the surface of Scotland's Highlands and islands in this list, and there are hundreds and hundreds more spots that are well worth your time. If you're planning an adventure this year or next to the Highlands and islands, Lonely Planet Scotland's Highlands & Islands is available on Amazon and will give you the ultimate itinerary for your trip of a lifetime in beautiful Scotland.

item.title: From Hogwarts to the home of Mr Darcy, these are the 10 most iconic filming locations in Britain that should be on every film buff’s bucket-list
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: As screen tourism continues to lead the way for travel trends, here is our guide to Britain’s most iconic filming locations that should all be on your itinerary, from Harry Potter to Bridgerton, and everything in between
item.content:encoded:
It is a truth universally acknowledged that what makes a brilliant film or tv series truly memorable is its setting - for what would Harry Potter be without its magical landscapes, or Downton Abbey without, well, Downton Abbey.

The beautiful British Isles have long inspired Britain's filmmakers, thanks to their diverse landscapes, incredible scenic beauty and countless historic sites, from ruined medieval castles and preserved Georgian streets to magnificent palaces and stately homes.

Set-jetting has continued to dominate travel trends in recent years, and, according to Airbnb, 40 per cent of adults are inspired to travel to locations they have seen in period dramas. Of course, the sites themselves have taken it upon themselves to monetise their links to Britain's most iconic films and TV series, with places like Highclere Castle, catapulting to fame when it was used as the location for Julian Fellowes' award-winning ITV drama, Downton Abbey, welcoming swathes and swathes of tourists every year who have come to see the 'real' Downton.

Of course, there are hundreds of filming locations across the British Isles to visit, and too many for us to list here. On Location: A Guide to Visiting the UK and Ireland's Best Film and TV Sights is available on Amazon and is a brilliant guide to the finest set-jetting locations across Britain, and exactly how to go about visiting them - an ideal holiday planning companion for culture vultures and film buffs.

When it came to choosing our guide to the most iconic filming locations in Britain, I had an impossible task at hand. But, after some serious deliberation, this is my guide to 11 of Britain's most iconic locations that should be on every film-lover's itinerary.

The 11 most iconic filming locations in Britain to visit

1. Old Royal Naval College, Greenwich, London

From Bridgerton to Four Weddings, what hasn't been filmed at the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich is the real question. This UNESO World Heritage site in London is Britain's most prolific filming location thanks to its beautifully preserved historic buildings.

Thanks to the rise in screen tourism, the Christopher Wren-designed Old Royal Naval College has launched new behind-the-scenes tours to help visitors get a little closer to the action of their favourite films.

Visitors will recognise the college from films such as The King's Speech, Les Misérables, The Duchess, Four Weddings and a Funeral, Victoria & Abdul and The Madness of King George, as well as many key scenes in Netflix's hit period dramas, The Crown and Bridgerton.

2. Alnwick Castle, Northumberland

Heading up north, almost to the Scottish border, Northumberland's Alnwick Castle deserved a place on my list of Britain's most iconic filming locations thanks to its starring role in the first two Harry Potter films, Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone and Harry Potter and the Chamber of Secrets.

The border fortress castle was built in the 11th century and is one of Britain's most spectacular Norman castles, with a fascinating history to match. It is the second largest inhabited castle in the UK (after Windsor Castle) and is home to the Duke of Northumberland and his family. Lions of the North: The Percys & Alnwick Castle. A thousand years of history is available on Amazon, by historian Dan Snow and the Duke, Ralph, Percy, and brilliantly explains the castle's incredible history.

Harry Potter fans will recognise Alnwick Castle's exteriors as the site of Harry's flying lessons, various scenes when the fab three are walking to Hagrid's Hut and the Forbidden Forest, and the brilliant scene in Chamber of Secrets when Harry and Ron crash the flying Ford Anglia.

3. Glenfinnan Viaduct

From one Harry Potter location to another, Scotland's Glenfinnan Viaduct in the West Highlands is instantly recognisable as the route of the Hogwarts Express train.

The incredible feat of Victorian engineering, completed in 1901, still carries the West Highland Line between Fort William and Mallaig today (this is undoubtedly one of Scotland's most beautiful train journeys), and the heritage train, known as the Jacobite, still delights fans in the spring and summer every year as it puffs its way through the valley.

Harry Potter author JK Rowling was famously inspired by Britain's landscapes for her series, so it was only right that the filmmakers worked on locations for the films. There are far too many Harry Potter filming locations for us to list here, but Harry Potter on location is available on Amazon and is an A-Z of all the filming spots in the UK that you can visit yourself.

4. Highclere Castle

So successful was the period drama in reviving the fortunes of Highclere Castle that it is referred to as the 'Downton Abbey effect' by stately home owners everywhere. It was in 2010 when Oscar-winning writer Julian Fellowes' Downton Abbey first hit our TV screens and Highclere Castle was used as the filming location for the home of the fictional aristocratic family, the Crawleys.

Six series and three major films later, and although Downton Abbey may be over, its legacy lives on within the walls of Highclere Castle, now synonymous with the beloved period drama. Visitors to the castle can walk in the footsteps of their favourite characters, see real props and costumes and enjoy afternoon tea, just as the Crawley sisters will have done.

Lady Carnarvon, Chatelaine of Highclere, has become the face of the castle since it has soared to fame, and has authored several books on what it is really like to live in a stately home and keep it running. Her most recent book, A Year At Highclere: Secrets and Stories from the Real Downton Abbey, is available on Amazon and is an exclusive look at daily life here at the real Downton Abbey.

5. Chatsworth, Derbyshire

Of all the Jane Austen filming locations to include in this list, I decided to choose my favourite, the beautiful Chatsworth in Derbyshire, also one of the nation's favourite stately homes.

Thanks to its grand neoclassical exterior, opulent interiors and sweeping Capability Brown-designed grounds, Chatsworth stood in as Pemberley, the home of Mr Darcy in the 2005 film adaptation of Pride & Prejudice, starring Keira Knightley and Mathew Macfayden.

The filmmakers for Austen's most beloved work hit the nail on the head when choosing Chatsworth, because it is believed that the author herself visited Chatsworth when she was in Derbyshire, and may well have been inspired by its grandeur when conjuring up Mr Darcy's impressive home.

If, like me, you can't get enough Jane Austen content, this Jane Austen TV & Film Locations Guide is available on Amazon and offers some excellent Austen-themed inspiration when you're planning your next trip.

6. Hampton Court Palace

When making historic films about Britain's turbulent past and the dramatic lives of its kings and queens, producers are spoiled for choice for filming locations, mostly because so many of the actual buildings that would have seen the events first-hand are still standing today.

Of all the royal palaces in London, Hampton Court Palace is perhaps the most impressive, thanks to its sheer size and the incredible history it holds within its walls as the favourite home of King Henry VIII. It is no surprise then that the BBC chose Hampton Court as a primary location for their TV adaptation of Hilary Mantel's Wolf Hall about the rise and fall of Thomas Cromwell. It is incredible, and haunting, to watch the scenes of Mark Rylance as Cromwell and Damian Lewis as Henry VIII filmed at the palace itself, walking in the footsteps of the real Tudor king and his right-hand man.

Hampton Court's historic architecture is also recognisable in historic films such as The Young Victoria and The Favourite, as well as in fictional stories like Bridgerton and even Pirates of the Caribbean: On Stranger Tides.

7. Edinburgh

It is no surprise that the cobbled and storied historic streets of Scotland's capital city have provided the backdrop for countless films and TV series, and visitors to the city can book into a range of different filming locations tours, all bookable on Tripadvisor.

From blockbusters like Avengers: Infinity War, Fast & Furious 9 and The Da Vinci Code, historic epics like Outlaw King and Chariots of Fire (filmed on Eric Liddell's home turf) and the cult classic, Trainspotting, Edinburgh's list of filming accolades is pretty hard to beat.

Of course, there are some television series too that wouldn't be the same without their Edinburgh backdrop, including Netflix's One Day and several filming locations from the beloved Scottish time-travelling series, Outlander, based on the book series by Diana Gabaldon.

8. Blenheim Palace

The birthplace of Winston Churchill, the magnificent Blenheim Palace, has enough fascinating history to draw tourists for centuries to come, but its starring role in countless films and television series only adds to its appeal.

From the home of Queen Charlotte in Queen Charlotte: A Bridgerton Story, where it stands in for Buckingham Palace, to its role in Ridley Scot's Napoleon, and Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix, its incredible Baroque architecture is a veritable playground for filmmakers.

In a similar vein to Lady Carnarvon at Highclere, Lady Henrietta Spencer-Churchill, whose family home is Blenheim itself, has written several books about Blenheim's history and her life there. Blenheim: 300 Years of Life in a Palace is available on Amazon and tells the story of what she describes as Britain's most important and most-visited stately home.

9. Doune Castle

Scotland is scattered with filming locations from the television phenomenon that is Outlander, and there are countless Scottish Outlander tours for tourists to book onto, available to book on TripAdvisor.

If I were to choose just one Outlander filming location every fan should visit, it would be Doune Castle near Stirling, which stands in for Castle Leoch from the first season onwards, as the home of Clan MacKenzie. It is here that our heroine Clare first meets Murtagh, Laoghaire MacKenzie, and Colum, and its monumental stone walls are mightily impressive.

Monty Python fans will also recognise Doune Castle as the primary filming location for Monty Python and the Holy Grail, where it stood in as Camelot, Castle Anthrax and Swamp Castle.

10. Eilean Donan Castle

From one Scottish castle to another, Eilean Donan Castle is the ultimate shortbread-tin view of Scotland, which is no surprise considering its romantic loch-side setting and iconic stone bridge.

Eilean Donan shot to fame for its starring role in Highlander in 1986, where it served as the home of the film's hero, Highlander himself, Connor MacLeod.

Eilean Donan Castle has also appeared in films, including Loch Ness, Made of Honour, and Elizabeth: The Golden Age.

It's no surprise that the National Trust takes the crown for providing the most number of filming locations across Britain, thanks to the sheer number of beautiful buildings in its possession, and the incredible landscapes in its protection. National Trust on Screen is available on Amazon and is your guide to everything filmed at National Trust spots across the UK, all of which are open for visitors - the perfect way to start planning your next set-jetting trip!

item.title: Was Charles Dickens a dandy? New display reveals the intimate sartorial secrets of the Oliver Twist author, and you’ll have the chance to see what he was actually wearing when he died
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: The London home of the Victorian author has announced an intriguing new display, featuring the real-life shirt Dickens was wearing when he died, and shining a whole new light on his grooming routine
item.content:encoded:
It's not often you get to see the death clothes of one of Britain's most famous icons up close and personal, not least 150 years after they actually died.

As of this Wednesday, 11 March, visitors to the Charles Dickens Museum at 48 Doughty Street, London, will be able to do just that as two extremely rare items of the author's clothing will go on display in his actual dressing room.

The museum is the only remaining London house where Charles Dickens lived and wrote the stories that made him an international superstar, including The Pickwick Papers (1837), Oliver Twist (1838) and Nicholas Nickleby (1839). The clothing items to go on display this week at the museum are a fascinating insight into the personal style of the famed Victorian author, as well as a unique chance for visitors to actually see the clothes that he is believed to have been wearing when he suffered the stroke that killed him - this is history at its finest.

When researching Charles Dickens' death, I have only just discovered that he has an unfinished novel, a detective story which he never finished before he died before he could complete it. The fact that it is a detective story makes it even more tantalising - generations of audiences have tried to solve the unfinished mystery, but, of course, we'll never know what he had planned for its ending. The Mystery of Edwin Drood is available on Amazon and would make a perfect reading companion for a visit to the museum to see his death clothing - perhaps the answer to the mystery lies here?

The two items that will be on display at the Charles Dickens Museum from this week are the linen shirt collar worn by Dickens when he suffered his fatal stroke, and a pair of black silk stockings that are part of the author's only surviving suit - these will join a new display at the museum of personal items related to his style and grooming.

It is believed that Dickens was wearing the collar, which has recently been acquired by the museum, when he suffered a stroke while having dinner at home at his house in Rochester, Kent, Gad's Hill Place, on 8 June 1870. He died the very next day.

After Charles Dickens died, the collar was owned by actor and music hall performer Bransby Williams, who was famed for his portrayals of characters from Dickens' novels.

Alongside the haunting shirt collar, the black silk stockings that will go on display were worn by Dickens alongside a dark jacket, trousers and a white waistcoat, as well as a sword, to a formal reception at St James's Palace on 6 April 1870, where the author met Edward, Prince of Wales, the future King Edward VII, and just two months before he died.

Dickens described the suit as 'Fancy Dress' and, though the stockings are one of the rare few items of his that survive, the written materials we have from the period paint a picture of Dickens as a flamboyant dandy. What on earth is a 'dandy', I hear you cry?

Broadly described as a man who emphasised his physical appearance, refined language and leisurely hobbies, the term 'dandy' originated in the late 18th, early 19th century, with synonyms being a 'fop', 'popinjay', 'Beau' (originating from the most famous Dandy, Beau Brummell), or a 'man-about-town'. The brilliant The Dandy: A People's History of Sartorial Splendour is available on Amazon and provides a strong background for this fascinating corner of the history of men in Victorian society.

The idea of Dickens as a dandy is strongly supported by a beautiful colourised photographic portrait of Dickens, which will also be on display at the museum. The photo, originally made by (George) Herbert Watkins in June 1858 and restored and colourised in 2020 by Oliver Clyde, shows the author standing at a desk, right hand on hip in an eye-catching outfit, with a gleaming gold watch and chain - displaying his sartorial style in all its glory.

Completing the new display are several other personal items belonging to Dickens, including a set of six silver razors with ivory handles, a perfume bottle, silver candle snuffers and a gold locket, containing photos and locks of hair from Charles Dickens and his son, Henry.

Emma Harper, Deputy Director at the Charles Dickens Museum, said, “Among all of the many qualities, passions and character quirks of Charles Dickens, we know that he was a real dandy. This makes it all the more frustrating that so few items of his clothing survive, but renders our collection of clothing and accessories especially precious. When paired with the many eyewitness reports of his flamboyance, those items that do remain can give us a real insight into his snappy style.”

The news of this new display at the London museum comes after the announcement of their brand new exhibition Extra/Ordinary Women, which explores the brilliant women who influenced the author during his lifetime and will run until September 2026.

And, if you can't get enough Dickens' content, I would highly recommend this autobiography Charles Dickens: The Man, The Novels, The Victorian Age which is available on Amazon, and co-authored by Lucinda Hawksley, the great-great-great-granddaughter of the novelist, and the Charles Dickens Museum London itself - two brilliant fountains of knowledge on the fascinating life of one of Britain's most important novelists.

item.title: Was Jane Eyre Charlotte Brontë’s autobiography? This is how the brilliant Victorian novelist smashed class and gender boundaries through her beloved female heroine
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: We chart the life and inspirations of the eldest of the Brontë sisters and discuss whether her most successful novel, Jane Eyre, was actually based on her own life experiences
item.content:encoded:
When Charlotte Brontë created her most beloved character, she told her sisters that she had created a 'heroine as plain, and as small as myself, who shall be as interesting as any of yours.'

The Yorkshire-born author's 1847 novel, Jane Eyre, secured her status as one of the most celebrated Victorian novelists and one of the best British writers in history.

Brontë's novel is a passionate demonstration of her views on the mistreatment of women in Victorian society, especially those who lacked the money and social status to make their voices heard. Just like the works of her sisters, fellow authors, Anne Brontë and Emily Brontë, Jane Eyre has earned its place as a key text in the feminist canon and has been celebrated since its publication as a classic example of a Victorian gothic, and one of the greatest love stories ever written, redefining romance as a partnership of equals.

If you haven't read Jane Eyre, then really, where have you been? With a new feature film announced for release next year, now is the perfect time to immerse yourself in Brontë's brilliant novel. There are beautifully bound versions, like this one available on Amazon, that make reading this British classic feel all the more special.

But what do we know of Charlotte Brontë herself, and how much of Jane Eyre is based on her own real-life experiences?

Charlotte Brontë's early life

The third of six children born to Maria Branwell and Patrick Brontë, Charlotte was born on the 21st April 1816 at the family's home in Thornton, Yorkshire.

In the first obvious parallel with her character, Jane Eyre, who was also motherless, Maria died when Charlotte was just five years old, and she was sent to the Clergy Daughters' School at Cowan Bridge in Lancashire, along with her three sisters, Maria, Elizabeth and Emily.

Conditions at the school were squalid and awful, and Charlotte's two eldest sisters, Maria and Elizabeth, fell ill there and died shortly afterwards at home. Charlotte attributed her lifelong ill-health to her time at Cowan Bridge and used it as the model for the Lowood School in Jane Eyre. In the novel, the orphaned Jane is sent to a harsh boarding school where her friend, Helen Burns, died.

After returning home to Yorkshire, Charlotte enrolled as a pupil at Roe Head School in Mirfield, but stayed only a year. She returned home to teach her sisters, Anne and Emily, at home.

Charlotte Brontë's time as a governess

In 1835, Charlotte accepted a job at Roe Head as a teacher, and in 1839, she accepted a job as governess to a local family, but stayed only a few months. Her experience as a governess is often cited as her inspiration for Jane Eyre's character being a governess, and the isolation she experienced in the role - a key theme throughout the novel. Jane Eyre was 'plain and small' but sought intellectual equality with men, as well as those wealthier than herself - "Do you think, because I am poor, obscure, plain, and little, I am soulless and heartless?".

In 1842, Charlotte travelled to Brussels to join the Heger Pensionnat as a pupil (and then later as a teacher) in order with the hope of training to be able to open a school of her own. It was here she met Constantin Heger, the school's director and the supposed inspiration for the character of Mr Rochester in Jane Eyre, and the subject of Charlotte's first published novel, The Professor. Charlotte was obliged to leave Brussels after falling in love with Constantin, a married man.

This little-known novel went unpublished until after her death, but is an invaluable insight into Charlotte's real experiences in Brussels. Much of the subject of the novel was reworked from the perspective of a female teacher, which became her later novel, Villette. Both novels are available on Amazon and are worth reading if you're interested in Charlotte's earlier life and influences.

Back in Yorkshire

When she returned home to Yorkshire, Charlotte, Emily, and Anne attempted to open a school in the town of Haworth, where they lived with their father and brother, but failed to attract any pupils.

Jane Eyre is published

It was in 1846 that the sisters published a collection of poems under the male pseudonyms Currer, Ellis and Acton Bell. The Professor, Charlotte's first novel, was rejected by publishers, but her second novel, Jane Eyre, was published in 1847.

The novel follows the life of a plain young woman, from her troubled orphaned childhood and unhappy schooldays, to her arrival in a new post as governess to a young girl in a secluded mansion in Yorkshire. Jane, just like Charlotte in Brussels, falls in love with her employer, Mr Rochester, who is secretly hiding his dangerous, mad first wife in the attic. Combining romanticism, naturalism and gothic melodrama, the novel broke new ground because of its first-person female perspective.

Just like her sister Emily's Wuthering Heights, Jane Eyre attracted both praise and controversy. Literary critic G. H. Lewes wrote that Jane Eyre was "an utterance from the depths of a struggling, suffering, much-enduring spirit", but others were not so quick to praise.

The critics' primary objection to the novel was the 'bold' Victorian heroine that Charlotte had created. It was labelled as 'coarse' and 'anti-Christian' as the principal character demanded equality and independence. Critics thought its themes of class transgression - i.e., a governess and her employer entering a romantic relationship - were dangerous and improper.

The Conservative critic, Elizabeth Rigby, notably accused the author of 'violating every code human and divine.'

Little did they know, it was a woman who had written these words.
