In 1848, the sisters' true identities were revealed, and by the following year, Charlotte was well-known in London literary circles. She became lifelong friends with Elizabeth Gaskell and Harriet Martineau.

Gaskell wrote her own biography of her friend Charlotte's life after her death, entitled The Life of Charlotte Brontë, which is available on Amazon. It was the first full-length biography of a woman novelist by a woman novelist, and Gaskell explores the genius of her friend - historians claim it was this work that almost single-handedly created the 'Brontë myth'; the romanticised, often distorted cultural image of the sisters as doomed geniuses writing passionate masterpieces in isolation on the bleak, swirling Yorkshire moors.

The deaths of Anne and Emily

By 1848, Charlotte had begun her third novel, Shirley, but the same year saw the deaths of all three of her remaining siblings within three months of each other. Branwell of mulitple problems after excessive drinking, and Emily and Anne of tuberculosis. The industrial world of Victorian Yorkshire was a dangerous place to be, and the death rate was almost as high as London's at the time the Brontës were living there.

After the death of her sisters, Charlotte used her writing as a way of dealing with her grief. Shirley was published in October 1849 and deals with themes of industrial unrest and, just like Jane Eyre, the role of women in society.

Did Charlotte Brontë marry?

Just like her heroine, who married her Mr Rochester, Charlotte found love with her father's curate, Arthur Bell Nicholls. They married in June 1854, and Charlotte became pregnant shortly afterwards.

How did Charlotte Brontë die?

Charlotte died less than a year after her wedding to Arthur, on 31 March 1855. Historians believe she died either of tuberculosis or from a complication of pregnancy.

Was Jane Eyre based on Charlotte Brontë's own life?

It shouldn't be a surprise that Jane Eyre was actually first called An Autobiography.

Charlotte Brontë believed art was most convincing when it was based on personal experience, and she demonstrates this in Jane Eyre, transforming her life experiences into novel form.

The parallels between the two women are impossible to ignore, although their lives didn't work out quite the same, it is almost as if Charlotte wrote Jane as the version of herself she wished she could be. The woman who won the heart of the man who was her 'superior' because she was bold, independent and strong-willed and would not adhere to the societal constraints that shaped and restricted the lives of Charlotte and her sisters.

The inspirations of the Brontë sisters are a constant fountain of interest, and there are many spots in Yorkshire to visit to understand their influences. To delve deeper into the mystery that is Charlotte Brontë, her mind, and Jane Eyre, I would recommend The Invention of Charlotte Brontë: Her Last Years and the Scandal That Made Her which is available on Amazon, a darker take on one of British history's most famous women, revealing a brilliant, but flawed character, and understanding the myth created in Elizabeth Gaskell's tell-all biography.

item.title: By order of the Peaky Blinders, these are the real-life The Immortal Man filming locations every fan should visit
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: As the new feature film Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man hits our screens, here are the real-life places fans can walk in the footsteps of Tommy Shelby and his gang
item.content:encoded:
Get your flat caps ready because Tommy Shelby is back on our screens this month in Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man. Netflix have taken on the film version of the beloved period drama set in Birmingham, following the dramatic lives of the notorious criminal gang.

Of course, following on from the phenomenally popular series and its iconically grimy, industrial aesthetic, the producers for the film had to get it exactly right when choosing where to film. Luckily, the abundance of historic buildings and diverse landscapes in this country mean there are countless iconic filming locations in Britain, providing the backdrops for everything, from Harry Potter to Jane Austen.

From Victorian aqueducts to grand Georgian streets and incredible stately homes, Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man takes us the length of England's industrial west, from Liverpool to Birmingham and North Wales in between.

When is Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man out?

Peaky Blinders will be out in selected cinemas on Friday 6 March 2026, and available on Netflix on 20 March.

What is Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man about?

With Cillian Murphy reprising his role as the Birmingham gangster, the film will continue the multi-award-winning series following the rise and falls of the Shelby family between 1919 and 1934, with the new film taking place during the Second World War.

Although screenwriter Steven Knight has kept the plot firmly under wraps, he commented that it would be “an explosive chapter in the Peaky Blinders story. No holds barred. Full-on Peaky Blinders at war." I can hardly wait.

If you're keen to refresh your memory of the series before the film comes out, and learn the real story behind the drama, the brilliant box set Peaky Blinders The Real Story by Carl Chinn is available on Amazon and explores the legacy the Peaky Blinders created in Birmingham and beyond.

Where was Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man filmed?

If you're a keen set-jetter, and will be inspired to book your travels to visit real-life filming locations, here, we've listed seven Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man filming locations you can visit yourself.

1. Pontcysyllte Aqueduct, north Wales

The only Peaky Blinders filming location in Wales, the Pontcysyllte Aqueduct is a suitably dramatic location for the antics of the Peaky Blinders. The longest aqueduct in Britain and the highest canal aqueduct in the world, it is also one of Britain's incredible UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

Cillian Murphy, as Tommy Shelby, was spotted filming on a narrowboat at the 126-foot-high structure in December 2024, and appears in The Immortal Man 's first trailer.

2. Digbeth, Birmingham

Right in the heartland of the home of the Peaky Blinders, most of the filming for The Immortal Man took place at Digbeth Loc. Studios in Birmingham's creative quarter. Digbeth is known for its industrial heritage - perfect for the Peaky Blinders aesthetic - its vibrant street art, and unique culture.

Don't miss the shops, cafes and bars inside the iconic Custard Factory, housed in the restored Victorian headquarters of Bird's Custard.

3. Black Country Living Museum, Dudley, West Midlands

Another old stomping ground of the real-life Peaky Blinders, so of course it is only fitting that Dudley features heavily in The Immortal Man, specifically the Black Country Living Museum, where you can live and breathe the West Midlands' industrial heritage.

Steven Knight used the Black Country Living Museum many times during the hit series when it was on the BBC, most famously, its historic boat dock serves as the backdrop of Charlie Strong's Yard, and the Anchor Forge, Rolling Mill and Canal Street Bridge also feature.

The museum returns in The Immortal Man, and to celebrate, it is hosting immersive Peaky Blinders Nights. Visitors are encouraged to dress up in 1920s attire as they step into the gritty world of the Peaky Blinders and walk through the real-life filming locations.

Get your tickets for the Peaky Blinders Nights via Expedia here.

4. Little Germany, Bradford

Bradford's Little Germany provided a perfect backdrop for the producers of Peaky Blinders because it is home to no less than 55 historic listed buildings, one of the highest concentrations in Britain. The Shelby brothers' warehouse is filmed here in Bradford, and Cillian Murphy and his co-stars were spotted filming here for The Immortal Man in 2024.

5. Calke Abbey, Derbyshire

The beautiful National Trust-owned Calke Abbey in Derbyshire became a film-set for the very first time in 2024 as the cast and crew of Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man descended upon its historic halls.

The Grade I listed building was left partly abandoned and is often used as an example of the decline of the country house, so it provided an atmospheric backdrop for the film that required almost no alteration.

Standing in as Tommy Shelby's house, left in disrepair as he returns from World War Two, key shots for the film took place in the ground-floor passageways, kitchen, schoolroom and night nursery. The stable yard and riding school were also transformed with vintage cars, rather than their normal agricultural tools.

6. St George's Hall and St George's Quarter, Liverpool

Also appearing in The Batman, Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them and as a key filming location for Neftlix's House of Guinness, the grand St George's Hall and St George's Quarter in Liverpool is no stranger to film crews.

Visitors can take a guided tour of the neoclassical St George's Hall, where they can admire the grandeur of the concert hall and even peek into the prison cells.

The St George's Quarter is home to top-class galleries, great museums and a huge library. As well as inside the hall, Peaky Blinders filmed at the historic William Brown Street, which you can take a stroll down today.

If our tour of Peaky Blinders country has inspired you, there are plenty more set-jetting locations in Britain to inform your next adventure. Whether you'd like to visit the swirling moors of Wuthering Heights and its filming locations, the grand stately homes of Bridgerton or the iconic Scottish landscapes of Outlander, Britain really is a veritable playground for filmmakers.

item.title: Fancy a sneak peek inside the private rooms of Queen Elizabeth II? Here’s how to get your once-in-a-lifetime ticket for this summer’s limited tours
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: To celebrate the centenary of her birth, this summer, visitors to Scotland’s official royal residence will have a rare and special chance to glimpse inside Queen Elizabeth II’s private rooms
item.content:encoded:
For the first time in 100 years since she was born, this summer, the public will be given the unique chance to glimpse inside the real private rooms of Queen Elizabeth II at her official Scottish residence in Edinburgh.

One of the key royal sights in Britain to visit in 2026 to celebrate her centenary year, the Palace of Holyroodhouse will be opening the doors of the rooms used by Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip during their visits to Scotland, for the public to peek inside at long last.

The news comes as part of a year of celebrations to mark this milestone, as Queen Elizabeth II was born 100 years ago this year. The major event will take place in London at Buckingham Palace's The King's Gallery, with the largest exhibition of her fashion ever staged, but these very special tours at the official royal residence in Edinburgh are Scotland's answer to celebrations.

The baroque palace that stands at the end of Edinburgh's famous Royal Mile has a fascinating history, and has been the site of many of the most dramatic events in Scottish history, including the notorious murder of David Rizzio, Mary, Queen of Scots' Italian secretary. The official Holyrood Palace guidebook is available on Amazon and delves deeper into the history of this important place, from Mary, Queen of Scots, to Bonnie Prince Charlie and Queen Elizabeth II herself.

Centenary tours at the Palace of Holyroodhouse - how to get tickets

The private apartments used by Queen Elizabeth II at Holyrood Palace will be open for the first time in 2026, and available only this year.

Running for 100 days from 21 May to 10 September 2026, the tours will see small groups take behind-the-scenes tours, led by expert guides, to discover the history of the spectacular rooms and see for themselves the real places that Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip lived during their visits.

Subscribers to the Royal Collection Trust emails will be given priority access to book tickets at an exclusive offer price from Monday 9 March, with tickets going on general sale on Thursday 12th March. Book your tickets via the Royal Collection Trust website here.

What can you see inside the rooms?

Dating back to the 17th century, the rooms have been used by successive monarchs and members of the Royal Family since the reign of Queen Victoria.

The lucky ticket holders will be able to admire incredible, historic objects from the Royal Collection as well as some pieces from the personal collections of Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip, many of which reflect their longstanding love and affection for Scotland.

With her mother a Scotswoman, it is no surprise Queen Elizabeth II loved Scotland, joining a long line of British monarchs who enjoyed spending time here very much.

The Palace of Holyrood in particular was the setting for many of the most significant occasions during her reign. While visiting Edinburgh and Scotland, the Queen and her husband would occupy this lovely suite of private rooms that sit on the palace's east side. They offer stunning views of the gardens and Holyrood Park and, though modestly decorated, offer a rare and very special glimpse into the personal, private 'lived-in' spaces of the royal family, where they would take a private moment between their busy schedules of official duties.

The tours will include the Royal Breakfast Room, hung with tapestries at the request of Queen Elizabeth's grandparents, King George V and Queen Mary, and the Queen's actual Dressing Room. The latter in particular offers visitors the chance to see how the Queen would have prepared for official engagements, and there will be three particular ensembles of her wardrobe on display, all with special significance and Scottish heritage, which visitors will discover.

The tour will finish in the Sitting Room, where the late Queen would work and review the papers and documents presented to her in the Government red dispatch boxes. This room would also be used for private audiences and resting between engagements - she would often watch horse racing here, one of her favourite hobbies.

Throughout the rooms, visitors will also be able to admire highlights from Prince Philip's private art collection. He was Patron of the Royal Scottish Academy of Art and a keen collector of Scottish contemporary art, with many works by 20th-century Scottish artists adorning the walls of the palace and reflecting his passion for Scotland's landscapes and wildlife.

The Curator at the Palace of Holyroodhouse, Emma Stead, said, 'Queen Elizabeth II’s well-known love for Scotland will be given fresh context through this unique and special access to the private apartments, where visitors will enjoy a new perspective into both the formal and more informal use of Edinburgh’s royal palace.’

Admiring these beautiful rooms will be a rare chance to understand the private lives of one of the most famous couples in the history of the royal family, married for over 73 years. This lovely book, Elizabeth and Philip: A Story of Young Love, Marriage and Monarchy, available on Amazon, dives deeper into the love story of the royal pair and their extraordinary relationship.

item.title: The April/May 2026 issue of Discover Britain is out now! Get your copy here
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey, Editor
item.description: This issue we’re celebrating Constable’s 250th birthday by exploring his beautiful home county of Suffolk
item.content:encoded:
This issue, our travel special heads to the east of England, and, specifically, beautiful Suffolk, home to big skies and an even bigger sense of history.

In Strange Suffolk, on page 14, Kayleigh Rattle reveals some of the quirkier things to see and do across the region, while Constable Country on page 23 highlights some of the best ways to celebrate the British landscape painter, who grew up here, in his 250th anniversary year. Along the way, you’ll discover pretty medieval towns packed with stories and nostalgic seaside villages that will transport you back in time.

Talking about time travel, we have a couple of excellent history long-reads for you this issue. First up, in Grandmother of Europe (page 38) we explore how Queen Victoria’s influence over her nine children led to many of them marrying into the royal dynasties of Russia, Germany and Scandinavia.

In Spare to Heir (page 68), our Tudor expert Jo Romero examines what effect a young Prince Henry’s cultured upbringing in the royal court had on his later, unexpected, rule.

And we couldn’t let this issue go without looking into the film that everyone is talking about – Wuthering Heights. In her regular film and TV section, Scene it Before (page 74), Henrietta Easton lifts the lid on the real-life locations that have brought this reimagined classic to life on screen. If it doesn’t have you booking a trip to Yorkshire, I don’t know what will.

Shop the issue now here!

item.title: Anne Boleyn is dead - and the BBC’s terrifyingly relevant new period drama finally tells the stories of the women history forgot - all the details you need on the haunting new TV series
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Based on Ava Pickett’s play of the same name, 1536
item.content:encoded:
If, like me, you thought the BBC's production of Wolf Hall was brilliant and have been missing some Tudor drama in your life, you'll be pleased to hear the exciting news that just dropped about their upcoming series.

Based on the play of the same name by Ava Pickett (How To Get To Heaven from Belfast, The Great), the BBC has commissioned 1536, set in the heart of Tudor England, against the backdrop of the arrest of Queen Anne Boleyn.

The play made its debut in London's West End last year at the Almeida Theatre, and is a refreshing and still scarily relevant take on the well-known story of the second of Henry VIII's six wives, weaving the royal scandal that changed the history of the nation with the rural lives of 'normal' 16th-century women in rural England.

Because the BBC hasn't announced a release date yet, I'll be getting a copy of the play in paperback, available on Amazon to read beforehand- I can hardly wait to immerse myself in Tudor drama.

Following a sold-out run at the Almeida, 1536 will also transfer to London's Ambassador's Theatre from May 2026. Tickets are available to buy through the ATG website.

So, what is 1536 about?

The story centres on three young Tudor women, Anna, Mariella and Jane, who live in a small Essex village and are desperately waiting for their lives to begin.

When they hear the news that King Henry VIII has had his queen, Anne Boleyn, arrested, they never, ever think of the impact this will have on their lives. As the drama in the Tower of London unfolds, the three friends notice a rising tide of puritanism and misogyny around them.

The three women attempt to navigate their normal lives - marriage, relationships and births - whilst Anne Boleyn's life crashes before their eyes, and they begin to realise that Tudor England is a dangerous place to be a woman.

Pickett's angle on this is new and exciting because it keeps the royal drama in the background - the story we know so well provides the context for the narrative of characters who live in a totally different world from the queen. It offers a brilliant perspective on how the lives of kings and queens really affected the daily happenings of 'normal' people, a thought so often missed in period dramas that focus solely on the drama of court.

The audience begins to think about how the violent actions of one powerful man and his fragile ego could affect the lives of his subjects living hundreds of miles away, most of whom would never set foot in London or even catch a glimpse of the king. The story asks, if a King can murder his Queen, what does that mean for women elsewhere, and how much have things really changed?

Discussing sisterhood, friendships and the role of women in a changing world, Pickett's storytelling is darkly comic and brilliantly relevant, offering a powerful warning for modern audiences.

Watch Ava Pickett speak about writing 1536 below:

Rebecca de Souza, executive producer for Drama Republic, who is producing the series, said: “Ava Pickett’s writing is truly unique, thrilling, and funny, and we are so excited to be working with her in producing her first original series. Audiences were knocked out by her play 1536; it is so bold and entertaining, but it also creeps under your skin, leaving a profound warning for the modern world - a period show from a fresh and urgent viewpoint.”

1536 cast and release date

The BBC haven't yet announced details of a release date or any cast - keep your eyes peeled for further updates here!

For a woman who died nearly 500 years ago, Anne Boleyn has been in the news a lot recently, as her childhood home of Hever Castle announced a new exhibition of her portraits, and made a revelation that a hidden detail found in the famous Hever Rose portrait is now believed to be a witchcraft rebuttal.

If the premise of 1536 has taken your fancy, I'd recommend Juno Dawson's Queen B, available on Amazon, which is a fictional take on the idea of witchcraft in the Tudor court, and a pack of Anne's loyal followers who seek their revenge when she is beheaded - watch out, Henry!

item.title: From the Bard to the Queen of Crime, spanning 700 years of English literature this is our guide to the 12 best British writers of all time
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Britain’s literary landscape is as diverse as it is brilliant. Here is our ultimate guide to the very best British writers of all time, from the middle ages to the 21st century
item.content:encoded:
Whether it's our beautiful, diverse landscapes, our vibrant cultural landscape, or our turbulent, fascinating history, I think it would be very fair to say that Great Britain has produced some of the greatest writers in the world.

From novelists and playwrights to poets and children's authors, the best British writers in our history are diversely talented, and each has their own fascinating backstory that only makes their writing more interesting.

What's brilliant about the British literary scene, too, is that the timespan between the lives of our best writers stretches over nearly 700 years. The writings of our medieval and early modern writers remain as relevant today as those produced in the 21st Century.

Of course, there are far too many brilliant British writers throughout our history for us to name in one article, and the list would only continue to grow as more young writers join the ranks of literary greats every year.

For a brilliant, comprehensive guide to the best authors from British history, the COLOUR LIBRARY BOOK OF GREAT BRITISH WRITERS is available on Amazon and, though published nearly 40 years ago, is a keepsake coffee-table book filled with wonderful images that will always be worth perusal.

Here, though, I have done the impossible task of choosing my 12 favourite writers from British history, all of whom have done a phenomenal job of making up Britain's great literary landscape.

The 12 best British writers of all time

1. Geoffrey Chaucer (1343 - 1400)

You can't have a list of Britain's greatest authors without Geoffrey Chaucer.

A poet, writer and civil servant, Chaucer is often referred to as the father of English literature, and was the first writer to be buried in what is now Poets' Corner in Westminster Abbey, a high accolade indeed. Like Shakespeare he is also credited with the first usage of many words we know and use today, in fact almost two thousand English words are first attested in Chaucerian manuscripts.

The Canterbury Tales, Chaucer's most famous work, is a collection of 24 short stories written in Middle English and mostly written in verse. They are centred around a storytelling contest between a group of pilgrims travelling from London to Canterbury. The most famous include A Knight's Tale and The Wife of Bath. Even today, each story has a relevant, moral lesson and is full of wit and humour. They also provide us with a brilliant insight into life for the 'normal' person in medieval England.

If you haven't read Chaucer before, I highly recommend getting your hands on a copy of The Canterbury Tales, which is available on Amazon in this lovely, medieval-looking hardbound version.

2. William Shakespeare (1564-1616)

The Bard, of course, is the most famous of British writers. Shakespeare is still commemorated for having coined nearly 1,700 of the words and phrases we still use today. He began as a playwright and as an actor in London, although he is also known for his birthplace of Stratford-upon-Avon, where many of his plays are still performed.

Shakespeare died in 1616, leaving most of his estate to his daughter Susanna. The only mention of his wife, Anne Hathaway, in his last will and testament was to leave her his “second best bed”. The brilliant book, Hamnet, by Maggie O'Farrell, has just been made into an award-winning film and tells the story of Shakespeare's wife and children. You can buy Hamnet on Amazon and I'd highly recommend it as a read if you're interested in Shakespeare's life.

3. Jane Austen (1775-1817)

Paving the way for female British writers everywhere, for a woman to write, and to write about women, in her time was not the ‘done thing’. It comes as no surprise to hear that the name we know so well today, Jane Austen, published her novels anonymously.

Her books deal with the lives of the upper and middle classes in England. Sense and Sensibility came first, but all her books were a resounding success – Emma, Mansfield Park, Northanger Abbey, Persuasion. While Pride and Prejudice was famously described by Austen as her ‘darling child’ and remains a national favourite. Her house in Chawton in Hampshire is open to visit.

Countless television and film adaptations of her beloved novels have graced our screens, with more on the horizon this year, including Netflix's adaptation of Pride & Prejudice - here is everything you need to know about it.

The brilliant historian Lucy Worsley has written a wonderful biography about Jane Austen which I'd highly recommend. Jane Austen at home is available on Amazon and is a must for any Austenite.

4. Charles Dickens (1812-1870)

From A Christmas Carol to Oliver Twist, it is difficult not to recognise one of Charles Dickens’ iconic tales. The Victorian writer is quintessential of his time and his work is still adapted for stage and screen over and over again, remaining ever relevant today.

He dealt with the struggles of contemporary life with unforgettable characters. Dickens was also a lover of theatre – both writing and performing – and performed for Queen Victoria in 1851. His birthplace museum in Portsmouth is open for visitors, as well as the Charles Dickens House Museum in London, where he lived and wrote for many years.

5. The Brontë sisters (1816-1855)

Ok, so I know this is slightly cheating, but I couldn't include one Brontë sister without the other two.

Charlotte, Emily and Anne, the Yorkshire-born and bred lasses, are responsible for some of the most important novels of the Victorian period, and were trailblazing as female writers of their time.

The swirling Yorkshire moors on which the Brontë sisters grew up and the harsh realities of the Industrial Revolution were the backdrops for their novels, and each sister used their voice wisely to make comments on the role of women in Victorian society.

Charlotte's novel Jane Eyre, Emily Brontë's Wuthering Heights - which has just been adapted into a major film - and Anne Brontë's The Tenant of Wildfell Hall are their most famous novels, respectively, and are each beloved and reread over and over again.

It's worth reading each of their novels to fully understand their characters though - so I'd recommend buying a full-set of all seven novels on Amazon, like this one.

6. George Eliot (1819-1880)

Another incredible female writer who led the way for others, hiding behind her pen name, George Eliot, Mary Ann Evans wanted to be taken seriously as a novelist at a time when women’s writing was often associated with romantic novels.

She met her partner George Henry Lewes through her literary circle in London. As he was married, their relationship was shunned by friends and family. They lived together despite the scandal. Her most famous novel, Middlemarch helped her to gain social acceptance through her psychological insights.

7. Agatha Christie (1890 - 1976)

The best-selling author all time globally of course has to have a place in our best British writers. The Queen of Crime also has a well-earned spot in our greatest women in British history thanks to her sheer and utter brilliance.

Throughout her 66 published novels, over 150 short stories and more than 25 plays the Queen of Crime created such characters as Hercule Poirot and Miss Jane Marple, and is the author of the longest-running play in the history of the West End, The Mousetrap.

In 2026, the country is marking 50 years since her death with various Agatha Christie-themed events, including an exciting exhibition at the British Museum, to open later in the year. For now you can peruse our guide to all the places every Agatha Christie fan should visit in their lifetime.

8. J RR Tolkien (1892-1973)

A close college friend of fellow author on this list, CS Lewis, John Ronald Reuel Tolkien was the creator of one of the best-loved fantasy worlds of all time, Middle Earth. His novel The Hobbit and his trilogy The Lord of the Rings have fans all over the globe and have been adapted into block-busting, Oscar-winning films and now a major TV series prequel - Rings of Power.

His visions for Middle Earth were primarily inspired by his profession as a philologist (studying Old and Middle English and Germanic languages), his devout Catholic faith, and his profound experiences in World War I.

In his book The Tower and the Ruin: J.R.R. Tolkien's Creation, available on Amazon, Michael D C Drout discusses Tolkien's genius mind and how he invented myths, histories, cultures and even entire languages in the world of Middle Earth - it's a fascinating read if you're a Lord of the Rings fan like me.

9. CS Lewis (1898-1963)

Born in Northern Ireland, Lewis studied at Oxford University. After serving as a soldier in WW1, he settled into life as a professor first at Oxford and then Cambridge University. Although renowned for his children’s fantasy tales, he also wrote profusely on religion and theology.

However, his first book in his Chronicles of Narnia series, The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe, released in 1950, remains one of the most beloved of his published works.

As a lifelong Narnia fan however, it always surprises me how few people have read the entire series, or even know that The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe is not the first. To get the full Narnia experience, it is necessary to read all the novels in order in a complete Narnia collection, like this one available on Amazon - I challenge you to say The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe is your favourite afterwards.

What is interesting too reading it an adult is the subtle metaphors and analogies that you don't always pick up asa younger reader - did you know that Narnia was heaven, and Aslan was god?

10. George Orwell (1903-1950)

Born Eric Arthur Blair, Orwell adopted his pen name shortly before his first book in 1933 called Down and Out in Paris and London.

By the time he published his first big novel, Animal Farm in 1945, he was an established and indeed prolific, journalist.

He had dealt with British colonies, unemployed miners, civil wars and communism in his work – all of which had a profound impact on his writing. Animal Farm shows his anti-Stalin beliefs through a political, farmyard fable. Several years later Nineteen Eighty-Four came out and secured his longevity as an author.

11. Ian Fleming (1908-1964)

Although not necessarily my taste, there is no denying that the writer of the Bond novels created a winning framework for the world of spy literature.

However, he did not begin to create and develop the Bond character until the age of 43. After a successful career working in newspapers, as a broker and in Naval Intelligence – he settled in his house in Jamaica where Bond was born. After writing Casino Royale, the first adventure of James Bond, he continued to publish 13 more titles. They have all been played out in iconic blockbuster hits since. Diverging from the adult scene just once, he wrote the story of the flying car, Chitty Chitty Bang Bang for his only son Caspar.

12. Roald Dahl (1916-1990)

British novelist, Roald Dahl, has straddled both the adult short story and the children’s story genre. He is certainly one of the most-loved British writers of all time.

Many believe his first book to be James and the Giant Peach, but it was in fact a picture book called The Gremlins. It was adapted from the script for the potential, but unrealised, Walt Disney film. He did not publish another children’s book until he was a father. He decided to concentrate on his short stories for adults, which he later continued alongside his prolific books. Dahl created many wonderful characters such as the BFG, the Twits and Willy Wonka in his children’s books. He also wrote in a magical ‘whizz-popping’ language that still enthrals readers.

If you're intrigued by this 'whizz-popping' language, here is our pick of the 10 best Roald Dahl quotes of all time. Of course, all this talk of brilliant authors, but what about their best quotes? If you haven't done reading yet, here is our guide to the 10 best quotes from British authors of all time, from Tolkien to the Brontës.

item.title: Move over Tutankhamun: this new London exhibition reveals the shocking secrets of Egypt’s most decadent king
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: This brand new opening at Battersea Power Station will give you the chance to get up close and personal with nearly 200 incredible ancient Egyptian artefacts, all over 3,000 years old
item.content:encoded:
When we think of Egyptian pharaohs, the name that springs to mind is always, without fail, Tutankhamun. But a new exhibition will shine a light on a different ancient king for a change.

A brand new opening at South London's Batter Power Station will shine a light on the fascinating reign of Ramses the Great, and I am swiftly adding it to my list for the best things to do in London in 2026.

In partnership with Egypt’s Supreme Council of Antiquities and the Egyptian Museum Ramses and the Pharaohs’ Gold opened at the power station at the end of February and will run until 31 May 2026, celebrating the reign, power and legacy of this fascinating pharaoh and featuring 180 priceless ancient Egyptian artefacts.

Of course, to make the most of the exhibition, it's a good idea to read up on its subject before you go. Toby Wilkinson's Rameses the Great: Egypt's king of kings is available on Amazon and tells the story of the dramatic life of the subject of the exhibition, who had one of the longest reigns in ancient Egyptian history, was a megalomaniac, and who has been famed in literature as Ozymandias by poet Percy Shelley.

Ramses and the Pharaoh's Gold at Battersea

Hosted at NEON at Battersea Power Station, visitors to the exhibition will be able to get up close with incredible, opulent treasures from the reign of the king, including ornate sarcophagi, animal mummies, magnificent jewellery, royal masks and exquisite Egyptian amulets. They will also have the opportunity to actually see the wooden coffin that once held Ramses II's mummy. This is the stuff that Egytofile's dreams are made of.

All of the artefacts are over 3,000 years old and are on loan from museums and historical sites in Egypt and Egypt's Supreme Council of Antiquities.

Who was Ramses the Great?

King Ramses II, widely known as Ramses the Great, was part of the 19th dynasty and ruled Egypt for nearly 67 years. He is celebrated as the mighty warrior-king who helped define the height of Ancient Egyptian civilisation.

He is best known for his extensive monument-building, military strategy and diplomacy, as well as having more than 100 children.

Despite often being overshadowed by the likes of Tutankhamun and Cleopatra in popular culture, Ramses II is one of ancient Egypt's most iconic pharaohs, and, through immersive galleries and storytelling, the exhibition highlights his extravagance and relentless self-promotion, through glittering gold artefacts and beautiful, ornate carvings and art.

The exhibition will also feature an immersive virtual reality experience that will transport visitors deep into Ramses' legacy, from the resplendent Tomb of Queen Nefertari (the first of Ramses Great Royal Wives) to his monumental temples of Abu Simbel. For more on Queen Nefertari, this brilliant book The Tomb of Queen Nefertari: Egyptian Gods and Goddesses of the New Kingdom is available on Amazon and delves into the history of the 'Sistine Chapel of Ancient Egypt.'

Well-known historian and broadcaster Dan Snow has also been announced as the lead storyteller for the exhibition. He commented, “Ramses and the Pharaohs’ Gold is more than a display of objects; it is a monumental encounter with a titan of history, and I’m honoured to help bring his story to life. For Londoners, the opportunity to stand before these 180 priceless treasures - many of which have never left Egyptian soil - is a rare moment of historical connection. In a world that often feels fast and fleeting, there is something profoundly grounding about standing inches from the 3,000-year-old wooden coffin that once held Ramses II’s mummy. It obliterates the centuries that lie between then and now. History is not just an object in a glass box; it is an emotional response.”

View this post on Instagram

Secretary-General of Antiquities of Egypt, Dr Hisham El-Leithy, also said:“Ramses the Great was a remarkable pharaoh who helped shape the legacy of Egypt. Many of these treasured artefacts have never travelled outside the country before, and London is about to witness something extraordinary. This exhibition provides a rare opportunity for people to come face-to-face with the world of Ramses in all its glory. It is my hope that by discovering the story of Ramses, audiences will be inspired to explore Ancient Egypt further and to deepen their appreciation for its enduring cultural importance.”

As the iconic Power Station becomes the stage for Ramses the Great, who ruled over 3,000 years ago, I am reminded of how special London is - how incredible to be able to admire an ancient Egyptian sarcophagus before popping out for some lunch in one of the world's best cities.

The London year is ever changing and ever exciting - as Samuel Johnson said, when a man is tired of London, he is tired of life. To inspire your plans The London Year: What to Do and When is available on Amazon, and is an excellent guide to the seasonal calendar in the capital, from hidden gems to headline events. Happy planning!

item.title: Was Anne of Cleves really the ‘Flanders Mare’ that history will have us believe, or did Henry VIII rewrite the story to protect his ego?
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: We’re rewriting the narrative of Henry VIII’s fourth wife, Anne of Cleves - forever remembered in history as the ‘ugly one’, she was certainly the luckiest of them all
item.content:encoded:
Was Anne of Cleves really the ugliest of all Henry VIII's queens, or is this just what Henry wants us to believe because she wounded his pride?

Well, just like all the other histories of Henry VIII's six wives, Anne's legacy was written by the man who threw her aside, and she is remembered for her part in the song 'divorced, beheaded and died, divorced, beheaded, survived' - her line being the singular 'divorced.'

Anne of Cleves was Henry VIII's fourth wife, and their brief marriage (which ended in annulment) has gone down in history as being an utter disaster. Henry claimed the annulment was because she 'nothing so fair as she hath been reported', and that she was 'no maid.' Unfortunately for Anne, this is how she has forever been remembered, because wives of the king, especially tyrants like King Henry VIII, couldn't write their own histories.

Having not known that much about Anne of Cleves, my attention has historically been on the Howard cousins, Anne Boleyn and Catherine Howard. My head was turned recently when I read Philippa Gregory's Tudor novel, The Boleyn Inheritance, which is available on Amazon, and which I highly recommend.

Picking up as Henry marries his fourth wife, the novel follows Anne's story, and Gregory writes that she was beautiful, intelligent, and indeed that it was Henry who was the problem, rather than Anne, as it so often is.

So, who was Anne of Cleves, and how did she get to be Queen of England?

Anne of Cleves' early life

As with so many women of this period, sadly, we know very little of Anne of Cleves' early life, but we do know that she was born in Düsseldorf in 1515, the daughter of Maria of Julich-Berg and John III, Duke of Cleves.

She grew up in Cleves, a small state in what is today modern Germany, that was inside the Holy Roman Empire, and a key player in European politics in the 16th century.

Why did Henry VIII marry Anne of Cleves?

When Henry VIII's third wife, Jane Seymour, died just hours after giving birth to her son, the future King Edward VI, Henry began his search for his next wife because one male heir was not enough to secure his line.

His fourth marriage also needed to be politically, and religiously beneficial for the country. After Henry had broken from Rome in order to divorce his first wife, Katherine of Aragon and marry his second, Anne Boleyn, England was isolated and vulnerable. His new marriage had to be strategic and with a wife from a country that also challenged the Pope's authority.

Henry's chief minister, Thomas Cromwell, was handed the ultra-important task of playing matchmaker - his decision, historians would argue, ultimately led to his downfall.

Cromwell set his sights on Anne because Cleves, like England, was a Catholic state open to religious reform.

Anne was 23 years old and - this is important to remember for later on - had been engaged to the Duke of Lorraine since she was 11. However, nothing had come of the match, and Cromwell was assured by her family that she was free to marry.

Anne of Cleves' portrait by Hans Holbein

Anne of Cleves is most famously remembered in popular history by the infamous portrait that, according to Henry VIII, was not a true depiction of her.

When choosing his new wife, Henry was not prepared to rely purely on words alone - this was wife number four, it had to go well. Instead, Henry sent his court painter, Hans Holbein, responsible for some of the most famous Tudor paintings we know today, to Germany to paint Anne's portrait. To learn more about Holbein's influence on the Tudor court, Elizabeth Goldring's brilliant book, Holbein: Renaissance Master is available on Amazon and is a definitive biography of the artist whose paintings have shaped the way we see the Tudors - it even has the Anne of Cleves painting on its cover.

The painting, which now hangs in the Louvre in Paris, depicts Anne wearing the rich red fabrics that were the fashion in Cleves at the time, with fine jewels around her neck. Henry was clearly taken by her fabulous attire and cool, calm expression, because the portrait was enough to persuade him that she should be his fourth wife.

When did Anne of Cleves come to England?

When Anne of Cleves first came to England in 1539, she had never met her future husband, they didn't speak the same language, and there was a 24-year age gap between them - but that was pretty normal for the 16th century.

Of course, coupled with these natural anxieties from Anne, was the public knowledge of what had happened to his first three wives - I can only imagine how nervous she was.

Anne of Cleves and Henry VIII's embarassing first meeting

Anne arrived into Dover on 27 December 1539 and was accompanied north to Rochester Castle, where she would rest a few days before travelling on to London, where she would finally meet the king.

Little did she know that Henry had a surprise in store for her and was actually planning on a surprise first encounter in Rochester - one that didn't go at all as he'd planned.

Henry famously loved pranks, and especially, dressing up in disguises to surprise people. Anne, of course, didn't know this. One New Year's Day 1540, Henry dressed in disguise in a coloured cloak and burst into the room that Anne was in - some accounts say he tried to kiss her and she pushed him away. And why wouldn't she? A great, hairy giant of a man forcing himself upon her - any woman would have done the same.

Of course, this wasn't Henry's plan - it was supposed to be love at first sight. Ideally she would have recognised him instantly or been drawn to him, instead she rejected him. This was strike one for Anne from the egotistical King. Is this were it all began to unravel?

When did Anne of Cleves and Henry VIII get married?

Henry was furious and told Cromwell immediately that he did not want to marry her. He made attempts to find a loophole in their clause - including bringing up Anne's precontract to the Duke of Lorraine, but to no avail. Her family swore she was free to marry and, anxious not to lose the political alliance, Henry married her at Greenwich Palace on 6 January 1540.

Henry VIII and Anne of Cleves' wedding night?

Of course, we'll never truly know what happened between the unhappy couple on their wedding night, and, as discussed, Henry has skewed the story in his favour. The morning after, Henry is said to have told Cromwell that 'I liked her before not well, but now I like her much worse' and that they were unable to consummate the union.

Henry claimed that this was due to the fact that he didn't find her attractive, and that she had a 'bad odour', but lets remember, Henry was getting old, he was overweight and he was extremely unhealthy - chances are this was on him rather than Anne who, from her portrait, looked like a very beautiful, elegant young woman.

Some historians have claimed too that Anne was so inexperienced that she wouldn't know what was supposed to happen on her wedding night - but this doesn't sit right with me. Knowing her purpose of providing an heir to the king, her family would undoubtedly have explained her role and what needed to happen to her beforehand. This was definitely a Henry problem.

Let's not forget also, this tyrant king's fragile ego was wounded beyond repair in their first ever encounter - perhaps he was determined not to even give it a second chance - he couldn't get passed the embarrassment of his pride being hurt by a woman.

Why did Henry VIII 'divorce' Anne of Cleves

Just seven months after their union, Henry had his marriage to Anne annulled on the grounds that the marriage was non-consensual (on Henry's side) and unconsummated. Anne was not invited to testify. Unlike divorce, annulment declared the marriage had never been lawful or binding - in Henry's eyes, it didn't count.

Anne was told of the annulment just three days before it was finalised, and sensibly, she agreed to it. She must have been frightened for her life, knowing the fates of Katherine of Aragon and Anne Boleyn - this was a wise move, and one that made her the luckiest of all six queens.

But, Historian and novelist Phlippa Gregory commented that this was far from 'luck' for Anne and wise, clever move. She said that Anne ''knew very well how to manage a domestic tyrant: having suffered from a drunk and perhaps delusional father and a powerful brother."

Was Anne of Cleves actually ugly?

In popular history, we have all been taught that their marriage failed because Anne was 'ugly' - the nickname 'Flanders Mare' is one we all know - and that her portrait exaggerated her beauty. This is a one-sided story told by an egotistical man who was no beauty himself.

If we look closer at the events, there is a deeper tale of a lack of common ground between the couple (different cultures and languages), a lack of romantic chemistry (especially when you consider the passion between Henry and Anne Boleyn), and, of course, Henry's tyrannous, narcisitic personality. He preferred to choose his own lovers, and could never forgive anyone for wounding his pride, especially not a woman.

Indeed, before their marriage had even been annulled, Henry had his eye on wife number five, Anne of Cleves' lady-in-waiting, Catherine Howard.

What happened to Anne of Cleves after she divorced Henry VIII?

It will come as no surprise that Henry didn't just let Anne go back to Cleves a free-woman - he had to lay his claim on her forever more, as well as keep up appearances that their marriage was unlawful, rather than because he didn't like her enough.

Anne was presented with a generous settlement from the king as a thanks for agreeing to the divorce, but she had to remain in England, and she would be known as the 'king's sister'.

She would be given an annual income of £500 and she was bestowed vast amounts of property, including Richmond Palace, and later Hever Castle - the childhood home of Anne Boleyn. The town of Lewes in East Sussex is also home to Anne of Cleves House, one of the properties she was given which remains open as a museum.

After their marriage ended Anne was regularly invited back to court, where she was much admired as an elegant, beautiful, intelligent young woman. She became close with Henry's daughters, Mary and Elizabeth, and even with Henry's new wife Catherine. This was a good life for a Tudor woman, with freedom, money and respect - she had a narrow escape and a relatively happy life post Henry VIII.

When did Anne of Cleves die?

Anne of Cleves died aged 41 in July 1557, she had suffered from poor health for many years.

Where is Anne of Cleves buried?

Anne is the only one of Henry VIII's wives to be buried in Westminster Abbey - the traditional resting place of kings and queens of England, and a testament to the respect she garnered in her lifetime.

Certainly the luckiest of Henry VIII's queens, Anne of Cleves is often the one passed over when we think about Henry's six wives, but in my opinion, she is one of the most interesting. Despite setbacks and humiliation, she wisely navigated her way around one of the most dangerous men in British history, and managed to survive, with her head held high. Rumours even swirled that Henry was reconsidering her as a wife after he executed Catherine Howard. Anne of Cleves was not the 'ugly' wife we've been taught, and, as always, this is a narrative written by an egotistical, tyrannous man.

For a deeper dive into Anne of Cleves' story, Elizabeth Norton's Anne of Cleves: Henry VIII's Discarded Bride is available on Amazon and was the first full biography of the misremembered queen - as we begin to rewrite history from the perspective of women, I hope it will be the first of many.

item.title: This is your rare chance to see the Royal family’s most precious heirloom at Buckingham Palace exhibition this spring, worn by 62 royal babies
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Visitors will have the chance to see a rare piece of royal history up close as the Royal Collection announces that Queen Elizabeth II’s christening gown will be part of her centenary exhibition at Buckingham Palace this spring
item.content:encoded:
How many people can say that the Christening gown they wore was nearly 200 years old, and was first worn by Queen Victoria's eldest daughter? Well, unsurprisingly, very few.

Queen Elizabeth II is one of 62 royal babies to have worn this particular christening gown, which will go on display as part of the special exhibition to celebrate the centenary of her birth at Buckingham Palace this spring, Queen Elizabeth II: Her Life in Style, which is the largest exhibition of her fashion ever staged.

It is a rare appearance for the gown, which dates back to 1841 when Princess Victoria was christened, and it is thought to be the first time it has ever been on display to the public, making this a rare chance to see such rare royal history up close.

The exhibition will also feature pieces like the late Queen's glittering Coronation gown, as well as some of the most memorable dresses and outfits she's ever worn, from ballgowns to tweed coats and plastic raincoats, tracing the journey of her remarkable legacy through her iconic clothes.

If, like me, you'll be first in line to see the exhibition, which opens on 10 April 2026 and will run until 18 October 2026, the companion book to the exhibition makes for some great pre-reading and allows you to really immerse yourself in her world. Queen Elizabeth II: Fashion & Style by Caroline de Guitat (who also curated the exhibition) is available on Amazon celebrates the late Queen as a fashion icon of the 20th and 21st centuries, and even includes a tribute by Dame Anna Wintour CH, DBE.

Queen Elizabeth II's christening gown

The christening gown itself has been carefully passed down through consecutive generations of royal babies since it was first worn by Princess Victoria in 1841, and was worn by Princess Elizabeth - the future Queen Elizabeth II - at her christening in May 1926, when she was just one month old.

Caroline de Guitat commented, ‘The christening robe was the most significant garment worn by Princess Elizabeth in her infancy and is an exceptional example of British craftsmanship – something Queen Elizabeth went on to champion throughout her life. As the earliest piece of clothing worn by the Queen, and by so many royal babies before and after her, it holds a special place in her wardrobe. We are delighted to be able to share it with as many people as possible in this centenary year.’

What is the christening gown made of?

Queen Elizabeth II's christening gown is made of cream Spitalfields silk from East London, and is overlaid with fine Honiton lace made in Devon. It was created by Janet Sutherland, Queen Victoria's Scottish dressmaker and ‘Embroiderer to the Queen’.

Of course, silk is naturally susceptible to degradation, and the robe became fragile over time. It was repaired in the 1960s. In 2004, Queen Elizabeth decided it had become too delicate and made the decision to commission a replica, which has been worn for every subsequent royal christening since.

To go on display as part of the exhibition, the robe has undergone 100 hours of careful conservation by the Royal Collection Trust's textile conservator, Cecilia Oliver, in advance of going on display, including meticulous repairs to holes and being gently handwashed section by section.

Alongside the robe, visitors to the exhibition will also be able to see a handwritten note by Queen Elizabeth II herself, which shows her recording some of the babies who have worn the christening robe. This was a tradition that started with her grandmother, Queen Mary, after the robe came into her care from Queen Victoria.

Cecilia Oliver commented, ‘The robe has been lovingly cared for over the generations – we even found a note on its box instructing that it be washed in natural spring water after use, which helps explain its remarkable condition despite such frequent use. However, after 185 years and 62 christenings, it was in need of conservation. It has been an honour and a privilege to work on such a special historic garment.’

The robe will be on display as part of the exhibition at the King's Gallery at Buckingham Palace, from 10 April 2026. Tickets are available from the Royal Collection Trust's website here.

So, who were all the other babies that wore the christening robe? Some will have been Queen Victoria's children who went on to marry into the royal families of half of Europe. The extraordinary lives of her children and grandchildren are exploredin greater detail in Children of the Empire by Michael Farah, available on Amazon. Written entirely in first person, this is a fascinating deep dive into the minds of 47 children and grandchildren and the dramas of their lives, from haemophilia, coups, Nazis and even execution. It certainly gives the beautiful christening gown a new meaning!

item.title: Rule breakers, crown makers and trailblazers - these 18 firebrand women are the greatest in British history
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From Boudicca to Beatrix Potter, British history is peppered with the legacies of women who catapulted out of the boundaries of their sex to achieve something remarkable. Here is our pick of 18 of the greatest women in British history
item.content:encoded:
Although we all know our Shakespeares, our Charles Darwins and Dickens, and our William Wilberforces, I think it would be fair to say that the most interesting people in British history have been women.

What makes them so interesting compared to their male counterparts? Well, of course, I don't want to deny the importance of many of these male figures, but what makes these greatest British women so interesting is precisely that - they were women, in a world dictated by men.

When the women of Britain's past have broken out of the boundaries created for them by men, they have done things their contemporaries would think impossible - directed troops into battle, performed on stage, published novels and even won the vote for women.

Whether they be queens, nurses, authors or politicians, these women have stepped up to the plate in the capacity of men in a society that thought women the weaker sex, and for that they should always be remembered and celebrated.

Of course, I can't list every single great British woman in one article, and I'd never be able to do them all justice, so for a thorough and brilliant take on the subject, Great British Women by Charles Roy is available on Amazon and is a handy little pocketbook guide to Britain's greatest female pioneers, from Roman times to modern day.

It's worth saying too that women shouldn't always have to be 'remarkable' to be celebrated - most women in British history did what they could with what the world gave them, and that is important to remember too. But thanks to men's lack of interest in women's roles in the society they were living in, our recorded history of 'normal' women is very little.

That's what makes it even more important to celebrate the women whose stories we do know, and more often than not that is because they have done something 'remarkable'.

For now, narrowing it down to just 18, here is my guide to the greatest women in British history, from ancient Britain to modern day.

1. Boudicca (died 60/61AD)

First comes Boudicca, one of Britain's warrior queens of the ancient Iceni people. She led a rebellion that nearly ended Roman rule in Britain, when the Romans plundered the Iceni’s lands. They wiped out the Romans’ Ninth Legion and sacked their strongholds at Colchester, London and St Albans, took no prisoners (according to Roman historians) and massacred at least 70,000.

Boudicca is honoured with a dramatic statue, arms raised, by Thomas Thornycroft, near London’s Westminster Pier.

2. Queen Elizabeth I (1533-1603)

If Boudicca is the feistiest figure from Ancient Britain, Elizabeth I is surely the most famous in more recent history. Elizabeth was a powerful ruler, adept at boosting her popularity by ‘progressing’ around the country and playing up her image as “the Virgin Queen”.

The success of her troops against the Spanish Armada, to whom she famously addressed with the line "I know I have the body of a weak and feeble woman, but I have the heart and stomach of a king", is what she is best remembered for.

3. Nell Gwyn (1650-1687)

Of all royal mistresses down the ages, none has graced the job more joyously than “pretty witty Nell”, as Samuel Pepys called her. From an orange girl selling fruit to London theatregoers, Nell Gwyn rose to be an actress who caught the eye of the merry monarch, King Charles II. She ended up with a royal pension and a splendid house in Pall Mall.

4. Jane Austen (1775-1817)

Few lives could be more different from Nell and Emma’s than the morally upright spinsterhood of Jane Austen, our most celebrated woman novelist.

But while Regency London’s high society might be led by libertines, Jane’s demure heroines know full well that even a runaway romance which ended in marriage, like Lydia Bennet’s, brought shame and destroyed her sisters’ hopes of finding husbands. From Pride and Prejudice and Emma to Persuasion (Jane’s final story) it is Jane’s genius to observe, and sometimes satirise, their attention to status, manners and reputation.

5. Elizabeth Fry (1780-1845)

Elizabeth Fry's pioneering work as a prison reformer earns her a place in our list of the greatest British women. Her marriage to Joseph Fry united two old wealthy Quaker families, noted for piety and philanthropy, and Elizabeth became deeply involved in charity work.

She was appalled at the squalid conditions women prisoners suffered and she succeeded in reforming prisons and their conditions, introducing paid employment, female warders, and recognition that all inmates must be treated humanely.

6. Queen Victoria (1819-1901)

Victoria is the second queen who came to the throne by default after her royal uncles, King George IV and King William IV, failed to produce a surviving legitimate heir.

Crowned at just 18 in 1838, Victoria had to quickly learn the ropes of queenship and did so alongside her beloved husband, Prince Albert. She ruled for 63 years and mothered 9 children whilst on the throne, the first reigning queen to do so.

Thanks to the colonising power of British forces and trading companies, she became the most powerful woman in the world. For the most insightful autobiography on her as a queen, acclaimed historian Julia Baird's Victoria: The Queen: An Intimate Biography of the Woman who Ruled an Empire is available on Amazon.

7. Florence Nightingale (1820-1910)

The first woman to hold the Order of Merit and appear on a UK banknote, Florence Nightingale was a national treasure before she was 40.

Her pioneering work tending British troops in the Crimean War earned her the thanks of a grateful nation. The money raised in appreciation funded her nurses’ training school at London’s St Thomas’s Hospital. From there her influence and principles spread worldwide.

Every year, her birthday in May is marked at Westminster Abbey and East Wellow church, in Hampshire, where she was buried.

8. Emmeline Pankhurst (1858-1928)

Historians may disagree on the help or harm that Emmeline Pankhurst gave to the women’s suffrage movement. However, few doubt she was one of its most inspirational figureheads.

After years working for votes for women, but with little success, Emmeline established the Women’s Social and Political Union as a militant wing of the women’s movement. Their campaign of window-smashing, arson and violent demonstrations led to regular arrests, hunger strikes and brutal force feeding. Inevitably this drew mixed public reaction.

On the outbreak of war in 1914, Emmeline suspended the campaign. She encouraged women to put their efforts into war work instead. After peace was signed, women over 30 were granted the vote. Shortly before Emmeline’s death the age was reduced to 21, to match men’s votes.

9. Margaret Thatcher (1925-2013)

As Britain’s first female prime minister (1979), Mrs Thatcher’s place in history is guaranteed. Yet it is her 11 consecutive years as PM and her role as the first woman leader of a major Western democracy, that make her one of the most dominant figures in modern politics.

As leader of the Conservative Party, her pro-privatisation policy and public-spending cuts naturally brought her into open conflict with trade unions and socialists. She earned the nickname the Iron Lady. With victory in the Falklands War and her narrow escape from an IRA bomb in Brighton, her popularity soared and. In 1987, she won a then-unprecedented third general election.

But her Euro-sceptic and Poll Tax policies had caused division in her cabinet. In 1990, she was forced to resign as party leader. Two years later, she went to the House of Lords as Baroness Thatcher.

10. Queen Elizabeth II (1926-2022)

Like Elizabeth I and Victoria, the Princess Elizabeth Alexandra Mary was not expected to become queen. But the abdication of her uncle, King Edward VIII, propelled her father to the throne, as George VI. When he died tragically young, in 1952, Elizabeth found herself ruler of the UK and Commonwealth as Queen Elizabeth II, aged just 25.

She witnessed greater changes in her realms – in society, science, technology, medicine and world affairs – than any of her predecessors could have imagined. They would have been astonished at the millions of miles she travelled, hands she shook, and public engagements she fulfilled.

But they would have admired the unwavering sense of duty that gave Britain a head of state for 70 years. Her personal standards of service to her country were the envy of many countries.

In the centenary year of her birth, a new biography by historian David Cannadine celebrates her as an exceptional sovereign - you can get your copy of Queen Elizabeth II: A Concise Biography of an Exceptional Sovereign on Amazon.

11. Mary Wollstonecraft (1759-1797)

An early feminist, writer and philosopher, Mary Wollstonecraft is best known as an advocate of women’s education.

In 1792 she wrote her work A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, in which she argues that women are not naturally inferior to men but only appearedd to be because they had not received the same education. She also suggested that both men and women should be treated as rational beings, imagining a social order founded purely on reason. A Vindication of the Rights of Woman is available on Amazon and is worth perusing if, like me, you're interested in early feminism.

Mary was also the mother of another female firebrand, Mary Shelley, author of Frankenstein, which is widely considered to be the first ever science fiction novel.

12. George Eliot (1819-1880)

George Eliot was the pen name of the 'blue-stocking' novelist Mary Ann Evans, who penned Middlemarch and The Mill on the Floss, one of the most important novelists of the Victorian era.

Deriving from the 18th-century Blue Stockings Society - an intellectual group of women who hosted literary salons - the term 'blue-stocking' referred to a learned, intellectual woman of the time. Fellow author Henry James described her as a "great horse-faced bluestocking" (blue-stocking meant a le, a back-handed compliment and typical of a man at the time.

Famously, Mary Ann Evans lived with a married man, writer and philosopher George Henry Lewes, for 25 years as he was unable to divorce his wife, which, unsurprisingly, caused scandal in society, and she was shunned. However, Lewes supported her career and became her unofficial manager, so without him, she wouldn't have been able to write the fiction we know and love today.

13. Mrs Beeton (1836-1865)

A middle-class Victorian wife and mother, Isabella Beeton is probably the most 'normal' woman in this list. Her 'Book of Household Management’, published when she was just 25, is one of the most famous cookery books ever published and is still in print today. You can get your own copy of the Book of Household Management via Amazon here.

She was remarkable for having been published under her own name, though it was probably the subject of the work that deemed it 'apropriate' for a woman author. However, Isabella was a 'public woman', commuting to London for work and even taking over the editorship of her husband's magazine, The English Domestic Woman’s Magazine, for a number of years. According to the Oxford English Dictionary the term ‘Mrs Beeton’ became used as a generic term for ‘an authority on cooking and domestic subjects’ as early as 1891.

14. Beatrix Potter (1866-1943)

One of the world's most beloved authors, Beatrix Potter penned and illsutrated The Tale of Peter Rabbit and numerous other children's classics, but did you know she was also a co-founder of the National Trust?

She was also a shrewd businesswoman, a marketing expert, a scientist and a farmer, who strategically bought up great swathes of land, namely in her beloved Lake District. She was a passionate environmental campaigner, an eco-warrior and, undoubtedly, a trailblazing feminist.

16. Agatha Christie (1890-1976)

The best-selling novelist of all time, of course Agatha Christie is on our list of the greatest women in British history. Throughout her 66 published novles, over 150 short stories and more than 25 plays the Queen of Crime created such characters as Hercule Poirot and Miss Jane Marple, and is the author of the longest-running play in the history of the West End, The Mousetrap.

She was also a formidable woman, travelling the world, interested in science, psychology and fast cars, and not taking any nonsense from anyone. Rumours fly that she was also the first woman to standup on a surf board - but that can't be verified unfortunately.

My favourite autobiography of the novelist is the wonderful historian Lucy Worsley's, Agatha Christie: A Very Elusive Woman, which is available on amazon. As Worsley says Agatha Christie was, 'was thrillingly, scintillatingly modern.'

17. Flora McDonald (1722 - 1790)

Probably Scotland's most beloved heroine, Flora McDonald is best known for risking her life to save Bonne Prince Charlied from oncoming British troops after the Battle of Culloden in 1746.

Just 24 when she discovered the banished prince cowering on the island of Benbecula in the Outer Hebrides, Flora agreed to help him escape. They disguised the prince as an Irish maidservant and together they rowed, over the sea to Skye - yes, this is where the song comes from.

Betrayed by the Scotsmen that helped them row the boat, Flora was soon arrested an imprisoned in Dunstaffnage Castle, before she was transferred to the Tower of London. After her release she went straight home to Scotland and married Allan MacDonald of Kingsburgh, the son of Lady Margaret of Kingsburgh who had sheltered her and the prince for a night during their daring escape.

18. Mary, Queen of Scots

Our final candidate for the greatest British women in history needs very little introduction. The most romanticised of Scottish monarchs and probably the one who divides opinions the most, Mary, Queen of Scots lived a fascinating life, cursed by her own royal crown and bad advise from the men that surrounded her.

Whatever your opinion of her, there is no denying her brilliance and bravery in the face of danger. She led troops into battle, was fiercely protective of her son and heir, briefly wore the crown of France, and remained gracious and brave even when her the axe swung down on her neck. Her legacy lives on in plays, books and films and her dramatic life is one of the most studied in British history - a victim of her own circumstances and sex, who did what she could with what she had.

Of course, we've talked about all these women that broke out of societal norms to make the pages of history, whether with crowns on their heads, or money in their pockets, but what about all the women we don't know about?

As ever, many women historians have spent time discussing the subject, and my favourite historical fiction author Philippa Gregory has produced a brilliant tome on the subject, Normal Women: 900 years of making history which is avialable on Amazon. Her work, for the first time in written history, puts the invisible women of British history - 50% of the population lets not forget, at the forefront. A long time coming!

item.title: Celebrating 30 years of unforgettable walking holidays, Contours Holidays launches exclusive 2026 anniversary offers with limited availability across the UK’s most sought-after routes
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: To celebrate its 30th anniversary, Contours Holidays is offering lots of special deals throughout 2026. This is your chance to book your summer walking holiday with Contours...
item.content:encoded:
If you have ever dreamed of traversing the Lake District, tracing Cornwall’s coastal paths, or meandering through the heather-clad hills of Scotland, the team at Contours Holidays knows exactly how to make those dreams a reality.

A Contours Holiday puts you in charge, while simultaneously managing all the logistical headaches themselves. They launched their first Twin Centre Walking Holidays in 2025, with new destinations added in 2026, but also offer trail running holidays, dog-friendly trails and many other options.

Since 1996, this spirited, muddy-booted crew has been crafting self-guided walking holidays across England, Scotland and Wales – journeys designed with one simple idea in mind: the best adventures happen when you’re free to explore at your own pace.

Contours is the well-organised friend who plans every detail so you can enjoy the views. Your accommodations (breakfast included) are booked, your luggage is moved from stop to stop, taxis whisk you to and from the trail, if needed, and your bespoke holiday pack comes complete with maps, directions, and local insights. And should anything unexpected crop up, the team’s 24/7 support line is manned with well-practised colleagues ready to iron out the bumps.

In 2026, Contours celebrates its 30th anniversary – that’s three decades of blisters, breakthroughs, belly laughs and the satisfaction that comes from helping walkers from all over the world discover the UK’s wild and wonderful countryside. To mark the milestone, the company will be rolling out special offers and discounts throughout the year. But really, the heart of the celebration is the people who make Contours what it is.

This is a team built on passion – for walking, for the outdoors, and for each other. Every year, the team heads for the hills for orienteering and emergency skills training – a day that usually includes at least one muddy slip, several squelchy fields, and plenty of laughter.

These training days aren’t just about skills; they’re about staying connected to the experience their walkers have. After all, what better way to understand a tricky stile than by hauling a colleague over it?

Contours is, at its heart, a close-knit bunch. They’ve weathered storms both literal and figurative, and done it together with grit, humour and a kettle that, thankfully, only breaks occasionally. On those days, morale becomes the real emergency exercise.

That’s what sets Contours Holidays apart. Every itinerary is built by real people who know the trails, love the outdoors, and genuinely care about helping others discover the joy of slow, purposeful travel. No two tours are quite the same because no two walkers are and that’s just the way the team likes it.

Whether you’re a seasoned trekker or a curious first-timer, the Contours crew is ready to help shape your next adventure.

Visit www.contours.co.uk or call 01629 821900 and let the team (and perhaps an office dog or two) help craft a UK walking holiday you’ll never forget.

item.title: How to get the hottest ticket since the Middle Ages - slots to see the Bayeux Tapestry in London this year are expected to sell out quicker than an arrow to the eye
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: The British Museum have announced that tickets to see the Bayeux Tapestry - which will be on British soil for the first time in nearly 1,000 years - will go on sale this July. Here’s everything you need to know about this hotly anticipated event
item.content:encoded:
2026 marks a momentous year for the British Museum, as September will see the return of the Bayeux Tapestry, one of the most important artefacts in English history, to England for the first time since it was made, nearly 1,000 years ago.

The tapestry, a 70-metre-long embroidery masterpiece which tells the story of the Battle of Hastings, will be on loan to the UK for the first time since it was made, thanks to an historic agreement between the French and British governments.

The hotly anticipated tickets to see the tapestry will go on sale from 1 July 2026, and visitors will be able to see the hand-embroided medieval cloth, running at 70-metres long, which follows the events of the Norman conquest of England in 1066, and the defeat of King Harold by William the Conqueror, who became William I of England.

Unsurprisingly, this historic loan is expected to be one of the most important and popular events in the museum's history, with two ticket drops for slots between September and December 2026 and January and July 2027. Visitors have been advised to sign up to the British Museum's newsletter to receive updates on tickets.

While you are patiently waiting for the first ticket release, why not read up a little more on this momentous piece of history and what it really means for it to be back on English soil. I recommend perusing The Story of the Bayeux Tapestry: Unravelling the Norman Conquest available on Amazon, by historians David Musgrove and Michael Lewis. It recounts the history of one of the most important works of art in the history of the world, setting it in its historic context and answering all of the most important questions about this medieval artefact, including some of its most fascinating legends and mysteries.

The loan of the Bayeux Tapestry - how will it work?

In exchange for the loan of the tapestry, which is normally displayed at the Bayeux Museum in Normandy, France, British treasures, including artefacts from the Anglo-Saxon burial mounds at Sutton Hoo and the 12th-century Lewis chess pieces, will travel to museums in Normandy. The tapestry is being moved to the UK while the Bayeux Museum undergoes refurbishment.

What's on the Bayeux Tapestry?

Although the final part of the embroidery is still missing, the Bayeux Tapestry ends with the Anglo-Saxons fleeing at the end of the Battle of Hastings in 1066, when William the Conqueror became King of England. The magnificent, enormous embroidery includes 58 different scenes, 626 different characters and no less than 202 horses, all helping to give an account of the medieval period in Normandy and England and telling the story of the battle and military traditions of the time, as well as little details of everyday life in the Middle Ages.

Why is this such an important event?

Dr Nicholas Cullinan OBE, Director of the British Museum, said: “The Bayeux Tapestry is one of the most important cultural artefacts from the Medieval world and has been a source of inspiration and wonder for generations.

“It’s difficult to capture just how extraordinary the opportunity to display the tapestry in the UK for the first time in 1000 years is. I’m glad we’ll now all be able to share in that excitement as people book their tickets and travel to London to take part in what is undoubtedly going to be one of the biggest cultural events the British Museum has hosted – one for the ages.”

George Osborne, Chair of Trustees British Museum, said: “This will be without doubt the biggest year in the Museum's history - as we expect 7.5m people to visit and the Bayeux Tapestry returns to UK soil for the first time in a 1,000 years.
