“It is a defining piece of our nation’s history and a truly once-in-a-lifetime experience. I hope people mark their calendars and seize the chance to see it when it arrives, in what promises to be an extraordinary moment for the country.”

Recent public polling carried out for the British Museum also revealed that 71% of parents want their child to learn more about the story of the tapestry while it is in the UK, reflecting support for an increasing awareness of the events of 1066 and the Norman Conquest.

In response to this, the Museum have announced that there will be dedicated opening hours for British schoolchildren to see the tapestry, to guarantee as much access as possible. The museum is also undertaking plans for a national programme about the Battle of Hastings and the tapestry to support the learning of those unable to make it to London.

For parents and children, or those who enjoy the silly side of history, Terry Deary's Horrible Histories books, which I grew up reading, are unmatched when it comes to teaching children history. The Stormin' Normans, available on Amazon is a hilarious and highly educational and accurate recount of the events that led to the Norman Conquest, and that changed English history forever. See you in the ticket queue!

item.title: London’s Villages: Marylebone
item.dc:creator: The London Magazine Editors
item.description: This city-centre neighbourhood offers a village lifestyle with boundless connectivity, culture and community
item.content:encoded:
Once home to Dickens, Byron and Paul McCartney and known worldwide for its salubrious private medical clinics, in recent years Marylebone has reinvented itself as one of the most desirable villages in prime central London.

First impressions

“Marylebone Village boasts the golden formula today’s buyers desire: a perfect balance of connectivity and community,” says Nick Poppe, co-head of sales at Savills Marylebone. ”It’s for these reasons that once people put down roots here, they rarely choose to leave, instead opting to upsize or downsize as their needs evolve.”

Its location is a major draw. “Marylebone village is one of London’s most sought-after neighbourhoods, with consistently high demand from families, professionals and international buyers. It’s central enough to walk to the West End but also providing easy access out of the city for weekend escapes – meaning it’s an ideal choice for those seeking a pied‑à‑terre,” says Ali Mathews, head of Marylebone sales at Knight Frank.

The high street is a carefully curated mix of independent boutiques, restaurants and cafes, overseen by the Howard de Walden Estate. “The estate works closely with the retailers and hospitality outlets to organise seasonal activities and community events, creating a lovely atmosphere for residents and local workers,” says Mathews. “The restaurant scene in particular has seen some notable new openings, including

The Hart (from the team behind Notting Hill’s The Pelican and The Fat Badger), Kudu and the second London outpost of Claude Bosi’s Josephine.”

Buyers and tenants are attracted to the range of property on offer, from Georgian townhouses and red-brick mansion blocks to more modest Georgian and early Victorian terraces. “In recent years, the area has also welcomed newer developments, which make ideal pieds-à-terre and appeal strongly to the first-time buyer market,” adds Poppe. Yet despite its stylish profile, Marylebone’s sense of community is clear. “At weekends, locals come together to enjoy the charms of the farmers’ market, stopping to chat with familiar faces and support local vendors,” he says.

A day out

9AM

From head to toe

Celebrate lighter days with a health reboot. Book some R&R at ReBase on St Vincent Street, where services range from ice baths to yoga. Alternatively, head to the Margaret Dabbs clinic on New Cavendish Street for beauty and body treatments or book yourself in for a haircut at Larry King. If you’re feeling peckish, breakfast spots include The Ivy Cafe, Lina Stores and Natural Kitchen.

11AM

Culture vulture

Stroll to the Wallace Collection on Manchester Square, a hidden gem of world-class 18th century French art, armour and porcelain showcased in opulent period rooms. In March, look out for special events around the influences of Caravaggio (workshops on 2-3 March).

1PM

Dine and shop

Try neighbourhood favourites such as Jikoni. Newly opened Kudu on Moxon Street, serves a twist on South African food. Elsewhere, you can try Junsei’s new menu, Mexican eatery Cavita, and pub The Prince Regent on the corner of the high street. Post-lunch high street wander destinations will include Daunt Books for literary fans and the newly opened Travellers’ Tales. French favourites Sezane and Oh My Cream! will sate fashion and beauty shoppers, while Oh Pioneers uses Liberty fabrics for its clothing collections.

3PM

Spring step

Head north to Regent’s Park where March is the time for cherry blossom trees and tulips to bloom. Meander through the rose gardens, gaze out over the boating lake (boating and pedalo hire opens in April), or simply find a bench. Get into the spirit of the season even further by embarking on Marylebone Village’s The Big Egg Hunt from 24 March.

5PM

Hotel inspector

Explore hotels with history. Famed for its palm trees, The Landmark opened in 1899 as the Great Central Hotel, and even hosted suffragettes such as Emmeline Pankhurst at a ‘welcome back from prison’ breakfast. Meanwhile, The Welbeck Hotel, has a new stay inspired by Sherlock Holmes opening in May for the detective’s birthday, including a visit to the Sherlock Holmes Museum.

Ask a local: Jayne Alexander, founder of Travellers' Tales London

"Marylebone has a rare balance that's increasingly hard to find in London. It feels village-like, yet intellectually alive. A place where independent cafes, family-run restaurants and excellent galleries sit alongside world-class culture.

I love the everyday rituals here: a morning coffee in Marylebone High Street, lunch at a long-standing favourite, like Fischer's, or a viewing The Wallace Collection. It's an area that rewards curiosity and exploration, which mirrors how I believe travel should be experienced. It’s also why Travellers’ Tales feels so at home here.

It’s a space for browsing, conversation and discovery, where travel begins with a book, a question or a spark of curiosity. From there, those ideas are shaped into carefully designed itineraries and expeditions."

Food & Drink

Carlotta

77-78 Marylebone High Street

The vibrant Italian trattoria is from the famed Big Mamma Group which has satisfied the appetites of many Londoners. Showcasing classics such as truffle fettuccine and seafood alla vodka, Carlotta promises generous sharing dishes. Its walls adorned with vintage Italian family wedding photos, a must-try (if there’s room) is the wedding cake.

ULI

15 Seymour Place

The stylish, contemporary pan-Asian restaurant offers a sophisticated-yet-relaxed dining experience. Sibling to ULI Notting Hill, Marylebone’s branch brings the same commitment to authentic Asian flavours, drawing from traditional Chinese, Thai, Malaysian and Singaporean influences. The curated menu has a selection of sharing dishes, including handmade dumplings, fragrance curries and satay skewers.

Fischer’s

50 Marylebone High Street

Step into this charming Viennese-style cafe that’s part of the Wolseley Group. Fischer’s welcomes guests to experience a modern version of the Austrian capital’s 20th century grand cafe culture, with graceful curved banquettes and vintage artwork. The menu is filled with hearty Austrian dishes, such as chicken schnitzel, sausages with sauerkraut and indulgent desserts.

Roketsu

12 New Quebec Street

Led by chef-patron Daisuke Hayashi who trained at the Michelin-starred Kikuni, Roketsu brings intimate Kappo-style cuisine. Designed to reflect Japanese tradition, the interiors feature pieces crafted by Kyoto master carpenters. Roketsu has recently unveiled a renewed menu, organised into categories including raw, grilled, sushi and tempura.

The Italian Greyhound

62 Seymour Street

The all-day dining haven founded by brothers Gabriel and Marcello Bernardi, brings generous Italian dining with British accents. Menu highlights include pastas, wood-fired pizzas and its acclaimed Sunday roast feasts, which bridges the gap between the two cuisines seamlessly.

Schools

”Top schools bring families to Marylebone,” confirms Susannah Massey, lettings director at Dexters Marylebone. The Wetherby group, offers seamless all-through education for boys. Wetherby Marylebone on Bryanston Square opens a new reception class this September, and goes up to year eight. Planting the seeds for success, there's strong progression into top schools, including neighbouring Wetherby Senior on Marylebone Lane. For girls, established in 2002, Queen’s College Prep on Portland Place offers pre-prep and prep education in a warm environment within a grand townhouse. Senior sister day school Queen’s College on Harley Street celebrates its 178th year educating girls in an environment they can thrive in. A stone's throw from Regent's Park, Francis Holland Regent's Park (above) is a leading day school for girls, where pupils are intellectually ambitious and go on to forge successful careers. The school's next open morning for 11+ is on 19 March.

On the market: Marylebone

Ask an agent: Rosy Khalastchy, Beauchamp Estates

“Marylebone is city-village living at its very best. With a unique mix of architecture and one of the best high streets in London, there's a sense of intimacy and community – you can ‘live local’ in this beautiful London enclave, with everything you need to feed your soul and taste buds within walking distance. Waitrose and specialist food retailers, like the Fromagerie and Fish Works, fashion, cafe life, the latest London venue from Big Mamma Group – Italian restaurant Carlotta – and art and parks to contemplate and enjoy. Property for sale in the market has already absorbed last year’s shift in sentiment, with the majority now reflecting that change in the asking price. Vendors who adjusted early are seeing genuine buyers express interest as they respond to the new reality and underlying value.”

DEVONSHIRE PLACE

Set within a Grade II-listed Georgian property, this exceptionally renovated one-bedroom lateral apartment offers the best of both worlds, with high ceilings and period details enhancing contemporary fixtures and fittings. And with two balconies and a private terrace, it offers rare outdoor space in the heart of buzzing Marylebone. £2.25m Agent: beauchamp.com

SEYMOUR PLACE

Freshly developed and thoughtfully designed, this two-bed split-level apartment benefits from outdoor space and its position just minutes from the High Street and Hyde Park. £2.25m Agent: savills.com

PARK CRESCENT WEST

This five-bedroom penthouse duplex enjoys views of Regent's Park, as well as 24-hour concierge service, garden access, valet parking, and a host of wellness facilities. £24.75m Agent: knightfrank.com/savills.com

item.title: This is your definitive guide to all of the UK’s National Parks - from the munros of the Cairngorms, to the sweeping valleys of the South Downs
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Home to no less than 15 National Parks, the UK’s landscapes are as diverse as they are beautiful. This is your guide to all of the National Parks in the UK, and a timeline of when they joined the ranks with protected status
item.content:encoded:
It was in the early 19th century that Romantic poet William Wordsworth claimed his beloved Lake District as “a sort of national property, in which every man has a right and interest who has an eye to perceive and a heart to enjoy”

By the late 19th century, the first 'freedom to roam' bill was introduced to Parliament by MP James Bryce. Although it took nearly another 50 years and two world wars for change to come about, in 1949, the government passed an Act of Parliament establishing National Parks to conserve and enhance their natural beauty and provide recreational opportunities for the public.

There are now 15 designated National Parks across the UK - 10 in England, three in Wales and two in Scotland. Each one has been designated as a protected landscape because of its own distinct and special qualities, and is an oasis for wildlife. There are thousands of kilometres of public rights of way across the parks, over 1,300km of which are suitable for those with accessibility issues, giving everyone the chance to explore these beautiful landscapes.

In celebration of the 75th anniversary of the creation of the first National Parks in the UK, award-winning writer and lover of the outdoors, Carey Davies, published his National Parks of the United Kingdom: The UK’s most inspiring landscapes. This wonderful book explores each landscape and its incredible diversity in great detail and contains over 200 stunning photos of these wonderful places.

Created to protect Britain's cultural heritage and strengthen our connections with nature, each of these National Parks should be on your bucket list, all so astonishingly different and packed full of ancient history, unrivalled heritage, unspoiled views and remarkable wildlife.

Here, we go on a journey through all 15 of the UK's National Parks, and highlight a few must-sees along the way.

1. The Peak District, England (1951)

Covering over 550 square miles, the Peak District was the UK's first designated National Park. This was mainly due to the actions of the 1932 Kinder Scout Trespass, which involved intense public protests against restricted access to the moorlands. Geoffrey Glasby D Sc's book Mass Trespass on Kinder Scout in 1932 tells the remarkable story of the history-changing protests.

Close to major UK cities like Manchester and Sheffield, the Peak District was a vital 'breathing' space for the public, and features beautiful, unique, and very dramatic scenery of gritstone edges, rolling limestone dales, and wild and open heather Moorland.

Some of the best things to do in the Peak District include the beautiful stately home of Chatsworth and the Georgian spa town of Buxton, which has a history dating back to Roman Britain.

2. The Lake District, England (1951)

Following hot on the heels of the Peaks, the Lake District also became a designated National Park in 1951, and remains England's largest, covering 912 square miles of beautiful lakes, mountains and valleys.

Long admired by poets and writers like Wordsworth, Keats and Coleridge, the Lake District's incredibly unique landscape also became one of the UK's UNESCO World Heritage Sites in 2017.

In the northwest of Cumbria, visitors flock to its picturesque villages, shimmering lakes and waterfalls and dramatic peaks - it is also home to England's highest mountain, Scaffell Pike.

The Lake District's loveliest spots include Grasmere, once home to William Wordsworth and Windermere, England's largest lake.

3. Dartmoor, England (1951)

Also established in the same year at the Lakes and Peaks, Dartmoor in the South-West of the country was one of England's first National Parks, covering 368 square miles.

Its National Park status was granted to protect its wild landscape, including rugged granite tors, extensive moorland, and conserve its rich archaeology and wildlife.

Highlights of the swirling, moody moors of Dartmoor include the dramatic Great Staple Tor, pretty villages like Widecombe-in-the-Moor and the haunted castle of Okehampton, one of the most supernatural spots in Britain.

4. Eryri, Wales (1951) - previously Snowdonia

Also joining the ranks in the same year, the magical landscapes of Eryri National Park are steeped in Welsh myth and legend. Its status was established to safeguard its rugged mountains, lakes, and forests while providing public access for recreation.

It's 823 square miles include 15 peaks over 3,000 ft (including Wales's highest mountain, Snowdon), deep valleys, and significant wildlife habitats. It has more national nature reserves than any other park in England and Wales.

From medieval castles to places that swirl with legends of King Arthur and surprising links to Mount Everest, there are plenty of brilliant things to do in Eryri National Park.

5. North York Moors, England (1952)

The UK's fifth National Park followed in 1952, when the 554 square miles of moorland in North Yorkshire were given protective status, containing one of the largest expanses of heather moorland in the UK.

Abounding with natural wonders, from sweeping moorland to ancient woods, and incredible historic sites, as well as postcard-worthy fishing villages and wide sandy beaches, these are the landscapes that inspired the Brontë sisters.

Must-see sights in the North York Moors include the pretty village of Goathland, home to the North York Moors Railway and a stand-in for Hogsmeade station in Harry Potter, the incredible Castle Howard, inspiration for Brideshead Revisited, the ruins of Rievaulx Abbey, and the beautiful Mallyan Spout Waterfall.

6. Pembrokeshire Coast, Wales (1952)

Covering 186 miles of some of the most stunning coastline in Britain, Pembrokeshire Coast National Park was given protected status in 1952 to conserve its rugged clifftops, wide-open beaches and winding estuaries.

A favoured holiday spot for many Brits, this beautiful coastline includes the Pembrokeshire Coast Path, stretching from St Dogmaels in the north to Amroth in the south, along which you'll discover limestone cliffs and golden beaches, with pretty fishing villages along the way.

Highlights of the Pembrokeshire Coast National Park include the colourful seaside town of Tenby, the Blue Lagoon, famed for its vivid blue waters, the restaurants and galleries of the harbour village of Porthgain, and the thousands of puffins that flock to this coastline in the spring and summer.

7. Yorkshire Dales, England (1954)

Located primarily in North Yorkshire and Cumbria, with a small portion in Lancashire, the beautiful Yorkshire Dales became a National Park in 1954 to protect its outstanding scenery, rich cultural heritage and diverse wildlife.

Covering 841 square miles, the Yorkshire Dales are home to sleepy villages, historic castles, ancient valleys and majestic abbeys. There are towns like Wensleydale, famed for its cheese, the 12th-century Middleham Castle, the childhood home of the last Yorkist King, Richard III, and the haunting remains of lead mines, once hotbeds for industry.

The Dales are familiar to many for being the site of many of the All Creatures Great and Small filming locations, and, more recently, Emerald Fennell's Wuthering Heights was filmed here, in the heart of the Dales.

8. Exmoor, England (1954)

Heading back down to England's south coast, Exmoor National Park was another landscape that inspired Samuel Taylor Coleridge's poetry. Covering 267 miles of Devon and Somerset, Exmoor is wild and beautiful, with heather moorland, typically English undulating green hills, dramatic shoreline cliffs, dense woodland and tumbling rivers.

There are medieval villages, ancient castles and free-roaming ponies to admire, and legends of the devil and the fabled 'Beast of Exmoor' to hear stories of by the fire in a cosy pub after a day exploring.

Exmoor is also home to some of the best wild swimming spots in Britain, all of which can be discovered in this brilliant guide, Wild Swimming Walks Exmoor & North Devon: 28 river, lake & coastal days out.

9. Northumberland, England (1956)

This year, marking 70 years since its official designation as a National Park, Northumberland National Park is also a Dark Sky Park and is one of the best places in the UK for stargazing.

Stretching from the Scottish border with England to the ancient Roman Hadrian's Wall, Northumberland is known for its tranquil remoteness, and 70% of the park is unspoiled open moorland.

Northumberland National Park is home to 405 square miles of exceptional upland landscape and rich cultural heritage, including several incredibly preserved castles and many Roman sites, like Hadrian's Wall and Roman villas like Vindolanda.

10. Bannau Brycheiniog, Wales (1957) - previously Brecon Beacons

Joining the ranks in 1957 as the third Welsh National Park, Bannau Brycheiniog covers 530 square miles of outstanding natural beauty, including mountains and valleys, diverse wildlife and a rich cultural heritage.

Bannau Brycheiniog is home to the Central Beacons mountain range, including Pen Y Fan and the beautiful Fforest Fawr Geopark.

In 2023, the park, just like Eryri, adopted its Welsh name as its official title to reflect the Welsh government's renewed commitment to celebrating Welsh culture and language.

Published last year, Mal Morrison's 50 Gems of Bannau Brycheiniog (Brecon Beacons): The History & Heritage of the Most Iconic Places explores its diverse landscapes in more detail and is perfect for intrepid explorers to the area.

11. The Broads, England (1989)

Although not technically an official National Park like the others, The Broads in Norfolk is officially a protected landscape with equivalent status to a national park, covering 303 square kilometres of waterways and fens in East Anglia.

It is Britain's largest protected wetland, and was originally dug out in medieval times to provide peat for fuel. The Broads is also the only English National Park to have a city within its midst, Norwich.

Perfect for boating, walking, and wildlife spotting, there are castles to see, Roman ruins to explore, and charming villages to potter in, including the capital of the Broads, Wroxham.

12. Loch Lomond & The Trossachs, Scotland (2002)

Established as Scotland's first National Park in 2002, the bonnie, bonnie banks of Loch Lomond National Park covers 720 square miles, including Loch Lomond itself, the Trossachs hills, and surrounding glens.

Created with the aim of promoting sustainable tourism, as it is, understandably, one of the most popular tourist spots in all of Scotland, the National Park conserves its exceptional natural scenery of mountains, forests and waterways, biodiversity, and incredible historic sites, and to support its local communities.

Of course, Loch Lomond itself is the star of the show, but the Park is also home to 21 munros, two nature reserves and two forest parks.

13. Cairngorms, Scotland (2003)

Following suit to its Scottish cousin, the Cairngorms National Park gained its official status in 2003, and is the country's largest, covering a whopping 1748 square miles of Highland landscapes.

Beloved by Queen Victoria, and home to Balmoral, which is still used as a family home by the Royal Family today, the Cairngorms National Park is home to the shortbread-tin Scottish landscapes we know and love, including castles like Braemar and Blair, mountains that are ski hotspots in the winter, and some of Scotland's best whisky distilleries, such as Glenlivet.

The Cairngorms: A Secret History by Patrick Baker is a lovely book that spills the secret histories of this beautiful area of the country, and his own adventures in its wild, diverse landscapes.

14. New Forest, England (2005)

Packed full of history, and a camping holiday favourite for British families, the New Forest became a National Park in 2005 and covers 220 square miles across Hampshire and Wiltshire.

Its landscape is rare and ancient, with lowland heaths and woodlands, and is one of Europe's most important biodiversity landscapes. Its history is fascinating too. The forest has been managed for centuries, with its roots as a royal hunting ground established by William the Conqueror in 1079.

You might spot wild ponies or free-roaming donkeys, deer and even pigs on your journeys through this pretty National Park, as it is one of the only places in Britain where animals have right of way, thanks to the medieval law that still governs how the land is lived in and used.

There are quaint villages with thatched cottages, ancient churches and grand stately homes scattered throughout the New Forest, and it really is a lovely summer holiday destination.

15. South Downs, England (2010)

The UK's newest National Park, the green, rolling hills of the chalky South Downs, covers 631 square miles from Winchester to Eastbourne on the South Coast.

This is the green and pleasant land that William Blake described in his verse, And Did Those Feet in Ancient Time, which he wrote in the pretty village of Felpham. Across Hampshire, West Sussex and East Sussex, you'll discover the undulating downs dotted with sheep, ancient woodlands, vineyards creating some of the finest wine in England and rich biodiverse heathland, with views out to the sea.

There are Iron Age hilltop forts, Norman castles like Arundel Castle, Roman cities such as Chichester and Winchester, the seaside city of Brighton and pretty, historic county towns such as Lewes.

Of course, I grew up here so I could be biased, but the South Downs National Park really does have it all. This brilliant book, The South Downs by Peter Brandon, uncovers the millennia-long history of this incredible area of landscape, from some of Europe's oldest inhabitants and their farming rituals, right up until the 21st century.

item.title: On display for the first time in history, this scarce silver medal reveals the forgotten story of the Suffragette’s chief nurse, who risked her life to save theirs
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: A new display at the Florence Nightingale Museum tells the unknown story of the Suffragette’s chief nurse, Catherine Pine, whose job it was to look after the women of the movement
item.content:encoded:
When I think of the Suffragettes and their brave movement to change women's history forever, I think of the well-known faces at the forefront of their campaign, and those who went to great extremes, even the ultimate sacrifice, to draw attention to their worthy cause.

The Pankhursts, Emmeline and her daughters Christabel and Sylvia, are the best-known of the Suffragettes, the founders of the Women's Social and Political Union. And of course, there is Emily Davidson, who threw herself under the king's horse and died for her cause.

But what of the women behind the scenes of the movement? In particular, what of those whose role it was to look after the women who were risking their lives and their health to get Parliament to pay attention?

In their book Hidden Heroines: The Forgotten Suffragettes, the brilliant historians Maggie Andrews and Janis Lomas have collated 48 biographies of lesser-known suffragettes who tirelessly campaigned for the vote. From all walks of life and all corners of the British Isles, these women paved the way for the success of the movement.

To mark International Women's Day on 8 March, at the Florence Nightingale Museum in London, a previously unseen and extremely rare silver Suffragette Medal will go on display for the first time. The medal was awarded to Nurse Catherine Pine, the chief nurse to Emmeline Pankhurst, the leader of the movement, and has been missing for several decades.

In Focus: Nurse Catherine Pine will run from 8 March 2026, International Women's Day, until 4 October 2026, and visitors will be able to see the beautiful silver medal, which is adorned with eleven silver bars, and the familiar purple, white and green ribbons of the suffragettes.

The medal was awarded to Nurse Catherine 'For Duty' by the Women's Social and Political Union, and the dates are inscribed in the medal itself attest to eleven incidents in which Nurse Pine gave crucial care to the suffragettes. These 'incidents' were usually arrests and imprisonments under the Cat and Mouse Act.

The bar March 29th 1913, marks Sylvia Pankhurst's release from prison after hunger striking and being force-fed, and all other bars reference Emmeline's arrests and release from prison. The final date - July 18th 1914 - marks Emmeline's final arrest, after which she escaped to France rather than heading to prison in London.

Who was Nurse Catherine Pine?

Nurse Catherine Pine had trained at St Bartholomew's, where she rose through the ranks to become House Sister. She left the hospital in 1907 to set up a nursing home in Notting Hill, which later became a refuge for suffragettes recovering from hunger strikes and imprisonment. After caring for Emmeline Pankhurst there, Nurse Pine became her chief nurse.

In another daring move, there is one story that tells that Pine also helped Christabel and Sylvia Pankhurst to escape arrest by lending them nursing uniforms as disguises.

During the Second World War, Pine continued to serve Emmeline and supported her campaign for the welfare of the 'war babies' born outside of marriage when their fathers had been called up. She became the main caregiver of the four babies that were adopted by Emmeline and travelled with them to the US and Canada.

After Nurse Pine died in 1941, she bequeathed her medal to the British College of Nurses, but then historians somehow lost track of the medal. By chance, when a US professor Dr Hope Elizabeth May, saw a blog post by historian Elizabeth Crawford OBE in 2016, the medal was finally tracked down.

The medal was acquired at auction by Dr May in 2024, and she is helping the Florence Nightingale Museum with their special new display.

The Director of the Florence Nightingale Museum, Laura Sharpe, said, “We are so excited to be displaying Nurse Pine’s medal publicly for the first time. Pine is an overlooked figure in the Suffragette movement, yet her skill and passion for nursing is proven from her medal to have been incredibly significant. We are extremely grateful to the wonderful detective work of Elizabeth Crawford and Dr May who have dedicated themselves to giving Pine the credit she deserves and ensuring her last wishes are honoured. It feels fitting to be hailing her in a museum devoted to significant a woman of courage and care.''

You can discover more about Crawford and May's journey with the medal, here.

If you'd like to know more, this podcast from apple podcasts A Legacy of Learning: Nurse Catherine Pine's Bequest of Her Suffragette Treasures (April 21, 2024) features Elizabeth Crawford discussing the work of Catherine Pine and the journey of the mystery of her long-lost medal.

And of course, all this discussion of the Florence Nightingale Museum without mention of the Lady of the Lamp herself. Here, we discover the story of Britain's most famous nurse, Florence Nightingale, the Victorian pioneer who saved the lives of countless people.

item.title: This is the only timeline of all the kings and queens of England and Great Britain you’ll ever need - from the Saxons to our very own King Charles III
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From the very first Anglo-Saxon to unify the kingdoms of England, to the tyrannous reign of King Henry VIII and current King Charles III - this is our ultimate timeline of all the kings and queens of England and Great Britain in order
item.content:encoded:
The monarchy has been at the heart of British life for almost 1,200 years. Whether performing ceremonial duties or ruling absolutely, the Kings and Queens of England and Britain have shaped the direction, character and history of this great nation.

Of course, there are the household names that every school child learns the name of - King Henry VIII, Elizabeth I, King Charles I and Queen Victoria - famous for their uniquely extraordinary reigns, and the monarchs whose history is shrouded by mystery and murder, like King Richard III.

But England and Great Britain has had about 65 reigning monarchs since its very first in 827 (some rulers are disputed for various reasons), and 41 since William the Conqueror invaded from Normandy in 1066. So what happened to all these kings and queens?

This brilliant book, A Brief History of British Kings & Queens by Mike Ashley, was my trusty companion through my history degree and is the perfect introduction for anyone wanting to know more about Britain's kings and queens, from the royals of recorded history, as well as pre-recorded and semi-legendary kings like King Arthur. An encyclopedia of royalty, if you will, and probably the only kings and queens book you'll ever need.

So, who was the first king of England, who was the first queen, and what was the order they all came in? This is our cover-all timeline of all the kings and queens of England and Great Britain, from King Egbert to King Charles III.

A timeline of the Kings and Queens of England and Great Britain

The Saxon Kings (827 - 1066)

The first king of all of England is considered to be King Egbert of Wessex because he briefly established a unified Lordship over all the Anglo-Saxon Kingdoms. He conquered Kent, Sussex, Surrey, and Essex, before overcoming Mercia, a dominant force in southern England. Although some historians suggest Athelstan was the first king of England, for our timeline, Egbert is where we're kicking things off.

Egbert (827-839)

Aethelwulf (839-856)

Aethelbald (856-860)

Aethelbert (860-866)

Aethelred (866-871)

Alfred the Great (871-899)

Edward The Elder (899-924)

Athelstan (924-939)

Edmund (939-946)

Eadred (946-955)

Eadwig (955-959)

Edgar (959-975)

Edward the Martyr (975-978)

Aethelred the Unready (978-1016)

Edmund Ironside (1016-1016)

Canute (1016-1035)

Harold I (1035-1040)

Harthacanute (1040-1042)

Edward the Confessor (1042-1066)

The Norman Kings (1066 - 1087)

After his success at the Battle of Hastings and the death of Harold Godwinson, William of Normandy took the throne as King William I. He was crowned in Westminster Abbey on Christmas Day 1066. The following Norman kings succeeded him, his two sons and grandson.

William I – William the Conqueror (1066-1087)

William Rufus (1087-1100)

Henry I (1100-1135)

Stephen (1135-1154)

The Plantagenet Kings (1154 - 1399)

The mighty Plantagenet dynasty took the throne with the accession of Henry II in 1154. His accession marked the end of a civil war in England, The Anarchy, following the death of King Stephen, who had contested the throne with Henry's mother, Queen Matilda. The 1153 Treaty of Winchester recognised Henry as the rightful successor, and there was a peaceful transition in 1154.

Henry II 1154-1189

Richard I (1189-1199) - Richard the Lionheart

John (1199-1216)

Henry III (1216 -1272)

Edward I (1272-1307)

Edward II (1307-1327)

Edward III (1327-1377)

Richard II (1377-1399)

The House of Lancaster (1399 - 1461)

After Henry Bolingbroke deposed his cousin King Richard II, having been banished by him and then staging a coup, the House of Lancaster took over England, and he ruled as King Henry IV.

Henry IV (1399-1413)

Henry V (1413-1422)

Henry VI (1422-1461)

The House of York (1461 - 1485)

Descended from Edmund of Langley, first Duke of York, fourth son of King Edward III, the House of York took the crown during the Wars of the Roses in 1461. Henry VI's Yorkist cousin had him captured and imprisoned in the Tower of London and took the throne as King Edward IV.

Henry was briefly restored to the throne by Richard Neville ("Warwick the Kingmaker") in 1470, but, in 1471, Edward retook power, killing Henry's only son and imprisoning Henry once again. Henry died in the Tower in May 1471, possibly killed on the orders of King Edward.

Edward IV (1461- 1483)

Edward V (1483 – 1483) - the eldest of the Princes in the Tower

Richard III (1483-1485)

Tudors (1485 - 1603)

Probably the most famous ruling dynasty in British history, if not globally, the 118-years of Tudor rule were a soap opera of dramas, and oversaw the biggest religious change the country had ever seen - the Reformation. Of course, we all know about Henry VIII and his six wives, but who came before him?

The Tudor dynasty began with Henry VIII's father, Lancastrian Henry Tudor, who defeated King Richard III, the last Yorkist King, at the Battle of Bosworth in 1485. Henry married Elizabeth of York, daughter of King Edward IV, and the pair united the houses of York and Lancaster to end the Wars of the Roses after 30 years of violence and death.

Henry VII (1485 – 1509)

Henry VIII (1509-1547)

Edward VI (1547-1553)

Mary I – Bloody Mary (1553-1558)

Elizabeth I (1558-1603)

The Stuarts (1603 - 1649)

When Queen Elizabeth I died childless in 1603, she named her cousin, King James VI of Scotland, son of Mary, Queen of Scots, as her heir to the English throne. He was Elizabeth's closest living relative - was this an attempt to make amends for executing his mother in 1587? King James became the first monarch to rule England and Scotland simultaneously.

James I and VI of Scotland (1567/1603-1625)

Charles I (1625-1649)

Interregnum (1649-1660)

The Stuart rule came to an abrupt end when, after years of the English Civil War, James VI's son, King Charles I, was executed for high treason. This remains the first and only time a reigning monarch has been beheaded.

In a period known as the Interregnum, when there was no ruling monarchy, the country was ruled by Oliver Cromwell, a Puritan who had risen to power through his military prowess in the Civil War. He helped to secure victory against the king and played a key role in his execution in 1649. In 1653, he became Lord Protector and ruled as military dictator.

Oliver Cromwell (1653-1658)

Richard Cromwell (1658-1659)

The Restoration and the return of the Stuarts (1660 - 1688)

After his father died of natural causes in 1658, Oliver Cromwell's son Richard took over as Lord Protector. However, lacking his father's military standing, he lost control of the army, which forced him to dissolve Parliament and led to his resignation. Power then reverted to what was called the 'Rump Parliament', which led to months of instability, leading to the restoration of the monarchy with Charles I's son, King Charles II, the Merry Monarch.

Charles II (1660-1685)

James II and VII of Scotland (1685-1688)

William III & Mary II (1689-1702)

Anne (1702-1714)

The Hanoverians (1714 - 1910)

The long-reigning Stuart dynasty (which had ruled Scotland from 1371) ended with the death of Queen Anne in 1714, who died without any heirs. The throne was then passed to the Hanovers of Germany and King George I, Queen Anne's second cousin and her closest living Protestant relative. His accession came after the Act of Settlement in 1701, which excluded all Catholics from being eligible to the throne.

George I (1714-1727)

George II (1727-1760)

George III (1760-1820) - Mad King George

George IV (1820-1830)

William IV (1830-1837)

Victoria (1837-1901)

House of Windsor (1901 - present)

The Hanoverian dynasty ended with the death of Queen Victoria in 1901. As the last monarch of the House of Hanover, her death marked the transition to the House of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha (later the House of Windsor) with the accession of the eldest of her nine children, King Edward VII.

Edward VII (1901-1910)

George V (1910-1936)

Edward VIII (1936-1936)

George VI (1936-1952)

Elizabeth II (1952-2022)

King Charles III (2022 - present)

Of course, after over 1,000 years of rule and royal tradition, all (well, almost all) of these kings and queens had to have an official coronation ceremony. But what did that look like 1,000 years ago, and how much has it changed over the years? Here, we look at coronation ceremonies through British history, and what really happens (and what it really means) when a monarch gets crowned.

item.title: Behind the crown and away from the cameras - how Queen Elizabeth II spent her days in surprising ways and why a century-old tradition ends with King Charles III
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor, Laura Silverman
item.description: From corgis and horses to the more unusual stamp-collecting and even pigeon racing, in the centenary year of her birth, we’re celebrating the off-duty Queen Elizabeth II
item.content:encoded:
An animal-lover since she was a small child, our late queen surrounded herself with corgis for decades: she is believed to have owned at least 30 during her reign, and in popular culture Queen Elizabeth II and her canine companions are inseparable still. “My corgis are my family,” she once said.

But did you know Queen Elizabeth II also enjoyed the company of other animals, including horses and even pigeons? Or that she also had a penchant for stamp-collecting and Scottish country dancing?

As we celebrate the centenary of her birth in April 2026, which is being marked by Buckingham Palace with the largest exhibition of her fashion ever staged, we thought it was time to take a closer look at the life of the off-duty queen.

If you're interested in understanding her extraordinary life further, David Cannadine has gone deeper in his book, Queen Elizabeth II: A Concise Biography of an Exceptional Sovereign, released in 2025. The respected historian brilliantly unpicks the life of the most depicted person ever to have lived, tracking the monumental global changes that occurred during her life, and how she remained a steadfast, dutiful figure throughout her 96 years.

Cannadine writes in his work that Queen Elizabeth II's hobbies were her way of escaping from the pressures and demands of being queen. So, how did Queen Elizabeth II spend her time when she wasn't performing royal duties?

Queen Elizabeth II's love of dogs

Queen Elizabeth II's love of dogs went back to childhood, when her parents, the future King George VI and Queen Elizabeth (later the Queen Mother) added a corgi called Dookie to the family. For her 18th birthday, the Princess received a corgi of her own, Susan, who bobbed along on her honeymoon. A loyal dog, Susan is said to have bitten a policeman, a detective and a sentry.

Most of the Queen’s 30 or so corgis or corgi crossbreeds over the years were descended from her line. Their names ranged from the traditional (Emma, Holly, Monty) to the unusual (Brush, Honey, Whisky, Sherry, Spick, Span and Bisto Oxo, to name a few).

The Queen’s dogs, who Princess Diana called “the moving carpet” because they followed the Queen everywhere, had their own room at the Palace, where they slept in bespoke wicker beds. Supper was at 5pm, and might have been fresh rabbit, chicken and rice, rustled up by royal chefs. The Queen liked to feed them herself and take them on daily walks. They made the Queen feel more at ease with strangers, and were a sort of therapy, which the late Prince Philip called her “dog mechanism”.

Queen Elizabeth II's love of horses

A close rival for the Queen’s affections were her horses, another childhood passion. Her grandfather, George V, gave her a Shetland pony called Peggy for her fourth birthday. She kept some horses for riding at Frogmore Stables at Windsor Castle, and was often spotted trotting in Windsor Great Park with Terry Pendry, her head groom, who said she was “a fountain of knowledge in all things equine”. Sanction, the last horse the Queen rode before she switched to ponies, was a favourite.

Queen Elizabeth II also loved to watch horse racing, attending Royal Ascot almost every year since 1945. She even entered her own horses there, celebrating four winners in 1957. The Queen’s thoroughbreds, which were foaled at Sandringham, her country home in Norfolk, were ridden to victory 20 times by jockeys wearing the royal purple and scarlet silks.

Queen Elizabeth II and Scottish country dancing

One of the Queen’s racehorses shared its name with another of her passions. Eightsome Reel, who ran at Ascot in 2019, is also a Scottish country dance. The dance, which involves groups of couples stepping gaily to particular patterns, has been around since the 17th century. A ‘reel’ is one of the faster, livelier dances, which all have evocative names like Dashing White Sergeant.

The Queen, who was patron of the Scottish Country Dance Society since 1947, before she acceded the throne, took part once a year at Balmoral, her Scottish holiday home. Every summer, she threw a thank-you party for the castle staff and local community in the grand Ballroom. The Ghillies’ Ball, a tradition started by Queen Victoria, and still happens under King Charles III, is a merry affair with pipers, punch and plenty of tartan. The men in the Royal Family wear kilts made of the Balmoral tartan in red and grey; the women, including the late Queen, wear matching sashes. She is said to have had “great rhythm”.

Queen Elizabeth II's secret hobby - pigeon racing

Her Majesty’s generous patronage hinted at more private passions. She also lent her support to the Royal Pigeon Racing Association and National Flying Club (pigeons not planes). The royal hobby stretches back to 1886, when Albert, Prince of Wales, the future George VI, received racing pigeons from the King of Belgium. He housed them at Sandringham.

The late Queen kept about 200 pigeons there. Some were for breeding, but most were for racing, where the birds are released from a distance and have to fly home as fast as possible. The pigeons, which had a leg ring marked with the Queen’s cipher, used to compete in one or two club races each week and all national races of the season. They won all the main competitions across the UK.

Since succeeding the throne, King Charles III has ended royal patronage of the Royal Pigeon Racing Association and National Flying Club after 138 years. As patron of the Royal Society for the Protection of Birds he was responding to pressure from animal welfare groups regarding the sport's cruelty. Instead, he has adopted 31 retired laying hens from the British Hen Welfare Trust, which will live and roam freely at Highgrove Gardens, his Gloucestershire home.

Queen Elizabeth II's love of stamp collecting

Similarly specialist was the Queen’s love of stamp collecting. The Royal Philatelic Collection, which fills about 300 albums and 200 boxes, is kept in a vault at St James’ Palace, another royal residence. Started by Queen Victoria’s second son, Prince Alfred in 1864, it was once valued at £100million. A rare Mauritian stamp is worth £2million alone, while the Queen spent £250,000 on a set of Penny Blacks, dated the day they came into circulation in 1840.

Of course, Queen Elizabeth II stamps are no longer issued, but some vintage collections are still available to buy, like this collection of 100 Elizabeth II stamps or Queen Elizabeth II stamp catalogues like this one.

So Queen Elizabeth II was a woman of many talents, it seems, but what about her predecessors? Did you know that Queen Victoria was a keen diarist, or that Mary, Queen of Scots, enjoyed needlework? From Henry VIII to King Charles III, here are some of the most surprising royal hobbies you definitely didn't know about.

item.title: You definitely never knew these 7 curious facts about St David, the Patron Saint of Wales - the Welshman behind the legend
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From relics and daffodils to piglrims and playwrights, the medieval figure of St David will always be a fascinating enigma. As we celebrate St David’s Day, here are 7 facts to regale your friends with about Wales’ patron saint...
item.content:encoded:
''Gwnewch y pethau bychain mewn bywyd'' were supposedly the dying words of St David, translating to 'do the little things in life', encouraging his followers to find holiness in small, daily, compassionate actions.

But who really was St David, how did he become the patron saint of Wales, and what is the story behind St David's Day in Wales?

Of course, like all good medieval figures, we don't know an awful lot about St David himself. Most of what we know about him comes from the 11th-century Welsh scholar Rhygyfarch, who may have succeeded his father as Bishop of St David's Cathedral.

However, Rhygyfarch lived 500 years after the saint himself, so we can't be too sure about the accuracy of what he tells us. But it's all we really have to go off, and his writings have formed the basis of the stories and legends of St David that we know and love today.

If you'd like to read about St David from the writings of the expert himself, Rhygyfarch's The Life of St David is available to buy in a paperback version and is a really fascinating insight into the mind of a medieval scholar.

As we celebrate St David's Day on 1 March, what better time to brush up on your obscure knowledge of the patron saint. So with that in mind, here are seven things you definitely never knew about St David:

1. The nickname ‘Taffy’ for a Welshman links back to St David as the original and ultimate Welshman – the term dates back to the 17th century and derives from ‘Dafydd’, the Welsh for David.

2. The shrine of St David at his cathedral – located in Britain’s smallest city, St David's in Pembrokeshire – was thought of so highly by Pope Callistus II that he states that two pilgrimages to the shrine were worth one to the Vatican in Rome.

3. King Edward I took the head and the arm of St David from the cathedral after his 1284 conquest in Wales. He displayed the remains along with a number of other holy relics in London.

4. David was the only Welsh saint to be canonised by the Western Catholic Church.

5. Yet the abbey at Glastonbury claims St David as its patron.

6. St David’s Day was raised to principal feast status in the church calendar after Henry V’s success at the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, reportedly in thanks to the Welsh saint for intervening on his behalf against the French.

7. In Shakespeare’s Henry V, Fluellen’s English colleague, Pistol, dares to insult the humble leek on St David’s Day, Fluellen insists he eat the national emblem as punishment: “If you can mock a leek, you can eat a leek.” (Act V, Scene I).

Of course, St David is one of four patron saints of the British Isles, each with their own fascinating origins, myths and saint's day traditions. What better time than now to brush up on your knowledge of St Patrick, St George and St Andrew, whose legendary statuses have informed so much of our history today.

item.title: From Paddington to Potter - these easy World Book Day costumes celebrate the very best of British children’s literature
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: With World Book Day just around the corner, these costumes celebrate the very best of British children’s books and their authors
item.content:encoded:
As a child, World Book Day was one of my absolute favourite days of the school year - a chance to dress up as my favourite characters, read and listen to wonderful stories all day long - I wish adults still partook in this joyful celebration of literature.

Of course, the most important question of the day was, and still is for schoolchildren today, who shall I be this year? When it comes to choosing a character created by the greatest British writers, we are absolutely spoiled for choice.

From Harry Potter and Peter Pan to the weird and wonderful characters of Roald Dahl, here, I've picked some of my favourite British book characters and chosen a costume for each - perfect for any child who is unsure what to wear on World Book Day next week.

What is World Book Day?

This year held on 5 March 2026, but always takes place in March in the UK and Ireland (globally on April 23 2026), World Book Day is an annual charity-led event established by UNESCO in 1995 and dedicated to encouraging children to read for pleasure. As well as dressing up in their favourite costumes, children take part in reading activities and are each given a £1/€1.50 book voucher to choose a book of their choice.
