Once you're all kitted out in your fabulous costume, why not brush up on some of the best quotes from British authors so you can regale your friends, or your class, with the wise words of some of the most celebrated names in British literature, from Charles Dickens to Emily Brontë. Happy reading!

item.title: Miracles, myths and leeks - this is the truth behind St David’s Day and how the saint became the heart of Wales
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Each year on 1 March, St David’s Day, takes place across Britain. But who was St David and why is he considered the Patron Saint of Wales?
item.content:encoded:
The first day of March is celebrated as St David’s Day, a celebration of the Patron Saint of Wales, who is believed to have died on this date in 589AD.

As the first saints day in the calendar, St David's Day heralds spring for many and a chance to shine a spotlight on Welsh culture, heritage, history and language.

Across Wales (and all those with Welsh heritage around the world) will wear daffodils or leeks to show their national pride, eat delicious traditional Welsh food like cawl or Welsh cakes, and special parades and concerts often take place up and down the country.

For anyone wishing to know more about the fascinating life of the patron saint of Wales, the brilliant In Pursuit of Saint David: Patron Saint of Wales by Gerald Morgan is a digestable run through of everything you need to know about St David, from his life and beliefs, to the myths surrounding him, the history of St David's Day itself and all the churches he has given his name to.

But who is St David and why is he Wales's patron saint?

For now, here is a brief run-through of who St David was and why he is celebrated on 1 March.

Born into an aristocratic family around 500AD, St David is rumoured to have been educated in Cardiganshire (modern-day Ceredigion). Following his schooling, David travelled far and wide, eventually making his way to Jerusalem, where he was appointed Archbishop.

After his pilgrimages, St David is said to have settled in Glyn Rhosyn (St Davids), in Pembrokeshire, southwest Wales. Here he established a religious community, and the cathedral of St David’s became a popular centre of pilgrimage. St David was renowned for performing miracles, not least while preaching at Llandewi Brefi, when he is said to have elevated himself so the whole congregation could hear him.

Today, the cathedral stands on the site of St David’s 6th-century monastic settlement in Britain’s smallest city. The cathedral has had a tumultuous past, with invasions, earthquakes, royal visits and numerous refurbishments.

David was officially recognised as a Catholic saint in 1120, and the day of his death was decreed a national festival in the 18th century.

Why do we wear daffodils and leeks on St David's Day?

To mark St David’s Day, people around Wales wear one of the two national emblems: the leek or the daffodil.

As with all folklore, there is much speculation as to why these two objects exist as national emblems.

Records suggest that rulers of the Tudor dynasty introduced their guards to the wearing of leeks on St David’s Day. One story tells of an ancient king who advised men in battle to wear leeks as they fought against the Saxons to help differentiate between those who were allies and the enemy.

Another story goes that St David himself survived on only leeks and water. Like with all the best folklore, we'll never know the real answer, and there is probably some truth in all the rumours.

The daffodil, however, makes much more sense as it was more of a seasonal introduction as its spring sprouting coincides with the national day.

St David's Day celebrations in Cardiff 2026

Today, an annual colourful parade takes place in the centre of Cardiff and concerts, markets, curated walks and festivals scatter the rest of the land in celebration of St David’s Day.

On Sunday, 1 March 2026, the Cardiff National Parade will start at 12:30 pm at City Hall and move through the city centre towards the Hayes. At the Senedd at Cardiff Bay on Saturday 28 February and Sunday 1 March, there will be live music, choirs and workshops.

Other celebrations around the city include the Grade-I listed Insole Court and St Fagans National Museum of History, where you'll find workshops, activities and plenty of delicious Welsh food and drink. You can find out about all the St David's Day celebrations in Cardiff on the Visit Cardiff website here.

If you're celebrating this weekend, our little guide to 7 things about St David you probably never knew is essential for the day, Welsh or not. While you're at it can also read up on the patron saints of Ireland, England and Scotland, St Patrick, St George, and St Andrew.

item.title: London is about to be swept into Jane Austen’s glittering society – here is what to expect when the doors open
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: The brand-new Jane Austen Experience is about to open in London, a city she knew and loved. Here’s what to expect from the new immersive Regency exhibition...
item.content:encoded:
“I cannot see that London has any great advantage over the country, for my part, except the shops and public places. The country is a vast deal pleasanter, is it not, Mr. Bingley?” So said Mrs Bennet in Pride & Prejudice, but what did Jane Austen really think of London?

Despite never living the capital herself, any fans of the author would be advised to start any Jane Austen tour of the country in London, a place that she had many connections to and enjoyed spending time in.

As of Friday 27 February this will be made all the easier thanks to a brand-new opening; the Jane Austen Experience will open the doors to their brand-new London home, the second opening after their original Bath location, where Jane Austen lived for much of her life.

Kerry-Lee Pohl, General Manager of the Jane Austen Experience, commented: “Opening the Jane Austen Experience in London feels incredibly exciting. We’ve been talking about this for the past couple of years and, following Jane Austen’s 250th birthday last year, it felt like the perfect moment to celebrate again and share her story with everyone in a way that’s engaging and accessible.”

Although she often wrote about it negatively in her novels, depicting it as a place of vanity, frivolity and vice for her characters, London actually held a special place in the heart of the Regency author, and she enjoyed visiting very much.

If you are interested in the London that Jane Austen knew, this brilliant book, Walking Jane Austen’s London, by Louise Allen, will guide you through eight walking routes of the city, both of the city that the author would have known and that she wrote about in her novels. In today's London, walkers can visit the church where Lydia Bennett married Wickham, stroll with Elinor Dashwood in Kensington Palace Gardens or follow Jane's naval officer brothers as they stride down Whitehall to the Admiralty.

Jane Austen's brother Henry lived at a few different addresses in the city and often she visited him here, including the beautiful townhouse in Marylebone that is now a stylish, boutique hotel, Henry's Townhouse. Home to some rare, original Austen copies and decked out in fabulous Regency-core interiors, this is a great base for all Austen lovers visiting the city.

What can you do at the Jane Austen Experience, London?

Just a two-minute walk from Oxford Circus, and in the heart of the London that Jane herself would have known, the brand new Jane Austen Experience will feature a ground floor gift shop, for shopping all kinds of Jane-themed delights, and a permanent exhibition where visitors can immerse themselves in Jane's Regency world.

View this post on Instagram

Visitors can come up close and personal with Jane herself as they walk through the immersive exhibition, learning about her life, family and literary influences.

Dress up in elegant Regency attire, have a go at writing at Jane's writing desk, and walk through some of Jane's most beloved and famous Regency settings - both real and fictional, perhaps you'll meet your Mr Darcy along the way? There's also the chance to play some traditional Regency games, taste some Regency biscuits and have your picture taken with Jane herself - the world-famous Jane Austen waxwork.

At the end, visitors can shop a curated collection of literary-inspired Jane Austen gifts, from books and journals to limited-edition Jane Austen stamps, clothes and homewares.

The Jane Austen Experience, London will open on Friday 27 February 2026, with bookings open now. Adult tickets cost £19.50 each and children aged 6-16 cost £11.50. Under 5s go free.

If, like us, you can't get enough Jane Austen, you'll be pleased to hear of the BBC's brand new period drama, a continuation of Pride & Prejudice from Mary Bennet's perspective. Here is everything you need to know about The Other Bennet Sister, which is released on the BBC in March.

item.title: The 10 best stargazing spots in the UK – peaceful, wild and wonderfully dark destinations
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: From the highest peaks to the deepest valleys, Britain has some of the best Dark Sky Reserves offering unforgettable stargazing experiences. For budding astronomers, here is our guide to the UK’s 10 best stargazing spots...
item.content:encoded:
“When you consider things like the stars, our affairs don't seem to matter very much, do they?” said Virginia Woolf in Night and Day. The starry skies of the British Isles have long been a source of inspiration for all our art forms, from Woolf and Shakespeare to JMW Turner's paintings.

Thanks to the vast, clear skies, free from light-pollution of Britain's beautiful National Parks, the UK is fast becoming a hotspot for astrotourism, as lovers of the night sky search for the best stargazing experiences.

Home to several international Dark Sky Reserves, including Northumberland National Park, Exmoor, and Eryri in Wales, we are spoilt for choice when it comes to stargazing experiences in the UK.

Dan Doherty, Insights Manager at holiday specialists Awat Resorts, commented 'From remote national parks to hidden coastal gems, the UK is full of wonderful places to reconnect with nature and experience the awe of stargazing. Whether it’s spotting the Milky Way or catching a glimpse of the northern lights, these destinations make for truly memorable trips.

For a year-long guide to Britain's stars and where you should be visiting each season, Philip's annual stargazing guide is a perfect, practical guide to everything you need to know, and will suit both budding and more experienced astronomers.

Best viewed in wintertime when the stars are clear and bright, pack your flask and warm socks and come with us on an adventure through Britain's 10 best stargazing experiences.

1. Bannau Brycheiniog National Park, Wales

The beautiful Bannau Brycheiniog in Wales is an International Dark Sky Reserve, and on a clear night, you can spot the Milky Way stretching overhead. You'll likely see meteor showers, shooting stars and distant galaxies too. The dramatic backdrop of beautiful Welsh mountains, lakes and valleys makes it even more romantic too; starry nights here will really take your breath away.

If you're looking for somewhere to stay in the park, here are some of our favourite hotels.

2. Northumberland National Park, England

A gold-tier Dark Sky Reserve, the vast and stunning Northumberland National Park is home to the largest area of protected night sky in Europe and is a bucket-list stargazing experience for many. It is also one of the best places to see the Northern Lights in the UK, when conditions are right.

Head to the Kielder Observatory, set deep within the forest, for regular events and guided stargazing experiences.

3. Isle of Skye, Scotland

Of course, the wild and rugged Isle of Skye is one of Britain's most famous and beautiful landscapes, and its night sky is equally impressive.

Thanks to its small population, it has very little light pollution, so the night skies are clear, bright and breathtaking. The Quiraing and Neist Point for some of the island's best and most atmospheric spots for stargazing.

If you're a keen photographer, our editor recently took a trip to Skye to learn from the experts. You can read about her photography retreat here.

4. Eryri National Park, Wales

Home to the highest mountain in Wales, Yr Wyddfa (Snowdon), Eryri is also an International Dark Sky Reserve and offers pristine night skies with a stunning backdrop of quiet peaks and deep valleys. Stargazing events regularly take place at Coed y Brenin Forest Park, or you can find a quiet spot and take in the magical surroundings, swirling with legends of giants and dragons, and the incredible skies above.

5. Galloway Forest Park, Scotland

The UK's first designated Dark Sky Park, Galloway Forest Park, is the largest forest park in Britain, covering 300 square miles of outstanding natural beauty on the ground, and of an incredible night sky above our heads.

Known as 'the Highlands of the Lowlands,' there are over 7,000 stars visible on clear nights here. Head to one of the visitor centres in the park to join a guided stargazing walk with an expert.

6. Exmoor National Park, England

Another official Dark Sky Reserve, the wild, rugged moorlands of Exmoor, heaving with myths, legends and history, is certainly one of the most atmospheric stargazing experiences in the UK.

The wide skies offer uninterrupted views of the heavens, and from here you'll likely spot shooting stars and the faint glow of distant galaxies.

7. South Downs National Park, England

Running across the south coast through the counties of Hampshire, West Sussex, and East Sussex, the South Downs promise rolling green hills, chocolate-box villages and seaside towns, alongside incredibly clear night skies.

Also a Dark Sky Reserve, the South Downs is an easy jaunt from London, just an hour or so on the train, so it makes a perfect, easy weekend escape. Devil's Dyke near Brighton, Old Winchester Hill in Hampshire and the quiet Bignor Hill in West Sussex are all excellent stargazing spots.

8. The Lake District, England

Poetic inspiration for William Wordsworth, Keats and Taylor Swift alike, the beautiful Lake District is better known for its lakes and fells, but the skies above are equally impressive.

Also benefitting from very little light pollution, remote valleys like Wasdale and Ennerdale are perfect for spotting constellations and meteor showers.

9. The Cairngorms, Scotland

Of course, the beloved, vast and bonnie landscapes of Scotland's Highlands offer some of the darkest skies in the UK. In the Cairngorms National Park, the largest national park in the UK, you can spot constellations with the naked eye, and clusters and nebulae through a pair of binoculars.

Braving the cold of Scotland's winters will pay off, too, as you're very likely to spot the aurora borealis here.

10. Norfolk Coast, England

Heading back down south to England's eastern edge, the Norfolk Coast is famed for its wide-open skies, which make it an underrated and somewhat under-discovered gem for stargazing.

Keeping away from the busier towns and heading to the beautiful beaches of Holkham and Well-next-the-Sea offers a unique stargazing opportunity to see them rise over the sea; the combination of starry skies and comforting sea air is wonderfully peaceful.

Of course, after an evening spent gazing at the stars, a warm bed, cosy fire and a delicious home-cooked meal are in order. The Good Hotel Guide's best winter hotels in Britain offer lovely options in each of these stargazing spots - the perfect place to curl up and dream of the stars.

item.title: The most shocking royal arrests in British history – from treacherous heirs to scandal-plagued kings
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: This week’s royal news got us thinking - how many times has a member of the royal family had a run-in with the law? Here is a short history of royal arrests through British history
item.content:encoded:
With the news of the arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, former prince and the brother of King Charles III, I couldn’t help but wonder, how many times has a member of the royal family been in trouble with the law?

Well, unsurprisingly, throughout the turbulent timeline of Britain’s kings and queens, reigning monarchs and their kin have often been plagued by controversy, shady behaviour and, you guessed it, treason.

From plotting, spying, gambling and even execution, the history of royal run-ins with the law is a long and fascinating one.

For an entertaining deep-dive into every British monarch’s more ridiculous, unregal side, the brilliant David Mitchell’s best-selling Unruly is a hilarious ride through the history of England’s kings and queens, with all the really juicy bits left in - it’s Horrible Histories for adults. 

For now, here are four royal vagrants whose unroyal behaviour got them into serious trouble…

1. George, Duke of Clarence, 1449 - 1478

The Wars of the Roses brought out the worst sides to England’s kings and queens, and the bloody, ongoing feud between the two families of York and Lancaster was Romeo-and-Juliet levels of petty. 

George, the Duke of Clarence, was the brother of King Edward IV and the future King Richard III. Despite being a member of the House of York, George was clearly interested only in self-advancement and, with some easy persuasion from Richard Neville, Earl of Warwick, otherwise known as ‘the Kingmaker’, he switched sides to support the House of Lancaster. After being betrayed by his father-in-law, he reverted to his brothers of York, but this was the beginning of his undoing. 

When George’s wife, Isabel, died in childbirth, George was convinced she had been poisoned by one of her ladies in waiting, who he subsequently had abducted and hanged. Clarence’s mental state was clearly unsteady and, after inciting yet another rebellion against his own brother, Edward ordered George’s arrest in June 1477. After a trial where his brother convicted him guilty of "unnatural, loathly treasons’ he was ‘privately executed’ at the Tower of London. Rumours swirled that he had, in fact, rather horribly, been drowned in a vat of malmsey wine. 

2. King Charles I, 1600-1649

350 years ago was the last time a member of the royal family was actually arrested, and this time it was the head of it - King Charles I. 

Taken prisoner in 1647 after being defeated in the English Civil War by parliamentary forces, Charles I was put on trial and found guilty of high treason. A firm believer in the divine right of kings, Charles denied parliament’s conviction of him, claiming that no court had any jurisdiction over his God-given power. 

Charles’s reign had been tyrannical, and he had dissolved parliament for more than ten years. He made failed attempts to raise money through other means and sidestepped his constitutional responsibility. The Civil War broke out in 1642, and after five years of battles, sieges and failed negotiations, he was finally taken prisoner.

While in prison, he even plotted to take back England with the help of the Scots. This failed, and his death warrant was signed by 59 men, including Oliver Cromwell. The king was executed on 30 January 1649 outside the Banqueting House in Whitehall. He is the only monarch to have been executed by the state. 

3. James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth, 1649-1685

Like grandfather like grandson, it seems when it came to the Stuarts, James Scott, the 1st Duke of Monmouth and the eldest of Charles II’s (son of Charles I) illegitimate children, was arrested and executed for treason in 1685. 

At the time of the drama, James’s uncle, King James II, was on the throne. Having fought in the Second and Third Anglo-Dutch wars, James had gained a reputation as one of Britain’s finest soldiers and was put forward as a Protestant candidate for the throne at the time of the “Popish Plot’ (which turned out to be fictitious) in 1678. 

When his father, Charles II, died in 1685, James led the ‘Monmouth Rebellion’, an attempt to take the throne from his uncle James II. The Duke of Monmouth landed in England with a small Protestant army and accused James of poisoning the late king, usurping the throne and ruling unlawfully. 

After being defeated at the Battle of Sedgemoor, James was found hiding in a ditch near Ringwood, Hampshire and arrested on July 8 1685. He was taken to London and executed for treason just a week later. 

4. King Edward VII, 1841-1910

The British royal family has a reputation for playboys, and Edward VII, the eldest of Queen Victoria’s children, is one of the best-known. 

Unlike his unruly predecessors, however, Edward’s crimes weren’t treasonous, and instead were born from his deep-love of gambling, drinking, and women - hobbies that often took priority over his royal duties. 

Although not technically an arrest, while still Prince of Wales, Albert, as was his Christian name, was questioned in court as a witness during the melodramatic baccarat scandal, an illegal game of gambling which took place at the home of one of England’s richest men, in 1890. One of the players was accused of cheating, but denied the rumours, instead pointing the finger at other players. 

The scandal, which is still shrouded in a lot of mystery, was brought to the high court, where Prince Edward, the future king, stood as a witness. Unsurprisingly, it severely damaged his reputation with the public and with his mother, Queen Victoria. 

The future Edward II was the last member of the royal family to stand in a courtroom, until 130 years later, Prince Harry, son of King Charles III, stood in the exact same courtroom in 2026.   

If all this unruly behaviour has you intrigued, why not uncover more scandalous behaviour with the biggest royal scandals through history, peppered with family feuds, public fallings-out and illicit affairs.

item.title: Jane Seymour queen and Tudor enigma – was she really Henry VIII’s most beloved?
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: We look at the life of Henry VIII’s third wife and discuss whether Jane Seymour was really his favourite, or simply the only one he didn’t have a chance to ruin it with...
item.content:encoded:
‘While Anne would be portrayed as a witch, Jane would be forever remembered as a saint', said historian David Starkey, but was Jane Seymour really the demure, obedient queen history knows her to be?

The third of Henry VIII’s six wives, Jane Seymour, is always remembered to be his most beloved, and yet, to us historians, she is probably the most elusive. 

After the passion and drama of Anne Boleyn’s rise and fall, Henry VIII chose the total opposite for his second wife, whose motto was ‘bound to obey and serve.’ But was there more to Jane than meets the eye, and was she really Henry’s favourite, or simply the only one who didn’t wound his ego so much he had to accuse her of treason?

If, like me, historical fiction is your favourite way to learn about the past, and, in particular, women silenced by history, acclaimed Tudor historian Alison Weir has written a brilliant fiction series on each of Henry’s six queens. Jane Seymour: The Haunted Queen is a heartwrenching, empathetic retelling of the tragic story of Henry’s quiet third wife, who died just a few days after giving birth to her son, the future King Edward VI. 

Who was Jane Seymour?

We know very little about Jane Seymour’s early life, other than that she was born around 1508-9 and was the eldest daughter of ten children of Margery Wentworth and Sir John Seymour. She spent her childhood at her family home of Wolf Hall in Wiltshire, and her family were respectable lower gentry. 

She did, however, have royal blood; Jane Seymour was descended from Edward III on her mother’s side, which also made her a cousin to two of Henry’s other wives, Anne Boleyn and Catherine Howard. 

How did Jane Seymour become queen?

It is unclear when Henry VIII first met Jane Seymour, but we do know that she arrived in court in 1529 and assumed a position as lady-in-waiting in Catherine of Aragon’s household, Henry’s first wife. 

When Henry tossed Katherine aside and married Anne Boleyn in 1533, Jane retained her position and became the new queen’s lady-in-waiting. The chief historian at Historic Royal Palaces, Tracy Borman, says that there is some evidence that Jane was actually in the room with Anne Boleyn when she gave birth to her daughter, the future Queen Elizabeth I. 

When did Jane first catch Henry’s eye?

We aren’t totally sure about this, but we do know that in 1534, Eustace Chapuys, the Spanish ambassador, described an affair with the king and an unnamed woman in Anne’s household - some historians think this might have been Jane Seymour. 

In 1535, Henry and Anne also visited Wolf Hall together, but we don’t know if Jane was present. 

We do know that Henry and Jane began courting in 1536, because he sent her a purse of gold, which she refused. The story goes that she kissed the unopened letter and asked the messenger to return it. According to Chapuys, Jane replied that she could not accept the letter as there was ‘no treasure in the world that she valued as much as her honour’. If the King wanted to send another gift, she suggested he might wait until ‘such a time as God would be pleased to send her some advantageous marriage’.

Their courtship coincides with when Henry and Anne’s relationship began to turn really sour. Anne experienced a miscarriage, one of at least two since Elizabeth I was born, and this time the baby was a boy. Blaming Anne, his eyes began to wander, and those close to him, namely his right-hand man Thomas Cromwell, looked to remove Anne from power. 

The solution? The sweet, demure Jane Seymour - a quiet mouse of a girl who was the exact opposite of the confident, hotheaded Anne. 

Impressed by Jane’s modesty, Henry had her older brother, Edward Seymour, moved into Thomas Cromwell’s apartments at Greenwich Palace. This way, Edward could act as Jane’s chaperone to ensure her precious ‘honour’ when the King came to visit. 

But was Jane really so concerned with her honour, or was this a clever act? Being the total opposite of Anne was drawing Henry towards her, and she may very well have been holding out for the chance of being queen. Or, at least, the men in her family, hungry for power, were pushing her to this for their own benefit. 

For Henry, Jane was the perfect candidate for wife number three. Complicit, gentle, chaste and quiet after years of vicious quarrels with the passionate Anne. If you ask me, his ‘love’ for her was probably more of a relief that she didn’t answer back. She fed his ego by being obedient, which is exactly what the arrogant king was looking for. 

Of course, sons were also on Henry’s mind. Being one of 10 children, Jane clearly came from fertile stock. She was his best chance of getting the son he so desired, the one thing his first two wives were unable to give him. 

When did Jane Seymour become queen?

Anne Boleyn was executed on 19 May 1536, and Henry and Jane were engaged the next day. They married less than two weeks later on 30 May 1536 in the Queen’s Closet at Whitehall Palace. 

Was Jane Seymour really a passive and obedient queen?

Her motto of obedience and servitude certainly fits the image of the queen that history remembers, but as the daughter of lower gentry who rose from lady-in-waiting to queen, there was clearly more to Jane than we’ve been told. 

Her chaste, demure act could well have been a play to be as different to Anne Boleyn as possible. Henry VIII was a dangerous man, and this was a sensible move if you wanted to keep your head.

She presented herself as quiet and pious, who generally distanced herself from politics, but several events hint that she may well have been hiding her own opinions on the goings on of the day. 

During the 1536 Pilgrimage of Grace, when the north revolted after Henry closed the abbeys and seized church property to fill his own royal purse, according to the French ambassador, Jane begged Henry to ‘restore the abbeys’ - was this revealing her more conservative religious views, clearly different to Henry’s?

She was, unsurprisingly, shut down. Henry warned her that he had ‘often told her not to meddle with his affairs' and that she should remember what happened to his last queen. Also unsurprisingly, Jane obeyed. 

This little interaction suggested Henry liked her because of her obedience and chastity - quick to threaten her with the same fate as Anne Boleyn when she hinted she disagreed with him. Is that what real love looks like? I don’t think so. 

When did Jane Seymour give birth?

In 1536, Henry’s new Act of Succession confirmed that the princesses Mary and Elizabeth were illegitimate, and, luckily for Jane, good news came in March 1537 when she discovered she was pregnant. 

After a labour that lasted two days and three nights, the future King Edward VI was born on 12 October 1537. Henry, of course, was overjoyed. 

Jane had fulfilled her duty of providing him a son and heir - a feat that no other wife had so far achieved and an act that, of course, propelled her to the heights of his affections. 

How did Jane Seymour die? 

On 24 October 1537, just 12 days after giving birth, Jane Seymour died at Hampton Court Palace aged 26.

What did Jane Seymour die of?

Traditionally, historians have believed Jane died after contracting ‘childbed fever’ - an infection caused by poor hygiene during labour. But others suggest it could have been other post-birth complications, or even food poisoning. 

Henry did, genuinely grieve for Jane, and the court was plunged into mourning with the rooms of the palace draped in black fabric, while the king retreated to Westminster to be by himself for a while. 

Did Henry really love Jane Seymour the most?

Of course, the strongest piece of evidence for this is that he chose to be buried beside her in St George’s Chapel, Windsor.

But, really, who else would he have been buried with? He couldn’t choose Katherine of Aragon - annulled for not being his ‘true wife’, or Anne of Cleves, also annulled for a previous engagement (though we all know it's because he didn’t like her much), and Katherine Parr outlived him. Of course, there is no chance he could have chosen either Anne Boleyn or Catherine Howard - both executed for treason - so Jane was really the only option. 

Henry continued to include Jane in family portraits - most famously in The Family of Henry VIII portrait, completed in 1545, which can be seen in the haunted gallery of Hampton Court Palace today. 

Of course, Henry was to revere her - she gave him a son, and she didn’t survive long enough to really offend him. If any of his future wives had fulfilled this duty, perhaps she’d have been replaced in his affections. 

But, chaste, obedient, quiet Jane died without fault and was the mother of the future king. To me, his track record suggests that if she’d survived a little longer, his attention span would have expired and he’d have found a reason to replace her, just like he did with the other five. 

She was immortalised through her son Edward VI, the last boy king of England. But, just like his mother, he died before he could reach his true potential.

item.title: Pride and Prejudice rewritten? Inside the BBC’s surprising new series about Mary Bennet
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: The Other Bennet Sister will be on our screens this March - this is everything you need to know about the Austen adaptation that gives Mary her time to shine
item.content:encoded:
I've always felt that Mary Bennet was somewhat mistreated by her four sisters and by her parents. Pushed to the side, ignored and never given the chance to find her own Mr Darcy, it's about time someone gave her her time in the sun.

The BBC have released new-look images for their adaptation of Janice Hadlow's acclaimed novel, The Other Bennet Sister, which continues the story of Mary Bennet after Jane Austen's Pride and Prejudice ends.

There have been, of course, several excellent adaptations of Austen's most beloved novel for television, including 1995's utterly brilliant Pride & Prejudice starring Colin Firth and Jennifer Ehle, and Netflix have announced their own serial version of the story to be released later this year. But this is the first time someone other than Lizzie, Jane and Lydia will get their moment to shine.

What is The Other Bennet Sister about?

Hadlow's novel, The Other Bennet Sister, which is a brilliant read, was released in 2020 to much excitement; we'd never had Mary's version of events before. Would she marry Mr Collins? Would she finally get to finish playing her piano uninterrupted?

The novel shows a different side to the middle Bennet sister, the one we all laughed at in Austen's story, and have continued to do so in every adaptation since. The plainest of them all, Mary is an introvert in a family of extroverts; she seems destined to live out her life in Longbourn with her parents. That is, until her father dies and the slimy Mr Collins inherits the house, suddenly her story seems to turn around.

The story offers a refreshing take on one of Austen's most unassuming and misunderstood characters as she goes on a journey of self-discovery and love when she is forced to step out of her sisters' shadows. This is one for all the shy, awkward girls who always felt like they didn't quite fit in, who felt like they were constantly disappointing their parents, and who never quite lived up to the beauty of their sisters - Mary is all of us in a way, really.

As Hadlow writes in her novel; 'It is a sad fact of life that if a young woman is unlucky enough to come into the world without expectations, she had better do all she can to ensure she is born beautiful. To be handsome and poor is misfortune enough; but to be both plain and penniless is a hard fate indeed.'

For lovers of Austen and Bridgerton, The Other Bennet Sister will take you back into Georgian society from the point of view of someone who is wonderfully normal and comfortingly insecure, as she finds love and discovers herself along the way.

When will the BBC's adaptation of The Other Bennet Sister be released?

Along with a brand new batch of images from the series, the BBC have announced this week that their adaptation of The Other Bennet Sister will hit our screens in March 2026, premiering exclusively on BBC One and BBC iPlayer, and in the US and Canada it will premiere on BritBox.

You can watch the official teaser trailer for The Other Bennet Sister below:

Who will star in The Other Bennet Sister?

The series will star Ella Bruccoleri (Call the Midwife and Bridgerton) as Mary Bennet, with Richard E. Grand and Rith Jones as Mr and Mrs Bennet. Indira Varma and Richard Coyle will play Mr and Mrs Gardiner, whilst Laurie Davidson and Dónal Finn play potential suitors Mr Ryder and Mr Hayward, respectively. Of course, we can't have a Pride & Prejudice spin-off without a Mr Collins, and everyone's favourite slimy reverend will be played by Ryan Sampson.

The series' release coincides with December 2025's celebration of Jane Austen's 250th birthday - which was celebrated all year last year. If, like me, you're utterly Austen obsessed, we've put together the ultimate Jane Austen tour of England here so you can experience all the places she lived, wrote and was inspired during her lifetime.

item.title: 10 unmissable things to do in Suffolk this spring – see the scenery that made John Constable famous and join the 250th birthday celebrations
item.dc:creator: Sally Coffey, Editor
item.description: Sally Coffey picks 10 of the best things to do in and around the Dedham Vale in the 250th anniversary year of the birth of John Constable
item.content:encoded:
John Constable was born in the Stour Valley village of East Bergholt 250 years ago, in 1776. He had a strong affection for his homeland of Suffolk, reportedly telling a friend: “I should paint my own places best; painting with me is but another word for feeling.”

Sitting on England's eastern edge, and forming part of East Anglia, Constable's childhood in Suffolk instilled in him a deep knowledge and love for the countryside here, inspiring his art and later becoming the hallmark of his paintings.

For a better understanding of this much-loved, but possibly least understood, artist, Constable: A Portrait by James Hamilton reveals the complex man behind the paintings we know and love, and dives deeper into the ways in which his environments, including beautiful Suffolk, inspired him.

If you're visiting Suffolk this spring, there are plenty of ways to celebrate the artist in his homecounty, particularly around the Dedham Vale where he lived and grew up. Here, our editor guides us through her picks of the very best ways to celebrate one of England's greatest artists.

10 unmissable things to do in Suffolk this spring to celebrate Constable's 250th birthday

1. Visit his hometown of East Bergholt

Born into a wealthy family – his father was a mill owner – he described his childhood as ‘careless’, and though East Bergholt Mansion, where he lived, was demolished a long time ago, a plaque in the village centre marks its spot. 

You can also see the site of his former studio (now part of the local garage) and visit the graves of his parents, Golding and Ann Watts Constable, who are buried in a family plot in the graveyard of St Mary the Virgin Church, which featured in his 1810 painting The Church Porch, East Bergholt. 

Interestingly, the church is different from others in the area in that it doesn’t have a bell tower. Though work on a tower began under Cardinal Wolsey in 1525, work was abandoned after his downfall, and a bell cage was put up ‘temporarily’ which remains today. It is the only bell cage where the bells are swung using just the force of the hand as opposed to the rope-and-wheel system seen elsewhere.

2. Visit Flatford, the setting for The Hay Wain

This little hamlet in the heart of the picturesque Dedham Vale was the real-life setting for arguably Constable’s most famous painting: The Hay Wain.

Featuring Willy Lott’s Cottage with a horse and cart in front, The Hay Wain, completed in 1821, won plaudits due to Constable’s use of a lighter palette than was fashionable for the period and for both his sense of rural realism and ‘atmospheric truth’, which were quite radical ideas at the time.

Constable’s father owned Flatford Mill and knew Willy Lott personally – he featured in some of the artist’s other sketches. Today looked after by the National Trust, the Flatford Mill site, which includes Willy Lott’s Cottage, is a lovely place to visit.

There is a tearoom overlooking the river, while the granary is home to an interesting Constable exhibition. There’s also a restored 17th-century thatched cottage that gives further insight into rural life in the area, while guided tours, available from spring through to autumn, help bring to life Constable’s time spent here.

3. Stay in the Sun Inn

Flatford is a beautiful if muddy (depending on the season, sometimes crunchy) walk across the fields of Dedham Vale from the pretty village of Dedham, which strictly speaking lies just over the county border in Essex. Nevertheless this is undoubtedly Constable Country.

The Sun Inn is the kind of pub that would make any village proud and sits at the heart of local life. All wooden floorboards, muddy paw-prints, walking boots by the fire and plates piled high with local produce, it’s a place that brims with life from morning to night with locals gathering to catch up over a pint or a bite and generously welcoming visitors into the fold.

A Sunday roast with all the trimmings is the perfect way to round off a day tramping round the countryside, while the cosy rooms upstairs make it a great base for exploring Constable sights over a couple of days or more.

4. See a Constable church painting

Just across the road from The Sun Inn, work on this parish church (on the site of an earlier church) began in 1492, the year that Columbus discovered America.

The church will be familiar to fans of Constable’s work, as the church tower appears in many of his works, and it even features one of his paintings inside. The Ascension is one of just three religious paintings by Constable and was commissioned in 1821 by Edward Alston, Constable’s cousin by marriage, as an altarpiece for St Michael’s Church Manningtree. Alston, who was a local brewer, was no doubt hoping the painting would curry favour with the Archdeacon of Colchester who held the power to license public houses.

After the demolition of St Michael’s, the painting was moved to another church further away until the Trustees of the Constable Trust were eventually able to buy it and return it to the heart of Constable Country.

5. Mess about on the River Stour

Constable regularly painted the River Stour and far from just recreating picturesque scenes, his paintings would include barges and lighters, which showed the working side of the river.

There are a few options for visitors to get on the water themselves, including a charter aboard the restored John Constable Lighter, based at Sudbury, which you can hire for four hours for £220.

For a more genteel approach to touring the river, rowing boats can be hired from the Boatyard restaurant in East Bergholt in spring and summer and on weekends in September and October, on a first-come, first-row basis.

From the embarkation point, passengers can row themselves down toward Flatford for a river view of the hamlet, much as Constable himself would have seen it.

6. Discover Kersey

In the backwaters of Suffolk, between East Begholt and the beautiful wool town of Lavenham, lies another pretty place that is far below the radar, which Constable would probably have known.

Kersey is an unspoiled medieval village where timber-framed houses line the main street and an ancient ford known as The Splash crosses the River Brett. Kersey was another prosperous wool town, which created its own brand of twillcloth known as ‘Kersey cloth’. However, it has little of Lavenham’s fame and it is so charming and unassuming it’s featured on TV in both Magpie Murders and Lovejoy. Stop by, if only for a spot of lunch in the 13th-century Bell Inn.

7. Take an equestrian excursion

This year marks 65 years of The Munnings Art Museum in Dedham Vale, which displays the works of another son of a local mill owner who became an artist: Sir Alfred Munnings.

Housed in the old Tudor building complete with Georgian makeover where Munnings lived for more than 40 years and which he called the ‘house of my dreams’, it’s a great way to break up a Constable-heavy itinerary.

In 2026, His Majesty the King and Queen Camilla paid a visit to view the fine collection of the equestrian painter’s work, and if it’s good enough for them…

8. Pay homage to Gainsborough

Constable’s career owed a lot to one of his predecessors, Thomas Gainsborough, who also spent much of his childhood painting and sketching his Suffolk surrounds, this time near his hometown of Sudbury An accomplished artist who lived just 16 miles downriver from Constable, though Gainsborough later found fame largely for his portraits, landscape painting – especially of the Suffolk countryside – was his first love. From 25 April to 11 October, the museum and gallery inside his birthplace, known as Gainsborough’s House, will be hosting a Gainsborough, Turner and Constable exhibition that will essentially be a celebration of the art of landscape painting.

9. Be inspired on the Painters Trail

You can tie all these renowned artists together with a bike ride or amble through the 70-mile route known as the Painter’s Trail, which weaves through pretty villages like Nayland (pictured).

Many of the scenes and landscapes painted by Constable and Gainsborough remain largely unchanged since they caught the artists’ eyes, with the exception of the occasional modern addition.

Look out for the settings of paintings such as Stour Valley and Dedham Village, and Boys Fishing as you go, and if you’re so inclined, you can even pull up an easel and capture your own interpretation.

10. See a landmark exhibition

In 2026, the birth of Suffolk’s famous son will be celebrated in force at Ipswich’s Christchurch mansion, home to the largest collection of Constable works outside London.

From March 2026 to March 2027 the museum’s Constable 250 programme will include three exhibitions, featuring some rare artworks, including loans from the National Gallery, Tate, Victoria & Albert Museum, Royal Academy and private collections, among others.

Constable: A Cast of Characters (28 March – 14 June 2026) will celebrate some of the people who inspired and supported Constable – including Gainsborough – in his career, as well as examine the Regency era in which he worked.

Constable: Walking the Landscape, The Hay Wain (11 July – 4 October 2026) is a landmark exhibition that will see The Hay Wain go on display in the county it depicts for the first time ever. 

Finally, Constable to Contemporary (24 October 2026 – 28 February 2027) will explore the legacy Constable has left on those who have come after him, with pieces created especially for the exhibition by members of the local community.

Visit Suffolk has lots of great advice for visitors wishing to travel through the region, while Britain’s Best Guides can match you with an expert guide for a private tour to suit your needs.

item.title: What really inspired Wuthering Heights – tragedy, trauma and the violent destruction of the English idyll
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Here, we look at why the historic context of Wuthering Heights is more important, and much darker, than you could have imagined
item.content:encoded:
Behind the bodice-ripping romance of Emerald Fennell’s new Wuthering Heights, brought to life vehemently by Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi, there lies a salient commentary on Victorian society that, when enjoyed with a glass of wine and a gaggle of girlfriends, is in danger of being missed. 

Of course, Fennell’s adaptation of Emily Brontë’s beloved story can and should be enjoyed as a standalone piece of art, thanks to its maximalist, dream-like aesthetic and passionate acting of the principal actors. 

But, a little historic context will help you understand the story and the novel for what it really is - a brilliant, ahead-of-its-time reflection of Victorian social class, and the massive, cataclysmic effects of the Industrial Revolution on rural Britain.

Of course, Emily’s own short and tragic life played into her storytelling, too. To get the most comprehensive understanding of Emily’s passions, experiences and influences, this biography by Winifred Gerin is considered to be the best; an impressive feat for a figure who, despite being one of Britain’s most famous novelists, led a frustratingly mysterious life. 

Emily Brontë, whose life was plagued by tragedy after he mother died when she was very young, first published her debut and singular novel in 1847. The story is actually set more than 50 years earlier, spanning 1771-1802, harking back to the heyday of the Romantic movement in literature and art - a calculated choice on her behalf. 

So what was happening at the time Emily was writing Wuthering Heights, and did it influence her?

Well, the short answer is, yes, of course it did. A good novel is never just a good story, though it can be enjoyed as such, and Wuthering Heights is unremovable from its historical and social context. 

The story’s timeline overlaps with the beginnings of the Industrial Revolution and the expansion of the British Empire, enormous events in the 19th-century, that had massive, irreversible consequences on British society. 

Set during the shift from agriculture to industry, the novel explores the decline of the traditional landed gentry (the Earnshaws) and the rise of the working-class, self-made man (Heathcliff). 

Although Brontë’s native swirling, haunting Yorkshire Moors where Wuthering Heights was set, and where filming for Fennell’s adaptation took place (see the real-life filming locations here), seem far removed from the sooty factories of the Industrial Revolution, they were actually close to its heartlands. The now tranquil town of Haworth, where the Brontë sisters lived, the daughters of a Clergyman, and where the Bronte Parsonage Museum is now, had a death rate that equalled that of London in 1853, with the average age of death just 24. Brontë herself died of Tuberculosis, aged just 30. 

In the novel, Brontë uses the stark contrast between the two main houses, the poor, dark, groaning walls of Wuthering Heights and the grand, colourful Thrushcross Grange, to reflect the societal, economic and class shifts that the Industrial Revolution brought about, and her view of them as destructive of rural society,. 

She makes commentary on the immorality of wealth and the rich; wealthy characters are snobbish and badly portrayed in the novel, and, despite her hopes, Catherine’s lot in life is not improved by marrying Edgar. In the story, the eventual triumph of a new generation - the young Cathy and Hareton - reflects Brontë’s desire for a blending of old rural traditions with the benefits of new ‘civilised’ values. 

Heathcliff’s mysterious origins have a point too. He was picked up in the great industrial city of Liverpool, black-haired and speaking ‘some gibberish’. Liverpool was famed for its Transatlantic slave trade until about 1807, and historians of the novel have often linked these two events together. In the novel, however, he is also guessed to be ‘a little Lascar’ (an Indian sailor or military servant), a ‘gipsy brat,’or an ‘American or Spanish castway’, all of which could have been possible in such an international city, with maritime links across the empire. 

The novel’s late 18th/early-19th-century setting ties in with the Gothic tradition and Romantic movement popular at that time, reflected in its themes of death, the wild, isolated settings and even the supernatural. Brontë has created the character of the dark, mysterious Heathcliff and his obsessive, destructive passion as a quintessentially Byronic hero. 

And what female novelist of the 19th-century wrote a novel with a female protagonist without making a comment on the status of women in society? Wuthering Heights highlights how marriage was used as an economic transaction to secure property, leaving women in a vulnerable, submissive position. Cathy has to choose her husband based on her social upbringing, rather than the man she loves and you can guess Emily Brontë’s opinion of that because, no spoilers, it doesn’t work out very well for her. Brontë, of course, never married. 

Emily Brontë challenged traditional, narrow Victorian minds with her novel, which is probably why she published it first under a male pseudonym, and it was met with outrage and comments on its ‘vulgar depravity and unnatural horrors.’ But still she did it, and what a feat that was. 

Although the point of Emerald Fennell’s Wuthering Heights adaptation is certainly more Gothic pleasure than societal commentary, and don’t get me wrong, I love it for that, an understanding of its historic context brings a whole new level of meaning to the story. Fennell cleverly weaves these ideas into her film that you may not have noticed otherwise, from the rich splendour of Cathy’s married life to the dark, brooding world of Heathcliff’s, and I think Emily Brontë would have loved it, really.

item.title: How a tiny English town’s 600-year-old pancake race became a global phenomenon
item.dc:creator: Nancy Alsop, Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: British towns and villages often have their own quirky traditions, and few display them quite as ardently as Olney on Pancake Day, whose tradition pre-dates the Wars of the Roses
item.content:encoded:
At 11.55am on Pancake Day in the Buckinghamshire town of Olney, a church warden will command a group of 20-or-so well-primed women to toss their pancakes. This eccentric instruction will coincide with the sounding of the church shriving bell, its toll calling the townsfolk to be shriven of their sins before Lent.

This is the Olney Pancake Day race, undoubtedly the most famous pancake day tradition in Britain, and one that, unsurprisingly, has absolutely nothing to do with the origins of pancake day itself. With its own quirky origin story dating back nearly 500 years, the female-only race has expanded from a medieval tradition in this tiny Buckinghamshire town, to a global event.

Of course, it being such an old and established tradition, there is a wonderful book on the matter. The Story of the Olney Pancake Race: A Pictorial Archive Portraying the History of the Race by Graham Lenton compiles hundreds of wonderful photographs from the event over 50 years, a lovely keepsake for a peculiar British tradition.

What are the rules of the Olney Pancake Day race?

The 415-yard race, whose history stretches back centuries, is the oldest pancake race in the world. The criteria binding participants? They must be women, have lived in Olney for a minimum of three months and be aged over 18. 

That is not to say that the race is without rules. On the contrary, the good people of Olney take their flipping very seriously indeed – and it isn’t only speed that’s of the essence. The women must be able to toss their pancake intact at the finish line if they wish to be crowned victorious, all while wearing a skirt and a headscarf – because what is an eccentric British custom without a costume?

Mercifully, the townsfolk are generous with their rewards: as well as a prize for the fastest, there are also baubles for the oldest participant and the one who raises the most for charity. 

What is the history of the Olney Pancake Day race?

The Pancake Day tradition is said to stretch back to before the Wars of The Roses, having begun in 1445. Quite how it all started is still debated. The most popular theory goes that proceedings can be traced back to the year a harassed housewife scurried to church upon hearing the shriving bell, her frying pan – complete with pancake – still in hand. Another goes that the people of Olney, keen to kick off the day’s merriment, would bribe the sexton with pancakes to ring the bell early and thus initiate the revelries. 

Olney’s race has fallen into fallow periods over the centuries, notably during the Second World War. But ever since peace was restored, so the race has been too. And for that, Olney’s pancake flippers can thank the Reverend Canon Ronald Collins who, upon unearthing photographs in 1948 of festivities from the thirties, resolved to revive the custom.

The Olney Pancake Race in Kansas

It has stuck, even garnering international admirers. In 1950, the town of Liberal in Kansas, USA, began emulating the tradition and, to this day, the incongruously coupled towns compete annually for the fastest time. 

The tradition of eating pancakes on Shrove Tuesday (or Pancake Day) – the day when Christians historically confessed their sins to be granted absolution – marks the last hurrah before Lent’s 40 days of fasting, in part to use up stores of dairy products.

The races, meanwhile, were held because the faithful were expected not only to relinquish indulgent food but frivolous pastimes too. And, as befits the idiosyncrasies of British tradition, the day has thrown up a number of other unusual rituals over the centuries. 

Here are some more of the strangest pancake traditions in Britain:

The Greaze, Westminster School

One such is The Greaze. Since the mid-1700s, London’s elite Westminster School has hosted an annual pancake day scramble, a chaotic event that sees the school cook toss a pancake over an iron bar, while the pupils compete to grab the largest scrap, after which the Dean of Westminster grants the whole school a day’s holiday. In 1919, it was even attended by King George V and Queen Mary, with the two future monarchs in tow too: their sons, the future Edward VIII and George VI. 

But Westminster School is not alone in marking the event with a physical tussle. Shrove Tuesday seems to have elicited some violent tendencies amongst the British populace, with Samuel Pepys describing gobbling pancakes while watching ill-fated cockerels being tied to a stake and pelted to death.

Shrovetide football games

Thankfully, the 12th-century ‘Shrovetide’ football games were somewhat less violent, as whole communities would compete to successfully convey a ball from one end of the town to another. And, whilst that particular marking of Lent might have gone the way of the cockerel cudgelling, the Derbyshire town of Ashbourne still flies the flag for the tradition; an annual semi-lawless game of football sees the town fight it out over two days to land the ball into one or other of the goals, each three miles apart. 

These days, most of us celebrate with a twist of lemon and a sprinkling of sugar, probably using this excellent Victorian traditional pancake recipe as our guide. This year, however, we’ll do so in bonnet-wearing style while sparing a thought for Pepys’ poor cockerel and the pupils of Westminster School as they prepare for the rough and tumble of the fray.

item.title: The no-fuss 200-year-old pancake recipe – guaranteed to deliver perfect Shrove Tuesday crêpes
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: This tried and tested pancake recipe from 1852 is a foolhardy way to make the best pancakes this Pancake Day, plus some wonderfully British ways to brighten up your Shrove Tuesday
item.content:encoded:
After the long, dark weeks of January and early February, by the time Pancake Day comes around, the world is beginning to feel a little brighter, with hopes of spring, new beginnings, and Easter just around the corner.

Certainly, my favourite spring tradition in Britain, the origins of pancake day, like most of Britain's quirkiest traditions, lie in Paganism. It was adopted by the Church much later, marking the beginning of Lent and a way to use up our most indulgent ingredients before the period of fasting begins.

Whether or not you opt in to Lent, Pancake Day, or Shrove Tuesday, is a delicious way to brighten up your February. For hundreds of years, we have eaten pancakes 40 days and 40 nights before Easter. Recipes haven't changed much in that time, and simple is definitely best when it comes to pancakes, so I've found a Victorian recipe below that makes the best pancakes you've ever tasted - give it a try, and it will be the only recipe you'll ever need for all Shrove Tuesdays to come.

Like all the best British traditions, especially spring traditions that herald warmer months ahead, I think Pancake Day deserves special treatment. Below are some lovely ways brighten up your Pancake Day from some brilliant British brands, and to make sure you make your best-ever pancakes year after year.

Professional Granite Crepe Pan from ProCook

RRP£35.00

RRP£23

RRP£19
Available to shop from Amazon
Shop now

Try this Victorian traditional English pancake recipe this Shrove Tuesday

Now that you're all kitted out to make your pancakes, this Victorian pancake recipe makes the most delicious traditional pancakes, every time.

The recipe from a book called Good Things in England, which, first published in 1932, is a collection of 853 regional English recipes, some dating as far back as the 14th-century. It was written by Florence White, Britain's first ever freelance food journalist, and is a classic, wonderful cookbook that is still publishing new editions today. You can get your hands on a copy here.

This traditional pancake recipe hails from Somersetshire in 1852.

Ingredients:

  • 2 oz plain flour

  • 1 egg

  • 1 gill of milk (a historical measurement, translating to 142ml)

  • A pinch of salt

  • 2 oz clarified fat for frying

  • 2 oz caster sugar

  • Lemon juice

Method:

1. Mix the flour and salt together, and make into a batter with the egg and milk.

2. Heat the frying-pan, add a little fat.

3. Make it quite hot, and pour in enough batter to cover the pan thinly.

4. When golden brown on one side, toss or turn and fry the other.

5. Squeeze a little lemon juice over it, dust with caster sugar, roll up and serve dusted with caster sugar.

As you can see, this nearly 200-year old pancake recipe looks much like those we'd try now - Victorians weren't so different from us! Whether or not you take part in Lent, today Pancake Day is a delicious way of brightening up winter's darkest months, and has evolved now to be a staple day in the British calendar, complete with national and local events like the Olney Pancake Day race. Happy baking!

item.title: The secret history of Shrove Tuesday: pagan fire festivals, public confessions and the birth of pancake day
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: We explore the peculiar history of Shrove Tuesday in Britain, from its Pagan origins to its modern popularity as Pancake Day...
item.content:encoded:
Initially a feast to celebrate the arrival of spring, Pancake Day (or Shrove Tuesday) is still one of the most indulgent days of the year, and one of my favourites. What could be better than eggs, milk and flour, fried and covered in sugar and deliciousness?

As with most European Christian traditions, Shrove Tuesday, or Pancake Day, started out as a Pagan spring tradition in Britain.

The way it has evolved over the years to be a staple food-themed event in the British calendar is testament to how much of a sweet tooth we have as a country, as well as how much we love weird and wonderful traditions. From pancake races to flipping competitions, Pancake Day is unrecognisable now compared to its Pagan origins.

If you've always wondered what the origins are for some of Britain's more bizarre food traditions, like pancake racing and cheese racing, this brilliant book on Britain's foodie folklore by Ben Gazur uncovers how our food habits have been passed down through generations — it was also shortlisted for the Fortnum & Mason Debut Food Book Award in 2025, so it must be good.

For now, as we tuck into our delicious pancakes - will you have them for breakfast, lunch, supper, or all three? -here, are 10 facts about the origins of Pancake Day you probably never knew:

1. Before the Christian era, the Slavs believed that the change of seasons was a struggle between Jarilo, the god of vegetation, fertility and springtime, and the evil spirits of cold and darkness, and that they had to help Jarilo in his worthy efforts. The whole celebration of the arrival of spring lasted a week and a large part of this was making and eating pancakes. The hot, round pancakes symbolised the sun and the Slavs believed that by eating pancakes, they got the power, light and warmth of the sun.

2. The religious association of Shrove Tuesday began because the day preceding Ash Wednesday presented an opportunity to use up rich foods such as eggs, milk and sugar before the fasting season of the 40 days of Lent. The Christian liturgical fasting encouraged eating plainer food and avoiding food that would give pleasure – namely meat, dairy products and eggs.

3. The name Shrove Tuesday derives from the practice of Anglo-Saxon Christians going into confession the day before Lent, and being ‘shriven’ (absolved of their sins). 

4. A bell would be rung to call people to confession, which became known as the ‘Pancake Bell’ and is still rung today.

5. It is a moveable date on the Christian calendar as it always falls 47 days before Easter Sunday, so varies from year to year.

6. The traditional English pancake is very thin and is served immediately after frying, usually covered in golden syrup or lemon juice and caster sugar.

7. The tradition of tossing or flipping them is also very old: “And every man and maide doe take their turne, And tosse their Pancakes up for feare they burne.” (Pasquil’s Palin, 1619).

if you'd like to get involved with some of Britain's more bizarre Pancake Day traditions, you can discover the origins of the Olney Pancake Race here, which also has sister races in the US!

item.title: Scandal, secrecy and undying devotion: what The National Archives reveals about Elizabeth I and her most intimate confidant
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: Love Letters at the National Archives changed everything I thought I knew about Elizabeth and Dudley’s relationship, and made me fall in love all over again...and again....
item.content:encoded:
To be addressed by anyone as 'my gratious lady' would make any sane person's heart flutter, and that's exactly how Robert Dudley, Earl of Leicester, addressed Queen Elizabeth I in a letter in 1588, just days before his death.

Was the Virgin Queen really in love with her courtier? Historians continue to debate, but the great were they/weren’t they mystery of it all firmly places their love for each other - platonic or romantic - in our list of the greatest love stories in British history.

Dudley's 'Last Lettar' to Elizabeth I is currently on display at a totally free, exhibition at the National Archives in Kew, Love Letters, alongside other handwritten notes of affection from other greats of British history, including Oscar Wilde and Jane Austen.

If, like me, the exhibition had you feeling all soppy, the wonderful Stories for Lovers: 30 collects some brilliant short love stories from some well-known and much-loved female authors, including Margaret Atwood and Virginia Woolf, another Great Briton whose love story has gone down in history for being so unbelievably tragic.

And, there's no need to suppress your longing for romance because Love Letters at the National Archives is bringing visitors some of the most famous, and very often dangerous, love affairs in British history. I learnt a lot from the exhibition, in particular about Dudley and Elizabeth I's rumoured intimate relationship, which, to me, had only ever been just that - a rumour.

Learning that she kept Dudley's last letter to her by her bedside until she died and inscribed 'his Last Lettar' really pulled on my heartstrings and, felt like the first piece of conclusive evidence that they really were in love.

The letter had come from him at 'your old lodging at Rycott, the house where she and Dudley had stayed together in happier times, and there's no doubt that his death there after writing her this letter was significant. She was grief-stricken at his death, a friend whom she had known since childhood and who had been there with their through all the difficulties of her queenship.

Despite the fact that they, famously, never married, theirs is one of the greatest and my favourite love stories from Tudor England.

Another love story spotlighted in Love Letters is that from Catherine Howard to her supposed love Thomas Culpeper - is this the letter that led to her downfall and execution?

'It makes my heart die to think I cannot be always in your company', the young queen wrote to her childhood love, the courtier Thomas Culpeper, signing it, 'yours as long as life endures.' The only problem was that she was married to King Henry VIII at the time, a man famously not kind to his wives, Catherine being number five of six.

Although this was a fascinating letter to see in person, it didn't have quite the same romantic effect on me that Dudley's did, inciting more a feeling of sadness at the tragedy of a young, naive girl in love with a handsome young man, but forced to marry and nightly bed a man who was old enough to be her grandfather. Catherine, I'm sorry that happened to you.

The exhibition isn't just Tudor history, either; other writers of love letters of note include Oscar Wilde, Charles I, and the extraordinary culmination of the love affair that forced the king to abdicate his throne: Edward VIII and Wallis Simpson.

Another dying letter on display comes from Jane Austen, whose will is on show in the exhibition. This demonstrates not the romantic love that most of the other letters do, but the sisterly love she had for Cassandra Austen, to whom she left her manuscripts and most of her worldly possessions. For me, this was a welcome reminder of how important female friendships and sisters are in life, especially if, like Jane Austen, romantic love simply doesn't work out for you.

The Principal Records Specialist of Diverse Histories at the National Archives, Vicky Iglikowski-Broad, commented that the exhibition 'holds a surprising array of expressions of love — some criminalised, unconsummated, or tragically cut short — found across 500 years of state records.

'This exhibition takes visitors on a journey of emotional connection and reflection, revealing how love connects all kinds of people and takes many forms, often in the most unexpected of places.'

A journey of emotional connection is definitely what I experienced from the exhibition, and it was a whirlwind to say the least. But if you're looking to feel a little bit of extra love at the moment, I couldn't recommend it enough.

The exhibition runs until 12 April 2026.

item.title: Stop scrolling – these 4 Yorkshire hotels make living out your Wuthering Heights daydreams shockingly easy
item.dc:creator: Henrietta Easton, Digital Editor
item.description: As we all fall in love with Cathy, Heathcliff and the Yorkshire Moors that inspired Emily Brontë, these 4 Yorkshire hotels are the perfect place to live out your Wuthering Heights fantasies...
item.content:encoded:
We've always known that the Yorkshire landscapes that inspired the Brontë sisters were worth visiting, but Emerald Fennell's new "Wuthering Heights" movie adaptation has brought their heather-clad hills and romantic postcard villages to the attention of the world.

As the region becomes the next big beneficiary of screen tourism, following in the path of Bridgerton in Bath, travellers can look to some of Yorkshire's cosiest inns to provide a warming base after a day exploring the Moors, with roaring fires, hearty food and enormous beds you can climb into.

But, we think we can do one better. As set-jetting inspires travellers to explore Emily Brontë's hometown and inspirations in Yorkshire, we've put together a list of Wuthering Heights-inspired hotels, either because they are close to the real Wuthering Heights filming locations, or because they evoke the same sense of gothic, Victoriana maximalism that audiences will experience in the new film.

Screen tourism has taken Britain by storm in recent years, as directors look the country's beautiful landscapes and cultural heritage to provide the real backdrop of their storytelling. The CEO of the official UK tourism company VisitBritain commented that "Our research shows that more than 9-out-of-10 potential tourists are keen to visit the real-life film and TV sets of our vibrant destinations and stunning countryside. We look forward to rolling out the red carpet in West Yorkshire to future ‘set jetters’ when “Wuthering Heights” hits the cinema screens.”

If, after watching the new film, you are inspired to visit Yorkshire's windswept moors and do some brooding, Heathcliff-style, yourself, we have chosen 5 of our favourite Yorkshire hotels you should book to live out your Wuthering Heights fantasies...

1. Holdsworth House Hotel, Halifax

Just a twenty-minute drive from Haworth, where the Brontë Parsonage Museum is and where the Brontë sisters lived and wrote, and surrounded by their beloved Moors, this 4-star independent hotel is housed in a beautiful Jacobean mansion, and even offers its very own Brontë Country Escape Package.

View this post on Instagram

The package includes an overnight stay with breakfast, tickets to the Brontë Parsonage Museum, a souvenir guide and a walking map of Haworth, so guests can walk in the footsteps of the authors and see for themselves the landscapes that inspired them.

The hotel also has direct links to the film because it hosted Wuthering Heights director Emerald Fennell, and members of the production team on an early location recce in the area, and where stars Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi dined and stayed following their final day of filming on the shoot.

Book your stay at Holdsworth House Hotel via Expedia here.

To celebrate the release of the film, in collaboration with Airbnb, Holdsworth House Hotel has also created a replica of Cathy Earnshaw's bedroom from Wuthering Heights and is offering three lucky couples the chance to stay their, for free. Bookings open Friday 20 February - set your alarms!

2. Simonstone Hall, Hawes

Another hotel with direct connections to Emerald Fennell's Wuthering Heights is the country house hotel Simonstone Hall in Hawes. The cast, crew and Emerald Fennell reserved the entire 20 rooms of the hotel for two and a half weeks while filming on the Moors nearby, and ran through scripts and performed interviews onsite at the hotel.

Making the most of their spotlight, Simonstone Hall is offering Wuthering Heights-themed stays in February and March 2026 so guests can experience what Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi did during their stay.

The romantic two-night getaway for two includes Champagne and chocolate-covered strawberries upon arrival in your room, a two-course candelit dinner with wine pairings, and brandy and truffles to enjoy by the fire before bed. The stay is for a principal room which includes a superking bed, a large bath, a rainfall shower, and views of the surrounding Moors; modern luxuries we think Cathy would certainly have approved of, and essential after a day exploring the windswept filming locations nearby.

Book your stay at Simonstone Hall via Expedia here.

3. The Devonshire Arms Hotel, Skipton

Set in the grounds of the 30,000-acre Bolton Abbey Estate, a hauntingly Gothic cathedral ruined in the Dissolution that oozes Wuthering Heights style, The Devonshire Arms Hotel is in the Yorkshire home of the Duke and Duchess of Devonshire.

Certainly fit for the luxurious tastes of Cathy Earnshaw, the 18th-century restored hotel has two distinctive wings available to guests, the original wing with its traditional rooms rich in period charm and featuring four-poster beds, and the newer wing for those seeking more contemporary comfort. There are also two dining rooms inside the hotel, as well as a tranquil spa with a vitality pool, sauna, steam room and Rasul chamber.

The Devonshire Arms offers unrivalled access to all the Yorkshire Dales has to offer, and is a stylish base for exploring the Wuthering Heights filming locations, as well as the nearby Brontë sites like their house museum, and Top Withens.

Book your stay at The Devonshire Arms via Expedia here.

4. The Black Swan, Olstead

View this post on Instagram

This charming little country inn feels very Brontë-esque, neslted in the North York Moors National Park and near the impressive Byland Abbey ruin. But, it holds a very special secret.

Behind its historic walls and cosy interiors, The Black Swan is a Michelin-starred, 4 AA Rosette restaurant with rooms by chef Tommy Banks.

Inside, the decor is simple and traditional, and a rugged Heathcliff drinking from a frothy tankard after a day searching for Cathy on the Moors wouldn't look out of place here. But the menus are packed full of creative flare and offer modern, exuberant dining that go hand in hand with Fennell's vision for the her Wuthering Heights adaptation.

The nine bedrooms are named after the surrounding Dales and woodland and perfectly mix the traditional character of the inn, with the luxury the inn exudes - a welcome spot to retire to after a delicious meal.

Book your stay at The Black Swan via Expedia here.

If, like us, Wuthering Heights has inspired you to explore more real-life filming sets and places to stay in Britain, here is our guide to the very best Bridgerton-style hotels in the south of England - the perfect place to escape from the gossip of the ton.
